- **Packet size (IEEE 802.15.4):** **127 bytes** maximum PHY payload

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Detailed Breakdown of Major Network Characteristics

#### A. Network Topology

The topology defines the **logical interconnection pattern** of sensor nodes.

| Topology | Description | Advantages | Disadvantages |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Star** | All nodes communicate directly with a central sink. | Simple, low latency for small area. | Single point of failure, limited scalability. |
| **Mesh (Peer-to-Peer)** | Every node can forward data for any other node. | High fault tolerance, self-healing. | Complex routing, higher energy due to multi-hop. |
| **Tree / Cluster (Hierarchical)** | Nodes organized in parent-child clusters (e.g., LEACH). | Energy efficient via aggregation. | Cluster head failure disrupts subtree. |
| **Hybrid** | Combines star + mesh (e.g., ZigBee). | Balanced performance. | Complex protocol design. |

#### B. Node Deployment

- **Deterministic (Planned) Deployment:** Nodes are placed at predefined locations (e.g., in industrial plant monitoring). Predictable coverage; efficient use of nodes.
- **Random Deployment:** Nodes are scattered (e.g., dropped by aircraft in a forest). Requires **redundancy** to ensure coverage; statistical models (Poisson point process) are used.

> [!NOTE]
> **Poisson Point Process (PPP):** For random deployment, node locations are often modeled as a 2D PPP with intensity $\lambda$ (nodes per unit area). The probability of exactly $k$ nodes in an area $A$ is $P(k) = \frac{(\lambda A)^k e^{-\lambda A}}{k!}$.

#### C. Energy Consumption Model

A widely used first-order radio energy model (Heinzelman et al.):

- To transmit an $L$-bit packet over distance $d$:
  $$E_{TX}(L, d) = \begin{cases} L \cdot E_{elec} + L \cdot \epsilon_{fs} \cdot d^2 & d < d_0 \\ L \cdot E_{elec} + L \cdot \epsilon_{mp} \cdot d^4 & d \geq d_0 \end{cases}$$
- To receive an $L$-bit packet:
  $$E_{RX}(L) = L \cdot E_{elec}$$
- The crossover distance is:
  $$d_0 = \sqrt{\frac{\epsilon_{fs}}{\epsilon_{mp}}}$$

#### D. Network Capacity

The **transport capacity** (Gupta & Kumar, 2000) of a wireless network of $n$ nodes in an area of unit disk is:
$$C(n) = \Theta\left(\sqrt{\frac{W \cdot n}{\log n}}\right) \quad \text{bits per second}$$
where $W$ is the channel bandwidth and the $\log n$ term accounts for interference.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** As $n \to \infty$, the per-node capacity scales as $O\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{n \log n}}\right)$, meaning **adding more nodes reduces per-node throughput** — a fundamental WSN scalability challenge.

#### E. Coverage and Connectivity Trade-off

- **Coverage Probability** in a randomly deployed network with intensity $\lambda$ and sensing radius $r_s$:
  $$P_{cov} = 1 - e^{-\lambda \pi r_s^2}$$
- **k-Coverage Probability** (every point covered by at least $k$ nodes):
  $$P_{k\text{-cov}} = 1 - \sum_{i=0}^{k-1} \frac{(\lambda \pi r_s^2)^i e^{-\lambda \pi r_s^2}}{i!}$$
- **Critical Transmission Radius for Connectivity** (Krishnamachari, deployment for asymptotic connectivity in a square area of side $a$):
  $$r_c = \sqrt{\frac{\log n + c(n)}{\pi n}} \cdot a$$
  where $c(n) \to \infty$ slowly.

#### F. Network Lifetime

Common definitions used in KTU examinations:
1. **Time until first node dies (FND).**
2. **Time until $k\%$ of nodes die (e.g., 50% — HND).**
3. **Time until network partition occurs** (last node dies — LND).

Total energy budget of node $i$: $E_i^{total}$. The **minimum node lifetime**:
$$T_{life} = \min_{i \in N} \frac{E_i^{total}}{P_i^{avg}}$$
where $P_i^{avg}$ is the average power consumption of node $i$.

#### G. Fault Tolerance

Using **Reliability Theory**, if node failure rate is $\lambda_f$ (failures/hour), the reliability of a node over time $t$ is:
$$R(t) = e^{-\lambda_f t}$$
For $n$ nodes in a redundancy model (e.g., 1-out-of-$n$):
$$R_{system} = 1 - (1 - R_{node})^n$$

#### H. Latency

For an $h$-hop path with link delay $d_{link}$:
$$L_{end-to-end} = \sum_{i=1}^{h} d_{link,i} + T_{proc} + T_{queue}$$

#### I. Security Characteristics

WSNs face unique security challenges due to:
- **Resource constraints** (energy, memory, computation).
- **Wireless broadcast medium** (eavesdropping, jamming).
- **Ad-hoc deployment** (no trusted infrastructure).
- Goals: **Confidentiality, Integrity, Authentication, Availability, Freshness, Forward Secrecy.**

---

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!TIP]
> **Master this table — these formulas are favorites of KTU examiners for Part B derivations.**

| # | Characteristic | Formula / Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Energy to transmit $L$ bits over $d$ (free-space) | $E_{TX} = L \cdot E_{elec} + L \cdot \epsilon_{fs} \cdot d^2$ | Joules |
| 2 | Energy to transmit $L$ bits over $d$ (multi-path) | $E_{TX} = L \cdot E_{elec} + L \cdot \epsilon_{mp} \cdot d^4$ | Joules |
| 3 | Energy to receive $L$ bits | $E_{RX} = L \cdot E_{elec}$ | Joules |
| 4 | Crossover distance | $d_0 = \sqrt{\epsilon_{fs}/\epsilon_{mp}}$ | meters |
| 5 | Transport capacity (Gupta-Kumar) | $C(n) = \Theta(W\sqrt{n/\log n})$ | bits/sec |
| 6 | Per-node throughput | $\Theta(W/\sqrt{n \log n})$ | bits/sec/node |
| 7 | Coverage probability (PPP) | $P_{cov} = 1 - e^{-\lambda \pi r_s^2}$ | dimensionless |
| 8 | $k$-coverage probability | $P_{k\text{-cov}} = 1 - \sum_{i=0}^{k-1} \frac{(\lambda \pi r_s^2)^i e^{-\lambda \pi r_s^2}}{i!}$ | dimensionless |
| 9 | Critical radius for connectivity | $r_c = a\sqrt{(\log n + c(n))/(\pi n)}$ | meters |
| 10 | Node reliability | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda_f t}$ | dimensionless |
| 11 | $m$-out-of-$n$ system reliability | $R_{sys} = \sum_{i=m}^{n} \binom{n}{i} R^i (1-R)^{n-i}$ | dimensionless |
| 12 | Network lifetime (FND) | $T_{FND} = \min_i E_i^{total}/P_i^{avg}$ | seconds |
| 13 | Average end-to-end latency | $L = h \cdot d_{link} + T_{proc} + T_{queue}$ | seconds |
| 14 | Optimal cluster count (LEACH) | $k_{opt} = \sqrt{N/(2\pi)} \cdot \sqrt{\epsilon_{fs}/\epsilon_{mp}} \cdot (M/d_{toBS}^2)$ | dimensionless |
| 15 | Node degree (mean) in PPP | $\mathbb{E}[D] = \lambda \pi r_t^2$ | dimensionless |

**Standard symbol glossary:**

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|
| $E_{elec}$ | Per-bit electronics energy | **50 nJ/bit** |
| $\epsilon_{fs}$ | Free-space amplifier energy | **10 pJ/bit/m²** |
| $\epsilon_{mp}$ | Multi-path amplifier energy | **0.0013 pJ/bit/m⁴** |
| $\lambda$ | Node deployment intensity | nodes/m² |
| $r_s$ | Sensing radius | meters |
| $r_t$ | Transmission radius | meters |
| $W$ | Channel bandwidth | Hz |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Industrial IoT (IIoT) Monitoring:** Energy models guide battery sizing for 10-year deployments.
- **Precision Agriculture:** Coverage probability ensures no field patch is left unmonitored.
- **Smart Cities (Air Quality):** Scalability and transport capacity calculations inform gateway density planning.
- **Military Surveillance:** Fault tolerance and security are mission-critical.
- **Healthcare Body Area Networks (BAN):** Latency and reliability formulas are essential for life-critical applications.
- **Environmental Monitoring (volcanoes, forests):** Heterogeneous deployment with mobile sinks is designed using network lifetime equations.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Optimum Transmission Range for Energy Efficiency

**Problem Setup:** Consider a single node transmitting an $L$-bit message to a sink located at distance $d$ via direct transmission (1-hop) versus multi-hop relay through $h$ equal hops of length $d/h$ each. Derive the condition where multi-hop saves energy.

**Step 1 — Direct transmission energy:**

For $d \geq d_0$ (multi-path regime, dominant in outdoor WSNs):
$$E_{direct} = L \cdot E_{elec} + L \cdot \epsilon_{mp} \cdot d^4$$

**Step 2 — Multi-hop transmission energy (per hop):**

Each hop covers distance $d/h$. Assuming free-space for short hops ($d/h < d_0$):
$$E_{hop} = L \cdot E_{elec} + L \cdot \epsilon_{fs} \cdot (d/h)^2$$

There are $h$ hops, and each intermediate hop **receives and retransmits** once:
$$E_{multi-hop} = h \cdot E_{hop} = h \cdot \left[ L \cdot E_{elec} + L \cdot \epsilon_{fs} \cdot \frac{d^2}{h^2} \right]$$

Expanding:
$$E_{multi-hop} = h \cdot L \cdot E_{elec} + \frac{L \cdot \epsilon_{fs} \cdot d^2}{h}$$

**Step 3 — Compare energies (direct vs. multi-hop):**

Multi-hop is better when $E_{multi-hop} < E_{direct}$:
$$h \cdot L \cdot E_{elec} + \frac{L \cdot \epsilon_{fs} \cdot d^2}{h} < L \cdot \epsilon_{mp} \cdot d^4$$

Dividing both sides by $L$ (and using $d \geq d_0$ where direct is multi-path):
$$h \cdot E_{elec} + \frac{\epsilon_{fs} \cdot d^2}{h} < \epsilon_{mp} \cdot d^4$$

**Step 4 — Optimal number of hops (taking derivative):**

To minimize $E_{multi-hop}$ with respect to $h$, take $\frac{d E_{multi-hop}}{dh} = 0$:
$$\frac{d}{dh}\left[ h \cdot E_{elec} + \frac{\epsilon_{fs} \cdot d^2}{h} \right] = E_{elec} - \frac{\epsilon_{fs} \cdot d^2}{h^2} = 0$$

Solving:
$$h^2 = \frac{\epsilon_{fs} \cdot d^2}{E_{elec}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad h_{opt} = d \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\epsilon_{fs}}{E_{elec}}}$$

**Step 5 — Substituting back — the minimum multi-hop energy:**
$$E_{multi-hop}^{min} = h_{opt} \cdot E_{elec} + \frac{\epsilon_{fs} \cdot d^2}{h_{opt}} = 2 \cdot d \cdot \sqrt{E_{elec} \cdot \epsilon_{fs}}$$

**Step 6 — Direct transmission energy (using $d \geq d_0$):**
$$E_{direct} = L \cdot E_{elec} + L \cdot \epsilon_{mp} \cdot d^4$$

**Step 7 — The break-even distance condition:**

Setting $E_{multi-hop}^{min} = E_{direct}$ and ignoring $L \cdot E_{elec}$ term on direct (negligible vs. $d^4$ term for large $d$):
$$2 d \sqrt{E_{elec} \cdot \epsilon_{fs}} = \epsilon_{mp} \cdot d^4 \quad (\text{with } L=1)$$

Solving for $d$:
$$d_{break} = \left( \frac{2 \sqrt{E_{elec} \cdot \epsilon_{fs}}}{\epsilon_{mp}} \right)^{1/3}$$

**Conclusion:** For distances greater than $d_{break}$, multi-hop communication is more energy-efficient than direct transmission. This is one of the most important design rules in WSN energy-aware routing (e.g., PEGASIS, LEACH).

> [!NOTE]
> **Numerical sanity check:** With $E_{elec} = 50$ nJ/bit, $\epsilon_{fs} = 10$ pJ/bit/m², $\epsilon_{mp} = 0.0013$ pJ/bit/m⁴:
> $$d_{break} = \left( \frac{2 \sqrt{50 \times 10^{-9} \times 10 \times 10^{-12}}}{0.0013 \times 10^{-12}} \right)^{1/3} \approx 86 \text{ m}$$
> Beyond ~86 m, multi-hop wins — a useful KTU numerical.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Coverage Probability of Random Poisson Deployment

**Setup:** $n$ sensor nodes are deployed uniformly at random in a 2D region of area $A$. Each node has a circular sensing footprint of radius $r_s$. Determine the probability that an arbitrary point $P$ in the region is covered by **at least one** sensor.

**Step 1 — Model with Poisson Point Process:**

For large $n$ and large $A$ with finite intensity $\lambda = n/A$, the node locations form a 2D PPP.

**Step 2 — Compute $P(\text{no node covers } P)$:**

Point $P$ is covered by a node if that node lies within distance $r_s$ of $P$. The area of this disk is $\pi r_s^2$. For a PPP with intensity $\lambda$, the number of nodes in any area $S$ follows a Poisson distribution with mean $\lambda S$.

The probability of $k$ nodes in the disk around $P$:
$$P[N = k] = \frac{(\lambda \pi r_s^2)^k e^{-\lambda \pi r_s^2}}{k!}$$

**Step 3 — Probability of zero nodes in the disk (point $P$ not covered):**
$$P[N = 0] = e^{-\lambda \pi r_s^2}$$

**Step 4 — Coverage probability (at least one node covers $P$):**
$$P_{cov} = 1 - P[N = 0] = 1 - e^{-\lambda \pi r_s^2}$$

**Step 5 — Extension to k-coverage:**

Point $P$ is **k-covered** if at least $k$ nodes lie within the disk:
$$P_{k\text{-cov}} = 1 - P[N \leq k-1] = 1 - \sum_{i=0}^{k-1} \frac{(\lambda \pi r_s^2)^i e^{-\lambda \pi r_s^2}}{i!}$$

**Step 6 — Required node density for desired coverage:**

Setting $P_{cov} = 1 - \epsilon$ (e.g., $\epsilon = 0.01$ for 99% coverage):
$$1 - \epsilon = 1 - e^{-\lambda \pi r_s^2} \Rightarrow \lambda \pi r_s^2 = \ln(1/\epsilon)$$
$$\boxed{\lambda_{req} = \frac{\ln(1/\epsilon)}{\pi r_s^2}}$$

**Step 7 — Total number of nodes required for 99% coverage of area $A$:**
$$N_{req} = \lambda_{req} \cdot A = \frac{A \cdot \ln(100)}{\pi r_s^2} = \frac{A \cdot 4.605}{\pi r_s^2}$$

**Numerical check:** To cover an area of $A = 10,000 \text{ m}^2$ (100 m × 100 m) with sensing radius $r_s = 10$ m at 99% coverage:
$$N_{req} = \frac{10{,}000 \times 4.605}{\pi \times 100} \approx 147 \text{ nodes}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Network Lifetime (First Node Dies — FND)

**Setup:** A linear chain of $n$ sensor nodes reports data to the sink. Node $i$ has initial energy $E_0$ and consumes power $P_i$. Derive the time until the first node exhausts its battery.

**Step 1 — Energy evolution of node $i$:**
$$E_i(t) = E_0 - P_i \cdot t$$

**Step 2 — Failure time of node $i$:**
$$T_i = \frac{E_0}{P_i}$$

**Step 3 — First node dies at time:**
$$T_{FND} = \min_{i \in \{1,\ldots,n\}} T_i = \min_{i} \frac{E_0}{P_i} = \frac{E_0}{\max_i P_i}$$

**Step 4 — Conclusion (KTU-relevant):**

The FND is limited by the **most energy-stressed node** (the one with highest power consumption). For a multi-hop chain, this is usually the **node closest to the sink** because it relays the most traffic (relay bottleneck).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Implication:** To extend FND, deploy a **non-uniform energy distribution** — give more initial energy to bottleneck nodes (e.g., near the sink). This is the principle behind energy-balancing protocols like **PEGASIS**.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Network Characteristics

| Characteristic | Star Topology | Mesh Topology | Tree/Cluster |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Fault Tolerance** | Poor (single point) | Excellent | Moderate (CH failure) |
| **Energy Efficiency** | Poor for distant nodes | Good (multi-hop) | Excellent (aggregation) |
| **Latency** | Lowest (1 hop) | Higher (multi-hop) | Moderate |
| **Scalability** | Poor (limited by sink range) | Good | Good (with re-clustering) |
| **Setup Complexity** | Lowest | High | Moderate |
| **Example Protocol** | — | SPIN, Directed Diffusion | LEACH, PEGASIS |
| **Best Suited For** | Small deployments | Mission-critical, dynamic | Large-scale, energy-critical |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

```mermaid
    F --> F1[Energy]
    F --> F2[Latency]

```


```mermaid
graph TB

```

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Network Lifetime States

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Active
    Active --> Stable: All nodes alive
    Stable --> Degraded: First node dies, FND
    Degraded --> PartiallyOperational: Some nodes dead
    PartiallyOperational --> Partitioned: Network splits
    Partitioned --> Failure: Coverage lost, LND
    Failure --> [*]
    Stable --> Reconfigure: Periodic redeployment
    Reconfigure --> Stable
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1]**
*List any six key characteristics that distinguish a Wireless Sensor Network from a traditional wired network.*

**Model Answer:**

A Wireless Sensor Network differs from a wired network in the following characteristics:

1. **Severe energy constraints** — Nodes are battery-powered; energy is the primary design metric.
2. **Self-organization** — Nodes autonomously form a network without prior infrastructure.
3. **Multi-hop communication** — Data is relayed through intermediate nodes to the sink.
4. **Data-centric operation** — Routing is based on data content (attributes) rather than node IDs.
5. **Dense deployment and redundancy** — Hundreds to thousands of nodes are deployed with high spatial density.
6. **Application-specific design** — WSNs are designed for one specific application (e.g., fire detection, not generic data transfer).
7. **Fault tolerance** — Network must continue operating despite individual node failures.
8. **Harsh environmental operation** — Nodes operate in unattended, often hostile environments.

> **[Mark Distribution: 6 × 0.5 = 3 marks]**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1]**
*Define "Network Lifetime" in a WSN. State any two commonly used metrics to measure it.*

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** *Network Lifetime* is defined as the time duration during which the network continues to fulfill its intended application-level task (e.g., coverage, connectivity, sensing) from the moment of deployment until the moment it fails to do so.

**Two commonly used metrics:**

1. **FND (First Node Dies):** Time until the first sensor node exhausts its battery and stops functioning. This is a strict metric — the network is assumed compromised at the first failure.
2. **HND (Half Nodes Die):** Time until 50% of nodes have died. Useful for applications where some degradation in coverage is tolerable.
3. **(Optional third, for bonus) LND (Last Node Dies):** Time until the final node fails — equals network partition in most cases.

> **[Mark Distribution: Definition = 1.5 marks; Two metrics = 0.75 each = 1.5 marks; Total 3 marks]**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Focus: Energy, Coverage, Lifetime

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the first-order radio energy dissipation model used in WSNs. Derive the energy consumed to transmit an $L$-bit packet over distance $d$ in both free-space ($d^2$) and multi-path ($d^4$) propagation regimes. Define and derive the **crossover distance** $d_0$.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A WSN is randomly deployed over a $200 \text{ m} \times 200 \text{ m}$ field. Each node has a sensing radius of $r_s = 15 \text{ m}$. Using the Poisson Point Process model, determine:
   (i) The required node density $\lambda$ for 99% coverage probability.
   (ii) The total number of nodes $N$ required.
   (iii) The probability of a point being **3-covered**.

---

**Model Solution to Question A:**

**(a) Radio Energy Dissipation Model:**

In a WSN, the dominant energy consumers are **communication (radio) operations**, with sensing and processing being comparatively negligible. The first-order model (Heinzelman et al., 2000) decomposes the radio energy into two parts:

- **Electronics energy** $E_{elec}$: Per-bit energy consumed by the transmitter/receiver circuitry (encoding, modulation, filtering, spreading).
- **Amplifier energy** $\epsilon \cdot d^{\alpha}$: Per-bit energy used by the power amplifier to achieve acceptable SNR at distance $d$, where $\alpha$ is the **path loss exponent**.

**Free-space propagation** ($\alpha = 2$): Used when the transmitter and receiver have a clear, unobstructed line of sight and $d$ is small. The amplifier energy coefficient is $\epsilon_{fs}$.

**Multi-path fading propagation** ($\alpha = 4$): Used when there is no direct line of sight and the channel suffers from multipath scattering; dominant for larger $d$. The amplifier coefficient is $\epsilon_{mp}$.

**Energy to transmit an $L$-bit packet over distance $d$:**

$$E_{TX}(L, d) = L \cdot E_{elec} + L \cdot \epsilon_{fs} \cdot d^2 \quad \text{if } d < d_0$$

$$E_{TX}(L, d) = L \cdot E_{elec} + L \cdot \epsilon_{mp} \cdot d^4 \quad \text{if } d \geq d_0$$

**Energy to receive an $L$-bit packet:**

$$E_{RX}(L) = L \cdot E_{elec}$$
(Reception energy is independent of distance; the receiver demodulates whatever arrives.)

**Derivation of crossover distance $d_0$:**

At $d = d_0$, both propagation regimes give the same $E_{TX}$. Equating the amplifier energy terms (since $E_{elec}$ is the same in both):

$$\epsilon_{fs} \cdot d_0^2 = \epsilon_{mp} \cdot d_0^4$$

Dividing by $d_0^2$:
$$\epsilon_{fs} = \epsilon_{mp} \cdot d_0^2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{d_0 = \sqrt{\frac{\epsilon_{fs}}{\epsilon_{mp}}}}$$

**Typical values:** With $\epsilon_{fs} = 10 \text{ pJ/bit/m}^2$ and $\epsilon_{mp} = 0.0013 \text{ pJ/bit/m}^4$:
$$d_0 = \sqrt{\frac{10 \times 10^{-12}}{0.0013 \times 10^{-12}}} = \sqrt{7692.3} \approx 87.7 \text{ m}$$

**Standard parameters (memorize for KTU):**

| Parameter | Typical Value |
|---|---|
| $E_{elec}$ | **50 nJ/bit** |
| $E_{DA}$ (data aggregation) | 5 nJ/bit |
| $\epsilon_{fs}$ | **10 pJ/bit/m²** |
| $\epsilon_{mp}$ | **0.0013 pJ/bit/m⁴** |
| $d_0$ | **~87.7 m** |

> **[Marks: Diagram/text explanation = 3; Two equations = 2; Derivation = 2; Total = 7]**

---

**(b) Coverage and Density Calculation:**

**Given:** Field size $= 200 \text{ m} \times 200 \text{ m} = 40{,}000 \text{ m}^2$, $r_s = 15 \text{ m}$, target coverage $P_{cov} = 0.99$.

**(i) Required node density $\lambda$ for 99% coverage:**

Using the coverage probability formula:
$$P_{cov} = 1 - e^{-\lambda \pi r_s^2}$$

Setting $P_{cov} = 0.99$:
$$0.99 = 1 - e^{-\lambda \pi r_s^2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad e^{-\lambda \pi r_s^2} = 0.01$$
$$-\lambda \pi r_s^2 = \ln(0.01) = -4.6052$$
$$\lambda = \frac{4.6052}{\pi r_s^2} = \frac{4.6052}{\pi \times 225} = \frac{4.6052}{706.86}$$
$$\boxed{\lambda \approx 6.515 \times 10^{-3} \text{ nodes/m}^2}$$

**(ii) Total nodes required:**
$$N = \lambda \cdot A = 6.515 \times 10^{-3} \times 40{,}000$$
$$\boxed{N \approx 260.6 \approx 261 \text{ nodes}}$$

**(iii) Probability of 3-coverage at a point:**

A point is 3-covered if at least 3 nodes lie within distance $r_s$. The mean number of nodes in the sensing disk is:
$$\mu = \lambda \pi r_s^2 = 4.6052$$

The 3-coverage probability is:
$$P_{3\text{-cov}} = 1 - \sum_{i=0}^{2} \frac{\mu^i e^{-\mu}}{i!}$$
$$= 1 - \left[ \frac{\mu^0 e^{-\mu}}{0!} + \frac{\mu^1 e^{-\mu}}{1!} + \frac{\mu^2 e^{-\mu}}{2!} \right]$$
$$= 1 - e^{-4.6052} \left[ 1 + 4.6052 + \frac{(4.6052)^2}{2} \right]$$
$$= 1 - 0.01 \times \left[ 1 + 4.6052 + 10.604 \right]$$
$$= 1 - 0.01 \times 16.209 = 1 - 0.16209$$
$$\boxed{P_{3\text{-cov}} \approx 0.838 \text{ or } 83.8\%}$$

> **[Marks: (i) 2 marks; (ii) 1 mark; (iii) 4 marks; Total = 7]**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Focus: Topology, Fault Tolerance, Scalability

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare **Star, Mesh, and Cluster (Tree)** topologies in a WSN with respect to: (i) fault tolerance, (ii) energy efficiency, (iii) latency, (iv) scalability. Draw a neat labeled diagram of each. Justify which topology is most suitable for a **large-scale environmental monitoring application** covering a forest of $5 \text{ km}^2$.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A WSN has 100 sensor nodes. The failure rate of each node follows an exponential distribution with $\lambda_f = 0.0002$ failures/hour.
   (i) Derive the reliability of a single node after $t = 1000$ hours.
   (ii) If the application requires a system reliability of 0.95, determine the minimum number of nodes $m$ required in a 1-out-of-$m$ active redundancy configuration.
   (iii) Comment on how node failure impacts network lifetime in a 1-out-of-$m$ configuration.

---

**Model Solution to Question B:**

**(a) Topology Comparison:**

**Star Topology:** All sensor nodes communicate directly with a central base station (sink). No multi-hop; each node is one hop away.

```
        N1
         |
  N2 --- [Sink] --- N3
         |
        N4
```
- **Fault Tolerance:** Poor. Sink failure disables the entire network.
- **Energy Efficiency:** Poor for distant nodes (high $d^4$ loss).
- **Latency:** Lowest (1 hop, no queuing).
- **Scalability:** Limited (only nodes within sink's range can join).

**Mesh Topology:** Every node can forward packets to any other node; multiple paths exist.

```
    N1 --- N2
    |  \   |
    |   \  |
    N3 --- N4 --- Sink
    |       |
    N5 --- N6
```
- **Fault Tolerance:** Excellent (multiple paths; self-healing).
- **Energy Efficiency:** Moderate (multi-hop reduces per-hop energy but increases total relays).
- **Latency:** Moderate to high (multi-hop + queuing).
- **Scalability:** Good (add nodes without changing core topology).

**Cluster (Tree) Topology:** Nodes form local clusters with cluster heads (CHs); CHs aggregate and forward to sink.

```
    CH1   CH2
   / | \  /|\
  N1 N2 N3 N4 N5
        |
      [Sink]
```
- **Fault Tolerance:** Moderate (CH failure disrupts its cluster, but other clusters survive).
- **Energy Efficiency:** High (intra-cluster communication is short; data aggregation reduces traffic).
- **Latency:** Moderate (intra-cluster: 1–2 hops; CH-to-sink: 1 hop).
- **Scalability:** Excellent (hierarchical; periodic re-clustering balances energy).

**Most suitable for $5 \text{ km}^2$ forest monitoring: Cluster (Tree) Topology** — because:
1. Forest is large — star would be infeasible due to range.
2. Sensors must operate for years on batteries — energy efficiency via aggregation is critical.
3. Periodic re-clustering (e.g., LEACH) prevents energy holes.
4. Hierarchical architecture scales gracefully to thousands of nodes.
5. Moderate fault tolerance is acceptable since environmental events are tracked over hours, not seconds.

> **[Marks: 3 diagrams × 1.5 = 4.5; comparison table = 1.5; justification = 1; Total = 7]**

---

**(b) Reliability and Network Lifetime:**

**(i) Single node reliability at $t = 1000$ hours:**

Exponential reliability model:
$$R(t) = e^{-\lambda_f t}$$
$$R(1000) = e^{-0.0002 \times 1000} = e^{-0.2}$$
$$\boxed{R(1000) \approx 0.8187 \text{ or } 81.87\%}$$

This means each individual node has about an 81.87% chance of surviving the first 1000 hours without failure.

**(ii) Minimum $m$ for 1-out-of-$m$ redundancy with system reliability 0.95:**

In a 1-out-of-$m$ system, the system functions as long as **at least one** of the $m$ parallel redundant nodes is alive. The system fails only if **all** $m$ nodes fail.

System reliability:
$$R_{sys} = 1 - (1 - R_{node})^m$$

Setting $R_{sys} = 0.95$ and $R_{node} = 0.8187$:
$$0.95 = 1 - (1 - 0.8187)^m$$
$$(0.1813)^m = 0.05$$
$$m \cdot \ln(0.1813) = \ln(0.05)$$
$$m \cdot (-1.7068) = -2.9957$$
$$\boxed{m = \frac{2.9957}{1.7068} \approx 1.755 \Rightarrow m = 2 \text{ (rounded up)}}$$

**Verification:** With $m = 2$: $R_{sys} = 1 - (0.1813)^2 = 1 - 0.0329 = 0.9671 > 0.95$ ✓
With $m = 1$: $R_{sys} = 0.8187 < 0.95$ ✗

**So a minimum of 2 redundant nodes per logical sensing point is required.**

**(iii) Impact on network lifetime:**

In a 1-out-of-$m$ configuration:
- **Lifetime is extended** because the logical "sensor" survives as long as one physical node is alive. With $m = 2$, the system lifetime is the time until both nodes fail.
- The expected lifetime is **longer than the mean time to failure (MTTF) of a single node** by approximately $\frac{1}{1 + \frac{1}{2} + \frac{1}{3} + \ldots + \frac{1}{m}}$ (for $m$ identical nodes).
- However, the **trade-off is cost and energy density** — more nodes mean more energy consumption in the network as a whole, more MAC contention, and more routing overhead.
- In WSNs, 1-out-of-$m$ redundancy is implemented as **$k$-coverage** (e.g., a region monitored by $k$ sensors such that loss of $k-1$ is tolerable).

> **[Marks: (i) 2; (ii) 3; (iii) 2; Total = 7]**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Confusing $E_{elec}$ in $E_{TX}$ and $E_{RX}$:** Many students forget to include $L \cdot E_{elec}$ in the transmission energy equation. *Examiner's tip:* Always write $E_{TX} = L \cdot E_{elec} + L \cdot \epsilon \cdot d^n$, never omit the electronics term.
> 2. **Mixing up $d^2$ and $d^4$ regimes:** State the crossover distance $d_0$ first, then apply the correct formula based on whether $d < d_0$ or $d \geq d_0$. Examiners deduct up to 2 marks for not specifying the regime.
> 3. **Forgetting units in numerical answers:** A coverage probability expressed as "0.99" without saying "or 99%" is fine, but a density without "nodes/m²" will lose 0.5 marks. Always include units.
> 4. **In topology questions, diagrams are MANDATORY:** Even a rough ASCII diagram is acceptable. Examiners allocate 1.5 marks specifically for the labeled diagram. Skipping it costs dearly.
> 5. **Reliability questions: always state the assumption.** Specify that node failures are independent and identically distributed (i.i.d.) for the 1-out-of-$m$ formula to be valid.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-minute KTU revision — read this section twice before the exam.**

**✅ Core Network Characteristics (must-know list):**

- [ ] **Topology types:** Star, Mesh, Tree/Cluster, Hybrid — know their trade-offs.
- [ ] **Deployment types:** Deterministic (planned) vs. Random (PPP-based).
- [ ] **Energy model:** $E_{TX}(L,d)$ and $E_{RX}(L)$ equations with $E_{elec}$, $\epsilon_{fs}$, $\epsilon_{mp}$, $d_0$.
- [ ] **Crossover distance:** $d_0 = \sqrt{\epsilon_{fs}/\epsilon_{mp}} \approx 87.7$ m with standard values.
- [ ] **Coverage probability:** $P_{cov} = 1 - e^{-\lambda \pi r_s^2}$.
- [ ] **k-coverage probability:** $P_{k\text{-cov}} = 1 - \sum_{i=0}^{k-1} \frac{(\lambda \pi r_s^2)^i e^{-\lambda \pi r_s^2}}{i!}$.
- [ ] **Network capacity (Gupta-Kumar):** $C(n) = \Theta(W\sqrt{n/\log n})$; per-node $= \Theta(W/\sqrt{n \log n})$.
- [ ] **Network lifetime:** FND, HND, LND definitions.
- [ ] **Reliability:** $R(t) = e^{-\lambda_f t}$; 1-out-of-$m$ system reliability.
- [ ] **Multi-hop advantage:** Break-even distance $d_{break} = \left(\frac{2\sqrt{E_{elec}\epsilon_{fs}}}{\epsilon_{mp}}\right)^{1/3}$.
- [ ] **Standard radio energy parameters:** $E_{elec}=50$ nJ/bit, $\epsilon_{fs}=10$ pJ/bit/m², $\epsilon_{mp}=0.0013$ pJ/bit/m⁴.
- [ ] **Fault tolerance via redundancy:** 1-out-of-$m$ configuration.
- [ ] **WSN distinguishing features vs. ad-hoc networks:** Energy-constrained, data-centric, dense, application-specific, self-organized.
- [ ] **Security goals:** CIA + AAA (Confidentiality, Integrity, Authentication; Availability, Authorization, Accountability) + Freshness.

**⚠️ Common Mistakes to Avoid:**

- Forgetting the $L \cdot E_{elec}$ term in $E_{TX}$.
- Using free-space model for $d > d_0$ (must switch to multi-path).
- Confusing sensing radius $r_s$ with transmission radius $r_t$.
- Forgetting to convert area to m² in density calculations.
- Writing $P_{cov} = e^{-\lambda \pi r_s^2}$ instead of $1 - e^{-\lambda \pi r_s^2}$.

**🎯 High-Yield Numericals for Practice:**

- Compute $P_{cov}$ given $A, N, r_s$.
- Compute $d_0$ given $\epsilon_{fs}$ and $\epsilon_{mp}$.
- Compute $R(t)$ and required $m$ for 1-out-of-$m$ redundancy.
- Compute $T_{FND}$ given $E_0$ and $P_{avg}$.

**📌 Acronyms to Memorize:**

- **WSN** — Wireless Sensor Network
- **PPP** — Poisson Point Process
- **FND/HND/LND** — First/Half/Last Node Dies
- **LEACH** — Low-Energy Adaptive Clustering Hierarchy
- **PEGASIS** — Power-Efficient GAthering in Sensor Information Systems
- **SPIN** — Sensor Protocols for Information via Negotiation
- **MTTF** — Mean Time To Failure
- **QoS** — Quality of Service
- **CIA** — Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Unique Constraints and Challenges
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT634 - WIRELESS & SENSORS NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Unique Constraints and Challenges

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Unique Constraints and Challenges in Wireless Sensor Networks (WSN)** refer to the strict, design-defining limitations imposed by the operating environment, hardware miniaturization, application domain, and communication medium that collectively differentiate WSNs from classical wired networks and conventional wireless ad-hoc networks (MANETs).

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (PEMRT634 Module 1), the **Wireless Sensor Network (WSN)** is formally defined as:

> A *self-configuring* infrastructure of low-cost, low-power, multifunctional **sensor nodes** that communicate wirelessly over short distances to cooperatively sense, process, and forward data toward a **sink node** (base station) through a multi-hop routing topology.

A sensor node is composed of four fundamental units:

1. **Sensing Unit** — transducer + ADC
2. **Processing Unit** — microcontroller + memory
3. **Transceiver Unit** — radio + antenna
4. **Power Unit** — typically a non-rechargeable battery (AA/coin cell)

The **unique** word in the topic is critical from a KTU valuation standpoint. WSN constraints are *not generic* wireless challenges; they emerge from the simultaneous intersection of **energy scarcity**, **random deployment**, **resource poverty**, and **mission-critical application demands**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Emphasis:** Examiners in PEMRT634 specifically test whether a student can *differentiate* WSN constraints from MANET/traditional network constraints. Always highlight the *intersection* of multiple limitations.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Firefighter Team in a Burning Building"

Imagine a **team of 10 firefighters** deployed inside a **smoke-filled, collapsing building**:

- Each firefighter carries a **tiny battery-powered radio** (energy constraint) — they cannot recharge mid-mission.
- They carry only a **mini backpack with limited oxygen** (memory & processing constraint).
- The walls are thick concrete (radio signals weaken → **propagation challenges**).
- One firefighter is injured and collapses — the team must **self-reorganize** without a central commander (self-management).
- They have no pre-assigned seating chart — **addresses are dynamically discovered** (addressing challenge).
- New firefighters may be air-dropped inside at any time (**scalability & dynamic topology**).
- Their mission-critical data (survivor locations) must reach the exit door (sink) **within seconds** (real-time constraint).
- A saboteur could mimic a firefighter's radio signature (**security challenge**).

This is **exactly** what a WSN node experiences. Each constraint is not independent — they are *coupled* and *multiplicative*.

## 1.3 The Seven Pillars of WSN Constraints (KTU Syllabus Mapping)

| # | Constraint | Syllabus Tag | Exam Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Energy | Hardware + Protocol | Very High |
| 2 | Communication (Bandwidth & Wireless) | MAC + Physical | High |
| 3 | Hardware (Size, Memory, CPU) | Architecture | High |
| 4 | Deployment & Environment | Application | Medium |
| 5 | Self-Management & Autonomy | Network Layer | Very High |
| 6 | Scalability | Network Layer | High |
| 7 | Security & Privacy | Cross-layer | Medium |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Trick:** "Compare WSN challenges with MANET challenges" is a recurring 7-mark sub-question. Always conclude with: *"WSNs are **more energy-constrained** and **less mobile** than MANETs, but face **harsher deployment environments**."*

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Mandatory Bold)

- **Battery energy density:** ~2500 J/g (Alkaline AA cell)
- **Standard supply voltage:** **3.3 V** (typical motes like MicaZ, TelosB)
- **Free-space path loss exponent (n):** **2**
- **Obstructed path loss exponent (n):** **3 to 4**
- **ISM Band frequencies:** **433 MHz, 868/915 MHz, 2.4 GHz**
- **IEEE 802.15.4 standard data rate:** **250 kbps**
- **Typical node lifetime goal:** **1–3 years** (target deployment metric)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of WSN Constraints — Structural Breakdown

The constraints are classified by **origin layer** as per the ISO-OSI adapted WSN protocol stack.

### Layer A — Hardware-Level Constraints (Bottom-Up)

These are *immutable physical* limitations — they cannot be solved by software alone.

**A1. Energy Constraint (THE dominant constraint)**

- Sensor nodes are **battery-powered** and often deployed in **inaccessible terrain** (volcanoes, forests, battlefields, human body).
- Battery replacement is **economically or physically infeasible**.
- Energy is consumed in **three operations**:
  - **Sensing:** $E_{sense} = V_{dd} \cdot I_{sense} \cdot t_{active}$
  - **Computation:** $E_{proc} = C \cdot V_{dd}^2 \cdot f_{clk} \cdot N_{cycles}$ (CMOS dynamic power model)
  - **Communication:** $E_{comm} = E_{tx} + E_{rx}$ (often dominates by **70–80%**)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Communication consumes 1000× more energy than computation** in WSNs. Therefore, **in-network processing** and **data aggregation** are mandatory, not optional.

**A2. Hardware Constraint (Size, Memory, Processor)**

- Typical mote specifications (e.g., Mica2/TelosB class):
  - **CPU:** 8-bit ATmega128L @ **8 MHz** OR 16-bit MSP430 @ **4–8 MHz**
  - **RAM:** **4–10 KB**
  - **Flash:** **48–128 KB**
  - **Radio:** Chipcon CC1000/CC2420 (IEEE 802.15.4 compliant)
- Such constraints preclude **TCP/IP full stack**, **heavy cryptographic suites**, and **large routing tables**.

### Layer B — Communication-Level Constraints

**B1. Limited Bandwidth**

- IEEE 802.15.4 physical layer offers only **250 kbps** (O-QPSK at 2.4 GHz).
- Effective throughput after MAC/PHY overhead: typically **30–80 kbps**.

**B2. Wireless Channel Issues**

- **Multipath fading**, **shadowing**, **path loss**, **inter-symbol interference (ISI)**.
- **Co-channel interference** from Wi-Fi (2.4 GHz overlap).

**B3. Broadcast Nature → Insecurity**

- Eavesdropping, jamming, and message injection are trivially possible.

### Layer C — Network/Protocol-Level Constraints

**C1. Self-Organization**

- No pre-configured topology.
- Nodes must perform **neighbor discovery**, **cluster formation**, and **time synchronization** autonomously.
- Algorithms such as **LEACH, PEGASIS, SPIN, Directed Diffusion** were born from this need.

**C2. Scalability**

- Networks range from **10 to 10,000+ nodes**.
- Protocols must scale **sub-linearly** with node count, otherwise energy per node becomes unsustainable.

**C3. Dynamic Topology**

- Nodes may **die** (battery depletion), **fail** (hardware), or **be added** (incremental deployment).
- Routing protocols must be **adaptive** but **lightweight** (no reactive flooding overhead).

**C4. Data-Centric vs Address-Centric Addressing**

- Classical IP addressing is infeasible for **10,000 nodes** (IPv6 header = 40 bytes vs. 127-byte IEEE 802.15.4 frame).
- WSNs use **attribute-based** or **data-centric** naming (e.g., *SPIN's meta-data queries*).

### Layer D — Application/Environment-Level Constraints

- **Harsh environments:** temperature extremes, corrosion, vibration, water immersion.
- **Mission-criticality:** forest fire detection must trigger alarm in **< 10 seconds**.
- **Heterogeneity:** nodes may have different sensor modalities (temperature, humidity, vibration, gas).

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> The following formulas are the **exact set** KTU examiners test. Memorize units and assumptions.

| # | Formula / Equation | LaTeX Form | Physical Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | First-order radio energy — **Transmit** | $E_{tx}(k,d) = E_{elec} \cdot k + \varepsilon_{amp} \cdot k \cdot d^{n}$ | Energy to send k bits over distance d | Joules (J) |
| 2 | First-order radio energy — **Receive** | $E_{rx}(k) = E_{elec} \cdot k$ | Energy to receive k bits | J |
| 3 | Total energy for 1-hop | $E_{hop} = E_{tx} + E_{rx}$ | Per-hop cost | J |
| 4 | Network lifetime (definition) | $T_{life} = \min_i (E_{residual,i} / P_{avg,i})$ | Time until first node death | seconds (s) |
| 5 | CMOS dynamic power | $P_{dyn} = \alpha \cdot C \cdot V_{dd}^{2} \cdot f_{clk}$ | Computation power model | Watts (W) |
| 6 | Free-space path loss | $PL_{dB} = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 32.44$ | d-scale attenuation | dB |
| 7 | Path loss (generalized) | $PL(d) = PL(d_0) + 10n\log_{10}(d/d_0) + X_{\sigma}$ | Log-normal shadowing model | dB |
| 8 | Battery capacity (energy) | $E_{bat} = V \cdot Q$ | V=Voltage, Q=Charge | J |
| 9 | Duty cycle (energy saving) | $DC = t_{active} / (t_{active} + t_{sleep})$ | Fraction of time awake | unitless (0–1) |
| 10 | Throughput efficiency | $\eta = D_{app} / R_{raw}$ | Useful data / raw channel rate | unitless |
| 11 | Multi-hop hop count | $k_{opt} = d_{total} / \sqrt{E_{elec}/\varepsilon_{amp}}$ | Optimal hops for energy | hops |
| 12 | Energy per bit / meter squared | $E_{bit} = E_{tx} / (k \cdot d^2)$ | Energy efficiency metric | J/bit/m² |

### Critical Constants to Memorize (Board Exam Gold)

- **$E_{elec} = 50$ nJ/bit** (transmitter/receiver electronics energy)
- **$\varepsilon_{amp} = 100$ pJ/bit/m²** (free-space amplifier, n=2)
- **$\varepsilon_{amp} = 0.0013$ pJ/bit/m⁴** (multipath amplifier, n=4)
- **Path loss exponent n:** 2 (free space), 3–4 (shadowed urban), 4–6 (obstructed indoor)

### Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application | Dominant Constraint Addressed |
|---|---|---|
| Precision Agriculture | Soil moisture monitoring | Energy + Scalability |
| Structural Health | Bridge vibration sensing | Harsh environment + Reliability |
| Healthcare | Wearable ECG monitoring | Size + Security |
| Military | Battlefield surveillance | Security + Self-organization |
| Environmental | Volcanic eruption prediction | Energy + Real-time |
| Industrial IoT | Factory machine monitoring | Bandwidth + Reliability |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Optimal Hop Count for Minimum Energy (Heinzelman Model)

This is a **classic KTU 14-mark derivation** that appears in **December 2019, December 2022, and June 2024** past papers.

### Problem Statement
Given a linear chain of $k$ relay hops covering a total source-to-sink distance $D$, derive the **optimal number of hops $k_{opt}$** that minimizes total transmission energy, using the first-order radio model.

### Given (Standard Assumptions)
- Total distance: $D$ meters
- Number of hops: $k$ (equal spacing: $d = D/k$)
- Message size: $L$ bits
- $E_{elec}$ = 50 nJ/bit
- $\varepsilon_{amp}$ = 100 pJ/bit/m² (free space, $n=2$)
- Path loss exponent: $n = 2$

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Energy per hop (transmit only, single hop of length $d$)**

$$
E_{tx}(L, d) = E_{elec} \cdot L + \varepsilon_{amp} \cdot L \cdot d^{n}
$$

**Step 2 — Energy per hop (with reception at each relay)**

For each intermediate relay, the radio must *receive* and *re-transmit*:

$$
E_{relay} = E_{rx}(L) + E_{tx}(L, d) = E_{elec} \cdot L + (E_{elec} \cdot L + \varepsilon_{amp} \cdot L \cdot d^{n})
$$

$$
E_{relay} = 2 \cdot E_{elec} \cdot L + \varepsilon_{amp} \cdot L \cdot d^{n}
$$

**Step 3 — Total energy over $k$ hops**

Since all hops are equal length $d = D/k$:

$$
E_{total}(k) = k \cdot \left[ 2 \cdot E_{elec} \cdot L + \varepsilon_{amp} \cdot L \cdot \left(\frac{D}{k}\right)^{n} \right]
$$

$$
E_{total}(k) = 2k \cdot E_{elec} \cdot L + \varepsilon_{amp} \cdot L \cdot D^{n} \cdot k^{1-n}
$$

**Step 4 — Differentiate with respect to $k$ and set to zero**

For $n = 2$:

$$
E_{total}(k) = 2k \cdot E_{elec} \cdot L + \varepsilon_{amp} \cdot L \cdot D^{2} \cdot k^{-1}
$$

Taking derivative:

$$
\frac{dE_{total}}{dk} = 2 \cdot E_{elec} \cdot L - \varepsilon_{amp} \cdot L \cdot D^{2} \cdot k^{-2} = 0
$$

Solving for $k$:

$$
2 \cdot E_{elec} = \varepsilon_{amp} \cdot D^{2} \cdot k^{-2}
$$

$$
k^{2} = \frac{\varepsilon_{amp} \cdot D^{2}}{2 \cdot E_{elec}}
$$

**Step 5 — Final closed-form expression for optimal hop count**

$$
k_{opt} = \sqrt{\frac{\varepsilon_{amp}}{2 \cdot E_{elec}}} \cdot D
$$

$$
\boxed{k_{opt} = \frac{D}{\sqrt{2 E_{elec} / \varepsilon_{amp}}}}
$$

**Step 6 — Numerical Example**

Let $D = 100$ m, $E_{elec} = 50$ nJ/bit, $\varepsilon_{amp} = 100$ pJ/bit/m².

Convert units: $E_{elec} = 50 \times 10^{-9}$ J/bit, $\varepsilon_{amp} = 100 \times 10^{-12}$ J/bit/m².

$$
k_{opt} = \frac{100}{\sqrt{2 \times 50 \times 10^{-9} / (100 \times 10^{-12})}}
$$

$$
k_{opt} = \frac{100}{\sqrt{1000}} = \frac{100}{31.62} \approx 3.16
$$

Since $k$ must be an integer, **choose $k = 3$ hops** for minimum total energy.

### Engineering Interpretation

> [!TIP]
> This result proves a **counter-intuitive** but **exam-critical** insight:
> *Direct long-range transmission is **less** energy-efficient than multi-hop short-range transmission in WSNs.* This is the theoretical foundation of every WSN routing protocol (LEACH, PEGASIS, TEEN).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Network Lifetime Under Uniform Energy Drain

**Problem:** A WSN has $N$ nodes, each with initial energy $E_0$. Each node consumes average power $P$. Derive network lifetime (time-to-first-node-death).

**Step 1 — Define initial residual energy of node $i$**

$$
E_{residual,i}(t) = E_0 - P \cdot t
$$

**Step 2 — Node $i$ dies when residual energy reaches zero**

$$
t_{death,i} = \frac{E_0}{P}
$$

**Step 3 — For homogeneous network (all nodes identical)**

All nodes die simultaneously:

$$
\boxed{T_{life} = \frac{E_0}{P}}
$$

**Step 4 — For heterogeneous energy** (different $E_{0,i}$ values)

$$
T_{life} = \min_{i \in \{1,2,\ldots,N\}} \left( \frac{E_{0,i}}{P_i} \right)
$$

**Step 5 — Heterogeneous with duty cycling**

If node is active for $t_{on}$ out of $T_{cycle}$:

$$
P_{avg} = \frac{t_{on}}{T_{cycle}} \cdot P_{active}
$$

$$
\boxed{T_{life} = \frac{E_0 \cdot T_{cycle}}{t_{on} \cdot P_{active}}}
$$

### Numerical Example

$E_0 = 3$ J (coin cell), $P_{active} = 30$ mW, $t_{on} = 1$ ms, $T_{cycle} = 100$ ms.

$$
P_{avg} = \frac{1}{100} \times 30 \text{ mW} = 0.3 \text{ mW}
$$

$$
T_{life} = \frac{3}{0.3 \times 10^{-3}} = 10{,}000 \text{ seconds} \approx 2.78 \text{ hours}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Without duty cycling**, lifetime would be $3 / 0.03 = 100$ s only. This is why **MAC-layer sleep scheduling (S-MAC, B-MAC, T-MAC)** is essential — they boost lifetime by **100×–1000×**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Energy Saved by In-Network Aggregation

**Problem:** Compare energy for (a) raw data transmission vs (b) aggregated transmission.

**Setup:**
- $N$ sensor nodes each produce $L$-bit packet.
- Aggregation factor: $N \to 1$ packet of length $L$ at aggregator.

**Case A — Raw transmission (each node sends $L$ bits over distance $d$)**

$$
E_{A} = N \cdot (E_{elec} \cdot L + \varepsilon_{amp} \cdot L \cdot d^{2})
$$

**Case B — Aggregated (one node sends $L$ bits over distance $d$, others send shorter)**

If aggregation compresses $N \cdot L \to L$:

$$
E_{B} = (N \cdot E_{elec} \cdot L) + (E_{elec} \cdot L + \varepsilon_{amp} \cdot L \cdot d^{2})
$$

**Case B simplifies (for $N \gg 1$):**

$$
E_{B} \approx N \cdot E_{elec} \cdot L + \varepsilon_{amp} \cdot L \cdot d^{2}
$$

**Energy savings ratio:**

$$
\eta_{save} = 1 - \frac{E_{B}}{E_{A}} = \frac{(N-1) \cdot \varepsilon_{amp} \cdot L \cdot d^{2}}{N \cdot E_{elec} \cdot L + N \cdot \varepsilon_{amp} \cdot L \cdot d^{2}}
$$

**For large $N$:**

$$
\boxed{\eta_{save} \to \frac{\varepsilon_{amp} \cdot d^{2}}{E_{elec} + \varepsilon_{amp} \cdot d^{2}}}
$$

**Numerical check:** $d = 10$ m, $E_{elec} = 50$ nJ/bit, $\varepsilon_{amp} = 100$ pJ/bit/m²:

$$
\eta_{save} \approx \frac{100 \times 10^{-12} \times 100}{50 \times 10^{-9} + 100 \times 10^{-12} \times 100} = \frac{10^{-8}}{5.1 \times 10^{-8}} \approx 0.196
$$

So aggregation saves **~20%** energy even for free-space model. For $n=4$ (multipath), savings rise to **>60%**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This derivation is the theoretical basis of cluster-based protocols (LEACH) and data-centric protocols (Directed Diffusion, SPIN).**

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: WSN vs MANET vs Cellular

| Parameter | WSN | MANET | Cellular (4G/5G) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Node size | Tiny (cm³) | Laptop-sized | Tower-mounted |
| Energy source | Battery (non-rechargeable) | Rechargeable | Mains-powered |
| Mobility | Mostly **static** | Highly mobile | Mobile users, static BS |
| Node count | 100–10,000 | 10–100 | Millions |
| Primary goal | Sensing fidelity | Communication throughput | High data rate |
| Address scheme | Data-centric | IP-based | IP-based |
| Bandwidth | ~250 kbps | 1–54 Mbps | 100 Mbps–10 Gbps |
| Security priority | Low (pre-deployment) | High | Critical |
| Deployment | Random/aerial | Planned or random | Planned |
| Key design metric | **Energy per bit** | Throughput & delay | QoS & coverage |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU examiners often ask:** "Why can't TCP/IP be used directly in WSNs?" — Answer must cite: address space explosion, header overhead (40-byte IPv6 vs 127-byte 802.15.4 frame), no plug-in power, and energy cost of TCP retransmissions.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

---


**Energy split (typical MicaZ mote):**
- Transmit: ~**59%** of total energy
- Receive (idle listening): ~**22%**
- Sleep: ~**1%**
- Compute + Sense: ~**18%**

> [!NOTE]
> This is the empirical justification for KTU Module 1's emphasis on **MAC duty cycling** and **multi-hop routing** as the two pillars of energy conservation.

---

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 — `[KTU University Exam — June 2023]`

**Q: List any three unique challenges of Wireless Sensor Networks and explain why they do not arise in traditional wired networks.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — distribute as: 1 mark per challenge + 0 mark for naming, 1 mark for comparison depth):**

The three unique challenges are:

1. **Severe Energy Constraint:** Sensor nodes are battery-powered and deployed in inaccessible environments (forests, battlefields, volcanoes). Recharging or replacing batteries is economically and physically infeasible. Wired networks have **continuous mains power supply**, so this constraint is absent.

2. **Self-Organization and Auto-Configuration:** Thousands of randomly deployed nodes must autonomously discover neighbors, synchronize clocks, form topology, and route data without any pre-configured infrastructure. Wired networks use **static switches and routers with manual configuration**; this challenge is irrelevant.

3. **Harsh Deployment Environment:** WSNs often operate under extreme temperature, humidity, corrosion, and vibration. Wired networks are typically deployed in **controlled indoor environments** and use shielded physical media that is not affected by such stresses.

*(Examiner's note: Award 3 marks only if all three challenges are paired with an explicit comparison to wired networks.)*

---

### Question A2 — `[KTU University Exam — December 2023]`

**Q: Why is communication considered more energy-expensive than computation in a WSN node? Justify with a numerical example.**

**Model Answer:**

Communication typically consumes **1000× more energy per operation** than computation on a typical sensor mote.

**Numerical justification (3 marks):**

- Transmitting 1 bit over 100 m: $E_{tx} = 50$ nJ + $100 \text{ pJ} \times (100)^{2} = 50 + 1000 = 1050$ nJ ≈ **1.05 µJ/bit**
- Computing 1 instruction (8-bit MCU @ 8 MHz, 3.3 V): $E_{proc} \approx 1$ nJ/instruction

Ratio: $1.05 \, \mu\text{J} / 1 \text{ nJ} \approx 1050 \times$

Therefore, WSN protocols strongly favor **in-network processing**, **data aggregation**, and **multi-hop short-range transmission** over direct long-range transmission or raw data forwarding.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

### Question A — `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`

**(a)** Discuss in detail the **hardware-level constraints** of a sensor node. Explain why energy is considered the most dominant constraint and how it influences protocol design at **MAC**, **network**, and **application** layers. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **optimal number of hops** for minimum energy consumption in a multi-hop WSN using the first-order radio model. Assume a linear chain of $k$ equal-length hops covering total distance $D$ with free-space path loss ($n=2$). **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q-A(a)

**Hardware Constraints (3 marks):**

A sensor node comprises four units:

1. **Sensing Unit** — sensor + ADC
2. **Processing Unit** — microcontroller + memory
3. **Transceiver Unit** — radio + antenna
4. **Power Unit** — battery

Typical Mica2 / TelosB specifications:

| Component | Limitation |
|---|---|
| CPU | 8 MHz ATmega128L or 16-bit MSP430 |
| RAM | 4–10 KB |
| Flash | 48–128 KB |
| Radio | CC1000/CC2420, 250 kbps |
| Battery | 2 × AA alkaline, 3.3 V, ~3 J usable energy |

These constraints prohibit:
- TCP/IP full stack
- Heavy cryptography (RSA, AES-128 in software)
- Large routing tables
- High-power radio amplifiers

**Why Energy is Dominant (2 marks):**

- Energy is non-renewable in field deployments
- Communication consumes 70–80% of total energy
- Node death fragments the network → reduces coverage → application failure

**Cross-Layer Influence (2 marks):**

- **MAC layer:** duty cycling, sleep/wake schedules (S-MAC, B-MAC)
- **Network layer:** energy-aware routing (LEACH, PEGASIS), multi-hop
- **Application layer:** data compression, adaptive sampling rates

---

#### Model Solution for Q-A(b)

**Derivation of Optimal Hop Count (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Per-hop transmit energy:

$$
E_{tx}(L, d) = E_{elec} \cdot L + \varepsilon_{amp} \cdot L \cdot d^{n}
$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Per-hop energy (with relay reception):

$$
E_{relay} = 2 \cdot E_{elec} \cdot L + \varepsilon_{amp} \cdot L \cdot d^{n}
$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Total energy for $k$ hops with $d = D/k$:

$$
E_{total}(k) = 2k \cdot E_{elec} \cdot L + \varepsilon_{amp} \cdot L \cdot D^{n} \cdot k^{1-n}
$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Differentiate w.r.t. $k$ and equate to zero (for $n=2$):

$$
\frac{dE_{total}}{dk} = 2 \cdot E_{elec} \cdot L - \varepsilon_{amp} \cdot L \cdot D^{2} \cdot k^{-2} = 0
$$

**Step 5 (2 marks):** Solve for $k_{opt}$:

$$
k_{opt} = \sqrt{\frac{\varepsilon_{amp}}{2 \cdot E_{elec}}} \cdot D = \frac{D}{\sqrt{2 E_{elec} / \varepsilon_{amp}}}
$$

**Conclusion (bonus 0.5 mark):** Multi-hop short-range transmission is more energy-efficient than direct transmission — this is the cornerstone of WSN routing protocol design.

---

### Question B — `[KTU University Exam — December 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the **unique communication challenges** in WSNs. Compare and contrast the addressing approaches used in WSNs versus traditional IP networks. Why is **data-centric addressing** preferred in WSNs? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A linear WSN with $N = 5$ equally spaced nodes covers a total distance $D = 200$ m. Each packet is $L = 4000$ bits. Using the first-order radio model with $E_{elec} = 50$ nJ/bit and $\varepsilon_{amp} = 100$ pJ/bit/m² ($n = 2$):

1. Calculate the **total energy** consumed for direct transmission from node 1 to sink.
2. Calculate the **total energy** consumed using multi-hop (5 equal hops).
3. Comment on which is more energy-efficient and by what factor. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q-B(a)

**Communication Challenges (3 marks):**

1. **Limited Bandwidth:** IEEE 802.15.4 offers only 250 kbps; effective throughput is 30–80 kbps after MAC/PHY overhead.
2. **Wireless Channel Impairments:** multipath fading, shadowing, co-channel interference (especially in 2.4 GHz ISM band shared with Wi-Fi/Bluetooth).
3. **Broadcast Insecurity:** open-air medium enables eavesdropping, jamming, message injection.
4. **Hidden Terminal Problem:** exposed-node and hidden-node issues dominate MAC design.

**Addressing Comparison (2.5 marks):**

| Aspect | IP Networks | WSNs |
|---|---|---|
| Address type | Node-centric (IP address) | Data/attribute-centric |
| Address space | 32-bit (IPv4) / 128-bit (IPv6) | Variable, content-based |
| Header size | 20–40 bytes | 4–8 bytes (e.g., SPIN meta-data) |
| Identity requirement | Unique global ID per node | Not required |
| Query mechanism | Host-to-host | Publisher-subscriber / interest diffusion |

**Why Data-Centric Preferred (1.5 marks):**

- Avoids assigning unique IDs to thousands of nodes
- Eliminates IPv6 header overhead in 127-byte 802.15.4 frames
- Supports **in-network aggregation** naturally
- Energy-efficient: queries are answered locally without full routing
- Used in **Directed Diffusion**, **SPIN**, **TinyDB**

---

#### Model Solution for Q-B(b)

**Given:** $N = 5$, $D = 200$ m, $L = 4000$ bits, $E_{elec} = 50$ nJ/bit, $\varepsilon_{amp} = 100$ pJ/bit/m², $n = 2$.

**Part 1 — Direct Transmission (3 marks):**

$d = 200$ m

$$
E_{direct} = E_{elec} \cdot L + \varepsilon_{amp} \cdot L \cdot d^{2}
$$

$$
E_{direct} = (50 \times 10^{-9})(4000) + (100 \times 10^{-12})(4000)(200)^{2}
$$

$$
E_{direct} = 2 \times 10^{-4} + (4 \times 10^{-7})(40000)
$$

$$
E_{direct} = 2 \times 10^{-4} + 1.6 \times 10^{-2} = 1.62 \times 10^{-2} \text{ J}
$$

**Part 2 — Multi-Hop (2 hops for 5 nodes) (3 marks):**

For 5 nodes equally spaced: 4 hops, each of length $d = 50$ m.

$$
E_{1 hop} = E_{elec} \cdot L + \varepsilon_{amp} \cdot L \cdot d^{2}
$$

$$
E_{1 hop} = (50 \times 10^{-9})(4000) + (100 \times 10^{-12})(4000)(50)^{2}
$$

$$
E_{1 hop} = 2 \times 10^{-4} + (4 \times 10^{-7})(2500) = 2 \times 10^{-4} + 1 \times 10^{-3} = 1.2 \times 10^{-3} \text{ J}
$$

Each intermediate relay (3 of them) consumes $E_{relay} = E_{rx} + E_{tx}$:

$$
E_{relay} = (50 \times 10^{-9})(4000) + 1.2 \times 10^{-3} = 2 \times 10^{-4} + 1.2 \times 10^{-3} = 1.4 \times 10^{-3} \text{ J}
$$

Total energy:

$$
E_{multi} = E_{1 hop, source} + 3 \cdot E_{relay} + E_{final, receive}
$$

$$
E_{multi} = 1.2 \times 10^{-3} + 3(1.4 \times 10^{-3}) + 2 \times 10^{-4}
$$

$$
E_{multi} = 1.2 \times 10^{-3} + 4.2 \times 10^{-3} + 2 \times 10^{-4} = 5.6 \times 10^{-3} \text{ J}
$$

**Part 3 — Comparison (1 mark):**

$$
\text{Energy ratio} = \frac{E_{direct}}{E_{multi}} = \frac{16.2 \times 10^{-3}}{5.6 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 2.89
$$

**Conclusion:** Multi-hop transmission consumes only **~35%** of direct transmission energy — i.e., saves **~65%** energy. This validates the multi-hop principle in WSNs.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Unit confusion:** Forgetting to convert nJ to J or pJ to J. Always convert to base SI units before arithmetic.
> 2. **Missing receiver energy:** In multi-hop calculations, students often forget the $E_{rx}$ term at each intermediate relay.
> 3. **Assuming $d^{2}$ when problem states $n=4$:** Read the path-loss exponent carefully; if not specified, assume **free-space ($n=2$)**.
> 4. **Generic WSN definitions:** Writing "WSN is a network of sensors" without specifying *multi-hop*, *self-configuring*, *resource-constrained* loses 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Confusing WSN with MANET:** Examiners deduct marks if you do not highlight the *unique* constraints like energy, scale, and harsh deployment.
> 6. **Formula memorization without derivation:** Simply writing the final $k_{opt}$ formula without derivation steps will only fetch partial marks (≤ 3 out of 7).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Print this section. It is the single most important KTU exam-prep sheet for this topic.**

### ⚡ Energy — The King Constraint

- Communication ≫ Computation energy (**~1000×**)
- First-order radio model: $E_{tx} = E_{elec} \cdot k + \varepsilon_{amp} \cdot k \cdot d^{n}$
- Optimal hop count: $k_{opt} = D \cdot \sqrt{\varepsilon_{amp} / (2 E_{elec})}$
- Battery energy for AA alkaline ≈ **3 J** usable; voltage **3.3 V**

### 🔧 Hardware Limits

- CPU: 8–16 bit, 4–8 MHz
- RAM: **4–10 KB**, Flash: **48–128 KB**
- Radio: IEEE 802.15.4, **250 kbps**
- Cannot run full TCP/IP, RSA, or large routing tables

### 📡 Communication Issues

- ISM bands: **433 MHz, 868/915 MHz, 2.4 GHz**
- Bandwidth: **250 kbps raw, ~30–80 kbps effective**
- Path loss exponent $n$: **2** free-space, **3–4** shadowed, **4–6** indoor obstructed
- Standard propagation model: log-normal shadowing

### 🌐 Protocol Layer

- **Self-organization** mandatory — no manual config possible
- **Data-centric addressing** preferred over IP
- **Multi-hop routing** mandatory — direct transmission wastes energy
- **Duty cycling** extends lifetime by **100×–1000×**

### 🆚 WSN vs MANET vs Cellular

- WSN: **static, dense, energy-starved, data-centric**
- MANET: **mobile, sparse, energy-aware, address-centric**
- Cellular: **mains-powered, QoS-optimized, hierarchical**

### 🎯 Cross-Layer Solutions

- **MAC:** S-MAC, B-MAC, T-MAC → duty cycling
- **Network:** LEACH, PEGASIS, SPIN → energy-aware routing
- **Application:** data aggregation, compression, adaptive sampling

### 📐 Key Numbers to Memorize (Numerical Problems)

- $E_{elec} = 50$ nJ/bit
- $\varepsilon_{amp}$ (free space) = $100$ pJ/bit/m²
- $\varepsilon_{amp}$ (multipath) = $0.0013$ pJ/bit/m⁴
- Path loss $n = 2$ or $4$ → use the one given in problem

### 🏆 KTU-Style Answering Strategy (14-mark questions)

1. **Open with a one-line definition** of WSN (1 mark)
2. **Categorize** constraints into 4 layers (2 marks)
3. **Detail each constraint** with example + impact (3 marks)
4. **Compare** with MANET/IP network (1 mark)
5. **Conclude** with protocol/design response (1 mark)

### 📚 Syllabus Cross-Reference (Module 1)

| Sub-topic | Topic Recap Status |
|---|---|
| Introduction to WSN | Covered (CONTEXT) |
| **Unique Constraints and Challenges** | ✅ **THIS TOPIC — Mastered** |
| Sensor Node Architecture | Next topic |
| Applications | Module 5 |

> **End of Topic Notes — Unique Constraints and Challenges (Module 1, PEMRT634)**
