## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define a WSN Gateway. List any four core functions performed by it.**

**Model Answer:**
A **WSN Gateway** is an intermediate device that bridges the constrained wireless sensor network (typically using IEEE 802.15.4, ZigBee, or LoRaWAN) with the conventional IP-based network infrastructure (Ethernet, Wi-Fi, cellular).

**Four core functions:**

1. **Protocol Translation:** Converts 6LoWPAN/CoAP/MQTT-SN frames from sensor nodes into HTTP/MQTT/IP packets for the internet.
2. **Data Aggregation:** Combines multiple sensor readings (averaging, min/max) to reduce uplink traffic.
3. **Security Enforcement:** Terminates DTLS/TLS sessions, manages encryption keys, and authenticates nodes.
4. **Network Management:** Maintains routing tables (RPL DODAG), handles OTA firmware updates, and synchronizes clocks.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse a *gateway* with a *router*. A router forwards packets within the same network domain. A **gateway** performs **cross-domain protocol translation**. Always highlight this distinction in your answer.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Differentiate between a single-hop gateway and a multi-hop gateway in WSN.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Single-Hop Gateway | Multi-Hop Gateway |
|---|---|---|
| **Topology** | Star (direct sensor-to-gateway) | Mesh (sensors relay through intermediate nodes) |
| **Scalability** | Limited (~50 nodes) | High (>1000 nodes) |
| **Energy Efficiency** | High at gateway, low at distant sensors | Better distributed energy load |
| **Latency** | Low (one hop) | Higher (multiple hops) |
| **Failure Tolerance** | Low (single point of failure) | High (alternative paths) |
| **Use Case** | Indoor home automation | Large-scale field monitoring |

---

### 5.2 Part B: Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the architectural position of a gateway in the WSN protocol stack. Describe in detail the protocol translation process from IEEE 802.15.4 / 6LoWPAN to IPv6 / TCP. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A WSN deployment covers an area of **800 m × 600 m**. Each gateway has a communication radius of **60 m**. Assuming hexagonal packing efficiency, compute the minimum number of gateways required for full coverage. Show all derivations. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Architectural Position and Protocol Translation:**

The gateway sits at the **intersection of the WSN field network and the IP backbone**. In the protocol stack, it performs translation across **four critical layers**:

- **Physical/MAC Layer:** Receives IEEE 802.15.4 frames operating in the **2.4 GHz ISM band** with a maximum frame size of **127 bytes**.
- **Adaptation Layer:** Performs **6LoWPAN header compression** to fit IPv6 packets (minimum 40-byte header) into the small 802.15.4 frames using **IPHC (IP Header Compression)**.
- **Network Layer:** Reconstructs the full IPv6 datagram by decompressing the 6LoWPAN header. Routes the packet via RPL (if multi-hop).
- **Transport/Application Layer:** Translates UDP/CoAP from the sensor side to TCP/HTTP or MQTT on the IP side.

The **translation sequence** is: `802.15.4 Frame → MAC Decapsulation → 6LoWPAN Decompression → IPv6 Routing → CoAP/MQTT-SN → MQTT/HTTPS → Cloud`.

**(b) Numerical Derivation:**

Given: $A = 800$ m, $B = 600$ m, $r = 60$ m, $\eta_p = \frac{\pi}{2\sqrt{3}} \approx 0.9069$

Step 1 — Total area:

$$
A_{total} = 800 \times 600 = 480000 \text{ m}^{2}
$$

Step 2 — Effective area per gateway (hexagonal packing):

$$
A_{eff} = \eta_p \cdot \pi r^{2} = 0.9069 \times \pi \times 60^{2}
$$

$$
A_{eff} = 0.9069 \times 3.1416 \times 3600 = 10256.6 \text{ m}^{2}
$$

Step 3 — Minimum number of gateways:

$$
N_g = \left\lceil \frac{480000}{10256.6} \right\rceil = \lceil 46.80 \rceil = 47 \text{ gateways}
$$

**Answer:** **47 gateways** are required for full coverage with 5% redundancy margin over the ideal hexagonal packing.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks Lost Here:** Students forget to apply the **hexagonal packing efficiency** $\eta_p$ and simply use $\pi r^{2}$, leading to an underestimated count (~42 gateways). This costs **2 marks**. Also, students often forget to take the **ceiling function** $\lceil \cdot \rceil$ — partial gateways don't exist in practice.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**(a)** Discuss the role of a gateway in providing **security** and **clock synchronization** in a WSN. Mention the relevant protocols. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A gateway serves **200 sensor nodes**, each transmitting a packet of **64 bytes** every **30 seconds**. Using $E_{elec} = 50$ nJ/bit, calculate the **total receive energy consumed by the gateway in 24 hours**. Comment on whether a battery-powered gateway is feasible. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Security and Clock Synchronization:**

**Security Role:**
The gateway acts as the **central trust anchor** for the WSN. It performs:

- **Key Management:** Acts as the **Key Distribution Center (KDC)**, distributing symmetric AES-128 keys to sensor nodes during network formation.
- **Authentication:** Implements **IEEE 802.1X** port-based authentication for the IP backbone and **DTLS handshake** for sensor-to-gateway links.
- **Encryption Termination:** Decrypts DTLS-protected traffic from sensors and re-encrypts with TLS for cloud transmission.
- **Intrusion Detection:** Monitors anomalous traffic patterns using lightweight IDS rules.

**Clock Synchronization Role:**
The gateway is the **master clock** for the WSN. It runs the **FTSP (Flooding Time Synchronization Protocol)** or **PTP (Precision Time Protocol)** and periodically broadcasts synchronization beacons. This is critical for:

- **TDMA scheduling** in MAC protocols.
- **Time-stamping** sensor data for correlation.
- **Coordinated sleep/wake cycles** for energy conservation.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

Given: $N = 200$, $k = 64$ bytes, $T = 30$ s, $E_{elec} = 50$ nJ/bit, Duration = 24 hours.

Step 1 — Bits per packet:

$$
L = 64 \times 8 = 512 \text{ bits}
$$

Step 2 — Bits per second from one node:

$$
R_{node} = \frac{512}{30} = 17.067 \text{ bits/s}
$$

Step 3 — Total incoming bit rate:

$$
R_{total} = 200 \times 17.067 = 3413.3 \text{ bits/s}
$$

Step 4 — Receiver power:

$$
P_{rx} = E_{elec} \cdot R_{total} = 50 \times 10^{-9} \times 3413.3 = 1.707 \times 10^{-4} \text{ W}
$$

Step 5 — Energy per second; then per 24 hours (86400 s):

$$
E_{24h} = P_{rx} \times 86400 = 1.707 \times 10^{-4} \times 86400 = 14.74 \text{ J}
$$

**Feasibility Comment:**
A **2× AA alkaline battery** stores approximately **10,000 J** of energy. The gateway consumes only **14.74 J/day**, which would theoretically last **~679 days**. However, in practice, a gateway also performs **protocol translation, encryption, and uplink transmission** (often via cellular modems drawing ~2–5 W), which dramatically increases consumption. Hence, **battery-only gateways are feasible only for low-throughput, low-duty-cycle deployments**; most commercial gateways are **mains-powered with battery backup**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Marks Distribution Warning:** The 7-mark sub-part (b) carries marks for: (i) correct unit conversion **1 mark**, (ii) correct formula selection **2 marks**, (iii) accurate arithmetic **2 marks**, and (iv) feasibility commentary **2 marks**. Skipping the commentary will cost you **2 marks** even if the calculation is perfect.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Gateway Concepts:**

- **Definition:** A gateway is a **protocol-translating, security-enforcing, data-aggregating edge device** between WSN and IP networks — **not** just a router.
- **Core Functions:** Protocol translation, data aggregation, security, clock sync, network management, OTA updates.
- **Key Protocols:** IEEE 802.15.4, 6LoWPAN, RPL, CoAP, MQTT/MQTT-SN, DTLS/TLS.
- **Coverage Formula (Hexagonal):** $N_g = \left\lceil \frac{2\sqrt{3} \cdot A \cdot B}{\pi^{2} \cdot r^{2}} \right\rceil$
- **Latency Formula:** $T_{e2e} = T_{prop} + T_{trans} + T_{queue} + T_{proc}$
- **Energy Model:** $E_{tx}(k,d) = E_{elec} \cdot k + \epsilon_{amp} \cdot k \cdot d^{\alpha}$
- **Compression Ratio:** $\eta_c = 1 - \frac{D_{out}}{D_{in}}$
- **Standard Frequency:** **2.4 GHz** ISM band for IEEE 802.15.4.
- **Speed of Light Constant:** $c = 3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s (always use this exact value unless otherwise stated).
- **Ceiling Function:** Always apply $\lceil \cdot \rceil$ when counting discrete entities (gateways, sensors).
- **Gateway vs Sink:** A *sink* is the logical destination; a *gateway* is the physical bridge.
- **Typical Coverage:** ZigBee ~30–100 m; LoRa ~2–10 km.
- **Typical Nodes per Gateway:** 1,000 to 65,000 depending on protocol.
- **Examiner Loves to Ask:** "Differentiate gateway from router/sink/cluster head."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Operating Systems and Execution Environments
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT634 - WIRELESS & SENSORS NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Operating Systems and Execution Environments

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

**Operating System (OS) for a Wireless Sensor Node** is a specialised, resource-constrained system software layer that manages the node's hardware (CPU, memory, radio, sensors, timers), schedules tasks, enforces concurrency, handles power management, and provides an abstraction layer (Execution Environment) to upper-layer applications in a Wireless Sensor Network (WSN).

An **Execution Environment (EE)** is the runtime substrate on top of which application and system components execute. It defines how computations are triggered (event-driven, thread-driven, or hybrid), how state is preserved, and how modules communicate. Together, the OS and the EE determine the node's programmability, energy footprint, and real-time behaviour.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The official course PEMRT634 expects students to differentiate between **event-driven** (TinyOS) and **multi-threaded / protothread** (Contiki) execution models, and to understand the role of components, interfaces, and configuration in the **nesC** programming model.

### 1.2 The Trio of Constraints in Sensor Node OS Design

| Constraint | Typical Value in Motes | Implication for OS |
|---|---|---|
| **RAM** | **2 – 10 KB** | No in-place dynamic memory, no paging |
| **ROM / Flash** | **48 – 256 KB** | Whole OS image must be statically linked |
| **CPU** | 8-bit (e.g., ATmega128) at **4 – 8 MHz** | No heavy context switching |
| **Energy** | Coin cell / 2×AA, lifetime in **years** | Radio must sleep 99 % of the time |
| **Radio duty cycle** | < **1 %** | OS must be interrupt/post-event driven |

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The Hospital ER

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Picture a tiny rural hospital with only one doctor, one nurse, and one bed (the mote). Patients (events) keep arriving — a temperature spike, a packet arrival, a timer tick. A **traditional desktop OS** is like a hospital that insists on giving every patient a separate room and a dedicated nurse. The hospital collapses.
>
> A **sensor-node OS** works like a smart ER: the doctor (CPU) reacts to whichever alarm rings first (event), finishes the immediate treatment, and goes back to sleep. There is no idle "nurse" waiting for a future patient. This **event-driven, run-to-completion** philosophy is the heart of TinyOS, while **protothreads** (Contiki) let the doctor *pretend* he has multiple nurses, but at the cost of a tiny bookkeeping trick. The **execution environment** is the ER's standard operating procedure (triage rules) that tells the doctor how to react to alarms.

### 1.4 Classification of Sensor Network Operating Systems

```
Sensor-Node OS
 ├── Event-Driven (single stack, no blocking)
 │     └── TinyOS (nesC)
 ├── Multi-Threaded / Protothread
 │     └── Contiki (C with protothreads)
 ├── Real-Time / RTOS
 │     └── FreeRTOS, RIOT, Zephyr
 └── Virtual Machine / Interpreted
       └── Mate, Squawk, VM-based
```

> [!TIP]
> For KTU exams, **TinyOS** and **Contiki** are the two reference OSes you must master. Treat FreeRTOS/RIOT as comparison/contrast points.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 What Does a Sensor-Node OS Actually Provide?

A sensor-node OS is functionally a **micro-kernel** offering a minimum set of services. Decomposing it into the six logical blocks the KTU examiner loves:

1. **Hardware Abstraction Layer (HAL)** — uniform register-level access (ADC, SPI, GPIO).
2. **Scheduler** — decides which task runs next (FIFO, priority, EDF, or no scheduler at all in pure event-driven systems).
3. **Communication Stack** — usually a thin layer over the radio driver (802.15.4 MAC in TinyOS 2.x).
4. **Power Manager** — controls duty cycling, CPU sleep modes, radio sleep.
5. **Memory Manager** — typically a *static* or *pool* allocator; no `malloc/free` in the general case.
6. **Execution Environment** — rules for writing *components* and *wiring* them (TinyOS), or for spawning *protothreads* (Contiki).

### 2.2 Event-Driven vs Multi-Threaded — The Central Trade-off

| Property | Event-Driven (TinyOS) | Multi-Threaded (Contiki) |
|---|---|---|
| Concurrency primitive | Task + interrupt + `post` | Protothread (cooperative) + event |
| Stack usage | **Single shared stack** | One stack per thread, but protothreads reuse one |
| Blocking calls | **Not allowed** | Allowed via `PT_WAIT_UNTIL` |
| Energy efficiency | Best (zero-cost sleep) | Slightly worse due to bookkeeping |
| Programming model | Components + wiring | Procedural C + protothreads |
| State preservation | Explicit `state machine variable` | Implicit (line numbers in `LC`) |
| Real-time guarantees | Weak (run-to-completion) | Better with priorities + preemptive scheduler |

### 2.3 TinyOS — The De-facto Event-Driven OS

#### 2.3.1 Design Philosophy

TinyOS, developed at UC Berkeley, was the first OS designed *from scratch* for motes. Its core principles are:

- **Single shared stack** — no process switch, hence no kernel-mode stack overflow risk.
- **Run-to-completion (RTC) tasks** — a `task` is an atomic, non-preemptible unit.
- **Two-level concurrency** — *Tasks* (deferred work, scheduled by the kernel) and *Hardware Event Handlers* (interrupts, may preempt tasks and other handlers of equal or lower priority).
- **Components** — all software is a component, communicating only through declared **interfaces**.

#### 2.3.2 nesC — The Language of TinyOS

**nesC** (network embedded systems C) is a dialect of C that adds:

- **Components** as the unit of modularity.
- **Interfaces** as bidirectional named sets of commands and events.
- **Wiring** that statically connects interface users to providers.
- **Split-phase operations** because every long-running call is split into a *command* (issue) and an *event* (signal completion).

```
Application  ── uses ──>  Component
   │                          │
   │                          ▼
   │                provides   Interface
   │                          ▲
   └────── wired by Configuration ──────┘
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why split-phase?** Because in a single-stack, non-blocking OS you can never wait for the radio to finish receiving a packet. The command `send()` returns immediately; later, a `sendDone()` event fires. This is the famous *"split-phase"* idiom that you must quote in any 14-mark question on TinyOS.

#### 2.3.3 TinyOS Scheduler (simplified)

The scheduler is a tiny **FIFO of `task` pointers**:

```
TOS Scheduler Pseudocode (FIFO, non-preemptive)
──────────────────────────────────────────────
loop:
    if task_queue is NOT empty:
        t = dequeue()
        t()                    // run-to-completion
    else:
        CPU.sleep()            // deep sleep until next interrupt
```

### 2.4 Contiki — Protothreads and the Hybrid Model

Contiki (Adam Dunkels, SICS) takes a different route: it preserves the **C programming style** familiar to embedded engineers but provides **protothreads** — extremely lightweight, stackless, cooperative threads.

#### 2.4.1 The Protothread Trick

A protothread is a C function that uses *local continuation* `LC` (or the higher-level `PT_*` macros) to remember where it left off. The mechanism exploits the C `switch` fall-through:

```c
static
PT_THREAD(send_thread(struct pt *p)) {
    PT_BEGIN(p);
    PT_WAIT_UNTIL(p, ev == EV_READY);
    radio_send(...);
    PT_WAIT_UNTIL(p, ev == EV_DONE);
    PT_END(p);
}
```

The compiler stores the `pt->lc` (a single `unsigned short`) which encodes the *line number* the thread last executed. Therefore:

- **Memory per protothread ≈ 2 bytes** (the `lc`).
- **Stack per protothread ≈ 0 bytes** (uses the function's own stack frame, which is released between yields).

This is the unique selling point that makes Contiki popular for **IPv6/6LoWPAN** stacks (e.g., RPL, CoAP).

#### 2.4.2 Contiki Process Model

A **process** in Contiki is declared with `PROCESS(name, "label")`, started with `PROCESS_BEGIN()`, and yields using `PROCESS_WAIT_EVENT_UNTIL(c)`, `PROCESS_YIELD()`, or `PROCESS_EXIT()`.

### 2.5 RTOS Alternatives (For Comparison)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Modern motes (e.g., OpenMote, nRF52840) often use full RTOSes such as **FreeRTOS** or **RIOT**. Unlike TinyOS/Contiki, these provide **preemptive priority-based scheduling**, semaphores, mutexes, and message queues. The trade-off is **higher RAM** (multiple stacks, TCBs) and **higher interrupt latency**.

| Feature | TinyOS | Contiki | RIOT / FreeRTOS |
|---|---|---|---|
| Scheduler | FIFO tasks | Event + protothread | Priority preemptive |
| RAM footprint | **< 1 KB** | **< 2 KB** | 4 – 16 KB |
| Language | nesC | C | C |
| IPv6/6LoWPAN | Limited | **Native** (uIP) | Native |
| Power efficiency | ★★★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★ |

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula / Metric Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> Although an OS topic is not heavy on formulas, a few quantitative metrics recur in KTU problems (e.g., duty-cycle, energy per task, scheduler worst-case latency).

$$
T_{\text{cycle}} = T_{\text{active}} + T_{\text{sleep}}
$$

$$
\text{Duty Cycle (\%)} = \frac{T_{\text{active}}}{T_{\text{cycle}}} \times 100
$$

$$
E_{\text{task}} = P_{\text{active}} \cdot T_{\text{active}} + P_{\text{sleep}} \cdot T_{\text{sleep}}
$$

$$
\text{Lifetime (hours)} = \frac{E_{\text{battery}} (\text{J})}{E_{\text{task}} \cdot N_{\text{cycles/hour}}}
$$

$$
T_{\text{latency, TinyOS}} = T_{\text{current task}} + T_{\text{context save}} + T_{\text{ISR}}
$$

$$
T_{\text{latency, RIOT}} = T_{\text{scheduler}} + T_{\text{context switch (2 stacks)}} + T_{\text{ISR}}
$$

$$
S_{\text{proto}} = \text{sizeof}(\text{struct pt}) \approx 2 \text{ bytes}
$$

| Symbol | Meaning | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $T_{\text{active}}$ | CPU + radio on time per cycle | s |
| $T_{\text{sleep}}$ | CPU + radio off time per cycle | s |
| $E_{\text{battery}}$ | Total stored energy | J (Joules) |
| $N_{\text{cycles/hour}}$ | Wake-ups per hour | h$^{\text{-1}}$ |
| $S_{\text{proto}}$ | Memory per protothread | bytes |
| $P_{\text{active}}$ | Power while active (mote) | **30 – 60 mW** |
| $P_{\text{sleep}}$ | Power while sleeping (mote) | **< 30 $\mu$W** |

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **TinyOS** is still used in academic testbeds (MoteLab, Indriya) and in long-running environmental monitoring.
- **Contiki** is the OS behind the early **6LoWPAN** IoT revolution and powers commercial stacks (Thingsquare, Cisco).
- **RIOT** is the OS used in **OpenWSN**, **FIT IoT-LAB**, and many Industry-4.0 deployments.
- A sound understanding of the execution environment is the single biggest factor in **writing bug-free, energy-efficient** firmware for billion-node IoT rollouts.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> This section gives you the **complete analytical workings** for the few quantitative problems KTU can ask on this topic — duty-cycle, energy, scheduler latency, and protothread memory — every step shown.

### 3.1 Analytical Problem 1 — Duty Cycle and Node Lifetime

**Given:** A TelosB mote with $P_{\text{active}} = 45$ mW, $P_{\text{sleep}} = 15$ $\mu$W, $E_{\text{battery}} = 9$ kJ (2×AA Alkaline). The mote wakes for $T_{\text{active}} = 50$ ms every $T_{\text{cycle}} = 5$ s. Find (a) duty cycle, (b) energy per cycle, (c) total lifetime in days.

#### Step (a) — Duty Cycle

$$
\text{Duty Cycle} = \frac{T_{\text{active}}}{T_{\text{cycle}}} \times 100 = \frac{0.050}{5} \times 100 = 1.0 \,\%
$$

**Interpretation:** The radio is on only **1 %** of the time, satisfying KTU's "energy-frugal" benchmark for WSN.

#### Step (b) — Energy per Cycle

$$
E_{\text{active}} = P_{\text{active}} \cdot T_{\text{active}} = 0.045 \,\text{W} \times 0.050 \,\text{s} = 2.25 \times 10^{-3} \,\text{J}
$$

$$
E_{\text{sleep}} = P_{\text{sleep}} \cdot T_{\text{sleep}} = 1.5 \times 10^{-5} \,\text{W} \times 4.950 \,\text{s} \approx 7.425 \times 10^{-5} \,\text{J}
$$

$$
E_{\text{cycle}} = E_{\text{active}} + E_{\text{sleep}} \approx 2.324 \times 10^{-3} \,\text{J}
$$

#### Step (c) — Node Lifetime

$$
N_{\text{cycles/hour}} = \frac{3600}{5} = 720 \;\text{cycles/h}
$$

$$
E_{\text{per hour}} = 720 \times 2.324 \times 10^{-3} \approx 1.673 \,\text{J/h}
$$

$$
\text{Lifetime (h)} = \frac{9000}{1.673} \approx 5378 \,\text{h} \approx 224 \,\text{days}
$$

**Result:** Approximately **224 days** of operation on 2×AA cells — typical of a TelosB-based environmental node.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Problem 2 — Worst-Case Scheduler Latency

**Given:** A TinyOS FIFO scheduler with 5 pending tasks of execution times $t_1, \ldots, t_5 = 3, 1, 4, 2, 6$ ms. An interrupt arrives just *after* task 1 starts. Find the worst-case latency to service the interrupt's deferred work (a `post()` task at the tail of the queue).

$$
T_{\text{latency, WC}} = \sum_{i=1}^{5} t_i + T_{\text{context}} = (3+1+4+2+6)\,\text{ms} + 0
$$

$$
T_{\text{latency, WC}} = 16 \,\text{ms}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Insight for KTU:** TinyOS offers **no real-time guarantee** because $T_{\text{latency}}$ is bounded only by the *sum of all pending tasks*. This is why a 14-mark question may ask you to compare with a *priority pre-emptive* RTOS where the same latency is bounded by a single context-switch time.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Problem 3 — Protothread Memory Footprint

**Given:** A Contiki application declares 4 protothreads. Each uses a `struct pt` of 2 bytes (the local-continuation word). Compare this with 4 traditional RTOS threads requiring 256-byte stacks + 32-byte TCBs.

$$
S_{\text{proto,total}} = 4 \times 2 = 8 \;\text{bytes}
$$

$$
S_{\text{RTOS,total}} = 4 \times (256 + 32) = 1152 \;\text{bytes}
$$

$$
\text{Saving} = \frac{1152 - 8}{1152} \times 100 \approx 99.3 \,\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This single calculation is a KTU "guaranteed 7-marker" because it directly proves *why* Contiki's protothread model dominates in memory-starved motes.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Problem 4 — Energy Saved by Event-Driven Sleep

**Given:** Same mote as Problem 1, but a *polling* (non-event-driven) implementation keeps the CPU awake $T_{\text{active}} = 200$ ms per 5-s cycle. Compare yearly energy.

$$
E_{\text{cycle, polling}} = 0.045 \times 0.200 + 1.5\times 10^{-5} \times 4.800 = 9.072 \times 10^{-3} \,\text{J}
$$

$$
E_{\text{cycle, event}} = 2.324 \times 10^{-3} \,\text{J}
$$

$$
\text{Saving ratio} = \frac{9.072 - 2.324}{9.072} \times 100 \approx 74.4 \,\%
$$

**Conclusion:** An event-driven execution environment saves nearly **3/4** of the energy of a naïve polling loop — the central reason TinyOS-style OSes won the academic mote wars.

---

### 3.5 KTU 14-Mark Analytical Pattern (Energy vs. Duty-Cycle Sweep)

| Duty Cycle $D$ (%) | $E_{\text{cycle}}$ (J) | Cycles / hour | Energy / hour (J) | Lifetime (days) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0.5 | 1.18 × 10$^{\text{-3}}$ | 720 | 0.85 | 441 |
| 1.0 | 2.32 × 10$^{\text{-3}}$ | 720 | 1.67 | 224 |
| 2.0 | 4.60 × 10$^{\text{-3}}$ | 720 | 3.31 | 113 |
| 5.0 | 1.14 × 10$^{\text{-2}}$ | 720 | 8.21 | 46 |

> Notice the **inverse-linear** lifetime behaviour: doubling duty cycle halves lifetime. This is a favourite KTU 14-mark "explain the table" question.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level Architecture of a Sensor-Node OS

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Application Components] --> B[Execution Environment]
    B --> C[Tiny Scheduler FIFO]
    B --> D[Power Manager]
    C --> E[Hardware Abstraction Layer]
    D --> E
    E --> F[CPU Core]
    E --> G[Radio 802.15.4]
    E --> H[Sensors ADC]
    E --> I[Timers and Sleep]
    J[Interrupt Hardware Events] --> B
    K[Post Task] --> C
```

### 4.2 TinyOS Component / Wiring Model (nesC)

```mermaid
graph LR
    App[AppC Configuration] -->|wires| C1[SensorC]
    App -->|wires| C2[RadioC]
    App -->|wires| C3[TimerC]
    C1 -->|uses IF_Read| H1[ADC Hardware]
    C2 -->|uses IF_Send| H2[Radio Driver]
    C3 -->|uses IF_Tick| H3[Timer HW]
    App -->|provides IF to app| USR[UserApp Component]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The *Configuration* file (e.g., `AppC.nc`) is the only place where **wiring** happens. The KTU examiner loves asking: *"Why is wiring done at compile time in nesC and what advantage does this give?"* — Answer: zero run-time binding cost, smaller image, energy savings.

### 4.3 Contiki Process Lifecycle

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Running : PROCESS_BEGIN
    Running --> Waiting : PT_WAIT_UNTIL
    Waiting --> Running : event fires
    Running --> Exited : PROCESS_EXIT
    Exited --> [*]
    Running --> Polling : PROCESS_YIELD
    Polling --> Running : next scheduler tick
```

### 4.4 Run-to-Completion Task Flow in TinyOS

```mermaid
graph TD
    start([Interrupt Arrives]) --> isr[ISR runs at hardware priority]
    isr --> post[Calls post task]
    post --> enqueue[Task placed in FIFO queue]
    enqueue --> idle{CPU idle?}
    idle -->|Yes| run[Scheduler dequeues and runs task]
    idle -->|No| enqueue
    run --> sleep[CPU enters LPM sleep]
    sleep --> start
```

### 4.5 Event-Driven vs Multi-Threaded — Side-by-Side Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph EventDriven[Event-Driven TinyOS]
        E1[Event Arrives] --> E2[ISR handler]
        E2 --> E3[post task]
        E3 --> E4[Task runs to completion]
        E4 --> E1
    end
    subgraph MultiThreaded[Protothread Contiki]
        M1[Event arrives] --> M2[Scheduler picks PT]
        M2 --> M3[PT resumes from lc]
        M3 --> M4[PT blocks on PT_WAIT_UNTIL]
        M4 --> M2
    end
```

### 4.6 Memory Layout of a Protothread (Local Continuation)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[struct pt] --> B[lc uint16 t]
    A --> C[state uint8 t]
    B -. encodes .-> D[Line number in switch fall-through]
    D --> E[Resumes correct PT_WAIT]
```

> [!TIP]
> When asked in KTU to draw the protothread stack, draw the *call-stack* of the **enclosing C function**, not a dedicated per-thread stack. This subtle point is worth 2 bonus marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> *Define an "Execution Environment" in the context of a sensor-node operating system. Give one example.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
An *Execution Environment* is the runtime substrate that defines **how** a piece of software on a sensor node is invoked and how it interacts with the rest of the system. It encompasses the scheduling policy, the calling convention (event vs. thread), and the state-preservation mechanism. *Example:* the **nesC component model with task queue** in TinyOS is the execution environment of TinyOS; alternatively, the **protothread continuation model** in Contiki is the execution environment of Contiki.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
> *List any two fundamental differences between TinyOS and Contiki.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Concurrency Model:** TinyOS uses a *two-level event-driven* model (tasks + interrupt handlers, single shared stack); Contiki supports *event-driven code* **plus** *protothreads* that look like blocking C threads.
2. **Programming Language:** TinyOS is written in **nesC** with explicit component wiring; Contiki is written in standard **C** with the `PT_*` macro library, which lowers the learning curve for embedded C developers.

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

#### ❓ Question A — 14 Marks (Choice 1)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]**
> **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the architecture of **TinyOS**. Discuss the role of *components*, *interfaces*, *commands*, *events* and *configuration* in the **nesC** programming model. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Explain **split-phase operations** in TinyOS with a suitable example. Why are blocking calls disallowed in TinyOS? **(7 marks)**

---

##### Model Solution to Question A

**Answer (a):** TinyOS architecture is built as a set of **statically-wired components**. The main abstractions are:

- **Component** — a self-contained software unit (module) that either *provides* or *uses* interfaces. Two kinds: *Module* (logic) and *Configuration* (wiring).
- **Interface** — a bidirectional named set of *commands* (downward calls, provided) and *events* (upward signals, used). Example: `Read` interface has command `read()` and event `readDone()`.
- **Wiring** — static connections in a Configuration file, resolved at **compile time**.

**Architecture Diagram (textual):**

```
        AppC.nc  (Configuration)
        ┌──────────────────────────────────────┐
        │  wires:                              │
        │   MainC.StdControl  ->  TimerC       │
        │   MainC.Boot        ->  PotC         │
        │   PotC.Read         ->  ADC          │
        │   PotC.Timer        ->  TimerC       │
        └──────────────────────────────────────┘
        ┌────────────┐  uses  ┌─────────────┐
        │ MainC.nc   │ <----- │ AppC.nc     │
        │ (Module)   │        │(Config)     │
        └────────────┘        └─────────────┘
```

The kernel (`TOS Scheduler`) is itself a component. The hardware drivers (Radio, ADC, Timer) and the application logic are also components. Because wiring is static, the **entire OS + application forms a single, monolithic image** that runs on a single shared stack.

**Answer (b):** A *split-phase operation* splits a single logical operation into two halves: a *command* that *initiates* the operation (non-blocking) and an *event* that *signals* its completion.

**Example — sending a packet:**

```c
// Command
command error_t Send.send(msg_t *m) {
    return call RadioStart.send(m);
}
// Event signalled later by the radio driver
event void Send.sendDone(msg_t *m, error_t err) {
    signal Send.sendDone(m, err);
}
```

**Why blocking is disallowed:** TinyOS uses a *single shared stack*. A blocking call would freeze the entire node, preventing the radio driver from servicing interrupts, leading to lost packets and missed timers. Split-phase design guarantees **no task can block**, so the scheduler can always return to the idle/sleep state.

---

#### ❓ Question B — 14 Marks (Choice 2)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model QP]**
> **(a)** Describe the **protothread** mechanism used in Contiki. How does it achieve stackless multithreading? **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Compare the **event-driven** execution model of TinyOS with the **protothread** model of Contiki. Mention at least four comparison points. **(7 marks)**

---

##### Model Solution to Question B

**Answer (a):** A *protothread* is a stackless, cooperative thread abstraction introduced by Adam Dunkels for memory-constrained sensor nodes. Each protothread is implemented as a C function that uses **local continuation** — a single 16-bit word — to remember the *point* in the function where it last blocked.

The continuation is updated using the C `switch`-statement fall-through trick:

```c
PT_THREAD(driver(struct pt *p)) {
    PT_BEGIN(p);
    PT_WAIT_UNTIL(p, cond1);
    /* critical code */
    PT_WAIT_UNTIL(p, cond2);
    PT_END(p);
}
```

The macros expand to a `switch(p->lc)` where the label cases encode the resume points. When a condition is false, the thread *yields* by storing the current line number in `p->lc` and returning to the scheduler. When the condition later becomes true, the scheduler calls the function again and the `switch` jumps to the right label, resuming execution **without saving any stack**.

**Memory cost:** ≈ **2 bytes per protothread**, regardless of how deep the function call tree is.

**Answer (b):** Comparison table:

| # | Criterion | TinyOS (Event-Driven) | Contiki (Protothread) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Concurrency primitive | FIFO tasks + ISRs | Protothreads + events |
| 2 | Blocking allowed? | **No** | **Yes** (`PT_WAIT_UNTIL`) |
| 3 | Memory per thread | 0 (no threads) | **~ 2 bytes** |
| 4 | Programming language | **nesC** | Standard C with macros |
| 5 | Code complexity for application | Higher (explicit state) | Lower (looks like linear C) |
| 6 | Power efficiency | ★★★★★ | ★★★★ |
| 7 | Best suited for | Data-collection motes | IPv6/IoT stacks |
| 8 | Real-time guarantees | Weak | Weak (cooperative) |

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in this Topic**
> 1. **Confusing "thread" with "protothread".** A protothread does *not* have its own stack; saying "each protothread has a 256-byte stack" will cost you 2 marks.
> 2. **Writing blocking pseudo-code in TinyOS solutions.** A TinyOS `task` must never call `while (!ready) ;`. Examiners scan for this — instant 3-mark penalty.
> 3. **Forgetting the term "split-phase".** This is a board keyword. If your answer to a TinyOS question doesn't mention "split-phase", you lose 2 marks.
> 4. **Skipping the wiring diagram.** A nesC answer without a Configuration / wiring block is incomplete; you forfeit 2 marks.
> 5. **Energy units.** Use **Joules (J)** and **Watts (W)** consistently; mixing mW·s and J confuses the examiner and triggers -1 mark.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- A **sensor-node OS** must satisfy *memory*, *energy* and *real-time* constraints far stricter than a desktop OS.
- The **execution environment** is the rule-book for *how* application code runs: event vs. thread, blocking vs. non-blocking, static vs. dynamic.
- **TinyOS** is **event-driven**, uses **nesC**, has a **single shared stack**, and forbids blocking via **split-phase operations**.
- **Components**, **interfaces**, **commands**, **events**, **configurations**, and **wiring** form the lexical backbone of any nesC answer.
- **Contiki** uses **C** with **protothreads** — stackless cooperative threads costing ~ **2 bytes** each.
- Protothreads save state in a **local continuation (lc)** using C `switch` fall-through — *not* a separate stack.
- **Split-phase** = the command/event pair used to avoid blocking (e.g., `send` + `sendDone`).
- **Duty cycle** $D = T_{\text{active}}/T_{\text{cycle}}$ is the single most important energy metric.
- **Lifetime (h)** $= E_{\text{battery}} / (E_{\text{cycle}} \times N_{\text{cycles/h}})$.
- **TinyOS scheduler** is non-preemptive FIFO → no real-time guarantee; latency = sum of pending tasks.
- **RTOS alternatives** (FreeRTOS, RIOT) give preemption at the cost of larger RAM footprint and context-switch overhead.
- **Energy saving** of event-driven over polling: typically **70 – 80 %** for the same workload.
- For KTU 2024 exams, expect a 7-mark sub-question asking to **draw a wiring diagram** and a 7-mark sub-question asking to **compare event-driven vs protothread** — prepare both with crisp tables.
- Always include the *physical meaning* of every formula (what the energy term represents) — KTU awards the +1 "interpretation" mark only when units and meaning are explicit.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
