topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT634 - WIRELESS & SENSORS NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Introduction to TinyOS and nesC

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📘 Introduction to TinyOS and nesC

> [!NOTE]
> **TinyOS** is the de-facto operating system for **Wireless Sensor Networks (WSN)**. It is not a general-purpose OS like Linux; it is an *embedded, event-driven, component-based* OS written specifically for **low-power, memory-constrained** sensor motes (typically with **< 10 KB RAM** and **< 48 KB flash**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **nesC** (pronounced "nested-C") is the **programming language** used to write TinyOS applications. It is a dialect of C, augmented with **component-oriented constructs** and a strict **event-driven concurrency model**. Without nesC, you cannot build TinyOS systems.

---

## 1.1 What is TinyOS? — Formal Definition

**TinyOS** is an open-source, BSD-licensed, lightweight, **event-driven operating system** designed by the University of California, Berkeley, specifically for **networked embedded systems (sensor motes)**. It is characterized by:

- A **component-based architecture** (no monolithic kernel).
- An **event-driven execution model** (no user-space processes/threads in the traditional sense).
- A **small memory footprint** (the entire core fits in about **400 bytes** of memory).
- A **split-phase (asynchronous) call** style to handle long-latency operations like radio packet transmission.

## 1.2 What is nesC? — Formal Definition

**nesC** is a **statically compiled, component-oriented** extension of the C programming language. It is the *only* language accepted by the TinyOS build toolchain. It introduces two new first-class abstractions:

1. **Components** — logical units of computation with strictly defined interfaces.
2. **Interfaces** — bidirectional, typed connection points between components.

> [!TIP]
> **Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)**
> 
> Imagine building a circuit board:
> - **Components** = the **chips/ICs** (e.g., a sensor chip, a radio chip, a microcontroller).
> - **Interfaces** = the **gold-plated pins** sticking out of each chip.
> - **nesC code** = the **circuit schematic** that wires one chip's pins to another's.
> - **TinyOS runtime** = the **PCB power rails and clock** that bring the whole board to life.
> 
> Just as a chip is useless without being soldered into a circuit, a nesC component is useless until it is "wired" (in nesC terminology, **wired**) to other components via shared interfaces.

## 1.3 Why do we need TinyOS and nesC?

WSN motes have **extreme hardware constraints**:

| Resource | Typical Mote (e.g., Mica2, TelosB) | A Modern Smartphone |
|---|---|---|
| RAM | **2 – 10 KB** | 4 – 12 GB |
| Flash | **48 – 128 KB** | 64 – 512 GB |
| CPU | 8-bit, **4 – 16 MHz** | Multi-core, GHz |
| Energy | **2x AA batteries** for months/years | Rechargeable, hours of use |
| Networking | Lossy, low-bandwidth radio | 4G/5G/Wi-Fi |

A standard RTOS (e.g., FreeRTOS, VxWorks) is too heavy, and a multi-threaded model with context-switching overhead wastes precious microjoules. TinyOS solves this with a **flat, single-stack, event-driven** model.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Misconception:** TinyOS does **not** support traditional preemptive multithreading. It uses a single stack and a **deferred work queue** called the **TinyOS task queue** for non-urgent processing.

## 1.4 History & Versions

- **v1.0 (2000):** Initial release by UC Berkeley.
- **v2.0 (2003):** Major rewrite; introduced the new **component model with explicit interfaces** (the version most aligned with the KTU 2024 syllabus).
- **v2.1.x:** Stable production series, used in academic and commercial WSN deployments (e.g., environmental monitoring, precision agriculture).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mapping:** This topic falls under **Module 2: Programming the Sensor Node**, and the expected outcome is the ability to *describe* the architecture and write/trace simple nesC programs.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 🔬 Deep Theoretical Analysis — Architecture & Execution Model

## 2.1 The TinyOS Component Model — The Big Picture

TinyOS is built out of three building blocks:

1. **Modules** (`module`)
2. **Configurations** (`configuration`)
3. **Interfaces** (`interface`)

### 2.1.1 Interfaces — The Communication Contract

An **interface** is a bidirectional named abstraction. It declares two kinds of functions:
- **Commands** → implemented by the *provider* component (e.g., `start()`, `read()`).
- **Events** → signaled by the *provider* component to the *user* (e.g., `readDone()`).

```c
interface Read {
  command error_t read();
  event error_t readDone(error_t result, uint16_t val);
}
```

> [!NOTE]
> Think of an interface as a **two-way walkie-talkie channel**: the user can press the "transmit" button to issue commands, and the provider can press the transmit button to signal events.

### 2.1.2 Modules — The Implementers

A **module** is a component that **provides** implementations of one or more interfaces. It contains the actual C code (variables, functions, logic). A module **uses** the interfaces it needs.

### 2.1.3 Configurations — The Wire-Up Blueprint

A **configuration** is a component that **wires** modules and other configurations together. It contains no algorithmic code; it only specifies:
- Which component provides which interface
- Which component uses which interface
- A `Main` component is always implicitly at the top of every TinyOS app.

### 2.1.4 The Wiring Rule

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The nesC Wiring Rule:** A connection between a `command` in the user's view and a `command` in the provider's implementation is called a *wire* in the configuration. If an interface is "used" but not wired, the program **fails to compile**. This is a **static guarantee** — all missing connections are caught at compile time.

## 2.2 Execution Model — Two Execution Contexts

TinyOS has **only two** types of executable units:

### 2.2.1 **Tasks** (`task`)

- Plain, run-to-completion units of work.
- **Deferred** — they sit in a FIFO task queue (`#define TOSH_MAX_TASKS_LOG2 5` ⇒ up to **32 tasks**).
- **Non-preemptive** — once a task starts, it runs until completion or until it `post`s a new task.
- Used for background, non-time-critical computation (e.g., data aggregation, logging).

### 2.2.2 **Events** (a.k.a. Hardware Event Handlers, or `async` functions)

- Triggered by **hardware interrupts** (timer, radio, ADC) **or** by **events signaled from other components**.
- **Atomic** — they preempt tasks but not other events (each event is run to completion, but no two events ever run concurrently).
- Used for time-critical, latency-sensitive reactions (e.g., packet reception, sensor sampling).

> [!TIP]
> **The Mental Model:** Imagine a single-lane bridge. **Events** are like *emergency vehicles* (they always go first). **Tasks** are *normal cars* (they queue up and wait). Two emergency vehicles never crash into each other because the bridge has a *run-to-completion* rule.

### 2.2.3 The Concurrency Theorem

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Theorem:** In TinyOS, *the state shared between a task and an event handler is the source of all race conditions*. The nesC compiler enforces a discipline called **`async`** to mark which functions can be called from asynchronous event contexts. A function not marked `async` may **only** be called from a task context. The compiler enforces this.

## 2.3 The Split-Phase Operation Pattern

TinyOS uses **split-phase calls** for every long-latency operation. There are **no blocking calls** in the entire system.

| Phase | Type | Returns | Latency |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Call phase** | `command` (e.g., `read()`) | Immediately (returns `SUCCESS` or `FAIL`) | ~1 µs |
| **Completion phase** | `event` (e.g., `readDone()`) | Result data | µs–ms later |

> [!WARNING]
> This is the **opposite** of conventional C programming. If a function is long, you **must** split it into a command and an event. The KTU board examiner *will* test this concept.

## 2.4 The nesC Compilation Pipeline

```
   .nc source files
        ↓
   ncc (nesC compiler)
        ↓
   C code (single flat file: `app.c`)
        ↓
   gcc / msp430-gcc / avr-gcc
        ↓
   .hex image (loaded onto mote)
```

The **`ncc` compiler** performs:
- **Component composition** (statically resolves all wirings)
- **Race-condition analysis** (checks `async` discipline)
- **Whole-program optimization** (since the entire app is known at compile time)

---

## 2.5 📋 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Statement | Boundary / Constraint |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Components | `module` (code) + `configuration` (wiring) | All apps have exactly one `Main` configuration |
| 2 | Interfaces | Bidirectional: `command` + `event` | `command` is user→provider; `event` is provider→user |
| 3 | Concurrency contexts | 2 only: `task` (deferred) and `event` (interrupt/async) | **32 tasks max** in the default queue |
| 4 | `async` keyword | Marks functions callable from event context | Non-`async` functions **cannot** be called from events |
| 5 | Split-phase | Every long call = `command` (returns fast) + `event` (signals completion) | **No blocking calls** allowed anywhere |
| 6 | Memory footprint | Core OS ≈ **400 bytes**; with stack ≈ **~1 KB** | Fits in MICA2/TelosB motes |
| 7 | Wiring | Static, resolved at compile time | Unwired interface ⇒ **compile error** |
| 8 | Scheduler | Priority-based preemption of tasks by events | Events never preempt events |
| 9 | nesC version | 1.3 (current stable, paired with TinyOS 2.1) | C99 compliant |
| 10 | Build tool | `ncc` → emits single C file → cross-compiled by `avr-gcc`/`msp430-gcc` | Output is an Intel HEX file |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Utility

TinyOS has powered landmark WSN deployments:
- **Great Duck Island Habitat Monitoring** (2002) — bird-nest microclimate monitoring using Mica motes.
- **Volcano Monitoring on Volcán Tungurahua** (2005) — seismic event detection.
- **Precision Agriculture** in vineyards across California (2006–2010).
- **Industrial Structural Health Monitoring** of bridges and high-rise buildings.

Its tiny footprint and proven reliability make it the canonical teaching platform in WSN courses worldwide — including **KTU's PEMRT634**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 🧮 Step-by-Step Analysis — A Walkthrough of a Classic nesC Program

We will dissect the canonical **"Blink" application** that ships with TinyOS 2.1. This is the exact type of code-tracing question KTU examiners ask.

## 3.1 The Full Program

```c
// File: BlinkC.nc  (the top-level configuration — wires modules)
configuration BlinkC {
}
implementation {
  components MainC, BlinkAppC, LedsC;
  components new TimerMilliC() as Timer0;
  BlinkAppC.Timer -> Timer0;
  BlinkAppC.Leds -> LedsC;
}

// File: BlinkAppC.nc  (the application configuration)
configuration BlinkAppC {
}
implementation {
  components BlinkAppC, LedsC, new TimerMilliC() as Timer;
  components MainC;
  App.Flash -> Timer.Timer;
  App.Leds  -> LedsC;
}

// File: Blink.nc  (the application module — actual logic)
module Blink {
  uses interface Timer<TMilli> as Timer;
  uses interface Leds;
}
implementation {
  event void Timer.fired() {
    call Leds.led0Toggle();
  }
  event void Boot.booted() {
    call Timer.startPeriodic(1000);   // 1000 ms = 1 s
  }
}
```

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Analysis

### Step 1 — Identify the Components
- `BlinkC` is the **top-level configuration** (root).
- `BlinkAppC` is an **intermediate configuration** (a "sub-assembled board").
- `MainC` provides the `Boot` interface (system initialization).
- `TimerMilliC` is a **parameterized component** (parameter `TMilli` selects millisecond resolution); the `new ...() as Timer0` syntax **instantiates** it.
- `LedsC` provides the `Leds` interface (3 LEDs).
- `Blink` is the **application module** containing our code.

### Step 2 — Trace the Wirings
The statement
```c
BlinkAppC.Timer -> Timer0;
```
means: *"The `Timer` interface used inside `BlinkAppC` is provided by the component instance `Timer0`."*

> [!NOTE]
> **Logical Deduction:** This is a directed edge in the **interface-graph**. The `->` is read as *"is-wired-to"* or *"is-provided-by"*.

### Step 3 — Trace the Boot Sequence
1. On power-on, hardware jumps to `MainC`'s assembly bootstrap.
2. `MainC` initializes hardware, then signals the `Boot.booted()` **event** upwards.
3. In our app, the wiring `App.Flash -> Timer.Timer` means `BlinkAppC`'s `Boot.booted()` triggers our `Blink.App.Boot.booted()` — which calls `Timer.startPeriodic(1000)`.
4. The timer starts, and every **1000 ms** it signals the `Timer.fired()` event.

### Step 4 — Trace the Event Handler
Each `Timer.fired()` event executes:
```
1. Atomically enter event context.
2. call Leds.led0Toggle()  →  command is forwarded over the wire to LedsC.
3. LedsC atomically toggles the GPIO bit.
4. Event completes; control returns to scheduler.
```

### Step 5 — Verify the `async` Discipline
- `Timer.fired()` is an **event**, therefore it runs in **async** context.
- The only call inside it is `Leds.led0Toggle()`, which is declared `command` in an `interface Leds`.
- `Leds.led0Toggle()` is itself implemented in `LedsC.nc` and is **not marked `async`** because it is a *command* (commands are always callable from event context per nesC rules).
- The compiler is happy. ✅

### Step 6 — Compute Energy Cost (Analytical Reasoning)

Let the mote run for **1 hour**. The blinking event fires once per second, so:
$$
N_{\text{events}} = 3600 \;\text{events/hour}
$$

Each `led0Toggle()` requires flipping a single GPIO bit — roughly **6 CPU cycles** on an MSP430. At **8 MHz**:
$$
t_{\text{toggle}} = \frac{6}{8 \times 10^6} = 0.75 \,\mu s
$$

Total active CPU time per hour:
$$
t_{\text{active}} = 3600 \times 0.75 \times 10^{-6} = 2.7 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 2.7 \text{ ms}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Insight for KTU answers:** The mote is awake for only **2.7 ms out of 3 600 000 ms** — a **duty cycle of 0.000 075 %**. The rest of the time, the CPU is in **LPM3 sleep**, drawing ~1 µA instead of ~1 mA. This is the **mathematical reason** a Mica2 mote can run for **> 1 year on 2 AA batteries** when running TinyOS.

### Step 7 — Concurrency Tree

```
        H/W reset
            │
        MainC.init()
            │
   event Boot.booted() ──── Blink.Boot.booted()
                                  │
                            Timer.startPeriodic(1000)
                                  │
                              [sleep...]
                                  │
                          Timer.fired()  (every 1 s)
                                  │
                          Leds.led0Toggle()
                                  │
                              [sleep...]
```

### Step 8 — Failure Modes a Student Must Know

| Mistake | What the Compiler/Scheduler Does | Marks Lost in Exam |
|---|---|---|
| Calling a non-`async` function from an event | **Compile error** in nesC | -2 |
| Blocking call (e.g., `delay()`) inside an event | Application hangs (or watchdog resets) | -3 |
| Forgetting to wire an interface | **Compile error** ("interface not bound") | -2 |
| Accessing a shared variable from both task & event without `atomic` | Race condition (intermittent bug) | -3 |

---

## 3.3 The BlinkC Analytical Matrix

| Layer | Component Type | Role | Provides | Uses |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 (HW) | Boot-time | Power-on reset vector | — | — |
| 1 | `MainC` (config) | Wires scheduler + hardware | `Boot`, `Init` | `Scheduler`, `PlatformInit` |
| 2 | `BlinkAppC` (config) | Wires our app to services | `Boot` (re-wired) | `Timer`, `Leds` |
| 3 | `Blink` (module) | Application logic | (none — top) | `Timer<TMilli>`, `Leds` |
| 4 | `TimerMilliC` (config + module pair) | Periodic timer service | `Timer<TMilli>` | `Timer`, `Alarm` |
| 5 | `LedsC` (config + module) | LED hardware abstraction | `Leds` | `HplLedsC` (hardware) |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 🗺️ Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level TinyOS Component Stack

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA["Application Module Blink nc"] 
    nodeB["Top Configuration BlinkC nc"]
    nodeC["Intermediate Configuration BlinkAppC nc"]
    nodeD["System Configuration MainC nc"]
    nodeE["Scheduler TinySchedulerC"]
    nodeF["Hardware Presentation Layer HPL"]
    nodeG["Physical Mote CPU Radio ADC LEDs"]

    nodeA -- uses Timer, Leds, Boot --> nodeC
    nodeB -- wires all to MainC --> nodeD
    nodeC -- wires App interfaces --> nodeD
    nodeD -- uses --> nodeE
    nodeE -- drives --> nodeF
    nodeF -- controls --> nodeG
```

## 4.2 Interface Wiring for the Blink Application

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph USER["User Component Blink module"]
        u1["uses Timer TMilli"]
        u2["uses Leds"]
        u3["uses Boot"]
    end

    subgraph PROV["Provider Components"]
        p1["TimerMilliC provides Timer TMilli"]
        p2["LedsC provides Leds"]
        p3["MainC provides Boot"]
    end

    u1 -- command startPeriodic, event fired --> p1
    u2 -- command led0Toggle --> p2
    u3 -- event booted --> p3

    p1 -- event fired --> u1
    p3 -- event booted --> u3
```

## 4.3 Execution Model — Tasks vs. Events

```mermaid
graph TD
    node1["Hardware Interrupt e g Timer ADC Radio"]
    node2["Async Event Handler runs to completion non preemptive among events"]
    node3["Command call to another component"]
    node4["Provider signals Event back to user"]
    node5["Task Queue FIFO up to 32 entries"]
    node6["Task executes run to completion non preemptive among tasks"]
    node7["Task calls post next task to queue"]
    node8["Scheduler picks next task"]

    node1 --> node2
    node2 --> node3
    node3 --> node4
    node4 --> node5
    node4 --> node8
    node5 --> node6
    node6 --> node7
    node7 --> node5
```

## 4.4 nesC Compilation Pipeline

```mermaid
graph TD
    nA["App nc files BlinkC nc BlinkAppC nc Blink nc"]
    nB["Library nc files TimerC LedsC MainC"]
    nC["ncc nesC compiler"]
    nD["Single C file app c"]
    nE["Cross compiler avr gcc or msp430 gcc"]
    nF["ELF object file app elf"]
    nG["msp430-objcopy to Intel HEX"]
    nH["app hex loaded onto mote flash"]

    nA --> nC
    nB --> nC
    nC --> nD
    nD --> nE
    nE --> nF
    nF --> nG
    nG --> nH
```

## 4.5 The Split-Phase Pattern

```mermaid
graph LR
    stepA["User calls command read"]
    stepB["Provider returns immediately with SUCCESS or FAIL"]
    stepC["Provider initiates long operation e g ADC conversion or radio transmit"]
    stepD["Operation completes in hardware"]
    stepE["Provider signals event readDone result data"]
    stepF["User resumes in event handler with data"]

    stepA --> stepB
    stepB --> stepC
    stepC --> stepD
    stepD --> stepE
    stepE --> stepF
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# ✍️ KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define TinyOS and list any four of its key features.**
**[Model Answer — 3 Marks]**

TinyOS is an open-source, event-driven, component-based operating system designed by the University of California, Berkeley, specifically for **resource-constrained wireless sensor motes**.

**Key features (any four, ½ mark each):**

1. **Component-based architecture** — system built out of modules and configurations.
2. **Event-driven execution model** — uses events and tasks instead of threads.
3. **Tiny memory footprint** — core fits in about **400 bytes** of RAM.
4. **Split-phase operations** — every long-latency call is split into a command and a completion event (no blocking).
5. **Static linking & whole-program optimization** — the entire app is known at compile time.
6. **Concurrency model** — only two contexts (`task` and `event`), no preemptive multithreading.

> **Mark distribution:** Definition (1M) + 4 features (½ × 4 = 2M).

---

### **Q2. What is nesC? How is it different from standard C?**
**[Model Answer — 3 Marks]**

**nesC** (pronounced "nested-C") is a **component-oriented, event-driven programming language** used to write applications for the TinyOS operating system. It is a dialect of C extended with two new first-class concepts: **components** and **interfaces**.

**Differences from standard C:**

| Aspect | Standard C | nesC |
|---|---|---|
| Execution model | Threads + blocking calls | Tasks + events; **no blocking** |
| Code organization | Files & functions | **Components** (modules + configurations) |
| Inter-module comm. | Global variables / headers | **Bidirectional, typed interfaces** |
| Race-condition support | None | Compiler-enforced `async` discipline |
| Compilation unit | Per-file | Whole-program; emits single `.c` file |

> **Mark distribution:** Definition (1M) + ≥4 valid differences (2M).

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)
> *Internal Choice: Answer either Question A OR Question B.*

---

### **📝 Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`**

**(a)** Explain the **TinyOS component model** in detail. With the help of a neat diagram, describe the role of **modules**, **configurations**, and **interfaces**, and explain the rules of **wiring**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Differentiate between a **task** and an **event** in TinyOS. Why does TinyOS not use a traditional thread-based model? Justify using two concrete examples. **(7 Marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution to Q.A(a)

**Introduction (1 M):** TinyOS is built around a **component-based software architecture**. The entire system, including hardware drivers, network stack, and user application, is decomposed into reusable software components.

**Definitions (3 M):**

- **Interface:** A named, typed, bidirectional abstraction. It declares a set of `command` functions (the *user → provider* direction) and a set of `event` functions (the *provider → user* direction).
  ```c
  interface Timer<TMilli> {
    command void startPeriodic(uint32_t ms);
    event void fired();
  }
  ```
- **Module:** A component that *implements* one or more interfaces using actual C code. It contains variables, the `implementation` block, and `uses` clauses to express its dependencies.
- **Configuration:** A component that *wires* modules and other configurations together. It contains **no algorithmic code**; it only declares the wiring topology using the `->` operator.

**Wiring Rules (2 M):**

1. An interface used by a component **must** be wired to a provider; otherwise, the program **fails to compile**.
2. A wiring line such as `App.Timer -> Timer0.Timer;` is a *directed edge* from a `uses` site to a `provides` site.
3. The `new TimerMilliC() as Timer0` syntax creates a **parameterized instance** of a generic component.

**Diagram (1 M):** A block diagram showing the user module, the configuration wiring the modules, and the provider modules, with labeled interface edges (refer to Section 4.2 above).

> **Mark distribution:** Intro (1M) + Definitions of module/config/interface (3M) + Wiring rules (2M) + Diagram (1M) = **7 M**.

#### ✅ Model Solution to Q.A(b)

**Comparison Table (3 M):**

| Property | Task | Event |
|---|---|---|
| Trigger | `post` from anywhere | Hardware interrupt or component signal |
| Preemption | Can be preempted by events | Cannot be preempted (atomic) |
| Context | Synchronous / deferred | Asynchronous / interrupt |
| Use case | Background processing | Time-critical I/O |
| Concurrency | Multiple tasks serialized | Events serialized, but preempt tasks |

**Why no threads? (2 M):** A WSN mote has only a single shared stack and a few KB of RAM. Preemptive threading would require:
- A separate stack per thread (≥ 200 B each × N threads ⇒ unsustainably high RAM).
- Context-switch overhead on every quantum (wastes microjoules of energy).
- Complex synchronization (mutexes, semaphores), which themselves consume code space.

**Two Concrete Examples (2 M):**

1. **Radio packet reception (Event-driven):** When a packet arrives, the radio raises an interrupt within microseconds. A thread (which might be blocked on a sensor read) would miss the packet. An event handler fires immediately, copies the bytes to a buffer, and posts a task for further processing.
2. **Hourly data aggregation (Task-based):** After 1 hour of event-driven sampling, the application `post`s a `computeAverage` task. The scheduler runs it whenever no event is pending, so the CPU sleeps in between, conserving energy.

> **Mark distribution:** Comparison table (3M) + Justification (2M) + 2 examples (1M each) = **7 M**.

---

### **📝 Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Explain the **nesC compilation model** and describe the role of the `ncc` compiler. List any **four compilation errors** that the nesC compiler specifically catches that a standard C compiler does not. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** What is the **split-phase operation** pattern in TinyOS? Compare it with a conventional **blocking call** in C, using a "read temperature sensor" example. Why is split-phase essential for WSN energy efficiency? **(7 Marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution to Q.B(a)

**Compilation Model (3 M):**
The nesC build process is fundamentally *whole-program*. The `ncc` compiler takes **all `.nc` files** (application + system library) as input, performs:

1. **Component composition** — statically resolves every interface wiring.
2. **`async` race-condition analysis** — checks that no non-`async` function is called from an event context.
3. **Emission of a single flat C file** (e.g., `app.c`).
4. **Invocation of the platform C cross-compiler** (e.g., `avr-gcc`, `msp430-gcc`) to produce an Intel HEX image for the mote.

**Role of `ncc` (1 M):** It is a **source-to-source translator** plus a **static analyzer**. It enforces TinyOS's component and concurrency rules.

**Four nesC-specific errors not caught by a C compiler (3 M, ¾ M each):**

1. **Unwired (unbound) interface:** "Component X uses interface Y, but Y is not provided" — `ncc` errors out; gcc would not even see the concept.
2. **Async violation:** A non-`async` function called from an event handler — `ncc` errors with "called from async context"; gcc would silently compile it, leading to a subtle runtime race.
3. **Interface type mismatch:** Wiring `Timer<TMilli>` to a provider that supplies `Timer<TMicro>` — `ncc` errors; gcc has no notion of generic interfaces.
4. **Component instantiation error:** A `new` expression for a component that is not a configuration, or wrong generic-parameter arity — `ncc` errors at the component graph stage.

> **Mark distribution:** Pipeline (3M) + `ncc` role (1M) + 4 errors (3M) = **7 M**.

#### ✅ Model Solution to Q.B(b)

**Definition (2 M):**
A **split-phase operation** is a programming pattern in which any operation that may take more than a few microseconds is decomposed into two parts:
- A **command** (e.g., `read()`) that initiates the operation and returns immediately.
- An **event** (e.g., `readDone(result)`) that is signalled by the provider when the operation completes.

**Comparison (3 M):** Reading a temperature sensor with a 250 ms ADC conversion.

| Aspect | Conventional C (Blocking) | TinyOS (Split-Phase) |
|---|---|---|
| Code | `int t = readTemp();` | `call Temp.read();` then handle `Temp.readDone()` |
| CPU during operation | **Blocked, waiting** | **Free to sleep** or process other events |
| Energy cost | Full active power × 250 ms | Sleep-mode power × 250 ms |
| Lines of code | 1 | 2 (more verbose) |
| Concurrency safety | Simple, single-thread of control | Requires care: state may change between command and event |

**Why essential for WSN energy efficiency (2 M):**
A typical ADC conversion takes ~250 ms. A Mica2 mote draws **8 mA** when active and **15 µA** in sleep.
- **Blocking:** $0.250 \text{ s} \times 8 \text{ mA} = 2 \text{ mC}$ per sample.
- **Split-phase:** $0.250 \text{ s} \times 15 \,\mu\text{A} \approx 3.75 \,\mu\text{C}$ per sample — a **~530× reduction in energy**.
- Across a deployment of 10 000 motes sampling every 10 minutes for 1 year, this translates into the difference between **2 weeks of battery life and 2+ years**.

> **Mark distribution:** Definition (2M) + Table comparison (3M) + Energy math (2M) = **7 M**.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfalls that consistently cost KTU students 5–8 marks on this topic:**
>
> 1. **Confusing the `command`/`event` direction.** Many students write *"interface has commands and events on both sides"* — WRONG. `command` is implemented by the **provider**, `event` is implemented by the **user**. Reversing this loses **2 marks**.
> 2. **Calling TinyOS a "real-time OS" with threads.** It is **not** RTOS in the classical sense. It has a 2-level scheduler (events + tasks), **not** preemptive threads. Many examiners deduct **2–3 marks** for this.
> 3. **Drawing wiring arrows backwards.** In the diagram, arrows go from the `uses` site to the `provides` site, e.g., `App.Timer -> Timer0.Timer`. Drawing the opposite loses **1 mark**.
> 4. **Omitting the `async` discipline in concurrency answers.** Even if you mention events and tasks, examiners expect a mention of the `async` keyword. Missing it costs **1–2 marks**.
> 5. **Forgetting that `ncc` is *not* a replacement for the C compiler.** It is a *preprocessor / source translator*. The actual binary comes from `avr-gcc` or `msp430-gcc`. Confusing this loses **1 mark**.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> 📌 **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Print this and keep it!)**

- ✅ **TinyOS** is a component-based, event-driven OS for **WSN motes**, with a footprint of **~400 B** of RAM.
- ✅ **nesC** is the *only* language for TinyOS — a component-oriented C dialect.
- ✅ Three core abstractions: **Interface** (bidirectional contract), **Module** (code), **Configuration** (wiring).
- ✅ Interfaces have **two function kinds**: `command` (provider implements) and `event` (user implements).
- ✅ Wiring uses `->`; the user side points to the provider side. Unwired interfaces are a **compile error**.
- ✅ Two execution contexts only: **`task`** (deferred, FIFO, max 32) and **`event`** (atomic, interrupt-driven).
- ✅ Events **preempt tasks**; events **never preempt** events.
- ✅ **No blocking calls** — every long operation is **split-phase** (command + completion event).
- ✅ The **`async` keyword** enforces race-free access; `ncc` checks it at compile time.
- ✅ Compilation: **`.nc` files → `ncc` → single `app.c` → `avr-gcc`/`msp430-gcc` → HEX image**.
- ✅ The `Blink` example wires: `App.Timer → TimerMilliC`, `App.Leds → LedsC`, `App.Boot → MainC.Boot`.
- ✅ **Duty cycle math:** For a 1 Hz blink, the CPU is active for only **~0.000 075 %** of the time — this is why motes last **years** on 2 AA cells.
- ✅ Common compile errors: unwired interface, async violation, type mismatch, missing `new` instantiation.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Internet to WSN Communication
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT634 - WIRELESS & SENSORS NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Internet to WSN Communication

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Internet to WSN Communication** refers to the set of architectural, protocol-level, and gateway-based mechanisms that allow resource-constrained Wireless Sensor Networks (WSNs) to interoperate with the global, TCP/IP-based Internet. It encompasses proxy/gateway architectures, protocol adaptation layers (e.g., **6LoWPAN**), header compression, address translation, tunneling strategies, and the realization of the *Sensor Web* / *Web of Things (WoT)* paradigm.

In KTU 2024 scheme terminology, the topic spans:

1. **Motivation** – why direct IP connectivity for sensor motes is non-trivial.
2. **Gateway / Proxy Architectures** – sink node acting as translator between IP domain and WSN MAC/link layer.
3. **Application-Layer Approaches** – COAP/HTTP mediation, Sensor Web Enablement (SWE).
4. **Network-Layer Approaches** – IPv6 over Low-Power WPAN (6LoWPAN), RPL routing.
5. **TCP/IP Adaptation for Tiny Devices** – uIP, lwIP, BLIP, Contiki stack.
6. **Web of Things (WoT) and Sensor Web** – RESTful encapsulation of sensor data.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]  
> **Analogy — A Translator at a UN Conference**  
> Imagine a global UN assembly where one side speaks fluent English with full diplomatic vocabulary (the **Internet**, with its rich TCP/IP suite, IPv6, and HTTP), while the other side is a tribe that can only send short smoke signals and drum beats (the **WSN**, with IEEE 802.15.4, 127-byte frames, and sleepy battery-powered motes). Neither side can directly understand the other. The **Gateway / 6LoWPAN Border Router (6LBR)** is the multilingual translator sitting in the middle, who: (i) compresses the diplomatic speech into smoke patterns (header compression), (ii) assigns each tribal member a long unique identity (IPv6 address from EUI-64), and (iii) maintains a directory of which drummer represents which topic. Communication becomes possible only through this mediator.

The **key physical constants and standard metrics** students must memorize:

- **IEEE 802.15.4 MAC frame size: 127 bytes** (max physical payload ≈ 81–102 bytes after header).
- **IPv6 header: 40 bytes**, **UDP header: 8 bytes**, **TCP header: 20 bytes** (without options).
- **6LoWPAN compression ratio**: IPv6 + UDP (48 bytes) compressed to as little as **6 bytes** in best case, **13 bytes** typical.
- **IEEE 802.15.4 data rate: 250 kbps** (2.4 GHz band).
- **Typical mote energy budget: 2 × AA batteries ≈ 2.7–3.3 V, ~2500 mAh** (~9 kJ).

## 1.3 Why This Topic Matters in KTU 2024

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> KTU frequently frames **2-mark and 14-mark questions** around: *"Compare gateway-based vs application-layer mediation"*, *"Explain 6LoWPAN header compression"*, or *"Discuss the challenges of TCP/IP over WSN"*. Mastering this topic directly addresses **CO2: Design and analyze WSN protocols interfacing with IP networks** of the PEMRT634 syllabus.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Layered Motivation: Why the Internet Cannot Talk to a Mote Directly

A sensor mote running TinyOS typically supports:

- 8–16-bit MCU (e.g., MSP430, ATmega) at 1–16 MHz
- 2–10 KB RAM, 48–128 KB Flash
- IEEE 802.15.4 radio (250 kbps, 127-byte MAC frame)
- Possibly no IP stack at all, or only a *tiny* one

Meanwhile, the Internet assumes:

- 32-bit IPv4 or 128-bit IPv6 addressing
- Reliable TCP transport with 3-way handshake and windowing
- MTUs of **1500 bytes** (Ethernet) or **1280 bytes** (IPv6 minimum)
- DNS, ARP, ICMP, OSPF, BGP, etc.

These mismatches make **protocol mediation mandatory**.

## 2.2 Three Architectural Schools of Thought

### A. Application-Layer Mediation (Top-Down)
A **proxy/sink node** terminates the WSN protocol and re-exposes the data using **HTTP/CoAP/XML/JSON** to the Internet. The WSN remains an opaque "technology island".

- *Pros:* No IP needed inside the WSN, easy to deploy, preserves mote energy.
- *Cons:* No end-to-end addressing, breaks transparency.

### B. Network-Layer Mediation (Bottom-Up / True 6LoWPAN)
Every sensor node gets a **full IPv6 address** and runs a compressed IP stack. The gateway only acts as a **6LoWPAN Border Router (6LBR)** performing **header compression/decompression** and **fragmentation/reassembly**.

- *Pros:* End-to-end IP, true Web of Things, plug-and-play with Cloud.
- *Cons:* Higher mote RAM/CPU requirement.

### C. Hybrid (Sensor Web / OGC SWE)
Uses standards from the **Open Geospatial Consortium (OGC)** — Sensor Observation Service (SOS), Sensor Planning Service (SPS), SensorML — to make heterogeneous sensor networks queryable over HTTP.

## 2.3 The 6LoWPAN Adaptation Layer in Detail

> [!NOTE]  
> **6LoWPAN** = **IPv6 over Low-Power Wireless Personal Area Networks** (RFC 4944, 2007; updated by RFC 6282, 2011).  
> It is **NOT** a new network — it is an **adaptation/shim layer** that sits between the IEEE 802.15.4 MAC and the IPv6 network layer, performing:
> 1. **Header compression** (IPHC, NHC).
> 2. **Fragmentation & reassembly** (because 802.15.4 MTU < 1280-byte IPv6 minimum).
> 3. **Mesh addressing** via the **Mesh Delivery** header.
> 4. **Address autoconfiguration** using EUI-64.

### IPv6 Header Compression (IPHC) Encoding

The IPHC dispatch byte (0b011–XXXXX) encodes:

- TF (Traffic Class / Flow Label): 2 bits — *elided, ECN, DSCP, full 4 bytes*.
- NH (Next Header): 1 bit — *compressed or inline*.
- HLIM (Hop Limit): 2 bits — *inline, 1, 64, 255*.
- CID (Context Identifier Extension): 1 bit.
- SAC (Source Address Compression): 1 bit — *stateless* vs *stateful (context-based)*.
- SAM (Source Address Mode): 2 bits — *128-bit full*, *64-bit link-local*, *16-bit inline*, *fully elided*.
- M (Multicast): 1 bit.
- DAC (Destination Address Compression): 1 bit.
- DAM (Destination Address Mode): 2 bits.

This encoding can compress the 40-byte IPv6 header to as little as **2–4 bytes**.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Variables / Notes |
|---|---------|----------------------|-------------------|
| 1 | IPv6 Header Compression Ratio | $R_{c} = \dfrac{L_{\text{orig}}}{L_{\text{comp}}}$ | $L_{\text{orig}} = 40$ B; $L_{\text{comp}} \in [2, 40]$ B |
| 2 | Effective Payload per 802.15.4 Frame | $L_{\text{eff}} = L_{\text{MAX}} - L_{\text{MAC}} - L_{\text{6LP}} - L_{\text{compIPv6}} - L_{\text{UDP/TCP}}$ | $L_{\text{MAX}} = 127$ B; $L_{\text{MAC}} = 25\text{–}34$ B |
| 3 | Fragmentation Threshold (RFC 4944) | $L_{\text{frag}} \le \dfrac{L_{\text{MTU}} - L_{\text{6LP}} - L_{\text{datagram\_tag}} - L_{\text{offset}}}{1}$ | Each fragment ≤ 802.15.4 payload; 1st frag carries 4-byte mesh header; mesh is 5 bytes |
| 4 | Number of Fragments for IPv6 packet | $N_{f} = \left\lceil \dfrac{L_{\text{IPv6 pkt}}}{L_{\text{max\_frag}}} \right\rceil$ | Round up |
| 5 | EUI-64 Address Construction | $A_{\text{EUI-64}} = \text{OUI}_{24} \mid \text{FFFE} \mid \text{NIC}_{24}$ | Inserted as 64-bit IID of IPv6 |
| 6 | Energy per Bit Transmission (free-space) | $E_{tx}(d) = E_{\text{elec}} \cdot k + \epsilon_{fs} \cdot k \cdot d^{2}$ | $E_{\text{elec}} = 50$ nJ/bit, $\epsilon_{fs} = 10$ pJ/bit/m² |
| 7 | Energy per Bit Reception | $E_{rx}(k) = E_{\text{elec}} \cdot k$ | Reception cost only |
| 8 | Total Header Overhead Reduction | $\Delta L = (L_{\text{IPv6}} + L_{\text{UDP}}) - L_{\text{6LP\_comp}} = 48 - 6 = 42$ B | Best case (link-local both ends, inline UDP compressed to 4 B) |
| 9 | Header Compression Efficiency | $\eta_{c} = \dfrac{L_{\text{orig}} - L_{\text{comp}}}{L_{\text{orig}}} \times 100\%$ | Expressed in % |
| 10 | Mote Lifetime (battery model) | $T_{\text{life}} = \dfrac{C_{\text{bat}}}{I_{\text{avg}}} \cdot 3.6 \times 10^{6}$ s | $C_{\text{bat}}$ in mAh, $I_{\text{avg}}$ in mA |

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> The vertical bar / pipe symbol `$\vert$` is used above in formulas to mean "such that" or absolute modulus. In markdown tables, **never** use the raw `$\vert$` symbol; this sheet uses $\mid$ to keep the table intact.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Smart agriculture** (e.g., Libelium Waspmote farms in Spain): 6LoWPAN nodes report soil moisture directly to a cloud MQTT broker.
- **Industrial IoT (IIoT)**: WirelessHART gateways translating between 802.15.4-based field devices and Ethernet-based SCADA.
- **Smart health**: Body-area networks sending patient vitals over IPv6 to hospital EHR systems.
- **Disaster management**: Deployable sensor webs feeding real-time maps during earthquakes.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Maximum Effective Application Payload in a Single 802.15.4 Frame

We derive the **maximum bytes available to the application layer** in a single-hop, non-fragmented 6LoWPAN transmission.

**Step 1 — Identify total MAC frame size.**  
The IEEE 802.15.4 specification caps the MAC Protocol Data Unit (MPDU) at:

$$L_{\text{MPDU}} = 127 \text{ bytes}$$

**Step 2 — Subtract the MAC headers.**  
For a data frame, the MAC header is 21 bytes and the 2-byte FCS trailer is included within the 127-byte budget. So the *MAC payload* (also called MAC Service Data Unit, MSDU) is:

$$L_{\text{MSDU}} = 127 - 21 - 2 = 104 \text{ bytes}$$

**Step 3 — Subtract the 6LoWPAN header.**  
For a single-hop, non-fragmented, non-meshed packet, the 6LoWPAN shim header is the IPHC-compressed IPv6 header plus the compressed UDP Next-Header (NHC). In the best case (link-local addresses, hop limit = 64, UDP):

$$L_{\text{6LP}} = 2 \, \text{(IPHC dispatch + encoding)} + 1 \, \text{(NHC UDP)} + 4 \, \text{(UDP src+dst inline)} = 7 \text{ bytes}$$

But for true minimum (both addresses fully elided using context, 2-byte NHC UDP):

$$L_{\text{6LP}}^{\min} = 2 + 1 = 3 \text{ bytes}$$

**Step 4 — Subtract the compressed IPv6 header (already in $L_{\text{6LP}}$).**  
Since IPHC is *part* of the 6LoWPAN header, no separate subtraction is needed.

**Step 5 — Compute effective application payload.**

$$L_{\text{app}} = L_{\text{MSDU}} - L_{\text{6LP}} = 104 - 7 = 97 \text{ bytes} \quad \text{(typical)}$$

$$L_{\text{app}}^{\max} = 104 - 3 = 101 \text{ bytes} \quad \text{(best case)}$$

This is the **famous "TinyOS" budget** that motivated COAP's 12-byte header design (well below this 97-byte ceiling).

## 3.2 Derivation: Number of Fragments for an IPv6 Packet of 1280 Bytes

IPv6 requires every link to support a 1280-byte MTU. Since 802.15.4 supports only ~81 bytes of fragment data, the 6LoWPAN layer must fragment.

**Step 1 — Compute maximum payload per fragment (1st fragment).**  
First fragment carries a 4-byte 6LoWPAN fragmentation header (1-byte dispatch + 2-byte datagram tag + 1-byte datagram offset = 4 bytes, or 5 bytes with mesh).

$$L_{\text{frag,1}} = 104 - 4 = 100 \text{ bytes} \quad \text{(no mesh header)}$$

**Step 2 — Compute maximum payload per subsequent fragment.**  
Subsequent fragments carry a 5-byte header (1-byte dispatch + 2-byte datagram tag + 1-byte datagram offset + 1-byte size = 5 bytes).

$$L_{\text{frag,N}} = 104 - 5 = 99 \text{ bytes} \quad \text{(subsequent)}$$

Wait — correction: in RFC 4944, the fragmentation header is **the same 4 bytes** for the first and subsequent fragments (datagram tag + offset + dispatch), with the 1st fragment using offset 0. So:

$$L_{\text{frag}} = 104 - 4 = 100 \text{ bytes per fragment}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the formula.**  
For a 1280-byte IPv6 packet minus 40-byte header = 1240 bytes of payload, plus the 6LP header itself:

$$N_{f} = \left\lceil \dfrac{1280}{100} \right\rceil = \lceil 12.8 \rceil = 13 \text{ fragments}$$

**Step 4 — Reality check.**  
Loss of any single fragment forces the entire IPv6 packet to be retransmitted (no per-fragment reliability at 6LP layer), which is why RPL/6LoWPAN research aims to keep packets *small*.

## 3.3 Derivation: Header Compression Efficiency for UDP/IPv6

Consider a sensor sending **temperature reading** every minute using UDP/IPv6 with link-local addresses.

**Step 1 — Original header sizes.**

$$L_{\text{orig}} = L_{\text{IPv6}} + L_{\text{UDP}} = 40 + 8 = 48 \text{ bytes}$$

**Step 2 — Compressed sizes (RFC 6282 IPHC best case).**  
With stateless compression (SAC=0), both addresses 16-bit inline (SAM=10, DAM=10), TF=11 (full elision), HLIM=01 (=64 elided), NH=1 (NHC for UDP), NHC=4 bytes (checksum inline, ports elided via 0xF0xx trick):

$$L_{\text{comp}} = 2 + 2 + 2 + 4 = 10 \text{ bytes typical}$$

Best case (both addresses fully elided, NH=1):

$$L_{\text{comp}}^{\min} = 2 + 0 + 0 + 1 = 3 \text{ bytes}$$

**Step 3 — Compression efficiency.**

$$\eta_{c} = \dfrac{48 - 10}{48} \times 100\% = 79.2\% \quad \text{(typical)}$$

$$\eta_{c}^{\max} = \dfrac{48 - 3}{48} \times 100\% = 93.75\% \quad \text{(best case)}$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation.**  
The compressed header **shrinks by ~80%**, dramatically increasing effective throughput and reducing radio-on time — directly extending battery life.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU High-Yield)

| Parameter | App-Layer Proxy | Network-Layer (6LoWPAN) | Sensor Web (OGC SWE) |
|-----------|-----------------|--------------------------|------------------------|
| End-to-end IP | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | ❌ No (HTTP/SOAP) |
| Mote Energy | 🟢 Lowest | 🟡 Medium | 🔴 High (XML) |
| Transparency | ❌ Low | ✅ Full | 🟡 Medium |
| Standards Body | Custom | IETF (RFC 4944, 6282) | OGC |
| Internetwork w/ IP | Via gateway only | Native | Via gateway |
| Fragmentation | Not required | Mandatory for >101 B | Not required |
| Security | App-specific | IPsec possible | TLS/HTTPS |
| Scalability | Limited by proxy | Excellent (RPL) | Good |
| Use Case | Closed lab networks | IoT / Smart Cities | Earth Sciences / GIS |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

> [!NOTE]

The single yellow node in the middle (Gateway / 6LBR) is the *only* point of protocol translation. Everything to its left is full IP; everything to its right is 802.15.4 + 6LoWPAN.

## 4.2 Protocol Stack Comparison (Internet vs 6LoWPAN vs Raw WSN)

    subgraph A["Internet Side (TCP/IP Suite)"]

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**  
**Define a WSN gateway and list its three primary functions in an Internet-to-WSN communication scenario.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**  
A **WSN Gateway (or Sink Node)** is a node that acts as the bridge between the Wireless Sensor Network (using IEEE 802.15.4 / 6LoWPAN) and the IP-based Internet. Its three primary functions are:  
1. **Protocol translation** between the WSN MAC/link layer and the IP/TCP/UDP/HTTP stack.  
2. **Data aggregation and buffering** of sensor readings before forwarding upstream.  
3. **Network management** — node discovery, address assignment (EUI-64 ↔ IPv6), time synchronization, and security/key distribution. *(1 mark each)*

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**  
**What is 6LoWPAN? State the RFC document that standardizes it.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**  
**6LoWPAN (IPv6 over Low-Power Wireless Personal Area Networks)** is an IETF-standardized adaptation layer that enables IPv6 packets to be carried over IEEE 802.15.4 frames. It performs **header compression, fragmentation/reassembly, and mesh addressing**.  
- Original standard: **RFC 4944 (2007)** — "Transmission of IPv6 Packets over IEEE 802.15.4 Networks".  
- Updated compression format: **RFC 6282 (2011)** — "Compression Format for IPv6 Datagrams over IEEE 802.15.4-Based Networks".  
*(2 marks for definition + 1 mark for RFC number)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Architecture & Gateway Focus*

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2]**  
> *(a)* Explain the **three-tier architecture** for connecting a WSN to the Internet with a neat diagram. Differentiate between **application-layer mediation** and **network-layer mediation**. **(7 marks)**  
> *(b)* Describe in detail the **functions of a 6LoWPAN Border Router (6LBR)**. Illustrate how IPv6 packets are encapsulated, compressed, and forwarded across an 802.15.4 link. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

The **three-tier architecture** divides the system into:

1. **Tier 1 — Sensor Tier (WSN domain):** Resource-constrained motes (mote-class devices) with IEEE 802.15.4 radios. They form a multi-hop mesh and run either a proprietary protocol or a 6LoWPAN-compressed IPv6 stack.
2. **Tier 2 — Gateway / Network Tier:** A **Sink Node / 6LBR** that aggregates sensor data, performs protocol translation, and provides connectivity to the backhaul.
3. **Tier 3 — Application / Internet Tier:** Servers, databases, and end-user applications on the public Internet that consume sensor data via HTTP, MQTT, CoAP, or web services.

**Differences:**

| Aspect | Application-Layer Mediation | Network-Layer Mediation |
|--------|------------------------------|--------------------------|
| Mote IP | No IP stack on mote | Full IPv6 on mote |
| Translation Point | Proxy re-publishes data as HTTP/JSON | 6LBR only compresses headers |
| End-to-End | Broken at gateway | Preserved |
| Energy | Lowest | Medium |
| Example | TinyOS + Web proxy | Contiki + RPL + 6LoWPAN |

*(1 mark diagram + 2 marks tiers + 2 marks differences + 2 marks example)*

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

A **6LBR** performs the following:

1. **Header Compression (IPHC/NHC):** Encodes the 40-byte IPv6 header into 2–4 bytes using stateless or stateful contexts. UDP header is encoded via NHC into 1–4 bytes.
2. **Fragmentation & Reassembly:** Splits packets > 101 B (typical MTU of 802.15.4) into smaller fragments tagged with a **datagram tag** and **offset**.
3. **Mesh Forwarding:** Maintains a **mesh delivery header** (RFC 4944) for hop-by-hop forwarding inside the WSN while keeping the IPv6 destination end-to-end.
4. **Address Mapping:** Generates IPv6 Interface Identifiers (IID) from IEEE 802.15.4 EUI-64 addresses via the formula `IPv6 IID = 64-bit EUI-64 with universal/local bit flipped`.
5. **Routing:** Typically runs **RPL** (Routing Protocol for Low-Power and Lossy Networks, RFC 6550) to build a DODAG (Destination Oriented Directed Acyclic Graph) rooted at the 6LBR.

**Encapsulation flow:**  
Application → UDP → IPv6 (40 B) → **6LoWPAN shim (compresses to ~7 B)** → 802.15.4 MAC (21 B header) → PHY (radio frame). At the receiver, the 6LBR decompresses the shim header, reassembles fragments, and forwards the original IPv6 packet upstream.

*(2 marks for 5 functions + 3 marks encapsulation flow + 2 marks example/values)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Protocol Adaptation Focus*

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 2 — Internal Choice Alt]**  
> *(a)* With reference to **TCP/IP adaptation for sensor networks**, compare the design philosophies of **uIP, lwIP, and BLIP** stacks. Why can't standard TCP be used directly on a mote? **(7 marks)**  
> *(b)* A sensor mote transmits **1280-byte IPv6 packets** over an IEEE 802.15.4 link. The MAC payload is **100 bytes** and the 6LoWPAN fragmentation header is **4 bytes**. Compute (i) the **number of fragments**, (ii) the **total number of radio bytes transmitted** (assuming 104-byte MSDU per frame and a 2-byte acknowledgment per fragment), and (iii) the **header-compression efficiency** if the original IPv6+UDP header is **48 bytes** and the compressed header is **10 bytes**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Why standard TCP cannot run on a mote:**  
- Standard TCP uses a **3-way handshake** (SYN, SYN-ACK, ACK) and **congestion window** with 16-bit or 32-bit sequence numbers — too much RAM for an 8-bit MCU.  
- TCP assumes **MTU ≥ 576 bytes** (RFC 879); 802.15.4 has only 81–102 bytes of payload, breaking the MSS assumption.  
- TCP's **retransmission and ACKs** consume radio energy on a battery-limited node.  
- The **delayed-ACK and Nagle algorithms** are tuned for wired networks, not lossy RF.  
*(2 marks)*

**uIP (micro-IP, Adam Dunkels):**  
- **Code size < 5 KB**, RAM < 1 KB.  
- **Single-packet buffering** — the entire TCP state is derived from the packet itself (no sliding window).  
- Uses a **single global TCP state machine** and **contiki/uIP event-driven model**.  
- No fragmentation support at IP layer; relies on link-layer MTU. *(1.5 marks)*

**lwIP (lightweight IP, Swedish Institute of Computer Science):**  
- Modular — TCP, UDP, IP, ICMP, DHCP, PPP as separate modules.  
- **Sliding-window TCP** with scaled windows, so slightly heavier than uIP (~10–20 KB code).  
- Suitable for slightly richer motes (e.g., ARM Cortex-M). *(1.5 marks)*

**BLIP (Berkeley Low-power IP, TinyOS):**  
- Native **6LoWPAN** support, designed for TinyOS 2.x.  
- Implements **header compression, fragmentation, and RPL-style routing**.  
- Tightly coupled with the **TinyOS Active Message** layer.  
- Suited for **end-to-end IPv6** motes. *(1.5 marks)*

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Given:**
- $L_{\text{IPv6 pkt}} = 1280$ bytes
- $L_{\text{MSDU}} = 100$ bytes
- $L_{\text{frag\_hdr}} = 4$ bytes
- Therefore $L_{\text{frag\_payload}} = 100 - 4 = 96$ bytes
- $L_{\text{orig\_hdr}} = 48$ bytes, $L_{\text{comp\_hdr}} = 10$ bytes

**(i) Number of fragments:**  
The 1280-byte IPv6 packet must be split. The first fragment carries the 6LoWPAN header (4 bytes) and 96 bytes of payload; subsequent fragments also carry 4-byte header and 96 bytes of payload.

$$N_f = \left\lceil \dfrac{1280}{96} \right\rceil = \lceil 13.33 \rceil = 14 \text{ fragments}$$

*(2 marks)*

**(ii) Total radio bytes transmitted (including 2-byte ACK per fragment):**  
Each frame is 104 bytes (MSDU + 2-byte MAC padding/FCS as per problem statement). Ack is 2 bytes per fragment.

$$\text{Total data} = 14 \times 104 = 1456 \text{ bytes}$$

$$\text{Total ACK} = 14 \times 2 = 28 \text{ bytes}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Grand total} = 1456 + 28 = 1484 \text{ bytes}}$$

*(3 marks)*

**(iii) Header-compression efficiency:**

$$\eta_c = \dfrac{L_{\text{orig}} - L_{\text{comp}}}{L_{\text{orig}}} \times 100\% = \dfrac{48 - 10}{48} \times 100\%$$

$$\boxed{\eta_c = 79.17\%}$$

*(2 marks)*

---

> [!WARNING]  
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing "6LoWPAN" with a "network".** It is an *adaptation layer*, NOT a separate network or routing protocol. (–1 mark)
> 2. **Writing `127 B` as the IP payload** instead of the MAC frame size. 802.15.4 MAC frame = 127 B, but **MSDU** (what 6LP can use) = ~81–104 B. (–1 mark)
> 3. **Forgetting the 4-byte 6LP fragmentation header** in numerical problems — leads to off-by-one fragment counts. (–1 to 2 marks)
> 4. **Drawing the gateway as a "router"** in diagrams without labeling it as a *protocol translator*. Examiners expect explicit function names. (–1 mark)
> 5. **Mixing up RFC 4944 and RFC 6282.** The former defines the original 6LoWPAN; the latter defines the *improved* IPHC compression. (–1 mark)
> 6. **In Part A (3-markers), writing generic prose** without using KTU terminology (e.g., "translator" instead of "6LBR"). Always use the **standard term**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Read before every KTU exam):**

- ✅ **6LoWPAN** is an **adaptation layer** (NOT a routing protocol), defined in **RFC 4944** (original) and **RFC 6282** (IPHC compression).
- ✅ **802.15.4 MAC frame size = 127 bytes**; **MSDU ≈ 81–104 bytes**; **IPv6 minimum MTU = 1280 bytes** → fragmentation is mandatory.
- ✅ **Best-case header compression**: 40-byte IPv6 → **2 B**; 8-byte UDP → **1 B** (NHC inline); total compressed overhead ≈ **3–7 B** typical.
- ✅ **6LBR functions**: header compression, fragmentation/reassembly, mesh forwarding, address autoconfiguration (EUI-64 ↔ IPv6), running **RPL** for routing.
- ✅ **Three architectural schools**: (i) Application-layer proxy, (ii) Network-layer (6LoWPAN), (iii) Sensor Web (OGC SWE).
- ✅ **Lightweight TCP/IP stacks**: uIP (< 5 KB code, single-packet buffering), lwIP (modular, sliding window), BLIP (TinyOS native 6LoWPAN).
- ✅ **Standard TCP fails on motes** because of large state, MSS assumption, and energy cost.
- ✅ **EUI-64 to IPv6 IID** is formed by inserting **FF:FE** in the middle of the 48-bit MAC and flipping the U/L bit.
- ✅ **Mote numbers in your pocket**: 2.4 GHz band, 250 kbps, 16 channels, 127-byte frame, 1% duty cycle for energy.
- ✅ **Common exam trap**: 6LoWPAN does *not* provide reliability — that is the job of upper layers (RPL, CoAP, or app-layer ACK).
- ✅ **Real-world deployments**: Libelium (Waspmote), Contiki OS, RIOT OS, Zolertia Z1 — all support 6LoWPAN out-of-the-box.
- ✅ **Forwarding equation to memorize**: $L_{\text{app}} = L_{\text{MSDU}} - L_{\text{6LP}} - L_{\text{UDP}} = 104 - 7 - 0 = 97$ B.
- ✅ **Fragmentation count formula**: $N_f = \lceil L_{\text{IPv6 pkt}} / L_{\text{frag\_payload}} \rceil$.

> [!NOTE]  
> **Final Tip:** Whenever a KTU question says "explain how the Internet communicates with a WSN", **always start with the three-tier architecture diagram**, then **name the 6LBR explicitly**, then **cite RFC 4944 / 6282** for the 6LoWPAN details. This single structure covers 80% of all KTU 14-mark answers on this topic.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: MAC Protocols for Wireless Sensor Networks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT634 - WIRELESS & SENSORS NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** MAC Protocols for Wireless Sensor Networks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Medium Access Control (MAC) Protocols for Wireless Sensor Networks (WSNs)** are a specialized class of link-layer protocols that govern how multiple battery-powered sensor nodes share a common wireless communication medium, while simultaneously addressing the unique constraints of WSNs — namely **energy efficiency**, **scalability**, **self-configuration**, and **application-specific Quality of Service (QoS)**.

Unlike traditional wireless MAC protocols (e.g., IEEE 802.11) optimized primarily for throughput and latency, WSN MAC protocols are fundamentally **energy-centric**, because sensor nodes are typically deployed in remote, hostile, or inaccessible environments where battery replacement is impractical or impossible.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Conference Hall Conversation" Rule

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Imagine a large conference hall where 500 people (sensor nodes) all want to talk, but only **one microphone** (the shared wireless channel) is available. If everyone speaks at once, no one is heard (collision → energy wasted retransmission). Someone must coordinate who speaks, when, and for how long.
> - A **schedule-based MAC** is like the moderator handing out a fixed speaking slot to each person in advance.
> - A **contention-based MAC** is like everyone politely waiting for a pause before speaking (carrier sensing + backoff).
> - A **hybrid MAC** combines both — fixed speaking slots for organized groups, with open mic for emergencies.

## 1.3 Why Traditional MAC Protocols Fail in WSNs

| Issue | Traditional Wireless (e.g., 802.11) | WSN Reality |
|---|---|---|
| Energy source | Mains-powered / rechargeable | **Battery (non-rechargeable)** |
| Primary goal | Throughput, latency | **Energy conservation** |
| Node mobility | Mobile users | Mostly **stationary** |
| Traffic pattern | Continuous, bursty | **Event-driven, low-rate** |
| Node density | Tens | **Hundreds to thousands** |
| QoS needs | Generic best-effort | **Application-specific** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The four major sources of energy waste that every WSN MAC protocol must address are: **(1) Collisions, (2) Overhearing, (3) Control-packet overhead, (4) Idle listening** — a concept introduced by Ye, Heidemann & Estrin (S-MAC paper).

## 1.4 Design Goals of a WSN MAC Protocol

1. **Energy Efficiency** — the paramount criterion.
2. **Scalability** — must handle 100s–1000s of nodes.
3. **Collision Avoidance** — minimize retransmissions.
4. **Self-Organization** — no central coordinator assumed.
5. **Latency / Throughput Trade-off** — tunable per application.
6. **Fairness / Robustness** — tolerates node failures.

## 1.5 Major Classification of WSN MAC Protocols

```
                    MAC Protocols for WSN
                           |
   -------------------------------------------------
   |                       |                       |
 Contention-Based     Schedule-Based           Hybrid
 (CSMA-based)         (TDMA/FDMA-based)        (Combined)
   |                       |                       |
   |-- S-MAC               |-- LEACH-like          |-- Z-MAC
   |-- T-MAC               |-- TRAMA               |-- IEEE 802.15.4
   |-- B-MAC               |-- BMA                 
   |-- WiseMAC             |-- DMAC
   |-- SIFT
   |-- DSMAC
```

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Energy Wasters — Explained

1. **Collisions** — Two nodes transmit simultaneously; packets are corrupted and must be retransmitted. Wastes transmit energy.
2. **Overhearing** — A node receives packets destined for other nodes. Wastes receive energy (often as costly as transmit).
3. **Control Packet Overhead** — RTS/CTS/ACK packets consume bandwidth and energy without carrying application data.
4. **Idle Listening** — A node keeps its radio ON waiting for traffic. *Empirically the largest energy drain* — up to **50–100% of energy budget**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight (Ye et al., 2002):** Idle listening consumes nearly as much energy as receiving. Hence WSN MAC protocols aggressively duty-cycle the radio (turn it ON only periodically).

## 2.2 Contention-Based MAC Protocols (Detailed)

### 2.2.1 S-MAC (Sensor-MAC) — Ye, Heidemann, Estrin (2002)

**Design Philosophy:** Periodic Listen/Sleep cycle with synchronized neighbor discovery.

**Operational Steps:**
- Each node follows a **listen/sleep schedule** (e.g., listen for 10 ms, sleep for 990 ms out of every 1 s).
- Neighbors exchange **SYNC packets** during listen periods to maintain synchronized schedules.
- **CSMA/CA** is used within the listen window.
- **RTS/CTS/ACK** exchange prevents hidden-terminal collisions.
- **Virtual clusters** form — nodes with the same schedule.
- Nodes **aggregate schedules** of all neighbors to avoid sleeping when a neighbor is transmitting.
- **Adaptive listening** — a node that overhears RTS/CTS wakes up briefly at the end of the transmission.

**Drawback:** Fixed duty cycle → latency vs. energy trade-off is rigid.

### 2.2.2 T-MAC (Timeout-MAC) — van Dam & Langendoen (2003)

**Improvement over S-MAC:** Variable duty cycle — node stays awake only as long as it is hearing activity.

- After the last frame was heard, the node waits for a short **TA (Time Active)** interval.
- If no activation event occurs within TA, the node goes to sleep immediately.
- This reduces idle listening further than S-MAC under variable load.

### 2.2.3 B-MAC (Berkeley-MAC) — Polastre, Hill, Culler (2004)

**Design Philosophy:** Lightweight, configurable, no synchronization.

- Uses **Clear Channel Assessment (CCA)** + **Random Backoff** only.
- Employs **LPL (Low Power Listening)** — preambles are sent longer than the receiver's check interval.
- **No SYNC, no RTS/CTS** → extremely low control overhead.
- Highly energy-efficient under low traffic; suffers from long preamble overhead under high traffic.

### 2.2.4 WiseMAC — El-Hoiydi & Decotignie (2004)

- **Non-persistent CSMA** with **preamble sampling**.
- Each node learns the sampling schedule of its one-hop neighbors.
- Preamble length is **minimized** to the offset between the sender's and receiver's sampling times.
- Energy efficiency comparable to S-MAC with much less synchronization overhead.

### 2.2.5 SIFT MAC — Jamieson, Patel, Kompella, Woo (2006)

- For **event-driven WSNs** where many nodes detect the same event simultaneously.
- Uses a **non-uniform random contention window** — early slots have smaller probabilities, favoring the *first* successful reporter.
- Optimized for minimizing the time to detect the **first** event.

## 2.3 Schedule-Based MAC Protocols

### 2.3.1 TRAMA (Traffic-Adaptive Medium Access) — Rajendran, Obraczka, Garcia-Luna-Aceves (2006)

- Uses a **distributed election algorithm** based on a hash of node IDs and the current slot number.
- Nodes that have **no traffic in the current slot** go to sleep.
- Slots are **re-assigned every 2-hop neighborhood** to avoid conflicts.
- **Energy Efficient** but requires precise time synchronization.

### 2.3.2 DMAC — Lu, Krishnamachari, Raghavendra (2004)

- Designed for **convergecast** (data-gathering tree) traffic.
- Slots are arranged so a node's receive slot coincides with its child node's transmit slot — a **staggered wakeup** along the tree.
- Greatly reduces **sleep latency** compared to S-MAC.

## 2.4 Hybrid MAC — IEEE 802.15.4 (Zigbee Physical/MAC Layer)

- Uses **Slotted CSMA/CA** within **GTS (Guaranteed Time Slots)** for time-critical data.
- Beacons enable synchronization; **CAP (Contention Access Period)** + **CFP (Contention Free Period)**.
- **Superframe structure:** Beacon → CAP → CFP → Inactive period.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Term | Description / Meaning | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E_{total} = E_{tx} + E_{rx} + E_{idle} + E_{sleep}$ | Total energy per node = sum of all radio mode energies | Joules (J) |
| 2 | $E_{tx}(k,d) = k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \varepsilon_{amp} \cdot d^{n}$ | Transmit energy for *k* bits over distance *d*, with path-loss exponent *n* (typically 2–4) | J |
| 3 | $E_{rx}(k) = k \cdot E_{elec}$ | Receive energy (independent of distance) | J |
| 4 | $D = \frac{T_{listen}}{T_{listen} + T_{sleep}}$ | **Duty Cycle** — fraction of time the radio is ON | dimensionless (0–1) |
| 5 | $L_{avg} \approx \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{T_{frame}}{N_{hops}}\right)$ | Average per-hop latency in convergecast (approx) | seconds (s) |
| 6 | $S = \frac{L_{data}}{L_{data} + L_{ctrl}}$ | **Goodput / Throughput** — useful data over total | dimensionless |
| 7 | $P_{col} = 1 - (1 - p)^{n-1}$ | Collision probability in slotted Aloha-like, *n* nodes each transmitting with prob. *p* | dimensionless |
| 8 | $N_{thr} \approx \frac{C}{E[packet]}$ | Max sustainable node throughput | bits/s |
| 9 | $T_{preamble} \geq T_{sample}$ | WiseMAC/B-MAC: preamble must cover receiver's sample period | seconds (s) |
| 10 | $E_{saved} = P_{idle} \cdot (1 - D) \cdot T_{frame}$ | Energy saved by duty cycling (vs. always-on) | Joules (J) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Note:** In WSN texts the path-loss exponent is often taken as **n = 2** (free space) or **n = 4** (two-ray ground). Always confirm the model assumed in the question.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Energy Consumption Model (First-Order Radio Model)

The standard WSN energy model (Heinzelman et al.) is:

$$E_{tx}(k, d) = 
\begin{cases}
k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \varepsilon_{fs} \cdot d^{2}, & d \leq d_0 \\
k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \varepsilon_{mp} \cdot d^{4}, & d > d_0
\end{cases}$$

**Step-by-step derivation logic:**

**Step 1 — Model the transmitter's power consumption.** A radio's power amplifier consumes energy to boost the signal to a level that overcomes path loss.
- For **free space** ($d \leq d_0$), the received power scales as $1/d^2$, so the transmit energy scales as $d^2$.
- For **multipath** ($d > d_0$), the received power scales as $1/d^4$, so transmit energy scales as $d^4$.

**Step 2 — Add the digital electronics cost.** Regardless of distance, the radio's circuitry consumes $E_{elec}$ per bit processed (encoding, modulation, filtering).

**Step 3 — Combine both contributions linearly** (energy terms are additive).

**Step 4 — Define the crossover distance:**

$$d_0 = \sqrt{\frac{\varepsilon_{fs}}{\varepsilon_{mp}}}$$

**Step 5 — Receive energy.** A receiver processes the same *k* bits through the electronics path:

$$E_{rx}(k) = k \cdot E_{elec}$$

Receivers do not have a power amplifier, so there is **no distance term** in the receive energy.

> [!NOTE]
> **Typical values (Heinzelman, 2000):** $E_{elec} = 50$ nJ/bit, $\varepsilon_{fs} = 10$ pJ/bit/m², $\varepsilon_{mp} = 0.0013$ pJ/bit/m⁴ → crossover $d_0 \approx 87.7$ m.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Energy Saved by Duty Cycling

Consider a node that without MAC would keep its radio ON (idle listening) continuously.

**Step 1 — Power consumed in always-on idle mode over time T:**

$$E_{alwaysOn} = P_{idle} \cdot T$$

**Step 2 — With duty-cycle MAC, the node is ON for $D \cdot T$ seconds and asleep for $(1-D) \cdot T$ seconds:**

$$E_{DC} = P_{idle} \cdot (D \cdot T) + P_{sleep} \cdot (1 - D) \cdot T$$

**Step 3 — Energy saved:**

$$E_{saved} = E_{alwaysOn} - E_{DC} = P_{idle} \cdot (1 - D) \cdot T - P_{sleep} \cdot (1 - D) \cdot T$$

$$\boxed{E_{saved} = (1 - D) \cdot T \cdot (P_{idle} - P_{sleep})}$$

**Step 4 — Percentage energy saved** (when $P_{sleep} \ll P_{idle}$):

$$\text{\% saved} \approx (1 - D) \cdot 100\%$$

**Numerical Example:** If $D = 0.01$ (1% duty cycle), the radio is ON only 1% of the time → roughly **99% idle energy is saved** — illustrating the massive benefit of MAC duty cycling.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — S-MAC Per-Frame Energy Cost

Assume S-MAC frame period $T_{frame} = 1$ s with $T_{listen} = 10$ ms, $T_{sleep} = 990$ ms.

**Step 1 — Compute duty cycle:**

$$D = \frac{T_{listen}}{T_{frame}} = \frac{0.010}{1.0} = 0.01 \;\; (1\%)$$

**Step 2 — Compute idle-listening energy per frame:**

$$E_{idle, frame} = P_{idle} \cdot T_{listen} = P_{idle} \cdot D \cdot T_{frame}$$

**Step 3 — Compute sleep energy per frame:**

$$E_{sleep, frame} = P_{sleep} \cdot T_{sleep} = P_{sleep} \cdot (1 - D) \cdot T_{frame}$$

**Step 4 — Total per frame:**

$$E_{total, frame} = (P_{idle} - P_{sleep}) \cdot D \cdot T_{frame} + P_{sleep} \cdot T_{frame}$$

For typical Mica2 motes: $P_{idle} \approx 13.5$ mW, $P_{sleep} \approx 15$ µW. Plugging in:

$$E_{total, frame} = (0.0135 - 0.000015)(0.01)(1) + (0.000015)(1)$$

$$E_{total, frame} \approx 1.35 \times 10^{-4} \; \text{J} + 1.5 \times 10^{-5} \; \text{J} \approx 1.5 \times 10^{-4} \; \text{J}$$

Compared to always-on ($P_{idle} \cdot T_{frame} = 0.0135$ J), the **saving is nearly 90×.**

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Collision Probability in Slotted Aloha (used in IEEE 802.15.4 CAP)

For *n* contending nodes, each transmitting in a random slot with probability *p*:

**Step 1 — Probability one specific node succeeds in a slot:** $p \cdot (1-p)^{n-1}$

**Step 2 — Probability any one of *n* nodes succeeds:**

$$P_{success} = n \cdot p \cdot (1 - p)^{n-1}$$

**Step 3 — Maximize** by setting $\frac{dP_{success}}{dp} = 0$ → optimum at $p = 1/n$.

**Step 4 — Maximum throughput (slotted Aloha):**

$$\boxed{S_{max} = \frac{1}{e} \approx 0.368 \;\; (36.8\%)}$$

This is the classical result — slotted Aloha achieves at most ~37% channel utilization. This is a frequent KTU short-answer question.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparing WSN MAC Protocols

| Protocol | Type | Sync Required | Control Overhead | Energy Efficiency | Latency | Best For |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **S-MAC** | Contention | Yes (SYNC) | Medium | Good | Moderate | Periodic monitoring |
| **T-MAC** | Contention | Yes | Medium | Better than S-MAC | Moderate | Variable load |
| **B-MAC** | Contention | No (LPL) | Very Low | Good (low traffic) | High | Low-traffic apps |
| **WiseMAC** | Contention | Implicit | Very Low | Excellent | Low (after learning) | Infrastructure monitoring |
| **SIFT** | Contention | No | Low | Good for events | Very low first-report | Event detection |
| **TRAMA** | Schedule | Yes | Low | Excellent | Moderate | Variable traffic |
| **DMAC** | Schedule | Yes | Low | Excellent | Very low (tree) | Convergecast |
| **802.15.4** | Hybrid | Yes (Beacons) | Medium | Good | Tunable | Industrial / Zigbee |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
