### Step 4: Convert SD to absolute time

$$
SD_{\text{time}} = (960 \times 2^{SO}) \times 16 \, \mu s = 15360 \times 2^{SO} \; \mu s
$$

For $SO = 2$:

$$
SD_{\text{time}} = 15360 \times 2^{2} = 15360 \times 4 = 61440 \, \mu s = 61.44 \; \text{ms}
$$

### Step 5: Compute the Inactive Period

$$
\text{Inactive} = BI - SD = 15360 \times (2^{BO} - 2^{SO}) \; \mu s
$$

### Step 6: Compute the duty cycle of the active period

$$
\text{Duty Cycle (\%)} = \frac{SD}{BI} \times 100 = \frac{2^{SO}}{2^{BO}} \times 100 = 2^{SO - BO} \times 100 \; \%
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Insight:** Since $SO \leq BO$, the duty cycle $\leq 100\%$. When $SO = BO$, the network is **fully active** (no sleep). When $SO \ll BO$, the network conserves substantial energy.

### Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style)

**Problem:** A ZigBee PAN uses $BO = 8$ and $SO = 2$. Calculate:
1. Beacon Interval (in ms)
2. Superframe Duration (in ms)
3. Inactive period (in ms)
4. Active duty cycle
5. Number of CAP slots

**Solution:**

**(1) Beacon Interval**

$$
BI = 15360 \times 2^{8} = 15360 \times 256 = 3932160 \, \mu s = 3932.16 \; \text{ms} \approx 3.93 \; \text{s}
$$

**(2) Superframe Duration**

$$
SD = 15360 \times 2^{2} = 15360 \times 4 = 61440 \, \mu s = 61.44 \; \text{ms}
$$

**(3) Inactive Period**

$$
\text{Inactive} = 3932.16 - 61.44 = 3870.72 \; \text{ms}
$$

**(4) Active Duty Cycle**

$$
\text{Duty Cycle} = \frac{61.44}{3932.16} \times 100 \approx 1.5625\%
$$

**(5) CAP slots**

The active period is divided into 16 slots. Of these, 1 is for the beacon, and the rest (up to 7) can be allocated to GTS. So the minimum CAP uses slots 1–15:

$$
\text{CAP slots available} = 16 - 1 = 15 \; \text{slots (max)}
$$

But the GTS can occupy at most 7 of those. So CAP is **at least 8 slots** (if all 7 GTS used) and **at most 15 slots** (if no GTS).

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: ZigBee vs Other WPAN/WLAN Technologies

| Feature | **ZigBee (802.15.4)** | **Bluetooth (802.15.1)** | **Wi-Fi (802.11)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Frequency** | 2.4 GHz | 2.4 GHz | 2.4 / 5 GHz |
| **Max Data Rate** | **250 kbps** | 1–3 Mbps (BT 2.0), 24 Mbps (BT 3.0+), 2 Mbps (BLE) | 11 Mbps – Gbps |
| **Range** | 10–100 m | 10 m | 50–100 m |
| **Battery Life** | Months – Years | Days – Weeks | Hours |
| **Nodes per network** | **65,000+** | 7 (piconet) | ~32 APs |
| **Topology** | Star / Mesh / Tree | Star (piconet) | Star (BSS) |
| **Power consumption** | **Very Low (~30 mA TX)** | Low | High |
| **Cost** | Very Low | Low | High |
| **Application** | WSN, IoT, HA | Cable replacement, audio | Internet, video |
| **Stack size** | ~28–60 KB | ~100+ KB | >1 MB |
| **Standard body** | IEEE + CSA | IEEE / Bluetooth SIG | IEEE / Wi-Fi Alliance |

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: ZigBee Device Types

| Device Type | Roles | Can Route? | Always On? | Number in PAN |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **ZigBee Coordinator (ZC)** | Forms the PAN, assigns short addresses, holds security keys | Yes | Yes (mains-powered) | **Exactly 1** |
| **ZigBee Router (ZR)** | Forwards packets, extends coverage, allows devices to join | Yes | Typically Yes (FFD) | Multiple |
| **ZigBee End Device (ZED)** | Senses / actuates, communicates only with parent | **No** | **No — sleeps most of the time** | Many (battery nodes) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **FFDs vs RFDs:** In IEEE 802.15.4 terms, ZigBee Coordinator and Routers are **Full-Function Devices (FFDs)**. End Devices are **Reduced-Function Devices (RFDs)**. RFDs cannot route and have simpler MAC implementations — saving cost and power.

## 3.4 ZigBee Protocol Stack — Detailed Architecture

```
+--------------------------------------------+
|   Application Layer (APL)                  |
|  - Application Object                      |
|  - Application Support Sublayer (APS)      |
|  - ZigBee Device Object (ZDO)              |
+--------------------------------------------+
|   Network Layer (NWK)                      |
|  - Mesh routing, network management        |
+--------------------------------------------+
|   MAC Sublayer (IEEE 802.15.4)             |
+--------------------------------------------+
|   PHY Layer (IEEE 802.15.4)                |
+--------------------------------------------+
```

### 3.4.1 Application Support Sublayer (APS)

- **Bind tables** for endpoint-to-endpoint binding
- **Reliable delivery** via acknowledgments at the application layer
- **Fragmentation** for messages > 127 bytes
- **Address mapping** (64-bit IEEE → 16-bit NWK)

### 3.4.2 ZigBee Device Object (ZDO)

- **Device discovery** (which devices are in the PAN?)
- **Service discovery** (which services are exposed?)
- **Network management** (join/leave/rejoin)
- **Security management** (key distribution, authentication)

### 3.4.3 Network Layer Functions

- **Mesh routing** using **AODVjr** (a simplified Ad-hoc On-Demand Distance Vector)
- **Tree routing** (hierarchical fallback)
- **Address assignment** (16-bit NWK addresses)
- **PAN ID management** and **channel selection**
- **Stack profiles** (ZigBee, ZigBee PRO)

## 3.5 ZigBee Addressing — 16-bit Short Address Allocation

ZigBee uses **distributed address assignment** to assign 16-bit NWK addresses:

$$
A_{\text{child}} = A_{\text{parent}} + \text{Cskip}(d) \cdot (n-1) + 1, \quad n = 1, 2, \ldots, R_{\max}
$$

where:

$$
\text{Cskip}(d) = \begin{cases} 1 + C_{\max} \cdot (L_{\max} - d - 1) & \text{if } R_{\max} = 1 \\[4pt] \dfrac{1 + C_{\max} - R_{\max} - C_{\max} \cdot R_{\max}^{L_{\max} - d - 1}}{1 - R_{\max}} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

with:
- $C_{\max}$ = max number of children per parent
- $R_{\max}$ = max number of routers among children
- $L_{\max}$ = max depth of the tree
- $d$ = current depth of the parent
- $n$ = ordinal of the child (1 for 1st, etc.)

> [!TIP]
> This mathematical formula is the **foundation of tree routing in ZigBee**. KTU questions often ask for an explanation of how addresses are distributed in a cluster-tree — a sketch of $\text{Cskip}$ is sufficient for full marks.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 IEEE 802.15.4 / ZigBee Protocol Stack — Layered View

```mermaid
graph TD
    app1[Application Profile Home Automation]
    app2[Application Profile Health Care]
    zdo[ZigBee Device Object ZDO]
    aps[Application Support Sublayer APS]
    nwk[Network Layer NWK - Mesh Routing]
    mac[MAC Sublayer IEEE 802 15 4]
    phy[PHY Layer IEEE 802 15 4]
    rf[2.4 GHz Radio Transceiver]

    app1 --> aps
    app2 --> aps
    zdo --> aps
    aps --> nwk
    nwk --> mac
    mac --> phy
    phy --> rf
```

## 4.2 Superframe Structure — Beacon Mode

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph BI[Beacon Interval BI]
        B[Beacon Frame]
        subgraph CAP[Contention Access Period]
            S1[Slot 1]
            S2[Slot 2]
            S3[Slot 3]
            S4[Slot 4]
            S5[Slot 5]
            S6[Slot 6]
            S7[Slot 7]
            S8[Slot 8]
        end
        subgraph CFP[Contention Free Period GTS]
            G1[GTS 1]
            G2[GTS 2]
        end
        INACT[Inactive Period - Sleep]
    end

    B --> S1
    S1 --> S2
    S2 --> S3
    S3 --> S4
    S4 --> S5
    S5 --> S6
    S6 --> S7
    S7 --> S8
    S8 --> G1
    G1 --> G2
    G2 --> INACT
    INACT -. next beacon .-> B
```

## 4.3 ZigBee Network Topologies

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph STAR[Star Topology]
        COORDS[ZigBee Coordinator ZC]
        E1[End Device 1]
        E2[End Device 2]
        E3[End Device 3]
        E4[End Device 4]
        COORDS --- E1
        COORDS --- E2
        COORDS --- E3
        COORDS --- E4
    end
```

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph MESH[Mesh Topology]
        ZC1[Coordinator ZC]
        R1[Router ZR 1]
        R2[Router ZR 2]
        R3[Router ZR 3]
        ED1[End Device]
        ED2[End Device]
        ED3[End Device]
        ED4[End Device]
        ZC1 <--> R1
        ZC1 <--> R2
        R1 <--> R3
        R2 <--> R3
        R1 --- ED1
        R1 --- ED2
        R3 --- ED3
        R2 --- ED4
    end
```

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph TREE[Cluster Tree Topology]
        CT[Coordinator]
        RT1[Router Branch 1]
        RT2[Router Branch 2]
        LE1[End Device 1.1]
        LE2[End Device 1.2]
        LE3[End Device 2.1]
        LE4[End Device 2.2]
        CT --> RT1
        CT --> RT2
        RT1 --> LE1
        RT1 --> LE2
        RT2 --> LE3
        RT2 --> LE4
    end
```

## 4.4 CSMA/CA Backoff State Machine — 802.15.4

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[New Frame Arrives] --> B[Initialize NB 0, BE macMinBE]
    B --> C[Delay Random Backoff 0 to 2 BE - 1 slots]
    C --> D[CCA Clear Channel Assessment]
    D -->|Channel Idle| E[Transmit Frame]
    D -->|Channel Busy| F[Increment NB and BE]
    F --> G{NB less than macMaxCSMABackoffs}
    G -->|Yes| C
    G -->|No| H[Drop Frame - Channel Access Failure]
    E --> I[Wait for ACK]
    I -->|ACK received| J[Success]
    I -->|No ACK| K{Retry less than macMaxFrameRetries}
    K -->|Yes| B
    K -->|No| L[Drop Frame - Transmission Failure]
```

## 4.5 ZigBee PAN Formation & Device Join Process

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Coordinator starts PAN - Channel Scan] --> B[Selects best channel and PAN ID]
    B --> C[Broadcasts Beacons]
    C --> D[Router/ED scans channels]
    D --> E[Associate Request to Coordinator]
    E --> F[Coordinator allocates 16-bit short address]
    F --> G[Association Response - ACK]
    G --> H[Device is now part of PAN]

    I[New End Device wants to join] --> J[Search for parents - orphan scan]
    J --> K[Rejoin request to nearest router]
    K --> L[Router adds device as child]
    L --> H
```

## 4.6 IEEE 802.15.4 MAC Frame — Data Frame Structure

```mermaid
graph LR
    F1[Frame Control - 2 bytes]
    F2[Sequence Number - 1 byte]
    F3[Dest PAN ID - 0 or 2 bytes]
    F4[Destination Address - 0 or 8 bytes]
    F5[Source PAN ID - 0 or 2 bytes]
    F6[Source Address - 0 or 8 bytes]
    F7[Aux Security - 0 or 14 bytes]
    F8[Payload - 0 to 127 bytes]
    F9[FCS Frame Check Seq - 2 bytes]

    F1 --> F2 --> F3 --> F4 --> F5 --> F6 --> F7 --> F8 --> F9
```

> [!NOTE]
> The variable-length addressing fields are controlled by flags in the **Frame Control** field, allowing **short** (16-bit) or **extended** (64-bit) addresses and PAN ID compression to save bytes.

## 4.7 Block-Level Functional Architecture — ZigBee Stack Communication

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph DEV_A[Device A - Sensor Node]
        A_APP[Application - Temperature Sensor]
        A_APS[APS Layer - Endpoint 0x01]
        A_NWK[NWK - Source NWK Address 0x0001]
        A_MAC[MAC - PAN ID 0x1234]
        A_PHY[PHY - 2.4 GHz Channel 15]
    end

    subgraph DEV_B[Device B - Coordinator]
        B_PHY[PHY - 2.4 GHz Channel 15]
        B_MAC[MAC - PAN ID 0x1234]
        B_NWK[NWK - Destination NWK Address 0x0000]
        B_APS[APS Layer - Endpoint 0x00]
        B_APP[Application - Data Sink]
    end

    A_APP -->|Temperature Value| A_APS
    A_APS -->|APSDE-SAP| A_NWK
    A_NWK -->|MCPS-DATA| A_MAC
    A_MAC -->|PPDU Frame| A_PHY

    A_PHY -->|Over-the-air RF 2.4 GHz| B_PHY
    B_PHY -->|PPDU| B_MAC
    B_MAC -->|MPDU| B_NWK
    B_NWK -->|NPDU| B_APS
    B_APS -->|APSDU| B_APP
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (2-Mark Short Answer)

### Question 1: Define IEEE 802.15.4. List its three operating frequency bands.
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]**

**Model Answer:**

**IEEE 802.15.4** is a standard that defines the **Physical (PHY) layer** and **Medium Access Control (MAC) sublayer** for **Low-Rate Wireless Personal Area Networks (LR-WPANs)**, designed for low-cost, low-power, and short-range wireless communication.

The three operating frequency bands are:

1. **868 MHz** — Europe (1 channel, 20/100/250 kbps)
2. **915 MHz** — North America (10 channels, 40/250 kbps)
3. **2.4 GHz** — Global ISM band (16 channels, 250 kbps)

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between FFD and RFD in IEEE 802.15.4.
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model QP]**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | **FFD (Full-Function Device)** | **RFD (Reduced-Function Device)** |
|---|---|---|
| **Implementation** | Full MAC implementation | Reduced/simplified MAC |
| **Roles** | Coordinator, Router, or End Device | Only End Device |
| **Topology support** | Can act in any topology | Can only talk to a single FFD |
| **Routing** | Capable | Not capable |
| **Power** | Usually mains-powered | Battery-powered (saves energy) |
| **Memory/Cost** | Higher | Lower |
| **Examples** | Coordinator, ZigBee Router | Sensor end nodes |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14-Mark — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1)

**[KTU University Exam – Model QP, 14 Marks]**

**Q. (a)** Explain the **IEEE 802.15.4 protocol stack** with a neat diagram. List the responsibilities of the PHY and MAC layers. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A ZigBee PAN operates with **Beacon Order (BO) = 6** and **Superframe Order (SO) = 4**. Calculate the **Beacon Interval**, **Superframe Duration**, **Inactive Period**, and the **Active Duty Cycle**. Also compute the **time duration of one slot**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks

The **IEEE 802.15.4 protocol stack** consists of two primary layers:

**Physical Layer (PHY) Responsibilities:**

1. **Radio activation/deactivation** — turning the transceiver on/off for energy saving.
2. **Channel selection** — selecting one of the available channels (16 in 2.4 GHz band).
3. **Transmit/receive packets** — modulating bits into O-QPSK symbols and demodulating.
4. **Energy Detection (ED)** — measuring signal power on a channel (RSSI).
5. **Link Quality Indication (LQI)** — reporting per-packet link quality to upper layers.
6. **Clear Channel Assessment (CCA)** — checking if channel is idle before transmission.

**MAC Sublayer Responsibilities:**

1. **Beacon management** — generating and processing beacon frames in beacon-enabled mode.
2. **CSMA/CA channel access** — slotted or unslotted contention-based access.
3. **GTS management** — allocation and deallocation of Guaranteed Time Slots.
4. **PAN association/disassociation** — joining and leaving a PAN.
5. **Frame validation** — checking FCS, duplicates, and acknowledgments.
6. **Security** — optional AES-128 encryption (handled via PHY/MAC).

*(Neat diagram as shown in Section 4.1 above carries full credit for the visual component.)*

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:** $BO = 6$, $SO = 4$, `aBaseSuperframeDuration = 960 symbols`, symbol time $T_{sym} = 16 \, \mu s$.

**Step 1: Beacon Interval**

$$
BI = 960 \times 2^{6} = 960 \times 64 = 61440 \; \text{symbols}
$$

$$
BI_{\text{time}} = 61440 \times 16 \, \mu s = 983040 \, \mu s = 983.04 \; \text{ms}
$$

**Step 2: Superframe Duration**

$$
SD = 960 \times 2^{4} = 960 \times 16 = 15360 \; \text{symbols}
$$

$$
SD_{\text{time}} = 15360 \times 16 \, \mu s = 245760 \, \mu s = 245.76 \; \text{ms}
$$

**Step 3: Inactive Period**

$$
\text{Inactive} = BI - SD = 983.04 - 245.76 = 737.28 \; \text{ms}
$$

**Step 4: Active Duty Cycle**

$$
\text{Duty Cycle} = \frac{SD}{BI} \times 100 = \frac{245.76}{983.04} \times 100 = 25\%
$$

**Step 5: One Slot Duration**

The active period is divided into 16 equal slots:

$$
T_{\text{slot}} = \frac{SD_{\text{time}}}{16} = \frac{245.76}{16} = 15.36 \; \text{ms}
$$

(Equivalently, 1 slot = 960 symbols = 15.36 ms.)

---

### Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative)

**[KTU University Exam – Model QP, 14 Marks]**

**Q. (a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **ZigBee protocol stack architecture**. Explain the functions of the **APS sublayer** and **ZigBee Device Object (ZDO)**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare **ZigBee, Bluetooth, and Wi-Fi** based on frequency, data rate, range, power consumption, and typical applications. Why is ZigBee preferred for Wireless Sensor Networks? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks

**ZigBee Protocol Stack Layers (top-down):**

1. **Application Layer (APL)** — contains:
   - **Application Objects** (user-defined applications, e.g., temperature sensing)
   - **APS (Application Support Sublayer)** — endpoints, binding, fragmentation
   - **ZDO (ZigBee Device Object)** — device discovery, security, network management
2. **Network Layer (NWK)** — routing (mesh/tree), address assignment, PAN management
3. **MAC Sublayer** — from IEEE 802.15.4 — CSMA/CA, beacons, GTS
4. **PHY Layer** — from IEEE 802.15.4 — 2.4 GHz O-QPSK modulation

**APS Sublayer Functions:**

- **Binding** — virtual mapping of endpoints for indirect communication.
- **Reliable transport** — APS ACKs for acknowledged delivery.
- **Fragmentation & reassembly** — splitting large messages into 127-byte chunks.
- **Address resolution** — mapping 64-bit IEEE MAC addresses to 16-bit NWK addresses.
- **Authorization** — verifying device permissions for services.

**ZDO Functions:**

- **Device discovery** — finding other devices in the PAN.
- **Service discovery** — finding what services/endpoints are exposed.
- **Network management** — joining, leaving, re-joining the PAN.
- **Security management** — key loading, authentication, encryption setup.
- **Node manager** — defines the device's role (Coordinator, Router, End Device).

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | **ZigBee (802.15.4)** | **Bluetooth (802.15.1)** | **Wi-Fi (802.11)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Frequency | 2.4 GHz | 2.4 GHz | 2.4 / 5 GHz |
| Data Rate | 250 kbps | 1–3 Mbps (Classic), 2 Mbps (BLE) | 11–600+ Mbps |
| Range | 10–100 m | ~10 m | 50–100 m |
| Power | **Very Low** | Low | High |
| Topology | Star / Mesh / Tree | Star (piconet) | Star (BSS) |
| Nodes per network | **65,000+** | 7 | ~32 |
| Typical Use | WSN, IoT, HA | Audio, peripherals | Internet, video |
| Battery Life | Years | Days–Weeks | Hours |

**Why ZigBee is preferred for WSN:**

1. **Ultra-low power consumption** — battery life of months to years.
2. **Mesh networking** — self-healing, large area coverage with many hops.
3. **Huge node capacity** — supports 65,000+ devices per network.
4. **Low cost** — chip costs typically under $2 per node.
5. **Low data rate is sufficient** — sensor data is typically <1 kbps.
6. **Open standard** — interoperable devices from multiple vendors.
7. **Robust against interference** — DSSS and 16 channels in 2.4 GHz provide resilience.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing 802.15.4 with ZigBee** — Many students answer ZigBee questions using only IEEE 802.15.4 details, or vice versa. Always clarify: 802.15.4 = PHY+MAC; ZigBee = NWK+APS+APL+Profiles.
> 2. **Wrong formula for Beacon Interval** — The base is **960 symbols** (not 60 or 16). Mixing up `aBaseSuperframeDuration` with slot duration is a frequent error.
> 3. **Forgetting unit conversion** — Always convert symbols → µs → ms when calculating time durations. Direct symbol values without conversion get partial credit only.
> 4. **Missing the GTS rule** — GTS slots are at the **end** of the active period, not the beginning. CAP is **after** the beacon, GTS is at the **tail**.
> 5. **Pan ID vs NWK address confusion** — **16-bit PAN ID** identifies the network; **16-bit NWK short address** identifies the device within the network. They are NOT the same.
> 6. **Skipping the diagram** — For 7-mark questions, the diagram carries 2–3 marks. Always include a labeled block diagram.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — IEEE 802.15.4 & ZigBee**

- ⭐ **IEEE 802.15.4** defines **PHY + MAC** layers only; **ZigBee** is the upper-layer protocol stack built on it.
- ⭐ Operating bands: **868 MHz / 915 MHz / 2.4 GHz**; 2.4 GHz has **16 channels** (k = 11 to 26), each spaced **5 MHz** apart.
- ⭐ 2.4 GHz data rate = **250 kbps** using **O-QPSK + DSSS**; chip rate = **2 Mchips/s**; **32 chips/symbol**.
- ⭐ `aBaseSuperframeDuration = 960 symbols` is the **most important constant**.
- ⭐ **BO = Beacon Order** (0–14); **SO = Superframe Order** (0 to BO).
- ⭐ $BI = 960 \times 2^{BO}$ symbols; $SD = 960 \times 2^{SO}$ symbols; **1 symbol = 16 µs**.
- ⭐ **One time slot = 60 symbols = 960 µs**; 16 slots in the active period.
- ⭐ **CAP** uses slotted CSMA/CA; **CFP** uses **GTS** for deterministic access.
- ⭐ **FFD** = Full-Function Device (Coordinator/Router); **RFD** = Reduced-Function Device (End Device only).
- ⭐ **CSMA/CA parameters:** `macMinBE = 3`, `macMaxBE = 5`, `macMaxCSMABackoffs = 4`, `macMaxFrameRetries = 3`.
- ⭐ **MAC frame max size = 127 bytes payload**; max total frame size = 152 bytes.
- ⭐ **ZigBee device types:** **ZC** (Coordinator, 1 per PAN), **ZR** (Router, multiple), **ZED** (End Device, can sleep).
- ⭐ **ZigBee mesh routing** uses **AODVjr** (on-demand); **tree routing** uses **Cskip**-based hierarchical addressing.
- ⭐ **ZigBee supports Star, Mesh, Cluster-Tree topologies** — IEEE 802.15.4 supports only **Star and Peer-to-Peer** natively.
- ⭐ **Security:** Optional **AES-128** encryption at the MAC layer; ZigBee adds application-level key management.
- ⭐ **Channel center frequency** (2.4 GHz): $f_c = 2405 + 5(k-11)$ MHz for $k = 11, \ldots, 26$.
- ⭐ **ZigBee 3.0** is the current unified standard — supports all application profiles (Home Automation, Light Link, Health Care, etc.) under one stack.
- ⭐ **Comparison memory trick:** ZigBee = "**L**ow-power, **L**ow-rate, **L**ong-life, **L**ots of nodes" — the four L's of IoT.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mediation Device Protocol
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMRT634 - WIRELESS & SENSORS NETWORKS > - **Module:** Module Module 3 > - **Topic:** Mediation Device Protocol # 📘 Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition The **Mediation Device Protocol (MDP)** is a network coordination mechanism defined for low-rate wireless personal area networks (LR-WPANs) and IEEE 802.15.4-based Wireless Sensor Networks (WSNs) in which a designated **Mediation Device (MedD)** — typically a Full-Function Device (FFD) acting as a PAN coordinator — assists other Reduced-Function Devices (RFDs) in gaining access to the network and synchronizing with the coordinator. The protocol governs how orphan or un-associated devices use a coordinator to mediate their association, channel scan, and data transfer operations. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** > A Mediation Device is a *Full-Function Device (FFD)* that has the capability to forward **Association Request**, **Disassociation Request**, and **Data Request** commands on behalf of an RFD that cannot directly reach the PAN coordinator due to range, power, or interference limitations. In the KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the Mediation Device Protocol belongs to the family of **MAC-layer assistance mechanisms** that extend the operational reach of star and peer-to-peer topologies without requiring a full-fledged router. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Analogy — "The Post Office Helper":** > Imagine a small village where a postman cannot reach the main city post office directly because the road is too long or blocked. The village appoints a trusted neighbor (the **Mediation Device**) who collects the postman’s letters, walks them to the city post office, and brings back the replies. The post office only deals with the trusted neighbor, not the postman directly. > > * Wireless Sensor Node (RFD) → The Village Postman > * Mediation Device (FFD) → The Trusted Neighbor > * PAN Coordinator → The Main City Post Office > * MAC Frame / Command → The Letters and Replies In this analogy: - The **RFD** has limited transmit power. - The **Mediation Device** has higher power and acts as a relay. - The **PAN Coordinator** only communicates with the Mediation Device on behalf of the RFD. - This extends network coverage and saves energy in the RFD. ## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics | Parameter | Standard Value (IEEE 802.15.4) | |---|---| | Operating Frequency Bands | **868 MHz / 915 MHz / 2.4 GHz** | | Maximum PHY Frame Size | **127 bytes** | | Maximum MAC Payload | **102 bytes** | | Number of Channels (2.4 GHz) | **16 channels (11–26)** | | Beacon Order (BO) Range | **0–15** | | Superframe Order (SO) Range | **0–15** | | Typical Symbol Rate (2.4 GHz) | **62.5 ksymbols/s** | | Bit Rate (2.4 GHz O-QPSK) | **250 kbps** | > [!IMPORTANT] > The Mediation Device Protocol is operational within the **2.4 GHz ISM band** and uses the standard **O-QPSK modulation** defined in IEEE 802.15.4, with the standard 16-channel set. --- # 📗 Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Architecture of the Mediation Device Protocol The Mediation Device Protocol follows a structured operational flow. The procedure can be broken down into the following logical steps: ### Step 1 — Channel Scan by RFD The RFD performs either an **Active Scan** (transmitting Beacon Request) or a **Passive Scan** (listening for Beacons) on a specified channel. ### Step 2 — Beacon Reception If a Mediation Device is within range, the RFD receives the **Beacon Frame** broadcast by the Mediation Device. The Beacon contains: - Source PAN ID - Source Address (Mediation Device address) - Superframe Specification - GTS (Guaranteed Time Slot) Information - Pending Address List ### Step 3 — Association via Mediation Device The RFD transmits an **Association Request Command** to the Mediation Device (not directly to the PAN Coordinator). The Mediation Device buffers this request. ### Step 4 — Forwarding by Mediation Device The Mediation Device forwards the Association Request to the PAN Coordinator on behalf of the RFD. ### Step 5 — Acknowledgement Propagation The PAN Coordinator returns an **Association Response Command** containing a 16-bit short address assigned to the RFD. The Mediation Device relays this back. ### Step 6 — Data Exchange All subsequent data frames between the RFD and the rest of the network now flow through the Mediation Device using a **short 16-bit address** rather than the 64-bit extended address. > [!NOTE] > **Why does the Mediation Device help?** > RFDs are designed to be simple, low-power, and inexpensive (e.g., temperature sensors, light sensors). They have: > - Limited transmit power > - Limited memory (often only a 64-bit address) > - Limited battery capacity > Using a Mediation Device allows the RFD to participate in larger networks without complex routing logic. ## 2.2 Frame Structure Used in Mediation A typical MAC frame used in the Mediation Device Protocol follows the standard IEEE 802.15.4 structure: $$ \text{MAC Frame} = \underbrace{\text{MHR}}_{\text{Header}} + \underbrace{\text{MSDU}}_{\text{Payload}} + \underbrace{\text{MFR}}_{\text{Frame Check}} $$ | Field | Length (bytes) | Purpose | |---|---|---| | Frame Control | 2 | Identifies frame type & addressing mode | | Sequence Number | 1 | Matches request/response pairs | | Destination PAN ID | 0 or 2 | PAN ID of receiver | | Destination Address | 0/2/8 | Receiver address (short/long/none) | | Source PAN ID | 0 or 2 | PAN ID of sender | | Source Address | 0/2/8 | Sender address | | Auxiliary Security | 0/5/6 | Optional security header | | MSDU Payload | Variable | Up to 102 bytes | | FCS (Frame Check Sequence) | 2 | CRC-16 for error detection | ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Equations for Mediation Device Protocol & Related 802.15.4 Computations** | Concept | Formula / Relation | Units / Notes | |---|---|---| | Beacon Interval (BI) | $BI = aBaseSuperframeDuration \cdot 2^{BO}$ | Symbol periods | | Superframe Duration (SD) | $SD = aBaseSuperframeDuration \cdot 2^{SO}$ | Symbol periods | | Active Portion (CAP) | $CAP = SD - CFP$ | Symbol periods | | Inactive Portion | $Inactivity = BI - SD$ | Symbol periods | | aBaseSuperframeDuration | $aBaseSuperframeDuration = 960$ | Symbols (always) | | Bit → Symbol Rate (2.4 GHz) | $4 \text{ bits} = 1 \text{ symbol}$ | 250 kbps / 62.5 ksymbols/s | | Frame Max Duration | $\leq 4.256 \text{ ms}$ at 2.4 GHz | At max MSDU | | Energy per Bit (battery budget) | $E_b = V \cdot I \cdot t / N_b$ | Joules/bit | | Throughput Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{L_{payload}}{L_{frame}} \cdot 100$ | Percentage | | Max Nodes per Coord (rough) | $\leq 255$ typical | Practically < 30 stable | | Orphan Device Re-association Time | $T_{assoc} = T_{scan} + T_{beacon} + 2 \cdot T_{turnaround}$ | Seconds | > [!NOTE] > For the **Mediation Device Protocol**, the most-tested KTU values are **BO**, **SO**, **aBaseSuperframeDuration = 960 symbols**, and the **3-channel minimum scan requirement** for channel page selection. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility The Mediation Device Protocol is deployed in: - **Smart Home Systems** (ZigBee end devices use mediators in the form of routers/ZED relays) - **Industrial WSN** (WirelessHART gateways act as mediators) - **Healthcare BANs** (Hub acts as a coordinator; mediators help wearable patches) - **Agricultural Sensor Networks** (Field sensors use a base-station mediator) - **Smart Metering** (Mesh Advanced Metering Infrastructure — AMI) The engineering value lies in **energy efficiency, range extension, and asymmetric role assignment** within a constrained WSN. --- # 📙 Section 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough & Derivations ## 3.1 Analytical Scenario: Beacon Interval Calculation > **Problem Statement (KTU-style):** > An IEEE 802.15.4 WSN PAN coordinator operates with **BO = 4** and **SO = 3** in the 2.4 GHz band. > Compute the **Beacon Interval (BI)** and the **Superframe Duration (SD)**. If the active CAP occupies the first 60% of the SD, find the CAP in symbol periods. Find the total inactive period per BI in milliseconds. ### Step 1 — Identify the Base Constant The IEEE 802.15.4 standard fixes: $$ aBaseSuperframeDuration = 960 \text{ symbols} $$ This is the **mandatory minimum SD** when SO = 0, and it is the scaling base for all superframe computations. ### Step 2 — Compute the Beacon Interval (BI) The Beacon Interval formula is: $$ BI = aBaseSuperframeDuration \cdot 2^{BO} $$ Substituting the given values: $$ BI = 960 \cdot 2^{4} $$ $$ BI = 960 \cdot 16 $$ $$ BI = 15{,}360 \text{ symbols} $$ ### Step 3 — Compute the Superframe Duration (SD) The Superframe Duration formula is: $$ SD = aBaseSuperframeDuration \cdot 2^{SO} $$ Substituting: $$ SD = 960 \cdot 2^{3} $$ $$ SD = 960 \cdot 8 $$ $$ SD = 7{,}680 \text{ symbols} $$ ### Step 4 — Compute the Contention Access Period (CAP) Given that CAP occupies the first 60% of the Superframe Duration: $$ CAP = 0.60 \cdot SD $$ $$ CAP = 0.60 \cdot 7{,}680 $$ $$ CAP = 4{,}608 \text{ symbols} $$ ### Step 5 — Compute the Inactive Period The inactive period is the portion of the BI that lies outside the SD: $$ \text{Inactive} = BI - SD $$ $$ \text{Inactive} = 15{,}360 - 7{,}680 $$ $$ \text{Inactive} = 7{,}680 \text{ symbols} $$ ### Step 6 — Convert Inactive Period to Milliseconds At 2.4 GHz with **62.5 ksymbols/s**, the symbol period is: $$ T_{symbol} = \dfrac{1}{62{,}500} \text{ s} = 16 \text{ μs} $$ Therefore: $$ \text{Inactive (ms)} = 7{,}680 \cdot 16 \text{ μs} $$ $$ \text{Inactive (ms)} = 122{,}880 \text{ μs} $$ $$ \text{Inactive (ms)} = 122.88 \text{ ms} $$ ### Final Consolidated Result $$ \boxed{BI = 15{,}360 \text{ symbols}, \quad SD = 7{,}680 \text{ symbols}, \quad CAP = 4{,}608 \text{ symbols}, \quad \text{Inactive} = 122.88 \text{ ms}} $$ ## 3.2 Analytical Scenario: Mediation Device Frame Latency > **Problem Statement:** > An RFD sends a data frame to a PAN coordinator via a Mediation Device. The RFD-to-MedD frame is 25 bytes; the MedD-to-Coord frame is the same payload. Each transmission includes a turnaround time of 192 μs (12 symbols) and an ACK of 11 bytes. Compute the total time consumed in transmitting one round-trip data exchange. Use 2.4 GHz PHY (250 kbps). ### Step 1 — Compute the Byte Transmission Time At 250 kbps, time per bit is: $$ T_{bit} = \dfrac{1}{250{,}000} = 4 \text{ μs} $$ Time per byte is: $$ T_{byte} = 8 \cdot T_{bit} = 8 \cdot 4 = 32 \text{ μs} $$ ### Step 2 — Compute Frame Transmission Times Each data frame is 25 bytes: $$ T_{data} = 25 \cdot 32 = 800 \text{ μs} $$ Each ACK is 11 bytes: $$ T_{ack} = 11 \cdot 32 = 352 \text{ μs} $$ Each turnaround gap is: $$ T_{turn} = 192 \text{ μs} $$ ### Step 3 — Sum the Round-Trip Components A complete round-trip involves: - RFD → MedD data frame - ACK from MedD - Turnaround - MedD → Coordinator data frame - ACK from Coordinator - Turnaround (return path) $$ T_{total} = 2 \cdot T_{data} + 2 \cdot T_{ack} + 2 \cdot T_{turn} $$ $$ T_{total} = 2 \cdot 800 + 2 \cdot 352 + 2 \cdot 192 $$ $$ T_{total} = 1{,}600 + 704 + 384 $$ $$ T_{total} = 2{,}688 \text{ μs} $$ $$ T_{total} = 2.688 \text{ ms} $$ ### Final Result $$ \boxed{T_{total} = 2.688 \text{ ms per round-trip data exchange through the Mediation Device}} $$ ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Decision Logic of Mediation Device | Condition | Action by Mediation Device | Resulting MAC Command | |---|---|---| | RFD sends Association Request | Buffer & forward to coordinator | Association Request Command (CMD = 0x01) | | Coordinator returns short address | Encapsulate in Association Response | Association Response Command (CMD = 0x02) | | RFD goes out of sync | MedD sends Beacon with PAN ID | Beacon Frame (CMD = 0x00) | | RFD disassociates | MedD relays Disassociation Notice | Disassociation Notification (CMD = 0x03) | | RFD has pending data | MedD polls coordinator on behalf | Data Request Command (CMD = 0x04) | | RFD needs to send orphan notification | MedD forwards Orphan Notification | Orphan Notification (CMD = 0x06) | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Tip:** The 6 MAC command frames used in the Mediation Device Protocol are commonly tested by their **Command Frame Identifier (CMD value)** — memorize them. --- # 📒 Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Network Topology Showing Mediation Device The diagram below illustrates the **star-of-stars** topology where the Mediation Device acts as an intermediate node between multiple RFDs and the central PAN Coordinator. ```mermaid flowchart TD PAN[PAN Coordinator - FFD
Network ID 0x1234]:::core MedD[Mediation Device - FFD
Short Addr 0x0001]:::mediator RFD1[RFD - Temp Sensor
Ext Addr 0xA1]:::leaf RFD2[RFD - Light Sensor
Ext Addr 0xA2]:::leaf RFD3[RFD - Humidity Sensor
Ext Addr 0xA3]:::leaf RFD4[RFD - Motion Sensor
Ext Addr 0xA4]:::leaf RFD1 -->|Association Req| MedD RFD2 -->|Association Req| MedD RFD3 -->|Association Req| MedD RFD4 -->|Association Req| MedD MedD -->|Forwards to Coord| PAN PAN -->|Assigns Short Addr| MedD MedD -->|Delivers to RFD| RFD1 MedD -->|Delivers to RFD| RFD2 classDef core fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#fff,color:#fff classDef mediator fill:#c00000,stroke:#fff,color:#fff classDef leaf fill:#70ad47,stroke:#fff,color:#fff ``` ## 4.2 Mediation Device Protocol — Sequence Flow The sequence below shows the **end-to-end handshake** between an RFD, Mediation Device, and PAN Coordinator during initial association. ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant RFD as RFD (Sensor Node) participant MedD as Mediation Device participant Coord as PAN Coordinator Note over RFD,Coord: Phase 1 - Channel Scan RFD->>RFD: Perform Active or Passive Scan RFD->>MedD: Receive Beacon Frame MedD-->>RFD: Beacon with PAN ID 0x1234 Note over RFD,Coord: Phase 2 - Association Request RFD->>MedD: Association Request Command MedD->>MedD: Buffer Request MedD->>Coord: Forward Association Request Note over RFD,Coord: Phase 3 - Association Response Coord->>MedD: Association Response (Short Addr 0x0005) MedD->>RFD: Deliver Short Address 0x0005 Note over RFD,Coord: Phase 4 - Data Exchange RFD->>MedD: Data Frame (using short address 0x0005) MedD->>Coord: Forward Data Coord-->>MedD: ACK MedD-->>RFD: ACK ``` ## 4.3 Functional Architecture of the Mediation Device The block diagram below depicts the **internal functional blocks** of a Mediation Device as a functional state machine. ```mermaid flowchart LR A[RF Transceiver
2.4 GHz O-QPSK]:::hw B[MAC Layer
802.15.4 Engine]:::mac C[Address Table
RFD Mappings]:::table D[Command Parser
CMD 0x01-0x07]:::parser E[Buffer Queue
Pending Frames]:::buf F[Association Manager]:::mgr G[Beacon Scheduler]:::sched A --> B B --> D D --> F F --> C F --> E B --> G G --> A classDef hw fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#fff,color:#fff classDef mac fill:#c00000,stroke:#fff,color:#fff classDef table fill:#ed7d31,stroke:#fff,color:#fff classDef parser fill:#7030a0,stroke:#fff,color:#fff classDef buf fill:#a5a5a5,stroke:#fff,color:#fff classDef mgr fill:#548235,stroke:#fff,color:#fff classDef sched fill:#bf8f00,stroke:#fff,color:#fff ``` ## 4.4 Superframe Structure with Mediation Timing ```mermaid gantt title IEEE 802.15.4 Superframe with Mediation Phase dateFormat X axisFormat %s section Beacon Phase Beacon Transmission :a1, 0, 960 section Active Period CAP RFD to MedD Access :a2, after a1, 1500 MedD to Coord Forwarding :a3, after a2, 1500 Contention Slots :a4, after a3, 1608 section Inactive Period Sleep Phase for RFDs :a5, after a4, 7680 section Next Beacon Next Beacon Cycle :a6, after a5, 0 ``` --- # 📕 Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (2 × 3 Marks = 6 Marks) ### Question 1 (3 Marks) **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** **Q: Define a Mediation Device in IEEE 802.15.4 WSN. List any two MAC command frames it handles.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** A Mediation Device is a **Full-Function Device (FFD)** in an IEEE 802.15.4-based WSN that assists **Reduced-Function Devices (RFDs)** in associating with a PAN coordinator and relaying their data when the RFDs cannot communicate directly with the coordinator due to range or power constraints. (2 marks) The two MAC command frames it commonly handles are: 1. **Association Request Command (CMD = 0x01)** — forwarded to the coordinator. (0.5 mark) 2. **Association Response Command (CMD = 0x02)** — relayed back to the RFD. (0.5 mark) --- ### Question 2 (3 Marks) **`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** **Q: Differentiate between a Full-Function Device (FFD) and a Reduced-Function Device (RFD) in WSN. Which one acts as a Mediation Device?** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** | Aspect | FFD | RFD | |---|---|---| | Role | Can be PAN coordinator, coordinator, or device | Can only be a simple end device | | Complexity | High — full MAC implementation | Low — reduced MAC implementation | | Power | Higher consumption | Very low power (battery-only) | | Memory & Address | Holds 64-bit & 16-bit addresses | Usually only 64-bit address | | Network Role | Router, mediator, or end node | Sensor or actuator only | A **Mediation Device is always a Full-Function Device (FFD)** because it must implement the full MAC command set, buffer management, and PAN coordination logic that an RFD cannot. (1 mark) --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions — Module Internal Choice (2 × 14 Marks) ### Question A (14 Marks) **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** **Q: (a)** Explain the architecture and operational phases of the **Mediation Device Protocol** in an IEEE 802.15.4-based WSN with a neat diagram. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Compute the Beacon Interval, Superframe Duration, and the Inactive Period (in ms) for a PAN with **BO = 5** and **SO = 3** in the 2.4 GHz band. If the CAP occupies 70% of the SD, compute the CAP in symbols. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution (a) — Architecture and Operational Phases (7 Marks) **Definition (1 Mark):** The Mediation Device Protocol is a MAC-layer assistance mechanism in IEEE 802.15.4 WSNs in which an FFD called a Mediation Device (MedD) coordinates the association, synchronization, and data exchange between RFDs and the PAN coordinator. **Architecture (3 Marks):** The architecture consists of three tiers: 1. **Tier 1 — PAN Coordinator (FFD)**: The top-level coordinator managing PAN ID, superframe structure, and short-address assignment. 2. **Tier 2 — Mediation Device (FFD)**: The middle-tier FFD that buffers MAC commands and forwards them on behalf of RFDs. 3. **Tier 3 — RFDs (sensor nodes)**: Low-power end devices that send Association Requests, Data Requests, and Orphan Notifications through the MedD. **Operational Phases (3 Marks):** - **Phase 1 — Channel Scan**: RFD performs Active/Passive scan; receives Beacon from MedD. - **Phase 2 — Association Request**: RFD → MedD → Coordinator. - **Phase 3 — Association Response**: Coordinator assigns short 16-bit address; relayed back. - **Phase 4 — Data Exchange**: All subsequent data flows via MedD using the short address. - **Phase 5 — Disassociation / Orphan Recovery**: MedD helps RFDs to leave or re-join the PAN. *(Neat diagram — see Section 4.2 sequence diagram.)* #### Model Solution (b) — Numerical Computation (7 Marks) **Given:** BO = 5, SO = 3, 2.4 GHz PHY, aBaseSuperframeDuration = 960 symbols. **Step 1 — Beacon Interval (2 Marks):** $$ BI = 960 \cdot 2^{5} = 960 \cdot 32 = 30{,}720 \text{ symbols} $$ **Step 2 — Superframe Duration (2 Marks):** $$ SD = 960 \cdot 2^{3} = 960 \cdot 8 = 7{,}680 \text{ symbols} $$ **Step 3 — CAP (1.5 Marks):** $$ CAP = 0.70 \cdot SD = 0.70 \cdot 7{,}680 = 5{,}376 \text{ symbols} $$ **Step 4 — Inactive Period in ms (1.5 Marks):** $$ \text{Inactive} = BI - SD = 30{,}720 - 7{,}680 = 23{,}040 \text{ symbols} $$ At 62.5 ksymbols/s: $$ \text{Inactive (ms)} = 23{,}040 \cdot 16 \text{ μs} = 368{,}640 \text{ μs} = 368.64 \text{ ms} $$ **Final Answer:** $$ \boxed{BI = 30{,}720 \text{ symbols}, \quad SD = 7{,}680 \text{ symbols}, \quad CAP = 5{,}376 \text{ symbols}, \quad \text{Inactive} = 368.64 \text{ ms}} $$ --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** **Q: (a)** With a clear block diagram, describe the **internal functional architecture** of a Mediation Device. Explain the role of each block. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A WSN with 12 RFDs communicates with a PAN coordinator through a single Mediation Device. Each RFD generates one 20-byte data frame per minute. Compute the aggregate throughput requirement at the Mediation Device (in bits per second) and the round-trip time per RFD frame (in ms) using the 2.4 GHz PHY. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution (a) — Internal Architecture (7 Marks) **Block Diagram (3 Marks):** Refer to Section 4.3 — Mermaid block diagram. **Role of Each Block (4 Marks):** - **RF Transceiver (2.4 GHz O-QPSK)**: Performs modulation/demodulation, channel selection, CCA (Clear Channel Assessment), and ED (Energy Detect). (1 Mark) - **MAC Layer Engine**: Implements the IEEE 802.15.4 MAC protocol including frame construction, CSMA-CA, and ACK handling. (0.75 Mark) - **Address Table**: Maintains a mapping table between the 64-bit extended addresses of RFDs and the 16-bit short addresses assigned by the coordinator. (0.75 Mark) - **Command Parser**: Decodes MAC command frames (CMD 0x01–0x07) such as Association, Disassociation, Data Request, Orphan Notification. (0.5 Mark) - **Buffer Queue**: Stores pending Association Requests and Data Requests awaiting forwarding to the coordinator. (0.5 Mark) - **Association Manager**: Orchestrates the entire association handshake and maintains state of each RFD. (0.25 Mark) - **Beacon Scheduler**: Generates and transmits periodic Beacons to enable RFD synchronization. (0.25 Mark) #### Model Solution (b) — Throughput and RTT Computation (7 Marks) **Given:** 12 RFDs, 1 frame/RFD/minute, 20 bytes/frame, 2.4 GHz PHY @ 250 kbps. **Step 1 — Aggregate Frame Rate (1.5 Marks):** $$ \text{Frame rate} = \dfrac{12 \text{ frames}}{60 \text{ s}} = 0.2 \text{ frames/s} $$ **Step 2 — Aggregate Data Throughput (2 Marks):** $$ \text{Throughput} = 0.2 \cdot 20 \cdot 8 \text{ bits/s} $$ $$ \text{Throughput} = 32 \text{ bits/s} $$ **Step 3 — RTT for a Single RFD Frame (3.5 Marks):** Time per byte at 250 kbps = 32 μs (as derived in Section 3.2). For a 20-byte data frame + 11-byte ACK + 2 × 192 μs turnaround: $$ T_{data} = 20 \cdot 32 = 640 \text{ μs} $$ $$ T_{ack} = 11 \cdot 32 = 352 \text{ μs} $$ $$ T_{turn} = 2 \cdot 192 = 384 \text{ μs} $$ $$ RTT = 640 + 352 + 384 = 1{,}376 \text{ μs} = 1.376 \text{ ms} $$ **Final Answer:** $$ \boxed{\text{Throughput} = 32 \text{ bps}, \quad RTT = 1.376 \text{ ms per RFD frame}} $$ --- ## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks:** > > 1. **Confusing MedD with a Router**: A Mediation Device is **not** a multi-hop router; it only mediates association and forwards specific MAC commands in a single hop. > 2. **Wrong aBaseSuperframeDuration**: Students often write 60 symbols or 192 symbols — the **correct value is 960 symbols** (always). > 3. **Forgetting the 4-bits-per-symbol mapping**: At 2.4 GHz, 1 symbol = 4 bits. Forgetting this gives wrong superframe ms conversions. > 4. **Mixing up BO and SO constraints**: The protocol enforces **SO ≤ BO**. Writing SO > BO yields invalid frames. > 5. **Missing the unit of "symbols"**: Always write the result of `960 · 2^BO` in **symbols**, not ms — convert at the final step. > 6. **Forgetting CAP ≤ SD − CFP**: The CAP cannot exceed the active SD minus the Contention-Free Period (CFP). > 7. **Incorrect PAN ID usage**: Both MedD and Coordinator must operate on the **same PAN ID**; otherwise Beacon acceptance fails. > 8. **Treating MedD as transparent**: MedD is **not** transparent — it performs buffering, parsing, and possible retransmission. --- ## 5.4 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - ✅ A **Mediation Device is an FFD** that assists RFDs in joining and communicating with a PAN coordinator. - ✅ The Mediation Device Protocol uses **6 key MAC command frames**: Association Request (0x01), Association Response (0x02), Disassociation Notification (0x03), Data Request (0x04), PAN ID Conflict (0x05), Orphan Notification (0x06). - ✅ **BI = 960 · 2^BO symbols**; **SD = 960 · 2^SO symbols**; **SO ≤ BO ≤ 15**. - ✅ At 2.4 GHz: 1 symbol = 4 bits; symbol rate = 62.5 ksymbols/s; bit rate = 250 kbps. - ✅ CAP occupies the start of the active SD; CFP follows CAP; GTS slots are inside CFP. - ✅ MedD performs **Active/Passive scan relaying**, **Association Request/Response forwarding**, and **Orphan Notification handling**. - ✅ The MedD architecture includes: RF Transceiver → MAC Engine → Command Parser → Association Manager → Buffer Queue → Beacon Scheduler. - ✅ Energy efficiency improves because RFDs use lower transmit power and shorter addressing (16-bit instead of 64-bit). - ✅ Practical deployments: ZigBee routers, WirelessHART gateways, 6LoWPAN edge routers, smart-home hubs. - ✅ Throughput efficiency: $\eta = \dfrac{L_{payload}}{L_{frame}} \cdot 100$; with a 25-byte payload in a 35-byte frame, $\eta \approx 71.4\%$. - ✅ Maximum theoretical PAN size: 65,536 nodes (16-bit addressing), but practical RFD count per MedD is < 30 for stable operation. - ✅ Frame security: Optional AES-128 CCM* security can be added in the MAC Auxiliary Security Header. - ✅ Beacon Order (BO) = 0 → no Beacon mode (non-beacon-enabled PAN); BO > 0 → beacon-enabled mode used by MedD for synchronization. --- > **End of Module 3 Notes — Mediation Device Protocol** > *Prepared per KTU 2024 Scheme — PEMRT634 Wireless & Sensors Networks*

topics.title: Wakeup Radio Concepts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT634 - WIRELESS & SENSORS NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Wakeup Radio Concepts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Wakeup Radio (WuR):** A secondary, ultra-low-power radio receiver co-located with the primary data transceiver in a wireless sensor node, which remains active during the main radio's sleep intervals to monitor the wireless channel for a designated wakeup signal (address/ID) and trigger the primary radio only upon positive detection.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (PEMRT634)**, Wakeup Radio Concepts form a critical MAC-layer energy-management technique that directly addresses the *idle listening* and *overhearing* problems that dominate the energy budget of duty-cycled Wireless Sensor Networks (WSNs).

A Wakeup Radio subsystem is characterized by four primary engineering parameters:

| Parameter | Typical Symbol | Standard Unit |
| :--- | :---: | :--- |
| Wakeup Receiver Sensitivity | $S_{wu}$ | **dBm** |
| Wakeup Power Consumption | $P_{wu}$ | **µW** (micro-watts) |
| Wakeup Latency | $T_{wu}$ | **ms** (milli-seconds) |
| Wakeup Range | $R_{wu}$ | **m** (meters) |

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]  
> **Analogy — The Apartment Intercom System:**  
> Imagine you live in a large apartment building. Instead of standing by your front door all day (wasting energy listening for visitors) — the **idle listening problem** — you install a battery-powered, low-power doorbell receiver (the *Wakeup Radio*) that listens 24×7. When a visitor presses the transmitter at the gate, your doorbell receiver *triggers* your main power-consuming home intercom (the *Primary Data Radio*) only at that exact moment. You talk to the visitor, then power everything back down. The doorbell consumes milliwatts; the intercom consumes watts. The result: massive energy savings, near-zero idle waste.

This is the fundamental philosophy of **Wakeup Radio Concepts** — *decouple channel monitoring from data communication*.

## 1.3 Why Wakeup Radio? — The Idle Listening Problem

In conventional duty-cycled MAC protocols such as **B-MAC**, **S-MAC**, and **T-MAC**, nodes periodically wake up to listen to the channel. The energy wasted in this *idle listening* state can account for **50%–100%** of total node energy consumption.

Mathematically, the inefficiency is captured by:

$$E_{idle} = P_{rx} \cdot T_{listen}$$

Where:
- $P_{rx}$ is the receiver power during listening (typically tens of mW)
- $T_{listen}$ is the cumulative listening time per duty cycle

A Wakeup Radio replaces this expensive listening with a sub-100 µW continuous monitor, yielding orders-of-magnitude energy reduction.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Wakeup Radio System Architecture

A WuR-equipped sensor node comprises **two** distinct radio subsystems operating in tandem:

1. **Primary Data Radio (PDR):** High-power transceiver (-100 dBm sensitivity, 50–100 mW active power) responsible for data packet transmission/reception.
2. **Wakeup Radio (WuR):** Ultra-low-power receiver (-30 to -50 dBm sensitivity, 10–500 µW active power) responsible for wakeup signal detection only.

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Operating Principle:** The PDR remains in *deep sleep* ($P_{sleep} \approx 1$–$10\ \mu W$) indefinitely. Only the WuR stays alert. Upon successful detection of its unique address (or a broadcast wakeup), the WuR raises an interrupt line (GPIO), enabling the PDR.

## 2.2 Classification of Wakeup Radio Designs

| Class | Power Range | Carrier Source | Range | Use Case |
| :--- | :---: | :--- | :---: | :--- |
| **Passive WuR** | 0 µW (harvested) | Incoming RF energy | < 5 m | RFID-style tags |
| **Semi-Active WuR** | 10 – 100 µW | Local oscillator | 5 – 30 m | Indoor WSN |
| **Active WuR** | 100 – 500 µW | Crystal oscillator | 30 – 100 m | Outdoor/Industrial WSN |

## 2.3 Wakeup Signal Types

Three principal wakeup signaling schemes are employed in KTU-referenced literature:

1. **Tone-based Wakeup:** A single unmodulated carrier at a designated frequency. Simplest; minimal overhead; no addressing.
2. **ID-based Wakeup:** A short On-Off-Keying (OOK) modulated packet containing the target node's unique identifier. Selective wakeup; higher overhead.
3. **Hybrid Wakeup:** Tone for channel reservation + ID for node addressing. Optimal for collision avoidance.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]  
> **High-Yield Equations for KTU University Examinations**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $E_{wu} = V_{dd} \cdot I_{wu} \cdot T_{wu}$ | Energy consumed by WuR per wakeup cycle | Joules (J) |
| 2 | $P_{avg} = P_{sleep} \cdot (1 - DC) + P_{active} \cdot DC$ | Average node power (duty-cycled) | Watts (W) |
| 3 | $DC = \dfrac{T_{active}}{T_{active} + T_{sleep}}$ | Duty Cycle ratio | Dimensionless (0–1) |
| 4 | $T_{latency} = T_{wu} + T_{setup} + T_{ack}$ | End-to-end wakeup-to-data latency | Seconds (s) |
| 5 | $E_{saved} = P_{rx} \cdot T_{listen} - P_{wu} \cdot T_{cycle}$ | Energy saved by WuR vs. idle listening | Joules (J) |
| 6 | $L_{node} = \dfrac{E_{battery}}{P_{avg}}$ | Node operational lifetime | Seconds / Years |
| 7 | $SNR_{wu} = P_{tx,wu} - S_{wu} - N_{floor}$ | Wakeup link budget (Friis-derived) | dB |
| 8 | $P_{r,wu} = P_{t,wu} \cdot G_t \cdot G_r \cdot \left(\dfrac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^2$ | Received wakeup power (Friis equation) | Watts (W) |

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Boundary Conditions to Memorize:**  
> • Typical WuR power $P_{wu} \in [10, 500]\ \mu W$  
> • Typical PDR Rx power $P_{rx} \in [30, 100]\ mW$  
> • Energy ratio $\dfrac{P_{rx}}{P_{wu}} \geq 100\times$ (this is the *WuR Advantage Factor*)  

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Wakeup Radio concepts are foundational in:
- **Industrial IoT (IIoT):** Battery-powered field sensors with 10-year lifetime targets.
- **Medical Body Area Networks (BAN):** Minimizing Specific Absorption Rate (SAR) and prolonging implant battery.
- **Smart Agriculture:** Soil and climate sensors with multi-year unattended operation.
- **Military / Tactical Networks:** Energy-starved covert sensor deployments.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Duty Cycle Average Power Consumption

**Starting Point:** A duty-cycled node alternates between *sleep* and *active* states. We seek the time-averaged power.

Let:
- $T_{sleep}$ = duration of sleep state per cycle (seconds)
- $T_{active}$ = duration of active (Rx/Tx) state per cycle (seconds)
- $T_{cycle} = T_{sleep} + T_{active}$
- $P_{sleep}$ = power drawn in sleep state (W)
- $P_{active}$ = power drawn in active state (W)

**Step 1:** Energy consumed in one complete cycle:

$$E_{cycle} = E_{sleep} + E_{active}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute energy = power × time:

$$E_{cycle} = P_{sleep} \cdot T_{sleep} + P_{active} \cdot T_{active}$$

**Step 3:** Average power = energy per cycle / cycle duration:

$$P_{avg} = \dfrac{E_{cycle}}{T_{cycle}} = \dfrac{P_{sleep} \cdot T_{sleep} + P_{active} \cdot T_{active}}{T_{sleep} + T_{active}}$$

**Step 4:** Introduce duty cycle $DC = \dfrac{T_{active}}{T_{cycle}}$:

Since $T_{active} = DC \cdot T_{cycle}$ and $T_{sleep} = (1 - DC) \cdot T_{cycle}$:

$$P_{avg} = \dfrac{P_{sleep} \cdot (1 - DC) \cdot T_{cycle} + P_{active} \cdot DC \cdot T_{cycle}}{T_{cycle}}$$

**Step 5:** Cancel $T_{cycle}$:

$$\boxed{P_{avg} = P_{sleep} \cdot (1 - DC) + P_{active} \cdot DC}$$

This is the **canonical KTU formula** for duty-cycled average power.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — WuR Energy Savings vs. Conventional Duty Cycling

**Scenario:** Compare a node using *conventional duty cycling* (periodically waking the PDR to listen) against a node using *WuR-assisted always-off PDR*.

**Step 1:** Energy wasted by idle listening in conventional scheme per cycle:

$$E_{idle,conv} = P_{rx} \cdot T_{listen}$$

**Step 2:** Energy consumed by WuR during the same cycle (PDR fully asleep):

$$E_{wu,assist} = P_{wu} \cdot T_{cycle}$$

**Step 3:** Net energy saved per cycle:

$$E_{saved} = E_{idle,conv} - E_{wu,assist}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute explicit terms:

$$E_{saved} = P_{rx} \cdot T_{listen} - P_{wu} \cdot T_{cycle}$$

**Step 5:** Introduce the WuR Advantage Factor $\alpha = \dfrac{P_{rx}}{P_{wu}}$. Assuming $T_{listen} \approx T_{cycle}$ for fair comparison:

$$\boxed{E_{saved} = (\alpha - 1) \cdot P_{wu} \cdot T_{cycle}, \quad \text{where } \alpha \gg 1}$$

For typical values $P_{rx} = 50\ mW$, $P_{wu} = 100\ \mu W$, $T_{cycle} = 1\ s$:

$$E_{saved} \approx (500 - 1) \cdot 100 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 1 \approx 49.9\ mJ \text{ per cycle}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Wakeup Link Budget (Friis-Based)

**Problem:** Compute the maximum wakeup range $d_{max}$ for a given transmit power $P_{t,wu}$.

**Step 1:** Received power at distance $d$ using the Friis free-space equation:

$$P_{r,wu}(d) = P_{t,wu} \cdot G_t \cdot G_r \cdot \left(\dfrac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^2$$

**Step 2:** The wakeup is successful only if received power exceeds WuR sensitivity $S_{wu}$:

$$P_{r,wu}(d_{max}) \geq S_{wu}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute and solve for $d_{max}$:

$$S_{wu} = P_{t,wu} \cdot G_t \cdot G_r \cdot \left(\dfrac{\lambda}{4\pi d_{max}}\right)^2$$

**Step 4:** Isolate $d_{max}$:

$$\left(\dfrac{\lambda}{4\pi d_{max}}\right)^2 = \dfrac{S_{wu}}{P_{t,wu} \cdot G_t \cdot G_r}$$

**Step 5:** Take the square root and invert:

$$d_{max} = \dfrac{\lambda}{4\pi} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{P_{t,wu} \cdot G_t \cdot G_r}{S_{wu}}}$$

**Step 6:** Convert to dB form (preferred for KTU numericals). Let all powers be in dBm and gains in dBi:

$$\boxed{d_{max} = \dfrac{\lambda}{4\pi} \cdot 10^{\left(\dfrac{P_{t,wu}^{dBm} + G_t^{dBi} + G_r^{dBi} - S_{wu}^{dBm}}{20}\right)}}$$

> [!NOTE]  
> **Sample Numerical (KTU Style):**  
> Given: $f = 868\ MHz$, $P_{t,wu} = 10\ dBm$, $G_t = G_r = 2\ dBi$, $S_{wu} = -40\ dBm$.  
> Compute: $\lambda = \dfrac{3 \times 10^8}{868 \times 10^6} \approx 0.346\ m$  
> Numerator exponent: $\dfrac{10 + 2 + 2 - (-40)}{20} = \dfrac{54}{20} = 2.7$  
> $\Rightarrow 10^{2.7} \approx 501.2$  
> $d_{max} = \dfrac{0.346}{4\pi} \times 501.2 \approx 13.8\ m$  

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Node Lifetime Extension

**Step 1:** Lifetime in seconds:

$$L_{node} = \dfrac{E_{battery}}{P_{avg}}$$

**Step 2:** Convert to years:

$$L_{years} = \dfrac{E_{battery}}{P_{avg} \cdot 3.154 \times 10^7}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute $P_{avg}$ from Derivation 1:

$$\boxed{L_{years} = \dfrac{E_{battery}}{(1 - DC) \cdot P_{sleep} + DC \cdot P_{active}} \cdot \dfrac{1}{3.154 \times 10^7}}$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — WuR-Equipped Sensor Node

> [!NOTE]  
> **Diagram Note:** The Mermaid block diagram below depicts the *functional interaction* between the Wakeup Radio, the Primary Data Radio, the Microcontroller Unit, and the power subsystem. This is the architecture typically expected in KTU 14-mark derivations.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA["RF ANTENNA DUAL BAND"] --> nodeSplitter{"RF SPLITTER DIPLEXER"}
    nodeSplitter -- "WAKEUP BAND" --> nodeWuRX["WAKEUP RECEIVER WUR"]
    nodeSplitter -- "DATA BAND" --> nodeDataTX["DATA TRANSCEIVER PDR"]
    nodeWuRX --> nodeDecoder["WAKEUP DECODER ID MATCH"]
    nodeDecoder -- "GPIO INTERRUPT" --> nodeMCU["MICROCONTROLLER UNIT"]
    nodeMCU -- "ENABLE LINE" --> nodeDataTX
    nodeDataTX -- "DATA PAYLOAD" --> nodeApp["APPLICATION LAYER"]
    nodeApp --> nodeMCU
    nodeMCU --> nodePower["POWER MANAGEMENT UNIT"]
    nodePower -- "ALWAYS ON 1.8V" --> nodeWuRX
    nodePower -- "GATED 3.3V" --> nodeDataTX
    nodePower -- "CORE 1.2V" --> nodeMCU
    nodeClockA["32 KHZ CRYSTAL"] --> nodeWuRX
    nodeClockB["16 MHZ CRYSTAL"] --> nodeDataTX
    nodeClockB --> nodeMCU
```

## 4.2 Wakeup Sequence — Temporal Flow Diagram

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant TX as TRANSMITTER NODE
    participant WU as WAKEUP RADIO
    participant MCU as MICROCONTROLLER
    participant PD as PRIMARY DATA RADIO
    participant RX as RECEIVER NODE

    TX->>WU: BROADCAST WAKEUP TONE OR ID PACKET
    Note over WU: CONTINUOUS LOW POWER LISTENING
    WU->>WU: SIGNAL DETECTED
    WU->>MCU: GPIO INTERRUPT RISING EDGE
    MCU->>PD: POWER ENABLE PDR
    Note over PD: TRANSITION FROM SLEEP TO RX
    PD->>PD: CHANNEL CLEAR ASSESSMENT CCA
    PD->>RX: RTS REQUEST TO SEND
    RX->>PD: CTS CLEAR TO SEND
    PD->>RX: DATA PACKET TRANSFER
    RX->>PD: ACK ACKNOWLEDGEMENT
    PD->>MCU: TRANSFER COMPLETE FLAG
    MCU->>PD: POWER DISABLE PDR
    Note over WU: RETURN TO CONTINUOUS MONITORING
```

## 4.3 Energy State Transition Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> DEEP_SLEEP
    DEEP_SLEEP --> WUR_MONITOR: BOOT INITIALIZATION
    WUR_MONITOR --> WU_DETECTED: ID OR TONE MATCH
    WU_DETECTED --> PDR_ACTIVE: MCU INTERRUPT PDR ENABLE
    PDR_ACTIVE --> DATA_EXCHANGE: CCA SUCCESSFUL
    DATA_EXCHANGE --> PDR_ACTIVE: RETRY OR CONTINUE
    PDR_ACTIVE --> DEEP_SLEEP: TX RX COMPLETE ACK
    DEEP_SLEEP --> WUR_MONITOR: REARM WATCHDOG
    WUR_MONITOR --> DEEP_SLEEP: BATTERY CRITICAL THRESHOLD
```

## 4.4 Component-Power Mapping (Textual Schematic)

| Subsystem | State | Voltage Rail | Current Draw | Active Duration |
| :--- | :--- | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| Wakeup Receiver (WuR) | Always ON | **1.8 V** | **50 µA** | 100% of cycle |
| MCU (Cortex-M0 class) | Always ON | **1.2 V** | **300 µA** | 100% of cycle |
| Primary Data Radio (CC2420) | SLEEP | 3.3 V | **1 µA** | 99% of cycle |
| Primary Data Radio (CC2420) | ACTIVE | 3.3 V | **18.8 mA** | 1% of cycle |
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Question A1 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define Wakeup Radio. Why is it considered superior to conventional duty cycling in WSN?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** A Wakeup Radio (WuR) is a secondary ultra-low-power receiver co-located with the primary data transceiver that continuously monitors the wireless channel for a wakeup signal (tone or address) and triggers the primary radio only upon successful detection.

> **Superiority — Two Key Points (2 marks):**
> 1. **Eliminates Idle Listening:** Conventional duty cycling wastes $P_{rx} \cdot T_{listen}$ energy; WuR replaces it with $P_{wu} \cdot T_{cycle}$ where $P_{wu} \ll P_{rx}$.
> 2. **On-Demand Activation:** The primary radio transitions from deep sleep to active *only* when traffic is destined for the node, achieving near-zero standby waste.

---

### Question A2 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**List and briefly explain any THREE classifications of Wakeup Radio designs.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| # | Type | Description (1 mark each) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Passive WuR** | Harvests operational energy from the incoming RF wakeup signal itself; no battery needed for the receiver; range < 5 m. |
| 2 | **Semi-Active WuR** | Uses a local oscillator and minimal baseband circuitry; consumes 10–100 µW; supports ID-based selective wakeup. |
| 3 | **Active WuR** | Full low-IF or super-heterodyne receiver; consumes 100–500 µW; supports higher data-rate wakeup packets over 30–100 m. |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

### Question B-A — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` (14 Marks)

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a WuR-equipped WSN node. Identify and justify the power domains for each subsystem. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for average power consumption of a duty-cycled WSN node. Using this derivation, compute the percentage reduction in average power when a WuR (consuming 100 µW continuously) replaces a primary radio that listens at 50 mW with a 5% duty cycle. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Block Diagram (3 marks):**

A WuR-equipped node comprises the following functional blocks:

1. **Dual-Band Antenna** — Single radiating element with integrated diplexer for wakeup and data bands.
2. **Wakeup Receiver (WuR)** — Ultra-low-power OOK or low-IF receiver, sensitivity −30 to −50 dBm.
3. **Wakeup Decoder** — Compares incoming ID against stored address; raises GPIO interrupt on match.
4. **Microcontroller Unit (MCU)** — Acts as system arbiter and protocol engine.
5. **Primary Data Radio (PDR)** — High-power IEEE 802.15.4 transceiver (e.g., CC2420).
6. **Power Management Unit (PMU)** — Provides gated and ungated voltage rails.

**Power Domain Justification (4 marks):**

| Subsystem | Voltage Rail | Justification |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| WuR + MCU | **Always-ON 1.8 V / 1.2 V** | Must remain alert for incoming wakeup signals regardless of PDR state. |
| PDR | **Gated 3.3 V** | Power MOSFET switch controlled by MCU; enabled only after WuR interrupt. |
| Sensors | **Application-gated** | Activated by MCU post-wakeup for data acquisition. |

The strict separation of *always-on* (WuR/MCU) and *event-gated* (PDR) domains is the **foundational design principle** of WuR systems.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Derivation (4 marks):**

For a duty-cycled node with active duration $T_{active}$ and sleep duration $T_{sleep}$:

$$P_{avg} = \dfrac{P_{sleep} \cdot T_{sleep} + P_{active} \cdot T_{active}}{T_{sleep} + T_{active}}$$

Let duty cycle $DC = \dfrac{T_{active}}{T_{cycle}}$, where $T_{cycle} = T_{sleep} + T_{active}$.

Substituting $T_{active} = DC \cdot T_{cycle}$ and $T_{sleep} = (1 - DC) \cdot T_{cycle}$:

$$\boxed{P_{avg} = P_{sleep} \cdot (1 - DC) + P_{active} \cdot DC}$$

**Numerical Evaluation (3 marks):**

**Given:**
- Conventional scheme: $P_{rx} = 50\ mW$, $DC = 5\% = 0.05$, assume $P_{sleep} \approx 10\ \mu W = 0.01\ mW$ (negligible)
- WuR scheme: $P_{wu} = 100\ \mu W = 0.1\ mW$, PDR always in sleep

**Conventional average power:**

$$P_{avg,conv} = 0.01 \cdot (1 - 0.05) + 50 \cdot 0.05$$
$$P_{avg,conv} = 0.0095 + 2.5 \approx 2.5095\ mW$$

**WuR-assisted average power:**

$$P_{avg,wu} \approx P_{wu} = 0.1\ mW \quad \text{(PDR idle)}$$

**Percentage Reduction:**

$$\text{Reduction} = \dfrac{P_{avg,conv} - P_{avg,wu}}{P_{avg,conv}} \times 100\%$$

$$\text{Reduction} = \dfrac{2.5095 - 0.1}{2.5095} \times 100\% \approx 96.0\%$$

**Conclusion:** The WuR reduces average power consumption by approximately **96%** — extending node lifetime by a factor of ~25× under identical battery capacity.

---

### Question B-B — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` (14 Marks) — **INTERNAL CHOICE**

**(a)** Compare and contrast tone-based, ID-based, and hybrid wakeup signaling schemes in WSN. Discuss the trade-off between false wakeup rate and addressing capability. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A WuR receiver operating at 868 MHz has a sensitivity of −45 dBm. The wakeup transmitter delivers +10 dBm through an antenna of 2 dBi gain. Assuming the receiver antenna gain is also 2 dBi, compute the maximum wakeup range using the Friis free-space equation. Comment on the implications if the path-loss exponent is increased to 3 (outdoor urban environment). **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Comparative Table (5 marks):**

| Criterion | Tone-Based | ID-Based | Hybrid |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Signal Content** | Unmodulated carrier | Modulated ID packet | Tone + ID |
| **Addressing** | Broadcast only | Selective per node | Selective |
| **Overhead** | Minimal (single tone) | Higher (full packet) | Moderate |
| **False Wakeup Rate** | **Highest** (any RF triggers) | **Low** (ID match required) | **Lowest** (tone pre-clears noise) |
| **Latency** | Sub-millisecond | Few milliseconds | Few milliseconds |
| **Power at WuR** | ~10 µW | ~100 µW | ~80 µW |
| **Use Case** | Emergency broadcast | Targeted data delivery | Large-scale deployments |

**Trade-off Discussion (2 marks):**

The fundamental trade-off is **selectivity vs. simplicity**. Tone-based schemes offer the lowest power and latency but suffer from false wakeups in noisy environments. ID-based schemes provide precise addressing but consume more WuR energy due to baseband decoding. Hybrid schemes balance both by using a tone for channel reservation (filtering ambient noise) followed by ID verification.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Friis Equation (Free-Space, n = 2) (3 marks):**

$$d_{max} = \dfrac{\lambda}{4\pi} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{P_{t} \cdot G_t \cdot G_r}{P_{r,min}}}$$

Where:
- $\lambda = \dfrac{c}{f} = \dfrac{3 \times 10^8}{868 \times 10^6} \approx 0.3456\ m$
- $P_t = 10\ dBm = 10\ mW$
- $G_t = G_r = 2\ dBi \Rightarrow 10^{0.1} \approx 1.585$ (linear)
- $P_{r,min} = -45\ dBm = 3.16 \times 10^{-5}\ mW$

**Step 2 — Compute the ratio (2 marks):**

$$\dfrac{P_t \cdot G_t \cdot G_r}{P_{r,min}} = \dfrac{10 \cdot 1.585 \cdot 1.585}{3.16 \times 10^{-5}}$$

$$= \dfrac{25.12}{3.16 \times 10^{-5}} \approx 7.95 \times 10^5$$

**Step 3 — Final calculation (1 mark):**

$$d_{max} = \dfrac{0.3456}{4\pi} \cdot \sqrt{7.95 \times 10^5}$$

$$= 0.0275 \cdot 891.6 \approx 24.5\ m$$

**Step 4 — Outdoor Urban Implications (1 mark):**

With path-loss exponent $n = 3$ (instead of 2), the received power decays as $d^{-3}$. Equivalently, the maximum range reduces by a factor of $10^{1/10} \approx 1.26$ per decade of dB loss, yielding an approximate range reduction to:

$$d_{max,urban} \approx d_{max} \cdot 10^{-\frac{(3-2)}{20 \cdot 1}} \approx 24.5 \times 10^{-0.05} \approx 21.9\ m$$

> **Conclusion:** Outdoor urban deployment reduces wakeup range by roughly **10–15%** under identical transmit parameters. To compensate, the wakeup transmitter power must be increased by approximately **3 dB** (doubling).

---

> [!WARNING]  
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**  
> • **Never confuse dBm with dBW.** 0 dBm = 1 mW; 0 dBW = 1 W. Conversion: $P_{dBm} = P_{dBW} + 30$.  
> • **Always include antenna gains** in the Friis equation. Omitting $G_t$ and $G_r$ is the most common mistake and costs 2 marks instantly.  
> • **State boundary conditions** explicitly when using the duty-cycle formula. KTU examiners award marks for writing $0 \leq DC \leq 1$ and identifying limiting cases.  
> • **In derivation questions**, write the substituted expression *before* canceling terms. Step-skipping results in zero marks for that sub-part.  
> • **Use $\mu$W or mW consistently** in numerical answers — mixing units (e.g., writing 100 µW as 100 W) is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]  
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU University Exam Ready**

- ✅ **Wakeup Radio (WuR)** = ultra-low-power secondary receiver; replaces expensive idle listening with ~100 µW continuous monitoring.
- ✅ **Idle Listening Problem** is the primary motivation; can account for **50–100%** of WSN node energy.
- ✅ **WuR Advantage Factor** $\alpha = P_{rx} / P_{wu}$ — typically **> 100×** in practical deployments.
- ✅ **Three WuR Classes:** Passive (harvested), Semi-Active (10–100 µW), Active (100–500 µW).
- ✅ **Three Signaling Schemes:** Tone (broadcast), ID (selective), Hybrid (balanced).
- ✅ **Canonical Duty-Cycle Formula:** $P_{avg} = P_{sleep}(1 - DC) + P_{active} \cdot DC$.
- ✅ **Friis Wakeup Range:** $d_{max} = \dfrac{\lambda}{4\pi} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{P_t \cdot G_t \cdot G_r}{S_{wu}}}$ — memorize in both linear and dB forms.
- ✅ **Always-ON Domain:** WuR + MCU + RTC. **Event-Gated Domain:** PDR + Sensors.
- ✅ **WuR Sensitivity** is *much worse* than PDR sensitivity (−30 to −50 dBm vs. −100 dBm) — this is the key trade-off.
- ✅ **Wakeup Latency Components:** $T_{wu}$ (detection) + $T_{setup}$ (PDR wakeup) + $T_{ack}$ (handshake).
- ✅ **Lifetime Extension Factor** under WuR: typically **10×–50×** vs. conventional duty cycling.
- ✅ **KTU Hot Topics for 14-Mark Questions:** (1) Energy comparison derivations, (2) Friis-based range computation, (3) Architecture block diagrams with power-rail justification.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Address and Name Management
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT634 - WIRELESS & SENSORS NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Address and Name Management

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition: Address Management

**Address Management** in a Wireless Sensor Network (WSN) refers to the set of protocols, algorithms, and mechanisms responsible for assigning, maintaining, and uniquely identifying sensor nodes within the network topology. Since WSNs are typically deployed as large-scale, dense, and resource-constrained ad-hoc networks, every node (and often every sensing event) must be unambiguously identifiable so that routing, data aggregation, and query resolution can be performed correctly.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Address management in WSN is the process of uniquely identifying sensor nodes through logical, physical, or geographical identifiers so that data packets can be routed from source to sink (base station) without ambiguity, while consuming minimum energy and memory.

The address is **NOT** a permanent Internet Protocol (IP) address as in the Internet. Instead, WSN addresses can be:
- **Flat IDs (numeric)** – e.g., 16-bit or 64-bit integers.
- **Hierarchical IDs** – tree-based, cluster-based.
- **Geographic/Location-based** – (x, y) coordinates from GPS.
- **Attribute-based** – describing what the node senses, not where it is.

### 1.2 Formal Definition: Name Management

**Name Management** in WSN is a data-centric mechanism where the *name* of the data (or phenomenon) replaces the *address* of the source node. Instead of asking "send data from node 17", the application asks "send temperature readings from region R1". This is essential because WSNs are queried by *content*, not by *origin*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Name Management?** In a WSN with thousands of nodes, a user typically does not care *which* node reports the data; the user only cares *what* the data represents (e.g., humidity > 60%, temperature > 30°C). Hence, names describe **attributes** of the sensed data, not the producing node.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

| Concept | Real-World Analogy | WSN Equivalent |
|---|---|---|
| Address | House number (e.g., 12B, MG Road) | Unique Node ID (16-bit / 64-bit) |
| Name | Item description (e.g., "red apples from Kerala") | Attribute set (temperature, location, time) |
| Address Management | Postal system assigning PIN codes | Distributed address assignment protocols |
| Name Management | Amazon search by product name | Data-centric routing, content-based naming |

**Intuitive Story:** Imagine a classroom of **1000** students where the teacher asks, "Who has a fever above 100°F?". 
- **Address-based approach:** The teacher calls roll number 1, 2, 3, … 1000 → *wasteful, slow, energy-expensive*.
- **Name/Attribute-based approach:** The teacher announces, "Anyone with temperature > 100°F, raise your hand." Only relevant students respond. This is **data-centric naming** — the WSN philosophy.

### 1.4 Physical and Standard Metrics

The following constants and metrics are universally used in KTU-level WSN problems:

- **Default radio address length:** **16-bit** (tinyOS motes) or **64-bit** (IEEE 802.15.4 EUI-64).
- **Maximum nodes in a flat 16-bit space:** **2¹⁶ = 65,536 nodes**.
- **Maximum nodes in a 64-bit space:** **2⁶⁴ ≈ 1.84 × 10¹⁹ nodes**.
- **Birthday Paradox threshold:** At ~**183 nodes** with 16-bit random IDs, collision probability crosses **50%**.
- **Transmission energy per bit (typical):** **E_elec = 50 nJ/bit**, **E_amp = 100 pJ/bit/m²**.

> [!NOTE]
> These numeric values are **must-remember** for KTU numerical problems and short-answer questions.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Address Management is Hard in WSN

Unlike the Internet, WSNs have **unique constraints** that complicate addressing:

1. **Energy Scarcity:** Address assignment involves communication → consumes battery.
2. **No Central Authority:** Nodes are deployed randomly (aerial drop); no pre-configuration.
3. **Scalability:** Networks may have **thousands** of nodes.
4. **Mobility & Failure:** Nodes die, links break, topology changes.
5. **Limited Memory:** A tinyOS mote has only **4–10 KB RAM**, cannot store huge routing tables.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Highlight: Address management schemes in WSN are classified as **Centralized**, **Distributed (Local)**, and **Cluster-based / Hierarchical**.

### 2.2 Classification of Address Assignment Schemes

#### A) Centralized Address Assignment
- Base station (sink) assigns IDs to all nodes.
- **Pros:** Guaranteed uniqueness.
- **Cons:** Single point of failure; not scalable; high latency.

#### B) Distributed / Local Address Assignment
- Each node picks a random ID from a shared pool (e.g., 16-bit space).
- **Birthday paradox** governs collision probability.
- **Pros:** Scalable, no central coordinator.
- **Cons:** Risk of duplicate IDs.

#### C) Hierarchical / Cluster-based Addressing
- Network divided into clusters; each cluster has a **Cluster Head (CH)**.
- Address = `<Cluster ID>.<Node ID>` (e.g., 4.27 means node 27 in cluster 4).
- **Example protocol:** **LEACH** (Low Energy Adaptive Clustering Hierarchy).
- **Pros:** Energy-efficient, scalable.
- **Cons:** Cluster head rotation overhead.

#### D) Geographic / Location-based Addressing
- Each node's address is its physical coordinates (x, y).
- Acquired via **GPS** or **GAF** (Geographic Adaptive Fidelity).
- **Pros:** Naturally unique; supports geographic routing (GPSR).
- **Cons:** GPS hardware expensive; not energy-efficient indoors.

### 2.3 Name Management Strategies

#### A) Data-Centric Naming (DC)
- Queries name *data*, not nodes.
- Example: `SELECT temperature FROM region A WHERE value > 50`.
- Protocols: **Directed Diffusion**, **SPIN**.

#### B) Attribute-Value Pair Naming
- Each data packet carries attribute set:
  `{type=temperature, location=Room-204, time=10:30, value=27.5}`

#### C) Content Hash Naming
- Data is named by a hash of its content (used in **content-centric networking**).

#### D) Hierarchical Task-Based Naming
- Queries addressed to *regions* or *tasks*, e.g., "vehicle detection in zone 3".

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Addressable nodes in *n*-bit space | $N_{max} = 2^n$ | Maximum unique IDs | nodes |
| Birthday collision probability | $P_{collision} \approx 1 - e^{-k^2/2N}$ | Probability two of *k* random picks collide | dimensionless |
| Number of nodes for 50% collision | $k_{50\%} \approx 1.18 \sqrt{N}$ | Birthday paradox threshold | nodes |
| Energy for address broadcast | $E_{tx} = k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot E_{amp} \cdot d^2$ | Transmitting *k*-bit address over distance *d* | Joules |
| Energy for receiving broadcast | $E_{rx} = k \cdot E_{elec}$ | Receiving *k*-bit address | Joules |
| Cluster address space | $A_{cluster} = C \cdot N_{perCluster}$ | C clusters × nodes per cluster | nodes |
| Energy saved by data-centric naming | $E_{saved} = (N - R) \cdot E_{tx} \cdot L$ | *N*=total nodes, *R*=relevant, *L*=packet length | Joules |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Note on Birthday Paradox:** If WSN uses random 16-bit IDs and has 183 nodes, the probability of **at least one collision** exceeds **50%**. KTU frequently asks to compute *k* for a given collision probability.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Smart Agriculture:** Each soil-moisture sensor is named `moisture-z3-tomato-field` (attribute-based).
- **Disaster Monitoring:** Each bridge-vibration sensor has a unique 16-bit address to avoid duplicate alerts.
- **Healthcare WSNs:** Patient vitals are named by attribute (`{patient_id=42, vital=BPM, time=t}`), not by sensor ID.
- **Military Surveillance:** Targets detected are named by *type* and *location*, not by which camera detected them.

> [!NOTE]
