> In modern IoT, **CoAP (Constrained Application Protocol)** and **6LoWPAN** use URI-based naming (e.g., `coap://[aaaa::212:7401:1:101]/temperature`) — a direct descendant of WSN name management concepts.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Maximum Nodes in a Given Address Space

**Problem:** A WSN uses *n*-bit flat addresses. Compute the maximum number of uniquely addressable sensor nodes.

**Step 1 — Define the address space:**
Each *n*-bit address represents a unique binary pattern.

$$A = 2^n$$

**Step 2 — For *n* = 16 bits (tinyOS convention):**

$$A_{16} = 2^{16} = 65{,}536 \text{ nodes}$$

**Step 3 — For *n* = 64 bits (IEEE 802.15.4 EUI-64):**

$$A_{64} = 2^{64} = 18{,}446{,}744{,}073{,}709{,}551{,}616 \text{ nodes} \approx 1.84 \times 10^{19}$$

**Step 4 — Engineering interpretation:**
- A 16-bit address space is **sufficient** for most practical WSNs (typically < 1000 nodes).
- A 64-bit address space is **virtually infinite** for WSN scale; used for global uniqueness (e.g., ZigBee EUI-64).

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Birthday Paradox — Collision Probability

**Problem:** *k* nodes each randomly pick an address from a space of size *N*. Find the probability that **at least two nodes** share the same address.

**Step 1 — Probability that *all k* addresses are distinct:**
The first node has probability 1 of being unique, the second has probability $(N-1)/N$, the third $(N-2)/N$, and so on.

$$P_{distinct} = \frac{N}{N} \cdot \frac{N-1}{N} \cdot \frac{N-2}{N} \cdots \frac{N-k+1}{N}$$

**Step 2 — Express as product:**

$$P_{distinct} = \prod_{i=0}^{k-1} \left(1 - \frac{i}{N}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Take natural log and apply Taylor expansion** $\ln(1-x) \approx -x$ for small $x$:

$$\ln P_{distinct} = \sum_{i=0}^{k-1} \ln\left(1 - \frac{i}{N}\right) \approx -\sum_{i=0}^{k-1} \frac{i}{N}$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the sum** $\sum_{i=0}^{k-1} i = \frac{k(k-1)}{2}$:

$$\ln P_{distinct} \approx -\frac{k(k-1)}{2N}$$

**Step 5 — Exponentiate:**

$$P_{distinct} \approx e^{-k(k-1)/2N}$$

**Step 6 — Collision probability:**

$$P_{collision} = 1 - P_{distinct} \approx 1 - e^{-k(k-1)/2N}$$

**Step 7 — Approximation for large *k*:**

$$P_{collision} \approx 1 - e^{-k^2/2N}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Threshold Number of Nodes for 50% Collision

**Problem:** Find the number of nodes *k* at which the collision probability equals **0.5**.

**Step 1 — Set $P_{collision} = 0.5$:**

$$1 - e^{-k^2/2N} = 0.5 \implies e^{-k^2/2N} = 0.5$$

**Step 2 — Take natural log of both sides:**

$$-\frac{k^2}{2N} = \ln(0.5) = -0.6931$$

**Step 3 — Solve for *k*:**

$$k^2 = 2N \cdot 0.6931 = 1.3863 \cdot N$$

$$k = \sqrt{1.3863 \cdot N} = 1.1774 \sqrt{N}$$

**Step 4 — Numerical example for *N* = 2¹⁶ = 65,536:**

$$k_{50\%} = 1.1774 \cdot \sqrt{65{,}536} = 1.1774 \cdot 256 \approx 301.4 \approx 301 \text{ nodes}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Correction Note:** Some textbooks quote **183 nodes for 16-bit space**; that figure uses a slightly different derivation. KTU accepts both ~300 (using $k = 1.18\sqrt{N}$) and ~183 (using $k = 0.7\sqrt{N}$ as in classical birthday paradox). The **classical birthday paradox** uses $k \approx 1.18\sqrt{N}$ for the standard "365 days" calendar — so **301** is the rigorous KTU value for 16-bit space.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Energy Comparison — Address-based vs Name-based Query

**Problem:** A WSN has *N* = 500 nodes, with *R* = 50 relevant nodes (e.g., fire sensors in a burning zone). Compare energy consumption of (a) address-based roll-call query vs (b) data-centric (attribute-based) query.

**Assumptions:**
- Each packet: *L* = 64 bytes = **512 bits**.
- Each transmission: $E_{tx} = 50$ nJ/bit, $E_{rx} = 50$ nJ/bit.
- Distance ignored (all neighbors): only $E_{elec}$ matters.

**Step 1 — Energy per transmission of one packet:**

$$E_{tx,1} = L \cdot E_{elec} = 512 \cdot 50 \text{ nJ} = 25{,}600 \text{ nJ} = 25.6 \mu J$$

**Step 2 — Energy per reception:**

$$E_{rx,1} = L \cdot E_{elec} = 25.6 \mu J$$

**Step 3 — Address-based query (sink queries each node):**
- Sink sends query: 1 transmission by sink.
- Each node responds: *N* transmissions + *N* receptions at sink.

$$E_{address} = 1 \cdot E_{tx,1} + N \cdot (E_{tx,1} + E_{rx,1}) = 25.6 + 500 \cdot 51.2 \text{ }\mu J$$

$$E_{address} = 25.6 + 25{,}600 = 25{,}625.6 \text{ }\mu J \approx 25.6 \text{ mJ}$$

**Step 4 — Data-centric query (only relevant nodes respond):**

$$E_{dc} = 1 \cdot E_{tx,1} + R \cdot (E_{tx,1} + E_{rx,1}) = 25.6 + 50 \cdot 51.2$$

$$E_{dc} = 25.6 + 2560 = 2585.6 \text{ }\mu J \approx 2.59 \text{ mJ}$$

**Step 5 — Energy saved:**

$$E_{saved} = 25.6 - 2.59 = 23.01 \text{ mJ} \quad (89.9\% \text{ saving})$$

**Step 6 — Efficiency ratio:**

$$\eta = \frac{E_{dc}}{E_{address}} = \frac{2.59}{25.6} \approx 0.10 \quad (10\%)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** Data-centric naming (name management) reduces energy consumption by nearly **90%** in this scenario — this is the central justification for adopting name-based data delivery in WSNs.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Address Management Schemes

| Scheme | Uniqueness | Energy Cost | Scalability | Failure Tolerance | KTU Tip |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Centralized (sink-based) | 100% | High | Poor | Very Low | Rarely used in WSN |
| Random / Distributed | Probabilistic | Low | Excellent | High | Use birthday formula |
| Cluster / Hierarchical | Guaranteed (within cluster) | Medium | Excellent | Medium | LEACH, PEGASIS |
| Geographic (GPS) | Guaranteed (if no overlap) | Very High (GPS) | Good | Medium | Indoor = poor |
| Content / Name-based | N/A (not used for routing) | Lowest | Excellent | Highest | SPIN, Directed Diffusion |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Architecture: Address Assignment Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Network Deployment] --> B0{Address Scheme?}
    B0 -->|Centralized| C0[Sink Assigns ID]
    B0 -->|Distributed| D0[Node Picks Random ID]
    B0 -->|Cluster-based| E0[Cluster Head Allocates ID]
    C0 --> F0[Unicast ID to Node]
    D0 --> G0{Birthday Collision?}
    E0 --> H0[Broadcast Cluster ID]
    G0 -->|No| I0[Accept ID]
    G0 -->|Yes| J0[Re-pick ID]
    F0 --> K0[Active WSN]
    I0 --> K0[Active WSN]
    J0 --> D0
    H0 --> K0[Active WSN]
    K0 --> L0[Routing and Data Delivery]
```

### 4.2 Architecture: Name Management Data-Centric Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Q1[User Query: temperature more than 50] --> S1[Sink Floods Interest]
    S1 --> N1[Sensor Node 1]
    S1 --> N2[Sensor Node 2]
    S1 --> N3[Sensor Node 3]
    S1 --> N4[Sensor Node 4]
    N1 --> M1{Temperature more than 50?}
    N2 --> M2{Temperature more than 50?}
    N3 --> M3{Temperature more than 50?}
    N4 --> M4{Temperature more than 50?}
    M1 -->|No| X1[Suppress]
    M2 -->|Yes| Y1[Send Data]
    M3 -->|No| X2[Suppress]
    M4 -->|Yes| Y2[Send Data]
    Y1 --> R1[Sink Aggregates]
    Y2 --> R1
    R1 --> O1[Reply to User]
    X1 --> O1
    X2 --> O1
```

### 4.3 Hierarchical Cluster Address Structure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[16-bit Flat Address Space] --> B1[Cluster ID 8 bits]
    A1 --> C1[Node ID 8 bits]
    B1 --> D1[Cluster 0]
    B1 --> D2[Cluster 1]
    B1 --> D3[Cluster 2]
    B1 --> D4[Cluster N minus 1]
    D1 --> E1[Node 0]
    D1 --> E2[Node 1]
    D1 --> E3[Node 255]
    D2 --> F1[Node 0]
    D2 --> F2[Node 1]
    D2 --> F3[Node 255]
    D3 --> G1[Node 0]
    D3 --> G2[Node 1]
    D3 --> G3[Node 255]
    D4 --> H1[Node 0]
    D4 --> H2[Node 1]
    D4 --> H3[Node 255]
```

### 4.4 SPIN Protocol Name Negotiation (3-Phase Handshake)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant SRC as Source Node
    participant SINK as Sink Node
    SRC->>SINK: ADV (Advertisement: meta-data name)
    SINK->>SRC: REQ (Request: send full data)
    SRC->>SINK: DATA (Full data with attribute name)
    Note over SRC,SINK: Only relevant data transferred; saves energy
```

### 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Address and Name Management

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph LAYER1[Application Layer]
        QRY[User Query by Name]
    end
    subgraph LAYER2[Name Resolution Layer]
        NMS[Name Management System]
        ATR[Attribute Parser]
    end
    subgraph LAYER3[Routing Layer]
        RT[Routing Engine]
        AB[Address Translator]
    end
    subgraph LAYER4[Address Management Layer]
        AMS[Address Management System]
        ALLOC[Address Allocator]
        COLL[Collision Handler]
    end
    subgraph LAYER5[Physical MAC Layer]
        RADIO[Radio Transceiver]
    end
    QRY --> NMS
    NMS --> ATR
    ATR --> RT
    RT --> AB
    AB --> AMS
    AMS --> ALLOC
    AMS --> COLL
    ALLOC --> RADIO
    COLL --> RADIO
    RT --> RADIO
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**"What is data-centric naming in WSN? Why is it preferred over address-based queries?"**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Data-centric naming is a name management scheme where queries are expressed in terms of **data attributes** (e.g., "temperature in region R1") rather than **node addresses** (e.g., "send from node 17"). It is preferred because:
1. It eliminates unnecessary transmissions from irrelevant nodes → **energy saving** (often 80–90%).
2. It naturally handles node mobility and failure — only the data matters, not the source.
3. It aligns with the actual WSN usage model: users want *information*, not *source identity*.
**(1 mark for definition + 1 mark each for two reasons = 3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**"List any three address assignment schemes used in WSN and state one advantage of each."**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
1. **Centralized (sink-based) Addressing** – *Advantage:* Guaranteed uniqueness, no collision.
2. **Distributed Random Addressing** – *Advantage:* Highly scalable, no central coordinator.
3. **Cluster-based (Hierarchical) Addressing** – *Advantage:* Energy-efficient via cluster heads (LEACH).
**(1 mark per scheme + advantage = 3 marks)**

---

### Part B: Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Pattern: Part B questions carry 14 marks, divided into two sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Students must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**(a) Explain the address management schemes in Wireless Sensor Networks with neat diagrams. Compare centralized and distributed approaches. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Definition (1 mark):**
Address management is the mechanism of assigning and maintaining unique identifiers to sensor nodes so that data routing and network operation are unambiguous.

**2. Schemes (5 marks — 1 mark each scheme + 1 mark for comparison):**

**(i) Centralized Address Assignment:**
- A single sink/base station assigns IDs to each node.
- Each node sends a "join request"; the sink replies with a unique ID.
- *Pros:* Guaranteed uniqueness, simple collision-free.
- *Cons:* Single point of failure; not scalable to thousands of nodes; high energy cost (many hops to sink).

**(ii) Distributed / Local Address Assignment:**
- Each node independently picks a random ID from a shared *N*-bit address space.
- Collision probability follows the **Birthday Paradox:** $P_{collision} \approx 1 - e^{-k^2/2N}$.
- *Pros:* Scalable, no central coordinator, fault-tolerant.
- *Cons:* Possible duplicate IDs requiring re-assignment.

**(iii) Cluster / Hierarchical Address Assignment:**
- Network is divided into clusters, each with a **Cluster Head (CH)**.
- Address format: `<Cluster_ID>.<Node_ID>` (e.g., 3.45).
- CHs assign IDs within their cluster — e.g., **LEACH** protocol.
- *Pros:* Energy-efficient (CH aggregates data), scalable.
- *Cons:* CH rotation overhead.

**(iv) Geographic / Location-based Address Assignment:**
- Address = physical (x, y) coordinates from GPS.
- *Pros:* Naturally unique; supports geographic routing like **GPSR**.
- *Cons:* GPS hardware expensive and energy-inefficient.

**3. Comparison Table (1 mark):**
| Aspect | Centralized | Distributed |
|---|---|---|
| Uniqueness | 100% | Probabilistic |
| Energy | High | Low |
| Scalability | Poor | Excellent |
| Failure tolerance | Low | High |

---

**(b) A WSN is to be deployed with random 16-bit address assignment. If there are 400 nodes, compute the probability that at least two nodes share the same address. State your interpretation. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- Address space $N = 2^{16} = 65{,}536$
- Number of nodes $k = 400$

**Step 1 — Apply Birthday Paradox formula (2 marks):**

$$P_{collision} = 1 - e^{-k(k-1)/2N}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the exponent (2 marks):**

$$\frac{k(k-1)}{2N} = \frac{400 \cdot 399}{2 \cdot 65{,}536} = \frac{159{,}600}{131{,}072} \approx 1.2177$$

**Step 3 — Compute probability (2 marks):**

$$P_{collision} = 1 - e^{-1.2177} = 1 - 0.2959 = 0.7041$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation (1 mark):**
There is approximately a **70.4% probability** that at least two nodes in the 400-node WSN will share the same address. Hence, **random address assignment is unsuitable** for this scale; a **cluster-based** or **deterministic** assignment scheme must be used to guarantee uniqueness.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a) What is Name Management in WSN? Describe the data-centric routing approach with an example. How does it differ from traditional address-based routing? (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Definition of Name Management (2 marks):**
Name management is a data-centric mechanism where each piece of sensed data is identified by a **set of attributes** (e.g., type, location, time) rather than the address of the producing node. The network routes data based on what the data *is*, not where it *came from*.

**2. Data-Centric Routing Example (3 marks):**
Consider a forest-fire detection WSN with 1000 temperature sensors.
- **User Query (Name-based):** "Send me all temperature readings from region R3 exceeding 60°C every 5 minutes."
- **Sink Action:** Floods an *interest* message with the attribute set `{type=temperature, region=R3, threshold=60, interval=5min}`.
- **Sensor Action:** Each node whose local reading matches the attribute set replies; others remain silent.
- This is implemented in **Directed Diffusion** and **SPIN** protocols.

**3. Difference from Address-based Routing (2 marks):**
| Aspect | Address-based | Name / Data-centric |
|---|---|---|
| Identifier | Node ID (e.g., node 17) | Data attributes (e.g., temperature, region) |
| Query type | "Send from node 17" | "Send temperature > 60 in R3" |
| Energy use | High (all nodes respond) | Low (only relevant nodes respond) |
| Robustness | Fails if node dies | Robust to node failure |
| Scalability | Poor | Excellent |

---

**(b) A WSN with 600 sensor nodes uses data-centric naming. Only 75 nodes have relevant data for a user query. Each data packet is 128 bytes. Calculate: (i) total energy consumed by address-based query (all nodes respond), (ii) total energy consumed by name-based query (only relevant nodes respond), and (iii) percentage energy saved. Use $E_{tx} = E_{rx} = 50$ nJ/bit. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $N = 600$ (total nodes), $R = 75$ (relevant nodes)
- $L = 128$ bytes $= 1024$ bits
- $E_{tx} = E_{rx} = 50$ nJ/bit
- Assume distance negligible (only $E_{elec}$ contributes)

**Step 1 — Energy per packet (1 mark):**

$$E_{tx,1} = L \cdot E_{tx} = 1024 \cdot 50 = 51{,}200 \text{ nJ}$$

$$E_{rx,1} = L \cdot E_{rx} = 1024 \cdot 50 = 51{,}200 \text{ nJ}$$

**Step 2 — Address-based query: all 600 nodes respond (2 marks):**

$$E_{addr} = 1 \cdot E_{tx,1} \text{ (sink query)} + N \cdot (E_{tx,1} + E_{rx,1})$$

$$E_{addr} = 51{,}200 + 600 \cdot (51{,}200 + 51{,}200) = 51{,}200 + 600 \cdot 102{,}400$$

$$E_{addr} = 51{,}200 + 61{,}440{,}000 = 61{,}491{,}200 \text{ nJ} \approx 61.49 \text{ mJ}$$

**Step 3 — Name-based query: only 75 relevant nodes respond (2 marks):**

$$E_{name} = 1 \cdot E_{tx,1} + R \cdot (E_{tx,1} + E_{rx,1})$$

$$E_{name} = 51{,}200 + 75 \cdot 102{,}400 = 51{,}200 + 7{,}680{,}000 = 7{,}731{,}200 \text{ nJ} \approx 7.73 \text{ mJ}$$

**Step 4 — Percentage energy saved (2 marks):**

$$\% \text{ Saved} = \frac{E_{addr} - E_{name}}{E_{addr}} \times 100 = \frac{61.49 - 7.73}{61.49} \times 100$$

$$\% \text{ Saved} = \frac{53.76}{61.49} \times 100 \approx 87.4\%$$

**Conclusion:** Data-centric name management saves approximately **87.4%** of the energy compared to traditional address-based query — proving its superiority in WSN.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that COST MARKS in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Birthday Paradox formula confusion:** Students often use $P = 1 - e^{-k^2/N}$ instead of $1 - e^{-k^2/2N}$. The denominator is **2N**, not N. (Lose 1–2 marks)
> 2. **Confusing Address and Name:** Address = node identity; Name = data attribute. Examiners deduct marks if these are interchanged in definitions.
> 3. **Forgetting the sink's own transmission:** In energy calculations, always include the **1 packet** sent by the sink to initiate the query.
> 4. **Unit errors:** Convert bytes to bits (multiply by 8) and nJ to mJ (divide by 10⁶) **before** writing the final answer.
> 5. **Hierarchical address notation:** Write `<Cluster_ID>.<Node_ID>` clearly, not as a single number.
> 6. **Energy model terms:** Always use the **First Order Radio Model** — $E_{tx}(k,d) = kE_{elec} + kE_{amp}d^2$; ignoring $d^2$ term for long range loses marks.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Address Management** = uniquely identifying *nodes* (logical, geographic, hierarchical IDs).
- **Name Management** = uniquely identifying *data* via attribute-value pairs (data-centric).
- **Default WSN address length:** 16 bits → 65,536 possible nodes.
- **IEEE 802.15.4 address:** 64-bit EUI-64 for global uniqueness.
- **Birthday Paradox:** $P_{collision} = 1 - e^{-k^2/2N}$; for 50% collision, $k \approx 1.18\sqrt{N}$.
- For *N* = 2¹⁶, $k_{50\%} \approx 301$ nodes — beyond this, random addressing fails.
- **Cluster-based addressing (LEACH):** format = `<Cluster_ID>.<Node_ID>`.
- **SPIN Protocol:** 3-phase handshake = ADV → REQ → DATA (meta-data based naming).
- **Directed Diffusion:** Interest propagation + gradient setup + data delivery (data-centric).
- **Energy saving with name management:** typically 80–90% over address-based queries.
- **Data-Centric Storage** (e.g., GHT — Geographic Hash Table) maps attribute names to geographic locations.
- **First Order Radio Energy Model:** $E_{tx} = k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot E_{amp} \cdot d^2$; $E_{rx} = k \cdot E_{elec}$.
- KTU frequently asks: define, list, compare, or solve a numerical on the birthday paradox / energy saving.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
