topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT634 - WIRELESS & SENSORS NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Assignment of MAC Addresses

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal Academic Definition

**MAC Address Assignment** in the context of Wireless Sensor Networks (WSNs) refers to the systematic process of allocating unique identifier addresses to sensor nodes at the Data Link Layer (Layer 2) for unambiguous identification, medium access arbitration, and reliable packet delivery within the network. Unlike traditional Ethernet networks that use globally unique **48-bit IEEE 802 MAC addresses**, WSNs typically employ **short-address schemes (8-bit or 16-bit)** to conserve scarce energy, memory, and bandwidth resources.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Assignment of MAC addresses is a fundamental MAC layer sub-procedure. The address must be **globally unique within the WSN's broadcast domain** to prevent frame collisions, false acknowledgements, and routing table corruption. The chosen scheme directly impacts **energy consumption, scalability, and convergence time**.

## The Fundamental Problem Statement

In dense WSN deployments (often **hundreds to thousands of nodes** scattered randomly over a geographic area), three critical challenges arise:

1. **Manufacturer-assigned 48-bit addresses** are wasteful (transmitting 48 bits per packet header consumes unnecessary energy).
2. **Static pre-programmed addresses** cannot be guaranteed unique when nodes are mixed from different production batches.
3. **Random deployment** prevents manual configuration of node addresses.

Therefore, WSNs require an **automated address assignment protocol** that runs during or immediately after deployment.

## Conceptual Analogy / Intuition (Real-World View)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — Apartment Building & House Numbers:**
> Imagine a newly constructed apartment complex with **500 identical flats**. The builder cannot pre-paint flat numbers because he does not know which family will occupy which flat, and tenants arrive in random order. To solve this, a *supervisor* is appointed. When a family arrives, the supervisor asks: *"Is this flat number already taken?"* If not, he assigns it; if yes, he picks the next available number. This is exactly how **Dynamic Address Allocation (DAA)** works — a designated coordinator hands out unique addresses one by one as nodes "wake up" and request them.
>
> Alternative analogy — **Roll Numbers in a School Assembly:** A class teacher arranges students in a line and calls out roll numbers 1, 2, 3 ... in order. The first student gets 1, second gets 2, and so on. This is the **Sequential Allocation** model.

## Key Metrics Driving the Design

| Metric | Significance in WSNs |
|---|---|
| **Address Length** | Typically **8 bits (256 nodes)** or **16 bits (65,536 nodes)** |
| **Conflict Probability** | Must be $< 10^{-3}$ for reliable operation |
| **Convergence Time** | Time until every node has a unique address — must be minimal |
| **Communication Overhead** | Control packets consumed during assignment — must be minimal to save energy |
| **Storage Requirement** | Memory (in bytes) required per node — must fit in sensor's **4–10 KB RAM** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## Classification of MAC Address Assignment Schemes

The protocols are broadly classified along three orthogonal axes:

### 2.1 Based on Timing of Assignment
- **Pre-deployment Assignment** (Manufacturer-programmed or pre-loaded)
- **Post-deployment Assignment** (Negotiated at network bootstrap)

### 2.2 Based on Address Space Strategy
- **Deterministic Schemes** — Address derived from a structured formula (e.g., node's position in a tree)
- **Probabilistic Schemes** — Address randomly drawn from a large pool
- **Hybrid Schemes** — Combine both

### 2.3 Based on Control Centralization
- **Centralized** — A coordinator/cluster head assigns addresses
- **Distributed** — Nodes negotiate addresses among themselves
- **Hierarchical** — Multi-level coordinators for large networks

## Detailed Mechanism — The Three Dominant Schemes

### Scheme A: Tree-Based (Deterministic) Assignment

In tree-based schemes, addresses are derived from a node's **logical position** in a spanning/routing tree rooted at a sink.

- Each node is assigned an address that encodes its parent and its depth.
- Common in protocols like **LEACH, PEGASIS, and ZigBee (16-bit NWK address)**.
- A node's address is computed as:

$$\text{Address}(n) = f(\text{parent}(n), \text{depth}(n), \text{branch\_index}(n))$$

For ZigBee's distributed address assignment, a parent at depth $d$ with maximum children $C_m$, max routers $R_m$, and max depth $L_m$ computes the **Cskip(d)** offset:

$$C_{\text{skip}}(d) = \begin{cases} 1 + C_m \cdot (L_m - d - 1) & \text{if } R_m = 1 \\[4pt] \frac{1 + C_m - R_m - C_m \cdot R_m^{L_m - d - 1}}{1 - R_m} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

Each router child is allocated a contiguous block of $C_{\text{skip}}(d)$ addresses.

### Scheme B: Probabilistic / Random Assignment

Each node independently selects a random address from the address space $\mathbb{A} = \{0, 1, \ldots, 2^k - 1\}$, where $k$ is the address length.

The **birthday-paradox** probability of at least one conflict among $n$ nodes is:

$$P_{\text{conflict}}(n, k) = 1 - \prod_{i=0}^{n-1}\left(1 - \frac{i}{2^k}\right)$$

Using Stirling's approximation for small $p$, this simplifies to:

$$P_{\text{conflict}} \approx 1 - e^{-n(n-1)/(2^{k+1})}$$

### Scheme C: Dynamic Distributed Address Allocation (DAAP / DAA)

A **coordinator node** maintains an address pool. When a new node joins:
1. New node broadcasts an `ADDRESS_REQUEST` message.
2. Coordinator replies with the next available address.
3. Neighbor nodes confirm uniqueness by listening for address duplications.

If two coordinators assign the same address (partition scenario), a **conflict resolution phase** is triggered using random back-off and re-request.

## KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Parameter | Expression | Use / Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Address space size | $N_{\text{addr}} = 2^k$ | Total distinct addresses available |
| Birthday conflict probability | $P \approx 1 - e^{-n^2/2^{k+1}}$ | Probability of at least one duplicate |
| Required address bits | $k \geq \lceil \log_2 n + \log_2(1/p_{\max}) \cdot 2 \rceil$ | Bits required for $n$ nodes, target conflict $p_{\max}$ |
| Overhead in bytes | $O_{\text{ctrl}} = n \cdot (H_{\text{req}} + H_{\text{reply}})$ | Control-packet overhead |
| ZigBee Cskip (general case) | $C_{\text{skip}}(d) = \frac{1 + C_m - R_m - C_m \cdot R_m^{L_m-d-1}}{1 - R_m}$ | Block size per router at depth $d$ |
| Convergence time | $T_{\text{conv}} \approx 2 \cdot T_{\text{prop}} \cdot h$ | $h$ = tree height, $T_{\text{prop}}$ = one-hop delay |
| Energy per assignment | $E_{\text{assign}} = V \cdot I \cdot T_{\text{tx}} \cdot (H_{\text{req}} + H_{\text{reply}})$ | Joules consumed by one node |
| Mean free-address pool | $\mu = N_{\text{addr}} - n$ | Expected unassigned addresses |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conflict-Probability Rule of Thumb (KTU Quick-Solve):** For $n$ nodes with $k$-bit addresses, the conflict probability is negligible ($\approx 10^{-6}$) only when $2^k \gg n^2$, i.e., $k \geq 2 \log_2 n + 4$.

## Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Industrial IoT & ZigBee Mesh Networks**: Tree-based assignment is the de-facto standard (e.g., Philips Hue smart lighting hubs manage up to **50,000 devices**).
- **Smart Agriculture & Environmental Monitoring**: Random-address schemes suit low-cost, throw-away deployments where conflict resolution is acceptable.
- **Body Area Networks (BANs)**: Hybrid schemes balance strict uniqueness with low energy budget on implantable sensors.
- **Logistics & Cold-Chain Tracking**: Centralized DAA used in container-tracking sensor pods at seaports.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## Derivation 1: Minimum Address Length for a Given Conflict Tolerance

### Problem Setup
Given $n$ nodes to be assigned $k$-bit addresses uniformly at random, derive the minimum $k$ such that $P_{\text{conflict}} \leq p_{\max}$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 —** Start with the exact probability that all $n$ addresses are unique:

$$P_{\text{unique}} = \prod_{i=0}^{n-1}\left(1 - \frac{i}{2^k}\right)$$

**Step 2 —** Take the natural logarithm of both sides. This converts the product into a sum (easier to handle):

$$\ln(P_{\text{unique}}) = \sum_{i=0}^{n-1}\ln\left(1 - \frac{i}{2^k}\right)$$

**Step 3 —** Use the inequality $\ln(1 - x) \leq -x$ (valid for $0 \leq x < 1$) to obtain an upper bound:

$$\ln(P_{\text{unique}}) \leq -\sum_{i=0}^{n-1}\frac{i}{2^k} = -\frac{1}{2^k}\cdot\frac{n(n-1)}{2}$$

**Step 4 —** Exponentiate both sides to recover the probability bound:

$$P_{\text{unique}} \leq e^{-n(n-1)/2^{k+1}}$$

**Step 5 —** Therefore the conflict probability satisfies:

$$P_{\text{conflict}} = 1 - P_{\text{unique}} \geq 1 - e^{-n(n-1)/2^{k+1}}$$

**Step 6 —** To enforce $P_{\text{conflict}} \leq p_{\max}$, we require:

$$1 - e^{-n(n-1)/2^{k+1}} \leq p_{\max}$$

$$e^{-n(n-1)/2^{k+1}} \geq 1 - p_{\max}$$

**Step 7 —** Taking the natural logarithm (note: $\ln(1-p_{\max}) \approx -p_{\max}$ for small $p_{\max}$):

$$-\frac{n(n-1)}{2^{k+1}} \geq \ln(1 - p_{\max})$$

$$\frac{n(n-1)}{2^{k+1}} \leq -\ln(1 - p_{\max})$$

**Step 8 —** Solve for $2^{k+1}$:

$$2^{k+1} \geq \frac{n(n-1)}{-\ln(1 - p_{\max})}$$

**Step 9 —** Apply $\log_2$ to isolate $k$:

$$k + 1 \geq \log_2\left[\frac{n(n-1)}{-\ln(1 - p_{\max})}\right]$$

**Step 10 —** Final closed-form result:

$$\boxed{\,k_{\min} = \left\lceil \log_2\left[\frac{n(n-1)}{-\ln(1 - p_{\max})}\right] \right\rceil - 1\,}$$

### Numerical Verification Example

Suppose $n = 100$ nodes and we want $p_{\max} = 10^{-3}$.

$$-\ln(1 - 10^{-3}) \approx 1.0005 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\frac{n(n-1)}{-\ln(1-p_{\max})} = \frac{100 \cdot 99}{1.0005 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 9.895 \times 10^{6}$$

$$\log_2(9.895 \times 10^{6}) \approx 23.24$$

$$k_{\min} = \lceil 23.24 \rceil - 1 = 24 - 1 = 23 \text{ bits}$$

So a **24-bit address space** safely accommodates 100 nodes with at most 0.1% conflict risk.

---

## Derivation 2: Energy Overhead for a DAA Protocol

### Problem Setup
A WSN node has a radio with TX current $I_{\text{tx}} = 17$ mA, RX current $I_{\text{rx}} = 19$ mA, and supply voltage $V = 3$ V. During assignment, each node transmits a 10-byte `ADDRESS_REQUEST` and receives a 10-byte `REPLY`. Estimate the total energy consumed per node.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 —** Time to transmit one byte at $250$ kbps (IEEE 802.15.4 O-QPSK PHY):

$$t_{\text{byte}} = \frac{8 \text{ bits}}{250 \times 10^3 \text{ bits/s}} = 32 \,\mu\text{s/byte}$$

**Step 2 —** Total transmission time for a 10-byte packet:

$$T_{\text{tx}} = 10 \cdot 32\,\mu\text{s} = 320\,\mu\text{s}$$

**Step 3 —** Total reception time (assuming symmetric rate):

$$T_{\text{rx}} = 320\,\mu\text{s}$$

**Step 4 —** Energy for transmission:

$$E_{\text{tx}} = V \cdot I_{\text{tx}} \cdot T_{\text{tx}} = 3 \cdot 17 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 320 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$E_{\text{tx}} = 3 \cdot 17 \cdot 320 \times 10^{-9} = 16.32 \,\mu\text{J}$$

**Step 5 —** Energy for reception:

$$E_{\text{rx}} = V \cdot I_{\text{rx}} \cdot T_{\text{rx}} = 3 \cdot 19 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 320 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$E_{\text{rx}} = 3 \cdot 19 \cdot 320 \times 10^{-9} = 18.24 \,\mu\text{J}$$

**Step 6 —** Total energy per node for address acquisition:

$$\boxed{\,E_{\text{total}} = E_{\text{tx}} + E_{\text{rx}} = 16.32 + 18.24 = 34.56 \,\mu\text{J}\,}$$

**Step 7 —** If we assume two such rounds (initial request + conflict-resolution retry on average), per-node energy is doubled to $\approx 69\,\mu\text{J}$, negligible compared to the **~ 5 J** total energy budget of a typical 2×AA-powered sensor mote.

---

## Comparative Analytical Matrix of the Three Schemes

| Parameter | Tree-Based (Deterministic) | Probabilistic (Random) | Dynamic DAA (Centralized) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Address uniqueness | **Guaranteed** (mathematically) | Probabilistic (depends on $k$) | **Guaranteed** after handshake |
| Communication overhead | Low (parent–child only) | **Very low** (no coordination) | High (request/reply per node) |
| Storage requirement | Stores only $C_{\text{skip}}(d)$ | None | Stores neighbor table |
| Convergence time | $O(h)$ tree levels | $O(1)$ (parallel) | $O(n)$ sequential |
| Failure tolerance | Poor (root failure) | Excellent (no central node) | Poor (coordinator failure) |
| Scalability | Limited by tree depth | Excellent | Limited by coordinator load |
| Suitability for mobile WSNs | Poor | **Excellent** | Poor |
| Typical address length | 16 bits (ZigBee) | 32–64 bits (to keep $P_{\text{conflict}}$ low) | 8–16 bits |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## Diagram 1: Functional Architecture of a Generic MAC Address Assignment Protocol

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Node Bootstraps] --> B{Has Pre-Assigned Address?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Use Pre-Loaded Address]
    B -- No --> D[Enter Discovery Phase]
    D --> E[Broadcast ADDRESS_REQUEST]
    E --> F{Coordinator Within Range?}
    F -- No --> G[Listen for Neighbour JOINs]
    G --> H[Negotiate with Peer Nodes]
    H --> I[Select Unique Address Randomly]
    I --> J[Enter Conflict-Detection Phase]
    J --> K{Duplicate Detected?}
    K -- Yes --> L[Random Back-off and Re-select]
    L --> I
    K -- No --> M[Lock Address and Announce]
    F -- Yes --> N[Send Request to Coordinator]
    N --> O[Receive Assigned Address]
    O --> P[Confirm with Neighbours]
    P --> Q{Confirmation OK?}
    Q -- Yes --> M
    Q -- No --> N
    M --> R[Node Operational]
    C --> R
```

## Diagram 2: Tree-Based Address Allocation (ZigBee-style Cskip Scheme)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Root[Sink Node Address 0x0000 Depth 0] --> A1[Router A Address 0x0001 Depth 1]
    Root --> A2[Router B Address 0x0001 + Cskip 0 Depth 1]
    A1 --> A1a[End Device Address 0x0002]
    A1 --> A1b[Router C Address 0x0003 Depth 2]
    A1b --> A1b1[End Device Address 0x0004]
    A1b --> A1b2[End Device Address 0x0005]
    A2 --> A2a[End Device Address 0x0001 + Cskip 0 + 1]
    A2 --> A2b[End Device Address 0x0001 + Cskip 0 + 2]
```

**Reading the diagram:** Each router parent allocates a contiguous address block of size `Cskip(d)` to its descendants. End devices get individual addresses within this block. The addressing formula is deterministic and known to all nodes, so no explicit signalling is required.

## Diagram 3: Probabilistic Assignment — Conflict Probability vs. Address Space

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Inputs
        N1[Number of Nodes n]
        K1[Address Bits k]
    end
    N1 --> Calc[Compute P conflict]
    K1 --> Calc
    Calc --> Decision{P conflict less than 10 power minus 3}
    Decision -- Yes --> Accept[Address Length Acceptable]
    Decision -- No --> Increase[Increase k and Re-evaluate]
```

## Diagram 4: DAA Message Exchange Sequence

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant New as New Node
    participant Coord as Coordinator
    participant Neigh as Neighbour Nodes
    New ->> Coord: ADDRESS_REQUEST (sender_ID, hop_count)
    Coord ->> New: ADDRESS_REPLY (assigned_address)
    New ->> Neigh: ANNOUNCE (new_address)
    Neigh ->> New: ACK or DUPLICATE_WARNING
    alt Duplicate detected
        New ->> Coord: ADDRESS_REQUEST (retry)
    else No duplicate
        New ->> New: Lock address in NVM
    end
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks = 6 Marks)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]
**Q: List any three advantages of using short MAC addresses (16-bit) instead of 48-bit IEEE MAC addresses in a Wireless Sensor Network.**

**Model Answer (3 key points):**
1. **Lower Energy Consumption:** A 16-bit header halves the energy spent on every transmitted packet compared to a 48-bit header. For a node transmitting 1000 packets/day, this translates to several mJ saved daily.
2. **Reduced Memory Footprint:** Short addresses fit easily in the limited RAM (typically 4–10 KB) of sensor motes, freeing space for application buffers.
3. **Faster Address Lookup:** Hash tables and routing caches operate faster on shorter keys, reducing per-packet processing latency and CPU wake-time.

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]
**Q: Define the term "address conflict" in the context of WSN MAC address assignment. State the mathematical expression for the probability of conflict under a probabilistic (random) assignment scheme.**

**Model Answer:**
- **Definition:** An address conflict occurs when two or more sensor nodes within the same broadcast domain are assigned the **same MAC address**, leading to ambiguous frame delivery, false ACKs, and routing instability.
- **Expression:** For $n$ nodes each picking a $k$-bit random address independently, the conflict probability is:

$$P_{\text{conflict}} \approx 1 - e^{-n(n-1)/2^{k+1}}$$

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Adapted]

**(a)** Explain the **Tree-based (deterministic) MAC address assignment scheme** used in WSNs. Derive the **Cskip(d)** formula for the ZigBee distributed address assignment scheme and describe how each router parent allocates address blocks to its children. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A WSN is organized as a tree rooted at the sink. The maximum number of children $C_m = 7$, the maximum number of routers $R_m = 4$, and the maximum depth $L_m = 5$. Compute the address block size $C_{\text{skip}}(d)$ for depths $d = 0, 1, 2$. Hence determine how many end devices a router at depth 2 can directly address. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Explanation & Derivation**

In tree-based assignment, every node's address is mathematically derived from its position in the spanning tree. The ZigBee scheme is the canonical example, where each router at depth $d$ owns a contiguous block of $C_{\text{skip}}(d)$ addresses that it can hand out to its descendants.

The general derivation proceeds as follows:

- Let $A_{\text{parent}}$ be the parent's address. The first router child gets $A_{\text{parent}} + 1$, the second gets $A_{\text{parent}} + 1 + C_{\text{skip}}(d+1)$, and so on.
- Each parent at depth $d$ must reserve:
  - **1 address** for itself.
  - **$C_m - R_m$** addresses for its non-router (end-device) children.
  - **$R_m$** sub-blocks, each of size $C_{\text{skip}}(d+1)$, for its router children.

Therefore the recurrence relation is:

$$C_{\text{skip}}(d) = (1 + C_m - R_m) + R_m \cdot C_{\text{skip}}(d+1)$$

Applying the boundary condition $C_{\text{skip}}(L_m) = 1 + C_m - R_m$ (no further routers below max depth) and solving the geometric series yields:

$$C_{\text{skip}}(d) = \frac{1 + C_m - R_m - C_m \cdot R_m^{L_m - d - 1}}{1 - R_m}$$

For the special case $R_m = 1$ (only one router child per parent), this simplifies to:

$$C_{\text{skip}}(d) = 1 + C_m (L_m - d - 1)$$

**Part (b) — Numerical Computation**

Given: $C_m = 7$, $R_m = 4$, $L_m = 5$.

**At depth $d = 0$:**

$$C_{\text{skip}}(0) = \frac{1 + 7 - 4 - 7 \cdot 4^{5 - 0 - 1}}{1 - 4} = \frac{4 - 7 \cdot 256}{-3} = \frac{4 - 1792}{-3} = \frac{-1788}{-3} = 596$$

**At depth $d = 1$:**

$$C_{\text{skip}}(1) = \frac{4 - 7 \cdot 4^{3}}{-3} = \frac{4 - 448}{-3} = \frac{-444}{-3} = 148$$

**At depth $d = 2$:**

$$C_{\text{skip}}(2) = \frac{4 - 7 \cdot 4^{2}}{-3} = \frac{4 - 112}{-3} = \frac{-108}{-3} = 36$$

**Final Answer:** A router at depth 2 can directly support **36 end devices** (i.e., it has a 36-address block, of which one is its own address and the remainder are distributed among children).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2023, Adapted]

**(a)** Describe the **Dynamic Address Allocation (DAA)** protocol for WSNs. Explain the roles of the coordinator, the message exchange, and the conflict-resolution mechanism. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A WSN has $n = 250$ nodes to be assigned using a **probabilistic scheme**. If the target maximum conflict probability is $p_{\max} = 10^{-4}$, determine the minimum number of address bits $k$ required. Justify with the birthday-paradox formula. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — DAA Protocol Description**

The Dynamic Address Allocation (DAA) protocol operates as follows:

1. **Coordinator Election:** After deployment, one node (usually the sink or the node with the lowest remaining ID) is elected as the **coordinator**. It maintains a free-address pool.
2. **Request Phase:** A new node (joining or freshly bootstrapped) broadcasts an `ADDRESS_REQUEST` message containing its unique hardware ID (e.g., 64-bit factory ID).
3. **Reply Phase:** The coordinator picks the lowest free address from its pool and unicasts an `ADDRESS_REPLY` back.
4. **Announcement Phase:** The new node broadcasts an `ANNOUNCE` containing its newly acquired address.
5. **Conflict Resolution:** If two partitions of the network each elect a different coordinator, the same address may be assigned twice. To detect this, neighbour nodes maintain an **address table** and report any duplicate. Upon detection, the newer node performs a random back-off and re-issues an `ADDRESS_REQUEST`.
6. **Persistence:** Once locked, the address is written to **non-volatile memory (NVM/EEPROM)** so that node reboots retain the same address.

**Part (b) — Numerical Computation**

We use the derived formula:

$$k_{\min} = \left\lceil \log_2\left[\frac{n(n-1)}{-\ln(1 - p_{\max})}\right] \right\rceil - 1$$

**Step 1 —** Compute numerator:

$$n(n-1) = 250 \cdot 249 = 62{,}250$$

**Step 2 —** Compute denominator:

$$-\ln(1 - 10^{-4}) \approx 1.00005 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 3 —** Compute the ratio:

$$\frac{62{,}250}{1.00005 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 6.2246 \times 10^{8}$$

**Step 4 —** Take $\log_2$:

$$\log_2(6.2246 \times 10^{8}) = \log_2(6.2246) + \log_2(10^8)$$

$$= 2.638 + 8 \cdot \log_2(10) = 2.638 + 8 \cdot 3.3219 = 2.638 + 26.575 = 29.213$$

**Step 5 —** Apply ceiling and subtract 1:

$$k_{\min} = \lceil 29.213 \rceil - 1 = 30 - 1 = 29 \text{ bits}$$

**Final Answer:** A minimum of **29-bit address space** (offering $2^{29} = 536{,}870{,}912$ unique addresses) is required for 250 nodes with a conflict probability not exceeding $10^{-4}$. In practice, designers round this up to **32 bits** for byte-alignment and protocol simplicity.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign error in Cskip formula:** Students frequently forget the minus sign in the denominator $(1 - R_m)$ and get a negative block size, losing 2 marks.
> 2. **Confusing $n$ with $2^k$ in birthday paradox:** The conflict formula uses $n$ (number of nodes), not $2^k$ (address space size). Mixing these up loses 3 marks.
> 3. **Skipping the boundary condition:** In Cskip derivation, failing to state $C_{\text{skip}}(L_m) = 1 + C_m - R_m$ loses 1 mark.
> 4. **Using 48-bit IEEE addresses in WSN design questions:** Examiners deduct 1 mark for ignoring the energy and memory constraints of WSNs.
> 5. **Forgetting the back-off mechanism in DAA:** Conflict resolution is mandatory in the description; mentioning only request/reply loses 2 marks.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ MAC address assignment in WSNs typically uses **short (8/16/32-bit)** addresses, not the 48-bit IEEE standard, to save energy and memory.
- ✅ Three main schemes: **Tree-based (deterministic)**, **Probabilistic (random)**, and **Dynamic/Coordinated (DAA/DAAP)**.
- ✅ ZigBee's **Cskip(d)** formula governs hierarchical address block allocation; it requires the parameters $C_m$, $R_m$, and $L_m$.
- ✅ Birthday-paradox conflict probability: $P \approx 1 - e^{-n^2/2^{k+1}}$; rule of thumb: $k \geq 2\log_2 n + 4$ for $P < 10^{-3}$.
- ✅ Tree-based: **guaranteed uniqueness**, low overhead, but vulnerable to root failure.
- ✅ Probabilistic: **no coordination needed**, excellent scalability, but requires larger address space.
- ✅ DAA: **guaranteed uniqueness** + small address space, but high overhead and single point of failure (coordinator).
- ✅ A complete DAA protocol includes **request → reply → announce → conflict-detection → back-off retry → NVM lock** phases.
- ✅ Energy consumed by one address assignment is on the order of **tens of microjoules**, negligible against the joule-scale node budget.
- ✅ Always state the **assumptions** (radio model, packet size, voltage) clearly when solving numerical problems.
- ✅ Address conflict probability must be calculated against the **network diameter / broadcast domain**, not the entire deployment area.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Routing Protocols
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT634 - WIRELESS & SENSORS NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Routing Protocols

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Routing in Wireless Sensor Networks (WSN)** is the process of selecting paths in a multi-hop sensor network along which data packets (sensed information) are forwarded from source sensor nodes to a designated sink (Base Station / Gateway), while simultaneously satisfying application-specific Quality of Service (QoS) requirements and minimizing resource consumption (energy, bandwidth, memory).

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, a **Routing Protocol** for WSN is a set of rules and algorithms that govern:
1. **Path Establishment** – how routes between nodes and sink are discovered.
2. **Data Forwarding** – how packets traverse the network.
3. **Route Maintenance** – how broken or suboptimal routes are repaired.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Unlike traditional IP routing (Internet), WSN routing is **data-centric**, **application-aware**, and **energy-driven**. There are no global IDs assigned to nodes.

## 1.2 Why Traditional Routing Fails in WSN

| Traditional Network (Internet) | Wireless Sensor Network |
| :--- | :--- |
| Stable topology, high bandwidth | Dynamic topology, low bandwidth |
| Abundant energy (mains powered) | **Scarce energy** (battery powered) |
| Unique IP address per node | No unique IDs, address-centric protocols fail |
| Optimizes delay & throughput | **Optimizes energy & lifetime** |
| Hundreds of nodes | **Thousands of densely deployed nodes** |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy – The Postal System in a Dark Forest**
>
> Imagine a **dark forest** where 10,000 lost people (sensor nodes) each hold a small piece of evidence (data) about a hidden treasure. They have **limited torch batteries** (energy) and **no global map**. They must relay their evidence to a single police station at the forest's edge (Base Station).
>
> - They cannot all shout at once (bandwidth limit).
> - They cannot individually walk to the station (energy limit).
> - Some become "team leaders" (Cluster Heads) to aggregate messages.
> - Some pass messages hop-by-hop using the loudest neighbor nearby.
>
> This collaborative, energy-thrifty, multi-hop relay strategy is exactly what a **WSN Routing Protocol** does.

## 1.4 Key Design Challenges in WSN Routing

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The four pillars of WSN routing design:
> 1. **Energy Efficiency** – Transmission consumes the most energy.
> 2. **Scalability** – Protocol must handle 1,000+ nodes.
> 3. **Data Aggregation / Fusion** – Combine redundant data en-route.
> 4. **Fault Tolerance** – Node/link failures must not collapse the network.

**Physical / Standard Metrics (per IEEE 802.15.4 stack):**
- Transmit Energy: $\approx$ **50 nJ/bit**
- Receive Energy: $\approx$ **50 nJ/bit**
- Aggregation Energy: $\approx$ **5 nJ/bit** (1000x cheaper than transmission)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of WSN Routing Protocols

The KTU 2024 syllabus categorizes routing protocols along **four axes**:

### A. Network Structure Based
1. **Flat-based routing** – All nodes play equal role (e.g., SPIN, Directed Diffusion).
2. **Hierarchical (Cluster-based) routing** – Nodes form clusters with a Cluster Head (CH) (e.g., LEACH, PEGASIS, TEEN, APTEEN).
3. **Location-based routing** – Position information of nodes is used (e.g., GEAR, GAF).

### B. Protocol Operation Based
1. **Proactive (Table-driven)** – Routes maintained continuously (e.g., DSDV – rare in WSN).
2. **Reactive (On-demand)** – Routes created on demand (e.g., AODV – adapted).
3. **Hybrid** – Combination of both (e.g., ZRP – Zone Routing Protocol).
4. **Negotiation-based** – Use metadata negotiation (e.g., SPIN).
5. **Query-based** – Sink queries the network (e.g., Directed Diffusion).
6. **Multipath-based** – Multiple paths maintained for reliability (e.g., REAR).

### C. Application Specific
- **Data-centric** – Routing based on attribute-value pairs.
- **Event-driven** – Activated only when an event occurs.
- **Continuous** – Periodic data flow.

## 2.2 Detailed Working of Major Protocols

### 2.2.1 SPIN (Sensor Protocol for Information via Negotiation)
- Family: **Negotiation-based / Data-centric**
- Solves the **implosion** and **overlap** problem of flooding.
- Uses 3 types of messages: **ADV** (advertisement), **REQ** (request), **DATA**.
- **Meta-data negotiation** ensures only interested nodes request data.

### 2.2.2 Directed Diffusion (DD)
- Family: **Data-centric / Query-based**
- Sink broadcasts **interests** (named data description).
- Gradients are set up from source back to sink.
- **Reinforcement** of the best path via low-latency route.
- Data sent only when interest matches.

### 2.2.3 LEACH (Low-Energy Adaptive Clustering Hierarchy)
- Family: **Hierarchical / Cluster-based**
- Most popular and frequently asked in KTU exams.
- **Two phases**: Set-up phase and Steady-state phase.
- Uses **randomized rotation of CHs** to balance energy.

### 2.2.4 PEGASIS (Power-Efficient GAthering in Sensor Information Systems)
- Family: **Hierarchical / Chain-based**
- Nodes form a **greedy chain**.
- Each node communicates only with a close neighbor.
- Only **one node (leader)** transmits to the BS per round.
- Energy savings over LEACH: $\approx$ **1.5x to 3x**.

### 2.2.5 TEEN & APTEEN
- Family: **Hierarchical / Reactive**
- **TEEN** (Threshold-sensitive Energy Efficient sensor Network) responds only when:
  - **Hard Threshold (HT)** – sensed value crosses a critical limit.
  - **Soft Threshold (ST)** – small change in sensed value.
- **APTEEN** adds periodic reporting capability.

### 2.2.6 GEAR (Geographic and Energy Aware Routing)
- Family: **Location-based**
- Uses **residual energy** AND **distance to destination** as cost function.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter / Formula | Symbol | Unit | Purpose |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | First Order Radio Model – Tx Energy | $E_{TX}(k,d) = k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \epsilon_{fs} \cdot d^{2}$ | Joule/bit | Transmitter cost (free space) |
| 2 | First Order Radio Model – Tx Energy (multipath) | $E_{TX}(k,d) = k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \epsilon_{mp} \cdot d^{4}$ | Joule/bit | Transmitter cost (long distance) |
| 3 | First Order Radio Model – Rx Energy | $E_{RX}(k) = k \cdot E_{elec}$ | Joule/bit | Receiver cost |
| 4 | Critical distance (d_crossover) | $d_{crossover} = \sqrt{\epsilon_{fs}/\epsilon_{mp}}$ | meter | Switch between fs and mp models |
| 5 | LEACH Optimal Cluster Head Probability | $p_{opt} = \sqrt{N \cdot k / (2 \cdot \pi)} \cdot M \cdot d^{-2}_{toBS}$ | dimensionless | Proportion of CHs |
| 6 | LEACH Cluster Count | $k_{opt} \approx \sqrt{N / (2\pi)} \cdot \sqrt{\epsilon_{fs}/\epsilon_{mp}} \cdot M \cdot d^{-2}_{toBS}$ | count | Number of clusters |
| 7 | Average Energy per Round (LEACH) | $E_{round} = k \cdot (2N - k) \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot E_{DA} + \dots$ | Joule | Total dissipation |
| 8 | Network Lifetime | $L = E_{total} / E_{per\_round}$ | Rounds | Operational duration |
| 9 | Hop distance for uniform deployment | $E[hop] = 0.5 \cdot \sqrt{Area / N}$ | meter | Mean distance between nodes |
| 10 | GEAR cost function | $c(N_i, R) = \alpha \cdot d(N_i, R) + (1-\alpha) \cdot e(N_i)$ | weighted units | Geographic + Energy Aware |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard values** in KTU textbook problems:
> - $E_{elec} = 50$ nJ/bit
> - $\epsilon_{fs} = 10$ pJ/bit/m²
> - $\epsilon_{mp} = 0.0013$ pJ/bit/m⁴
> - $E_{DA} = 5$ nJ/bit/signal

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **LEACH** is the foundation of **smart agriculture soil monitoring** (cluster of fields).
- **SPIN/PEGASIS** is used in **structural health monitoring** of bridges.
- **TEEN/APTEEN** powers **forest fire detection** (event-driven, saves energy).
- **Directed Diffusion** inspires modern **publish-subscribe IoT architectures** (e.g., MQTT, CoAP).
- **GEAR** is the building block of **vehicular ad-hoc networks (VANETs)** and **drone swarms**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: First Order Radio Energy Model (Heinzelman et al.)

This is the most asked derivation in KTU Module 3 questions.

### Assumptions
- $k$ = number of bits in the packet.
- $d$ = distance between transmitter and receiver.
- $E_{elec}$ = energy dissipated by transmitter/receiver electronics (per bit).
- $\epsilon_{fs}$ = amplifier energy in free space.
- $\epsilon_{mp}$ = amplifier energy in multipath fading.
- $d^2$ power loss: **free space**; $d^4$ power loss: **multipath**.

### Step 1 – Transmitter Energy

The transmitter must run the **radio electronics** and the **power amplifier**.

$$E_{TX}(k, d) = E_{TX-elec}(k) + E_{TX-amp}(k, d)$$

Substituting the per-bit energy model:

$$E_{TX}(k, d) = k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \epsilon_{amp} \cdot d^{n}$$

where $n = 2$ for free space and $n = 4$ for multipath.

For **short distance** ($d < d_{crossover}$), we use the free space model:

$$E_{TX}(k, d) = k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \epsilon_{fs} \cdot d^{2}$$

For **long distance** ($d \geq d_{crossover}$), we use the multipath model:

$$E_{TX}(k, d) = k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \epsilon_{mp} \cdot d^{4}$$

### Step 2 – Receiver Energy

The receiver only needs to run the radio electronics (no amplifier):

$$E_{RX}(k) = k \cdot E_{elec}$$

### Step 3 – Determining the Crossover Distance

Set free-space energy = multipath energy to find the boundary point:

$$k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \epsilon_{fs} \cdot d^{2} = k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \epsilon_{mp} \cdot d^{4}$$

Cancel $k$ and $E_{elec}$ on both sides:

$$\epsilon_{fs} \cdot d^{2} = \epsilon_{mp} \cdot d^{4}$$

$$d^{2} = \frac{\epsilon_{fs}}{\epsilon_{mp}}$$

$$d_{crossover} = \sqrt{\frac{\epsilon_{fs}}{\epsilon_{mp}}}$$

**Numerical Example (Standard KTU values):**
$\epsilon_{fs} = 10 \times 10^{-12}$, $\epsilon_{mp} = 0.0013 \times 10^{-12}$.

$$d_{crossover} = \sqrt{\frac{10 \times 10^{-12}}{0.0013 \times 10^{-12}}} = \sqrt{7692.3} \approx 87.7 \text{ m}$$

## 3.2 Derivation: LEACH Optimal Number of Clusters

### Step 1 – Energy Dissipated by a CH per Frame
A CH receives data from all its members, aggregates it, and transmits to BS.

$$E_{CH} = \frac{N}{k} \cdot k \cdot E_{elec} + \frac{N}{k} \cdot k \cdot E_{DA} + k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \epsilon_{mp} \cdot d^{4}_{toBS}$$

Simplify by combining $\frac{N}{k} \cdot k = N$:

$$E_{CH} = N \cdot E_{elec} + N \cdot E_{DA} + k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \epsilon_{mp} \cdot d^{4}_{toBS}$$

### Step 2 – Energy Dissipated by a Non-CH Member
A non-CH transmits $k$ bits to the CH at distance $d_{toCH}$.

$$E_{nonCH} = k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \epsilon_{fs} \cdot d^{2}_{toCH}$$

### Step 3 – Energy per Cluster
Total energy per cluster = energy of CH + energy of all $\frac{N}{k} - 1$ non-CH nodes:

$$E_{cluster} = E_{CH} + \left(\frac{N}{k} - 1\right) \cdot E_{nonCH}$$

For $N \gg k$, $\frac{N}{k} - 1 \approx \frac{N}{k}$:

$$E_{cluster} \approx E_{CH} + \frac{N}{k} \cdot E_{nonCH}$$

### Step 4 – Total Network Energy per Round
$$E_{total} = k \cdot E_{cluster}$$

$$E_{total} = k \cdot \left[ N \cdot E_{elec} + N \cdot E_{DA} + k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \epsilon_{mp} \cdot d^{4}_{toBS} \right] + k \cdot \frac{N}{k} \cdot \left[k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \epsilon_{fs} \cdot d^{2}_{toCH}\right]$$

### Step 5 – Differentiation w.r.t. $k$ (Optimal Cluster Count)
Assume uniform deployment so $E[d^{2}_{toCH}]$ is a constant. Differentiate $E_{total}$ with respect to $k$ and equate to zero:

$$\frac{d(E_{total})}{dk} = 0$$

After algebraic reduction (standard result from Heinzelman's LEACH paper):

$$k_{opt} = \sqrt{\frac{N}{2\pi}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\epsilon_{fs}}{\epsilon_{mp}}} \cdot \frac{M}{d^{2}_{toBS}}$$

where $M$ is the side length of the square deployment area.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This formula is **directly asked** in KTU University Exam 14-mark questions with numerical substitution.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU High-Yield)

| Feature | SPIN | Directed Diffusion | LEACH | PEGASIS | TEEN |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Topology | Flat | Flat | Hierarchical | Chain | Hierarchical |
| Data-Centric | Yes | Yes (strong) | No | No | No |
| Energy Efficient | Moderate | Moderate | High | **Very High** | High |
| Data Aggregation | No | No | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| Latency | Low | Medium | Medium | **High** (chain delay) | **Lowest** (event-driven) |
| Mobility Support | Limited | Limited | No | No | No |
| Query-based | No | **Yes** | No | No | No |
| Scalability | Good | Medium | **Good** | **Excellent** | Good |
| Best For | Metadata-heavy apps | Sink-initiated queries | Periodic monitoring | Long-lifetime apps | Critical event detection |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Classification Tree of WSN Routing Protocols

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[WSN Routing Protocols] --> B[Network Structure Based]
    A --> C[Protocol Operation Based]
    A --> D[Application Based]

    B --> B1[Flat-based]
    B --> B2[Hierarchical]
    B --> B3[Location-based]

    B1 --> B1a[SPIN]
    B1 --> B1b[Directed Diffusion]

    B2 --> B2a[LEACH]
    B2 --> B2b[PEGASIS]
    B2 --> B2c[TEEN]
    B2 --> B2d[APTEEN]

    B3 --> B3a[GEAR]
    B3 --> B3b[GAF]

    C --> C1[Proactive]
    C --> C2[Reactive]
    C --> C3[Hybrid]
    C --> C4[Negotiation based]
    C --> C5[Query based]
    C --> C6[Multipath based]

    D --> D1[Data Centric]
    D --> D2[Event Driven]
    D --> D3[Continuous]
```

## 4.2 LEACH Operational Flow (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
graph TD
    S[Start of Round r] --> P[Set-up Phase]
    P --> P1[Each node generates random number 0 to 1]
    P1 --> P2{Threshold T n check}
    P2 --> P3[Node becomes Cluster Head CH]
    P2 --> P4[Node becomes Member]
    P3 --> P5[CH broadcasts ADV message]
    P4 --> P6[Member joins nearest CH]
    P5 --> P7[CH creates TDMA schedule]
    P6 --> P7
    P7 --> ST[Steady State Phase]
    ST --> ST1[Member senses and transmits to CH in allocated slot]
    ST1 --> ST2[CH aggregates data]
    ST2 --> ST3[CH transmits aggregated data to Base Station]
    ST3 --> ST4{Network Energy left}
    ST4 --> P5
    ST4 --> E[End of Network Life]
```

## 4.3 Directed Diffusion Data Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    SINK[Base Station Sink] --> S1[Sink broadcasts Interest with attribute gradients]
    S1 --> N1[Sensor Node 1 sets up gradient to Sink]
    S1 --> N2[Sensor Node 2 sets up gradient to Sink]
    S1 --> N3[Sensor Node 3 sets up gradient to Sink]
    N1 --> R1[Node 1 matches interest and sends exploratory data]
    N2 --> R2[Node 2 matches and sends exploratory data]
    N3 --> R3[Node 3 matches and sends exploratory data]
    R1 --> SINK
    R2 --> SINK
    R3 --> SINK
    SINK --> RF[Sink reinforces best low latency path]
    RF --> S2[Nodes send data only along reinforced path]
```

## 4.4 PEGASIS Chain Formation

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Node 1] --> B[Node 2]
    B --> C[Node 3]
    C --> D[Node 4 Leader]
    D --> E[Node 5]
    D --> BS[Base Station]
    E --> F[Node 6]
    F --> A
    D --> BS
```

> [!NOTE]
> In PEGASIS, the **leader rotates** each round. Each node fuses its own data with the data received from its neighbor, and the **leader** finally transmits **one** aggregated packet to the BS. Only **one transmission per round** to the BS yields the highest energy savings.

## 4.5 Hierarchical Cluster Topology (LEACH Architecture)

```mermaid
graph TD
    BS[Base Station]
    CH1[Cluster Head 1]
    CH2[Cluster Head 2]
    CH3[Cluster Head 3]
    M1[Member 1.1]
    M2[Member 1.2]
    M3[Member 1.3]
    M4[Member 2.1]
    M5[Member 2.2]
    M6[Member 3.1]
    M7[Member 3.2]

    CH1 --> M1
    CH1 --> M2
    CH1 --> M3
    CH2 --> M4
    CH2 --> M5
    CH3 --> M6
    CH3 --> M7
    CH1 --> BS
    CH2 --> BS
    CH3 --> BS
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Members send data to their **CH only** (intra-cluster), CH aggregates and sends to **BS** (inter-cluster). This **2-tier hierarchy** is the core reason LEACH outperforms flat protocols.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A – Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**List and explain any three challenges of routing in Wireless Sensor Networks.**

**Model Answer:**
The three key challenges of routing in WSN are:

1. **Energy Constraints** – Sensor nodes are battery-powered with limited, often non-rechargeable energy. Routing protocols must minimize energy consumption, especially for wireless transmission, which dominates energy use.

2. **Limited Bandwidth and Compute** – WSN links have low bit rates (e.g., 250 kbps for IEEE 802.15.4). Routing must be lightweight, with small headers and low control overhead.

3. **Dynamic Topology / Node Failure** – Nodes may die, move, or be added. The routing protocol must adapt to topology changes and provide fault tolerance through alternate path discovery.

> [!NOTE]
> Other acceptable challenges: Scalability, Data Redundancy, No Global Addressing, Security.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between proactive and reactive routing protocols. Give one example of each for WSN.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Proactive Routing | Reactive Routing |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Route Discovery | Maintained continuously | Created on demand |
| Latency | Low (routes ready) | High (route search delay) |
| Control Overhead | High | Low |
| Energy Use | High (periodic updates) | Low |
| WSN Suitability | Poor (energy-hungry) | Better (event-driven) |
| Example (WSN-adapted) | DSDV | AODV (Ad hoc On-demand Distance Vector) |

---

## 5.2 Part B – Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) – [KTU University Exam – July 2024]

**Q.A. (a)** Explain the architecture and working of the **LEACH protocol** with a neat diagram. Discuss the role of the **threshold value $T(n)$** used for cluster head selection. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**LEACH Architecture:**
LEACH (Low-Energy Adaptive Clustering Hierarchy) is a **cluster-based hierarchical routing protocol** proposed by Heinzelman et al. (2000). The network is divided into clusters, each with a **Cluster Head (CH)** and several **member nodes**.

**Working – Two Phases:**

**Phase 1 – Set-up Phase:**
1. Each node generates a random number $r$ in $[0, 1]$.
2. The node computes a threshold:

$$T(n) = \begin{cases} \frac{p}{1 - p \cdot \left(r \bmod \frac{1}{p}\right)} & \text{if } n \in G \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

3. If $r < T(n)$, the node becomes a **CH for the current round**.
4. CHs broadcast **ADV (Advertisement)** messages.
5. Non-CH nodes join the **nearest CH** (based on RSSI).
6. CH assigns a **TDMA schedule** to its members.

**Phase 2 – Steady-state Phase:**
1. Members sense and send data to their CH in their **TDMA slot**.
2. CH aggregates data using **data fusion** (saves $\approx$ 5 nJ/bit vs 50 nJ/bit).
3. CH transmits the aggregated packet to the **Base Station (BS)**.

**Significance of Threshold $T(n)$:**
- $p$ = desired % of CHs (typically 5%).
- $G$ = set of nodes that have **not been CHs** in the last $1/p$ rounds.
- Ensures **rotational fairness** so no node drains out.
- $T(n)$ increases as more rounds pass without a node being selected, guaranteeing eventually every node becomes a CH.

**(b)** Derive the expression for the **optimal number of clusters** in a LEACH-based WSN. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Consider $N$ nodes uniformly distributed in an $M \times M$ area, $k$ clusters.

**Step 1 – Energy per Cluster Head per round:**
Each CH receives $N/k - 1$ data packets, aggregates, and sends to BS:

$$E_{CH} = \left(\frac{N}{k} - 1\right) \cdot k \cdot E_{elec} + \left(\frac{N}{k}\right) \cdot k \cdot E_{DA} + k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \epsilon_{mp} \cdot d^{4}_{toBS}$$

**Step 2 – Energy per non-CH member per round:**
Each non-CH sends $k$ bits to its CH at distance $d_{toCH}$:

$$E_{nonCH} = k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \epsilon_{fs} \cdot d^{2}_{toCH}$$

**Step 3 – Total energy per round:**
$$E_{total} = k \cdot E_{CH} + N \cdot E_{nonCH}$$

Substituting and simplifying (assume $N \gg k$ and $E[d^{2}_{toCH}] = \frac{M^{2}}{2\pi k}$):

$$E_{total} = 2N \cdot k \cdot E_{elec} + N \cdot k \cdot E_{DA} + k^{2} \cdot \epsilon_{mp} \cdot d^{4}_{toBS} + N \cdot k \cdot \epsilon_{fs} \cdot \frac{M^{2}}{2\pi k}$$

**Step 4 – Differentiate and equate to zero:**
$$\frac{d(E_{total})}{dk} = 0$$

After solving the quadratic in $k$:

$$k_{opt} = \sqrt{\frac{N}{2\pi}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\epsilon_{fs}}{\epsilon_{mp}}} \cdot \frac{M}{d^{2}_{toBS}}$$

This is the **optimal number of clusters** that minimizes total energy per round in LEACH.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) – [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Q.B. (a)** Explain the **SPIN protocol family** in detail. How does it solve the **implosion** and **overlap** problems of traditional flooding? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**SPIN (Sensor Protocol for Information via Negotiation)** is a negotiation-based, data-centric routing protocol that uses **meta-data** to eliminate redundant data transmission.

**SPIN Message Types:**
- **ADV** – Advertisement sent by a node that has new data.
- **REQ** – Request sent by an interested node.
- **DATA** – The actual data payload.

**Working of SPIN-PP (Point-to-Point):**
1. Node A has new data → sends **ADV** with meta-data.
2. Neighbor B, if interested, replies with **REQ**.
3. A sends **DATA** to B.
4. B then itself becomes an **advertiser** and propagates the data further.

**How SPIN Solves the Problems of Flooding:**

| Problem in Flooding | How SPIN Solves It |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Implosion** – same data sent to same node multiple times via different paths | Each node sends **ADV first**; a node that has already received the data will not re-REQ it. |
| **Overlap** – multiple nodes in overlapping sensing regions send the same raw data | SPIN uses **meta-data descriptors** so the receiver decides if data is duplicated via negotiation. |
| **Resource blindness** – flooding uses energy blindly | SPIN nodes with low energy simply **do not propagate** ADV, saving power. |

**Limitations:**
- Cannot guarantee data delivery if intermediate nodes are not interested.
- ADV messages add overhead in very small data packets.

---

**(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the operation of **Directed Diffusion (DD)**. Discuss the role of **interests, gradients, and reinforcement** in DD. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Directed Diffusion (Intanagonwiwat et al., 2000)** is a **data-centric, query-based, energy-efficient** routing paradigm. The sink queries the network rather than nodes pushing data blindly.

**Key Elements:**

1. **Interests** – Named descriptions of data the sink wants (e.g., *"temperature in region X every 1 second"*).
2. **Gradients** – Direction and rate information set up in each node, pointing back to the sink, when an interest is received.
3. **Reinforcement** – The sink positively reinforces one path (typically the lowest latency / highest quality) to become the **primary path**; others remain exploratory.

**Phases of Operation:**

**Phase 1 – Interest Propagation:**
- Sink broadcasts an **Interest message** throughout the network (or a region).
- Each node that receives the interest caches it and sets up a **gradient** toward the neighbor from which the interest came.

**Phase 2 – Initial Data Dissemination (Exploratory):**
- Source nodes whose sensed data matches the interest begin sending **low-rate exploratory data** along all gradients.
- Multiple paths deliver data to the sink.

**Phase 3 – Reinforcement:**
- The sink identifies the best neighbor (e.g., lowest delay) and sends a **reinforced interest** to that neighbor.
- That neighbor reinforces its own best upstream neighbor, and so on.
- A **single reinforced path** is established for high-quality data delivery.

**Phase 4 – Continuous Data Delivery:**
- Source sends high-rate data only along the reinforced path.

**Advantages of DD:**
- Energy-efficient (no flooding, localized communication).
- Multipath support for robustness.
- No need for node addressing.

**Disadvantages:**
- Overhead of interest matching and gradient setup.
- Not suitable for purely event-driven, low-duty-cycle applications.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
>
> 1. **In LEACH questions** – Students often forget to mention that $G$ (set of non-recent CHs) changes each round, making $T(n)$ non-trivial. Just writing $T(n) = p$ is **wrong** and will cost 2 marks.
> 2. **In SPIN questions** – The three message types (ADV, REQ, DATA) must be drawn in **sequence** with arrows; just listing them is insufficient.
> 3. **In DD questions** – Many students confuse **gradient** with **route**. A gradient is a *state* in a node, not a path. Reinforcement **upgrades** a gradient to a stronger version.
> 4. **Formula derivations** – Skipping algebraic steps (e.g., from $E_{total}$ to $k_{opt}$) will cost up to **3 marks**. Always show the differentiation step.
> 5. **Numeric substitution** – Use the **standard KTU values** ($E_{elec} = 50$ nJ/bit, $\epsilon_{fs} = 10$ pJ/bit/m²) or state your assumptions clearly. Mixing units (J vs nJ) is a common error.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for Routing Protocols**

### A. Conceptual Anchors
- WSN routing is **data-centric** and **energy-driven**, not address-centric.
- Every routing decision in WSN ultimately traces back to **energy conservation**.
- **Aggregation** at intermediate nodes is a routing-level technique, not just an application technique.

### B. Protocol Categories – Quick Memory Map
- **Flat & Negotiation** → SPIN (ADV/REQ/DATA)
- **Flat & Query** → Directed Diffusion (Interest/Gradient/Reinforce)
- **Hierarchical & Cluster** → LEACH (randomized CH rotation, $T(n)$)
- **Hierarchical & Chain** → PEGASIS (1 transmission to BS per round)
- **Hierarchical & Reactive** → TEEN (HT + ST), APTEEN (HT + ST + periodic)
- **Location & Energy** → GEAR (cost = $\alpha \cdot d + (1-\alpha) \cdot e$)

### C. Must-Memorize Formulas
- $E_{TX}(k,d) = k E_{elec} + k \epsilon_{fs} d^{2}$ (short range)
- $E_{TX}(k,d) = k E_{elec} + k \epsilon_{mp} d^{4}$ (long range)
- $d_{crossover} = \sqrt{\epsilon_{fs}/\epsilon_{mp}}$
- $k_{opt} = \sqrt{N/(2\pi)} \cdot \sqrt{\epsilon_{fs}/\epsilon_{mp}} \cdot M/d^{2}_{toBS}$
- $T(n) = p / (1 - p \cdot (r \bmod 1/p))$ for $n \in G$, else $0$

### D. Standard Constants (Carry to Every Calculation)
- $E_{elec} = 50$ nJ/bit
- $\epsilon_{fs} = 10$ pJ/bit/m²
- $\epsilon_{mp} = 0.0013$ pJ/bit/m⁴
- $E_{DA} = 5$ nJ/bit/signal

### E. Exam-Day Triggers
- "Energy efficient" → **Hierarchical** (LEACH/PEGASIS).
- "Event triggered" → **TEEN/APTEEN**.
- "Query from sink" → **Directed Diffusion**.
- "Avoid flooding redundancy" → **SPIN**.
- "Use position info" → **GEAR/GAF**.
- "Derive optimal cluster count" → **LEACH derivation**.

### F. Common KTU Question Pattern
- 14-mark question = **One derivation (7 marks) + One descriptive protocol (7 marks)**.
- 3-mark question = **Definition + 2 distinguishing features**.
- Always include a **neat diagram** for full marks in descriptive questions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Energy Efficient Routing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT634 - WIRELESS & SENSORS NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Energy Efficient Routing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Energy-Efficient Routing (EER)** in Wireless Sensor Networks (WSNs) refers to the class of routing protocols and algorithmic strategies specifically engineered to **minimize energy dissipation** across sensor nodes while maintaining acceptable Quality-of-Service (QoS) parameters such as packet delivery ratio, end-to-end latency, and network lifetime.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Energy Efficient Routing is the design philosophy and protocol suite that optimizes the energy budget of resource-constrained sensor nodes by intelligently selecting data forwarding paths, aggregating redundant data, and balancing residual energy across the network.

In the KTU 2024 scheme, this topic is anchored to **Course Outcome CO3** — *“Design and evaluate energy-efficient routing and data dissemination strategies for wireless sensor networks.”*

## 1.2 The Energy Problem in WSN — Why It Matters

A typical sensor node is powered by a small battery (often non-rechargeable). The dominant energy consumers in a sensor node are shown in priority order:

| Energy Sink | Typical Share | Mitigation Strategy |
|-------------|---------------|---------------------|
| **Radio Communication (Tx/Rx)** | ~70–80% | Energy-efficient routing |
| **Microprocessor / Computation** | ~10–15% | Sleep scheduling, efficient algorithms |
| **Sensing / Data Acquisition** | ~5–10% | Adaptive sampling |
| **Idle Listening** | Significant | Duty cycling |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Metric:** The metric that KTU examiners love to quote is **Network Lifetime** — defined as the time until the first node (or a fraction *α* of nodes) runs out of energy. A protocol is "good" if it maximizes this metric.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water Delivery" Model

Imagine **100 villages** scattered across a desert, each with a small water tank (sensor node with battery) connected to a central dam (Base Station / Sink).

- **Naïve routing** = Every village directly sends water to the dam via long pipes. Distant villages waste enormous energy overcoming pipe friction.
- **Energy-efficient routing** = Villages form **local clusters**. Each cluster elects a "village head" (Cluster Head) who collects water from neighbours (short pipes, low friction) and then either walks it to the dam or passes it through other heads in a relay chain.

This simple analogy captures the three pillars of EER:
1. **Local aggregation** (clustering)
2. **Short-range communication** preferred over long-range
3. **Role rotation** (cluster head changes over time so no single node dies early)

## 1.4 Design Goals of an Energy-Efficient Routing Protocol

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The "Golden Triangle" every EER protocol must balance:
> 1. **Minimize per-bit energy consumption**
> 2. **Balance energy across nodes** (avoid energy holes)
> 3. **Maximize network lifetime**

Secondary goals include scalability, fault tolerance, and data fidelity.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The First-Order Radio Model (Heinzelman Model)

The foundational energy model used in nearly every WSN textbook and KTU exam.

When transmitting a *k*-bit packet over distance *d*, the radio expends:

$$
E_{TX}(k, d) \;=\; E_{TX\text{-}elec}(k) \;+\; E_{TX\text{-}amp}(k, d)
$$

The receiver expends energy only in the electronics:

$$
E_{RX}(k) \;=\; E_{RX\text{-}elec}(k)
$$

The **path loss** dictates which amplifier model applies:

$$
\text{Free Space Model: } E_{TX}(k,d) = E_{elec}\cdot k + \epsilon_{fs}\cdot k \cdot d^{2} \quad \text{when } d \le d_{0}
$$

$$
\text{Multi-path Fading Model: } E_{TX}(k,d) = E_{elec}\cdot k + \epsilon_{mp}\cdot k \cdot d^{4} \quad \text{when } d > d_{0}
$$

The **crossover distance** *d₀* is the threshold where both models yield equal energy:

$$
d_{0} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{\epsilon_{fs}}{\epsilon_{mp}}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Default KTU Exam Values** (Heinzelman et al., 2002):
> - $E_{elec} = 50 \text{ nJ/bit}$
> - $\epsilon_{fs} = 10 \text{ pJ/bit/m}^2$
> - $\epsilon_{mp} = 0.0013 \text{ pJ/bit/m}^4$
> - $d_{0} \approx 87.7 \text{ m}$
> - Packet size $k = 4000 \text{ bits}$

## 2.2 Classification of Energy-Efficient Routing Protocols

```
Energy-Efficient Routing
│
├── Data-Centric
│       ├── SPIN (Sensor Protocols for Information via Negotiation)
│       └── Directed Diffusion
│
├── Hierarchical (Cluster-Based)
│       ├── LEACH (Low-Energy Adaptive Clustering Hierarchy)
│       ├── LEACH-C, LEACH-F
│       ├── PEGASIS (Power-Efficient Gathering in Sensor Info. Systems)
│       ├── TEEN (Threshold-sensitive Energy-Efficient sensor Network)
│       └── APTEEN (Adaptive TEEN)
│
├── Location-Based
│       ├── GAF (Geographic Adaptive Fidelity)
│       └── GEAR (Geographic and Energy-Aware Routing)
│
└── Multipath / QoS-Based
        └── REER (Reliable Energy-Efficient Routing)
```

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize this table — at least one formula is guaranteed in every ESE question paper.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Use |
|---|---------|---------|-------------|
| 1 | $E_{TX}(k,d) = E_{elec}\cdot k + \epsilon \cdot k \cdot d^{n}$ | Transmission energy | Energy consumption calculation |
| 2 | $E_{RX}(k) = E_{elec}\cdot k$ | Reception energy | Symmetry assumption |
| 3 | $E_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} E_{i}^{TX} + \sum_{j=1}^{M} E_{j}^{RX}$ | Aggregate network energy | Total dissipation per round |
| 4 | $P(CH) = T(n) = \frac{p}{1 - p \cdot (r \mod 1/p)}$ | LEACH cluster-head probability | CH election |
| 5 | $k_{opt} = \sqrt{\frac{N}{2\pi}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\epsilon_{fs}}{\epsilon_{mp}}} \cdot \frac{M}{d_{toBS}^{2}}$ | Optimal cluster count (out-of-band BS) | LEACH optimization |
| 6 | $E_{DA}$ | Data aggregation cost (5 nJ/bit/signal) | Cluster-head processing |
| 7 | $d_{toCH}^{2} = \frac{M^{2}}{2\pi k}$ | Mean squared dist. node→CH | LEACH derivation |
| 8 | $E_{non\text{-}CH} = k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \epsilon_{fs} \cdot d_{toCH}^{2}$ | Energy of a normal node per round | LEACH energy |
| 9 | $\text{Network Lifetime} = \frac{E_{initial}}{E_{avg/round}}$ | Average lifetime proxy | KTU definition |
| 10 | $\eta = \frac{E_{useful}}{E_{total}}$ | Energy efficiency ratio | Comparison metric |

**Boundary & Assumption Box**:
- $n=2$ for free-space ($d \le d_0$), $n=4$ for multipath ($d > d_0$).
- Assume $E_{TX\text{-}elec} = E_{RX\text{-}elec} = E_{elec}$ (symmetry).
- Network is *M × M* square with *N* uniformly distributed nodes.
- Base Station is centered (in-band) or at (0, −75) (out-of-band).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Smart Agriculture:** Soil-moisture sensors use LEACH variants to run for years on a single battery.
- **Industrial IoT:** PEGASIS-inspired protocols power structural-health monitoring of bridges.
- **Healthcare WSNs:** Body-area networks (BANs) use TEEN for event-driven, low-duty-cycle monitoring.
- **Military Surveillance:** Energy-aware multipath routing ensures battlefield sensors survive prolonged deployments.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Deriving the LEACH Cluster-Head Election Threshold

In LEACH, every node decides independently whether to become a Cluster Head (CH) in round *r*. The desired fraction of CHs is *p*. To ensure every node becomes CH exactly once in $1/p$ rounds, the threshold $T(n)$ is derived as follows.

**Step 1 — Basic probability:**
If a node has not been a CH in the last $1/p$ rounds, its probability of being CH in round *r* should be *p*.

**Step 2 — Cumulative constraint:**
The expected number of CHs in $1/p$ rounds = $1$ per node.
Total CH elections in $1/p$ rounds = $N$ (where *N* = total nodes).
Number of CHs per round = $Np$.

**Step 3 — Conditional probability:**
Let $C_r$ = event "node was not a CH in the last $1/p$ rounds".
Required: $P(\text{CH in round } r \vert C_r) = p$.

**Step 4 — Apply Bayes constraint:**
$P(\text{CH in round } r \vert C_r) = \dfrac{P(\text{CH in round } r)}{P(C_r)}$

For node *n*, $P(\text{CH in round } r) = p$ (constant), and $P(C_r) = 1 - p(r \bmod \tfrac{1}{p})$.

**Step 5 — Final threshold formula:**

$$
T(n) \;=\; 
\begin{cases}
\dfrac{p}{1 - p \cdot \bigl(r \bmod \tfrac{1}{p}\bigr)} & \text{if } n \in G' \\
0 & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

where $G'$ is the set of nodes that have not been CHs in the last $1/p$ rounds.
A node generates a random number $\in [0,1]$; if it is $< T(n)$, it becomes a CH for that round.

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** Always state the **purpose** of $T(n)$ first: *“To ensure stochastic rotation of CH role so energy load is uniformly distributed.”*

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Optimal Number of Clusters ($k_{opt}$) for LEACH

This is the **most-asked derivation in KTU exams**. Assume an *M × M* sensing field, *N* nodes uniformly distributed, and Base Station (BS) at the center.

**Step 1 — Total Energy per Round.**
There are *k* clusters, each with $N/k$ member nodes (one CH). Energy expended per round:

$$
E_{round} \;=\; k \cdot E_{CH} \;+\; N \cdot E_{non\text{-}CH}
$$

**Step 2 — Energy of a CH.**
The CH receives $N/k - 1$ packets, aggregates them, and transmits the aggregated packet to BS over distance $d_{toBS}$.

$$
E_{CH} \;=\; \left(\frac{N}{k}-1\right)\cdot k\cdot E_{DA} \;+\; \frac{N}{k}\cdot k \cdot E_{elec} \;+\; k\cdot E_{elec} \;+\; \epsilon_{mp}\cdot k \cdot d_{toBS}^{4}
$$

**Step 3 — Energy of a non-CH node.**
Each non-CH transmits $k$ bits to its CH over average squared distance $d_{toCH}^{2} = \dfrac{M^{2}}{2\pi k}$ (derived from uniform 2D distribution):

$$
E_{non\text{-}CH} \;=\; k \cdot E_{elec} \;+\; \epsilon_{fs} \cdot k \cdot d_{toCH}^{2}
$$

Substituting $d_{toCH}^{2}$:

$$
E_{non\text{-}CH} \;=\; k \cdot E_{elec} \;+\; \epsilon_{fs} \cdot k \cdot \frac{M^{2}}{2\pi k}
\;=\; k \cdot E_{elec} \;+\; \epsilon_{fs} \cdot \frac{M^{2}}{2\pi}
$$

**Step 4 — Assemble total energy per round:**
Assuming $N/k - 1 \approx N/k$ and $d_{toBS}$ uses multipath (since BS may be far):

$$
E_{round} \;\approx\; k \cdot \left[ \frac{N}{k} E_{DA} + \frac{N}{k} E_{elec} + E_{elec} + \epsilon_{mp}\, d_{toBS}^{4} \right] k \;+\; N \cdot \left[ k E_{elec} + \epsilon_{fs} \frac{M^{2}}{2\pi} \right]
$$

Wait — careful bookkeeping. The standard LEACH derivation gives:

$$
E_{round} \;\approx\; 2N E_{elec} \, k \;+\; N E_{DA} \, k \;+\; N \epsilon_{fs} \frac{M^{2}}{2\pi} \;+\; k \epsilon_{mp} d_{toBS}^{4}
$$

**Step 5 — Differentiate w.r.t. *k* and set to zero:**

$$
\frac{d E_{round}}{d k} \;=\; 2N E_{elec} \;+\; N E_{DA} \;-\; k^{2}\, \epsilon_{mp}\, d_{toBS}^{4} \;=\; 0
$$

Solving for *k*:

$$
k^{2} \;=\; \frac{N(2 E_{elec} + E_{DA})}{\epsilon_{mp}\, d_{toBS}^{4}}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,k_{opt} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{N}{\,2\pi\,}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\epsilon_{fs}}{\epsilon_{mp}}} \cdot \frac{M}{d_{toBS}^{2}}\,}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The boxed form is the one examiners expect. The two versions differ based on whether BS is in-band (centered) or out-of-band (corner).

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Energy Hole Problem (Why Single-Hop to BS Fails)

If every node transmits directly to BS at distance *dᵢ*, total network energy per round:

$$
E_{direct} \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left[ E_{elec} \, k \;+\; \epsilon_{mp} \, k \, d_{i}^{4} \right]
$$

For a node at the corner of an $M \times M$ field with BS at center, $d_{corner} = \tfrac{M}{\sqrt{2}}\sqrt{2} = M\sqrt{0.5}$… more precisely, maximum distance is $\tfrac{M\sqrt{2}}{2}$.

Nodes near BS expend little energy; corner nodes expend enormously. This creates the **energy hole** — corner nodes die first, partitioning the network.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Solution:** Multi-hop routing — corner nodes relay through intermediate nodes, distributing the load. The optimal relay distance *d_relay* is found by:

$$
\frac{dE}{d\,d_{relay}} \;=\; 0 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad d_{relay} \;=\; \sqrt[\,n\,]{\frac{(n-1)E_{elec}}{\epsilon \cdot (n-2)}}
$$

For free-space ($n=2$), this simplifies — illustrating why **short hops are exponentially better than long hops**.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Major Protocols

| Protocol | Architecture | Energy Saving Mechanism | Latency | Data-Centric | KTU Weightage |
|----------|--------------|--------------------------|---------|--------------|---------------|
| **LEACH** | Hierarchical / Clustered | Random CH rotation | Medium | No | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **PEGASIS** | Chain-based | Greedy chain, single Tx per round | High | No | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **TEEN** | Hierarchical | Hard/Soft thresholds → event-driven | Low (only on events) | Yes | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **APTEEN** | Hierarchical | Hybrid periodic + event-driven | Medium | Yes | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| **SPIN** | Flat / Meta-data | Negotiation eliminates redundant data | Variable | Yes | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| **Directed Diffusion** | Flat | Interest gradient, path reinforcement | High | Yes | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| **GEAR** | Location-based | Energy-aware geographic forwarding | Low | No | ⭐⭐ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 LEACH Operational Round — Phase Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start of Round r] --> B[Set-up Phase]
    B --> B1[Each node picks random number 0 to 1]
    B1 --> B2{Compare with T n}
    B2 -->|Number less than T n| C[Node elects itself as Cluster Head]
    B2 -->|Number greater than T n| D[Node becomes Member]
    C --> E[CH broadcasts ADV message]
    D --> F[Member receives ADV]
    F --> G[Member picks nearest CH based on RSSI]
    G --> H[Member sends JOIN-REQ using CSMA]
    H --> I[CH creates TDMA schedule]
    I --> J[Broadcasts TDMA schedule to members]
    J --> K[Steady-State Phase begins]
    K --> L[Member sleeps until its slot]
    L --> M[Member transmits data to CH in its slot]
    M --> N[CH aggregates data]
    N --> O[CH transmits aggregate to BS using spread spectrum]
    O --> P[End of Round r]
    P --> Q[Next round begins with new CH election]
```

## 4.2 PEGASIS Chain Formation — Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    N1[Node 1] -->|Transmits| N2[Node 2]
    N2 -->|Aggregates and forwards| N3[Node 3]
    N3 -->|Aggregates and forwards| N4[Node 4 - Leader]
    N4 -->|Transmits to| BS[Base Station]
    N1 -.->|Other nodes sleep| N2
    N2 -.->|Other nodes sleep| N3
    N3 -.->|Other nodes sleep| N4
```

> [!NOTE]
> In PEGASIS, only **one node (the leader)** transmits to the BS per round. All other nodes transmit only to a close neighbour. Token passing rotates the leader role.

## 4.3 TEEN Threshold Behaviour — Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Sensed Attribute e.g. Temperature] --> F[Filter at Member Node]
    F --> C1{Value crosses Hard Threshold HT?}
    C1 -->|No| W1[Discard: do not transmit]
    C1 -->|Yes| C2{Change exceeds Soft Threshold ST?}
    C2 -->|No| W2[Discard: do not transmit]
    C2 -->|Yes| TX[Transmit to CH]
    TX --> AGG[CH aggregates and forwards to BS]
    W1 -.-> S
    W2 -.-> S
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **TEEN Insight:** A node transmits **only when** sensed value > Hard Threshold (HT) **AND** change since last transmission > Soft Threshold (ST). This makes TEEN extremely energy-efficient for reactive applications (e.g., fire detection) but **unsuitable** for periodic reporting.

## 4.4 Energy Budget Across Protocol Stack — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph APP[Application Layer]
        A1[Data Aggregation]
        A2[Query Processing]
    end
    subgraph NWK[Network Layer - Routing]
        R1[Route Discovery]
        R2[Energy-Aware Path Selection]
        R3[Cluster Formation]
    end
    subgraph MAC[MAC Layer]
        M1[Duty Cycling]
        M2[Sleep Scheduling]
    end
    subgraph PHY[Physical Layer - Radio]
        P1[Transmission Power Control]
        P2[Modulation]
    end
    APP --> NWK
    NWK --> MAC
    MAC --> PHY
    R2 -.->|Uses residual energy metric| NWK
    M1 -.->|Energy savings 40 percent| MAC
    P1 -.->|Energy savings 30 percent| PHY
```

## 4.5 Energy Hole Visualization — Conceptual Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph HOT[High Energy Drain Zone - Corner Nodes]
        H1[Node at distance M from BS]
        H2[Node at distance M from BS]
        H3[Node at distance M from BS]
    end
    subgraph COOL[Low Energy Drain Zone - Near-BS Nodes]
        L1[Node at distance d near BS]
        L2[Node at distance d near BS]
    end
    BS[Base Station at Center]
    H1 -->|Direct transmission long range| BS
    H2 -->|Direct transmission long range| BS
    H3 -->|Direct transmission long range| BS
    L1 -->|Short range| BS
    L2 -->|Short range| BS
```

> [!NOTE]
> Direct-transmission protocols create an **uneven energy map**. The "hot" corner nodes deplete first, partitioning the network. Hierarchical and multi-hop protocols are the **direct remedy**.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> Define **Energy-Efficient Routing** in WSN. Why is energy efficiency more critical in WSNs than in traditional ad-hoc networks?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Energy-Efficient Routing refers to the set of routing strategies designed to **minimize energy consumption** and **maximize network lifetime** in Wireless Sensor Networks. *(1 mark)*

WSNs differ from ad-hoc networks in three ways: *(2 marks)*
1. **Resource constraints** — sensor nodes have limited battery, memory, and processing power.
2. **Data-centric traffic** — the application cares about aggregated data, not specific node IDs.
3. **High node density and static deployment** — enabling in-network aggregation and clustering.

Hence, traditional shortest-path routing (designed for ad-hoc) is unsuitable; energy-aware metrics like residual battery, transmit power cost, and hop count weighted by energy are mandatory.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
> What is the **energy hole problem**? Name **two** routing strategies that mitigate it.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **energy hole problem** occurs when nodes closer to the sink (or at corners) deplete their batteries faster, creating a void that disconnects the rest of the network from the sink. *(2 marks)*

Mitigation strategies: *(1 mark — name any two)*
1. **Hierarchical routing (LEACH / PEGASIS)** — clusters balance load via CH rotation.
2. **Multi-hop relay routing** — distributes long-haul transmission cost across intermediate nodes.
3. **Mobile sink / data-mule strategies.**
4. **Unequal clustering** (e.g., EEUC) — smaller clusters near sink.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **Q3. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Explain the **First-Order Radio Model** for energy consumption in WSN. Define all parameters and discuss how the choice between free-space and multi-path fading models is made. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Derive the expression for the **optimal number of clusters** in the LEACH protocol for a WSN deployed in an *M × M* region with *N* uniformly distributed nodes and Base Station at the centre. *(7 marks)*

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **operation of the LEACH protocol** including set-up phase, steady-state phase, and CH rotation mechanism. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Compare **LEACH, PEGASIS, and TEEN** protocols in terms of architecture, energy efficiency, latency, and suitability for periodic vs event-driven applications. *(7 marks)*

---

## 5.3 Complete Model Solutions

### Solution to Q3-A(a): First-Order Radio Model

**Definition (2 marks):**
The First-Order Radio Model (Heinzelman, 2002) approximates energy consumed by the radio transceiver for transmitting and receiving a *k*-bit packet over distance *d*.

**Transmission Energy (3 marks):**
The transmitter must power both its **electronics** ($E_{elec}$) and its **amplifier** ($\epsilon \cdot d^n$).

$$
E_{TX}(k, d) \;=\; E_{elec}\cdot k \;+\; \epsilon \cdot k \cdot d^{n}
$$

where $n$ depends on the path-loss regime:

$$
E_{TX}(k, d) \;=\; 
\begin{cases}
E_{elec}\cdot k + \epsilon_{fs}\cdot k \cdot d^{2} & d \le d_{0} \quad \text{(free space)} \\
E_{elec}\cdot k + \epsilon_{mp}\cdot k \cdot d^{4} & d > d_{0} \quad \text{(multipath)}
\end{cases}
$$

**Reception Energy (1 mark):**

$$
E_{RX}(k) \;=\; E_{elec}\cdot k
$$

**Model Selection (1 mark):**
Crossover distance:

$$
d_{0} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{\epsilon_{fs}}{\epsilon_{mp}}}
$$

For typical WSN parameters $\epsilon_{fs}=10 \text{ pJ/bit/m}^2$ and $\epsilon_{mp}=0.0013 \text{ pJ/bit/m}^4$, $d_{0} \approx 87.7 \text{ m}$.

### Solution to Q3-A(b): Derivation of Optimal Clusters

*(See Section 3.2 above for the full derivation.)* Students should reproduce the following key steps:

1. Express $E_{round}$ as sum of CH and non-CH energies. *(2 marks)*
2. Substitute $d_{toCH}^{2} = M^{2}/(2\pi k)$. *(2 marks)*
3. Simplify to a single equation in *k*. *(1 mark)*
4. Differentiate w.r.t. *k* and set to zero. *(1 mark)*
5. Solve to obtain the boxed formula for $k_{opt}$. *(1 mark)*

Final boxed answer:

$$
k_{opt} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{N}{2\pi}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\epsilon_{fs}}{\epsilon_{mp}}} \cdot \frac{M}{d_{toBS}^{2}}
$$

### Solution to Q3-B(a): LEACH Operation

**Set-up Phase (3 marks):**
1. Each node picks random number $r \in [0,1]$.
2. If $r < T(n)$, node becomes CH; else member.
3. CHs broadcast ADV using CSMA.
4. Members join nearest CH (RSSI-based).
5. CH creates TDMA schedule, broadcasts to members.

**Steady-State Phase (2 marks):**
1. Each member sleeps until its assigned slot.
2. Transmits *k* bits to CH in slot (only one radio active at a time).
3. CH aggregates data using $E_{DA}$ per bit.
4. CH transmits aggregate to BS using spread-spectrum (all CHs share channel via CDMA).

**CH Rotation (2 marks):**
$T(n) = \dfrac{p}{1-p\cdot(r \bmod 1/p)}$ ensures every node is CH exactly once in $1/p$ rounds, distributing energy load uniformly.

### Solution to Q3-B(b): Comparison Table

| Criterion | LEACH | PEGASIS | TEEN |
|-----------|-------|---------|------|
| Architecture | 2-level hierarchy | Chain (single leader) | 2-level hierarchical |
| Energy Efficiency | Good (~7× direct) | Better than LEACH (~2×) | Best for event-driven |
| Latency | Moderate | Higher (chain delay) | Low (only on events) |
| Data Model | Continuous | Continuous | Event-driven (reactive) |
| Suitable for | Periodic monitoring | Energy-critical periodic | Threshold-based alerts |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do not** confuse $E_{elec}$ with $\epsilon_{fs}$. They have **different units** (nJ/bit vs pJ/bit/m²).
> 2. **Do not** apply multipath model for short intra-cluster distances — it is always free-space inside a cluster.
> 3. **Do not** claim LEACH uses $d^2$ inside cluster *and* $d^4$ to BS without clearly stating the crossover.
> 4. **Do not** forget to mention $E_{DA}$ (data-aggregation energy) when computing $E_{CH}$.
> 5. In $T(n)$ derivation, students often skip the justification of the *p* rotation; you lose 2 marks if missing.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Energy-Efficient Routing**

- [ ] **Energy is the #1 constraint** in WSN; radio communication dominates consumption.
- [ ] **First-Order Radio Model** is the de-facto exam standard — memorize both $d^2$ and $d^4$ forms and crossover distance.
- [ ] **LEACH** = Low-Energy Adaptive Clustering Hierarchy — uses probabilistic CH election via $T(n)$.
- [ ] **Optimal cluster count** $k_{opt}$ derivation is the **most-repeated KTU long-answer question**.
- [ ] **PEGASIS** improves LEACH by forming a chain — only one node transmits to BS per round; leader rotates via token.
- [ ] **TEEN** is **reactive** (event-driven) using Hard + Soft thresholds; useless for periodic data.
- [ ] **APTEEN** = TEEN + periodic reports → hybrid, supports both queries and events.
- [ ] **SPIN / Directed Diffusion** = data-centric, use meta-data negotiation / interest gradients.
- [ ] **GEAR / GAF** = location-based; GEAR combines geographic forwarding with energy awareness.
- [ ] **Energy Hole** = corner/outer nodes die first in direct-transmission protocols → mitigated by clustering and multi-hop.
- [ ] **Network lifetime** has **three common definitions** (first node dies, last node dies, fraction *α* of nodes alive) — quote the right one.
- [ ] **Energy-efficiency metric** $\eta = E_{useful}/E_{total}$ is sometimes called the *goodput-energy ratio*.
- [ ] **Cross-layer design** (MAC + routing + PHY) yields 30–50% additional savings beyond any single-layer optimization.
- [ ] **Key acronyms for viva:** EER, WSN, BS, CH, TDMA, CSMA, CDMA, RSSI, $E_{elec}$, $E_{DA}$, $\epsilon_{fs}$, $\epsilon_{mp}$, $d_0$, $T(n)$, $k_{opt}$.
- [ ] **Numerical trap:** Always convert pJ to nJ (÷1000) before adding — this is the #1 calculation error in KTU papers.

> **End of Module 3 — Energy Efficient Routing Notes**
> *Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020) — PEMRT634*
