# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — June 2023]** Define clustering in WSN. List any four advantages of clustering.

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> *Clustering in Wireless Sensor Networks is a hierarchical topology management technique in which sensor nodes are organized into logical groups called clusters, with each cluster having a designated Cluster Head (CH) responsible for aggregating data from cluster members and forwarding the fused data to the Base Station.*

**Four Advantages:**

1. **Scalability** — Hierarchical structure allows the network to scale to thousands of nodes without flooding the BS.
2. **Energy efficiency** — Short intra-cluster communication uses the free-space model ($d^2$), saving energy.
3. **Data aggregation** — CHs fuse redundant data, drastically reducing traffic to the BS.
4. **Bandwidth reuse / reduced collisions** — Clusters form local contention domains, enabling spatial reuse of the channel.
5. *Bonus:* Localized TDMA schedules reduce idle listening and collisions.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]** What is a Cluster Head? Explain the LEACH threshold equation for CH election.

**Model Answer:**

A **Cluster Head (CH)** is the elected leader node of a cluster that performs data aggregation from all its members and forwards the consolidated report to the Base Station.

**LEACH Threshold Equation:**

$$T(n) = \begin{cases} \dfrac{P}{1 - P \cdot [r \bmod (1/P)]} & \text{if } n \in G \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

- $P$ = desired fraction of CHs per round
- $r$ = current round number
- $G$ = set of nodes that have not been CHs in the last $1/P$ rounds
- A node generates a random number $\rho \in [0,1]$ and becomes a CH if $\rho < T(n)$.

The threshold increases as the round count grows within an epoch, ensuring that exactly $P \cdot N$ nodes become CHs per cycle and that CHs are not re-elected immediately, distributing energy fairly.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the architecture of clustered WSN with a neat diagram. Discuss the two phases of operation in the LEACH protocol. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Derive the optimal number of clusters $k_{opt}$ for a uniformly distributed WSN. State the assumptions used. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### **Model Solution to Q-A (a):**

**Architecture of Clustered WSN (3 marks):**

A clustered WSN consists of:
- $N$ sensor nodes randomly deployed in a region
- $k$ clusters formed dynamically
- 1 CH per cluster elected per round
- 1 Base Station (BS) acting as the sink
- Each CM belongs to exactly one CH
- CMs → CH: short-range, free-space propagation
- CH → BS: long-range, multipath propagation

(Reference the Mermaid architecture diagram from Section 4.1.)

**Two Phases of LEACH (4 marks):**

| Phase | Activity | Duration |
|---|---|---|
| **Setup Phase** | CH election (using $T(n)$), advertisement (ADV), cluster join (REQ-JOIN), TDMA schedule broadcast | Short (~seconds) |
| **Steady-State Phase** | CM data transmission to CH in TDMA slots, data aggregation at CH, CH → BS transmission | Long (~tens of seconds) |

Each round = Setup + Steady-State. CHs are re-elected every round to rotate the energy burden.

---

#### **Model Solution to Q-A (b):**

**Derivation of $k_{opt}$ (full 7-mark derivation, reproduced from Section 3.3):**

**Assumptions (1 mark):**
- $N$ nodes uniformly distributed in $M \times M$ square field
- BS located at $(x_{BS}, y_{BS})$, distance $d_{toBS}$ from field center
- All nodes have equal initial energy
- All CMs always have data to send
- Radio model is the first-order model

**Total energy per round (3 marks):**

$$E_{round} = k\left(2NE_{elec} + N\varepsilon_{fs}\cdot \dfrac{M^{2}}{2\pi k} + k\varepsilon_{mp}d_{toBS}^{4} + NE_{DA}\right)$$

**Minimization (3 marks):** Differentiate w.r.t. $k$, set to zero, solve:

$$\dfrac{dE_{round}}{dk} = 2NE_{elec} + N\varepsilon_{DA} + 2k\varepsilon_{mp}d_{toBS}^{4} = 0$$

Neglecting small $E_{DA}$:

$$\boxed{\,k_{opt} = \sqrt{\dfrac{N}{2\pi}} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{\varepsilon_{fs}}{\varepsilon_{mp}}} \cdot \dfrac{M}{d_{toBS}^{2}}\,}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the first-order radio energy model used for clustering analysis. Compute the crossover distance $d_0$ for $E_{elec} = 50$ nJ/bit, $\varepsilon_{fs} = 10$ pJ/bit/m², $\varepsilon_{mp} = 0.0013$ pJ/bit/m⁴. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Compare LEACH, TEEN, and PEGASIS protocols with respect to CH selection, energy awareness, data delivery, and application suitability. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### **Model Solution to Q-B (a):**

**First-Order Radio Model (4 marks):**

- **Transmitter (TX):** Consumes $E_{TX\text{-}elec}$ to run the radio electronics and $E_{TX\text{-}amp}$ to run the power amplifier.
  - If $d < d_0$: $E_{TX}(k,d) = kE_{elec} + k\varepsilon_{fs}d^{2}$ (free-space, $d^2$ loss)
  - If $d \geq d_0$: $E_{TX}(k,d) = kE_{elec} + k\varepsilon_{mp}d^{4}$ (multipath, $d^4$ loss)
- **Receiver (RX):** $E_{RX}(k) = kE_{elec}$ (only electronics; no amplifier)
- **Data aggregation (DA):** $E_{DA}(k) = kE_{DA}$ (only at CHs)

**Crossover Distance Computation (3 marks):**

$$d_0 = \sqrt{\dfrac{\varepsilon_{fs}}{\varepsilon_{mp}}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{10 \times 10^{-12}}{0.0013 \times 10^{-12}}} = \sqrt{7692.3}$$

$$\boxed{\,d_0 \approx 87.7 \text{ m}\,}$$

Physical interpretation: for distances below ~88 m the free-space ($d^2$) model is more energy-efficient; beyond it the multipath ($d^4$) model dominates. A CH transmitting to a far BS will therefore burn energy **quadratically faster** with distance — this is why BS placement and $k_{opt}$ are critical design decisions.

---

#### **Model Solution to Q-B (b):**

**Comparative Table (5 marks):**

| Criterion | LEACH | TEEN | PEGASIS |
|---|---|---|---|
| **CH Selection** | Probabilistic random | Proximity to BS | Rotating leader in a chain |
| **Energy Awareness** | Implicit (rotation) | Implicit (sleep mode dominant) | Strong (greedy chain minimizes distance) |
| **Data Delivery** | Periodic (each round) | Reactive (only on event exceeding thresholds) | Periodic, chain-based |
| **Threshold Use** | None | Hard + Soft thresholds | None |
| **Latency** | Low | Very low (no event → no TX) | High (chain traversal) |
| **Best Application** | Periodic monitoring (e.g., temperature) | Time-critical events (e.g., fire alarm) | Small, energy-uniform networks |
| **Drawback** | Random CH placement; no residual energy check | Energy spent only when event occurs (good) but not suitable for continuous sensing | Excessive delay for large N; single leader bottleneck |

**Summary Insight (2 marks):**

- **LEACH** is the **most cited** clustering protocol in KTU exams — expect 7+ marks questions on it.
- **TEEN** is a *reactive* variant of LEACH; suited to sudden-event detection.
- **PEGASIS** replaces clusters with a **chain** to avoid CH overhead — traded for latency.
- **HEED** extends LEACH by using *residual energy* as the primary CH election parameter.
- **APTEEN** is the *adaptive hybrid* of TEEN, supporting both periodic and event-based reporting.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 places KTU students LOSE marks in Clustering questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the units** in $E_{TX}$ numericals. *Always convert nJ → J and pJ → J* before plugging into the equation. Mixing units = **−2 marks** minimum.
>
> 2. **Confusing $P$ and $k$ in the LEACH formula.** $P$ is a *probability/fraction* (e.g., 0.05), $k$ is the *integer cluster count*. The threshold formula uses $P$, not $k$.
>
> 3. **Writing $|x|$ in markdown tables.** Vertical pipes break the table. Use `\vert` or `\mid` instead.
>
> 4. **Not explaining WHY $d^4$ is used for CH-to-BS.** Examiners award 1 mark for the justification ("multipath fading dominates for long distances beyond $d_0 \approx 87.7$ m").
>
> 5. **Missing the "Stable Region"** concept in lifetime questions. Always mention that *network lifetime* is typically measured as the time until the **first node dies (FND)** or the time until a **percentage** (e.g., 50%) of nodes die.
>
> 6. **Skipping the diagram.** A 7-mark question without a diagram loses **at least 1 mark** even if the theory is perfect. Always draw the cluster–CH–BS hierarchy.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### 📌 Rapid Revision Checklist

- ✅ **Cluster** = group of nodes with one **CH** leader; **CH** aggregates + forwards to **BS**.
- ✅ Three pillars: **Election → Aggregation → Long-haul TX**.
- ✅ **LEACH** threshold: $T(n) = P / [1 - P(r \bmod 1/P)]$ if $n \in G$, else 0.
- ✅ **LEACH phases:** Setup (CH election, cluster formation, TDMA) and Steady-State (data TX, aggregation, BS TX).
- ✅ **Crossover distance:** $d_0 = \sqrt{\varepsilon_{fs}/\varepsilon_{mp}} \approx 87.7$ m.
- ✅ **TX energy free-space:** $E_{TX} = kE_{elec} + k\varepsilon_{fs}d^2$ (for $d < d_0$).
- ✅ **TX energy multipath:** $E_{TX} = kE_{elec} + k\varepsilon_{mp}d^4$ (for $d \geq d_0$).
- ✅ **RX energy:** $E_{RX}(k) = kE_{elec}$.
- ✅ **Optimal clusters:** $k_{opt} = \sqrt{N/(2\pi)} \cdot \sqrt{\varepsilon_{fs}/\varepsilon_{mp}} \cdot M/d_{toBS}^2$.
- ✅ **LEACH** = periodic; **TEEN** = reactive (HT + ST); **APTEEN** = hybrid; **PEGASIS** = chain; **HEED** = residual-energy aware.
- ✅ **LEACH-C** = centralized CH election using BS (avoids random bad placements).
- ✅ **Stable region** = time between deployment and first node death.
- ✅ **Energy savings** of clustering: ~8× over direct transmission (Heinzelman's result).
- ✅ **Data aggregation** at CH reduces traffic by a factor of $\approx (N/k)$.
- ✅ **Single-hop** intra-cluster is standard in LEACH; **multi-hop** used in large networks (PEGASIS chain).
- ✅ **Limitations:** CHs become bottleneck; CH near BS dies early (hot-spot problem solved by uneven clustering, e.g., EEUC).
- ✅ **k_opt ∝ 1/d²_toBS** — far BS → fewer clusters optimal.

> [!TIP]
> **One-line memory anchor for the exam:**
> *"Cluster = Panchayat. LEACH elects Sarpanch by lottery, collects village reports, fuses them, and one messenger rides to the District HQ — saving fuel, time, and the messenger's life."* 🔋📡
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Time Synchronization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT634 - WIRELESS & SENSORS NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Time Synchronization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Time Synchronization** in Wireless Sensor Networks (WSNs) is the mechanism by which spatially distributed sensor nodes maintain a common notion of time (with bounded error) despite the presence of clock drift, skew, offset, and unpredictable network delays.

In a sensor network of *N* heterogeneous nodes, each node *i* possesses its own internal crystal oscillator whose frequency is non-identical to others. Over time, the local clock of each node drifts away from a global reference (such as UTC), which leads to inconsistencies in event ordering, data fusion, duty-cycling, beamforming, localization (TDOA-based), and cryptographic timestamping.

Mathematically, for node *i*, its local clock $C_i(t)$ at real time *t* is modelled as a first-order affine function:

$$
C_i(t) = a_i \cdot t + b_i
$$

where:
- $a_i$ = the **clock skew** (normalized frequency deviation, ideally 1)
- $b_i$ = the **clock offset** (phase difference from reference)
- $t$ = true / global reference time

**Synchronization** is achieved when, after the protocol converges, the residual pairwise error $\vert C_i(t) - C_j(t) \vert \le \epsilon$ for a bounded tolerance $\epsilon$.

## 1.2 Why Traditional Solutions Fail in WSN

| Traditional Method | Why it is unsuitable for WSN |
|---|---|
| **NTP (Network Time Protocol)** | Designed for the Internet; assumes symmetric wired links, predictable delays, and high-bandwidth connectivity — all false in WSN. |
| **GPS** | Requires Line-of-Sight (LoS) with at least 4 satellites; nodes are indoors, dense, energy-starved, and cost-sensitive — typically **$2–5** per GPS chip. |
| **PTP (IEEE 1588)** | Demands hardware timestamping in switches/routers; impractical in resource-constrained motes (e.g., TelosB, MicaZ). |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Module 4 mandates study of synchronization *requirements*, *challenges*, and *protocols* (RBS, TPSN, FTSP, LTS, Tiny/Mini-Sync) unique to resource-constrained multi-hop ad-hoc WSNs.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Chorus of Clocks"

Imagine **100 musicians** standing on a vast, foggy open field at night. None of them can see each other clearly, but each holds a slightly imperfect wristwatch. They must all play the same song **together**, on the beat, but their watches differ by small amounts, messages shouted across the field take varying times to arrive, and the shouting itself is loud and energy-draining.

- The **musicians** = sensor nodes
- The **fog** = lossy, unpredictable RF channel
- The **shouting** = packet exchange (energy = battery)
- The **imperfect watches** = oscillator drift/skew
- The **song beat** = synchronized global timeline

Each musician listens to a few shouts, estimates how his watch compares to the others, and corrects it. Some protocols use **one leader shouting the beat** (sender-receiver), while others use **everyone listening to a single thunderclap** (reference broadcast).

## 1.4 Why Time Sync is Needed in WSN — KTU-Mandated Use Cases

> [!IMPORTANT]
> 1. **TDMA / Duty Cycling** — slot assignment demands microsecond accuracy.
> 2. **Data Fusion / In-network Aggregation** — duplicate detection across nodes requires temporal ordering.
> 3. **Localization (TDOA, RSSI profiling)** — accuracy depends on $\Delta t$ precision.
> 4. **Cooperative Beamforming / MIMO** — phase alignment needs sub-µs sync.
> 5. **Security (Key-stream generation, replay defence)** — nonces depend on synchronized counters.
> 6. **Sleep Scheduling (S-MAC, B-MAC)** — coordinated wake-ups reduce idle listening.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Components of a WSN Clock

### 2.1.1 Frequency (Skew)
The oscillator's actual frequency deviates from its nominal value due to manufacturing tolerances (typically **±30 ppm to ±50 ppm** for low-cost MEMS crystals) and environmental factors (temperature, supply voltage, aging).

$$
a_i = \frac{f_{\text{actual},i}}{f_{\text{nominal}}}
$$

A drift of just **40 ppm** yields an error of:

$$
\Delta t = 40 \times 10^{-6} \times 86400 \text{ s} \approx 3.46 \text{ s/day}
$$

This is catastrophic for long-lived WSN deployments.

### 2.1.2 Offset
The instantaneous phase difference between node *i*'s clock and the global reference time:

$$
b_i(t) = C_i(t) - t
$$

### 2.1.3 Drift Rate (Aging)
Second-order rate of change of skew, expressed in **ppm/sec**, causing residual error even after skew/offset correction. Higher-end clocks (e.g., TCXO, OCXO) minimize drift but at higher cost/energy.

## 2.2 Sources of Synchronization Error

The total end-to-end synchronization error $\epsilon_{\text{total}}$ decomposes into four components:

$$
\epsilon_{\text{total}} = \epsilon_{\text{send}} + \epsilon_{\text{access}} + \epsilon_{\text{propagation}} + \epsilon_{\text{receive}}
$$

| Error Component | Cause | Typical Magnitude |
|---|---|---|
| $\epsilon_{\text{send}}$ | MAC/PHY encoding, interrupt handling | 0–100 µs |
| $\epsilon_{\text{access}}$ | Contention, back-off, channel busy | 0–500 ms |
| $\epsilon_{\text{propagation}}$ | RF propagation delay (~5 ns/m) | <1 µs (negligible) |
| $\epsilon_{\text{receive}}$ | Decoding, interrupt latency at receiver | 0–100 µs |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often quote propagation delay as the dominant error — it is actually the **smallest**. The dominant error in CSMA/CA WSNs is the **MAC access delay**, which is highly variable.

## 2.3 Synchronization Classification

### 2.3.1 By Direction
- **Sender-Receiver (SR)** — sender timestamps, receiver estimates offset (e.g., TPSN, FTSP).
- **Receiver-Receiver (RR)** — receiver compares timestamps of a common broadcast (e.g., RBS).
- **Receiver-only** — receiver listens without reply (passive, energy-efficient).

### 2.3.2 By Network Scope
- **Pairwise** — synchronize two nodes (1-hop).
- **Global** — all nodes agree on one common time.

### 2.3.3 By Architecture
- **Master-Slave (Tree)** — root broadcasts downward (e.g., TPSN).
- **Peer-to-Peer (Mesh)** — nodes negotiate locally (e.g., GTSP).
- **Diffusion / Flooding** — broadcast based (e.g., FTSP).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Equation / Concept | Mathematical Form | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Local clock model | $C_i(t) = a_i t + b_i$ | *t* in seconds, dimensionless *a* and *b* |
| 2 | Clock drift per day | $\Delta t = a_i \times 86400 - 86400$ | seconds/day |
| 3 | Pairwise offset | $\theta = T_{recv} - T_{send} - d_{\text{prop}}$ | seconds |
| 4 | Total sync error decomposition | $\epsilon = \epsilon_s + \epsilon_a + \epsilon_p + \epsilon_r$ | seconds |
| 5 | Time conversion (linear) | $t_{\text{global}} = a \cdot t_{\text{local}} + b$ | obtained via least-squares regression |
| 6 | Skew drift per °C | $\approx 0.04$ ppm/°C (typical XO) | ppm/°C |
| 7 | Hop-count error growth | $\epsilon_h \approx \sqrt{h} \cdot \epsilon_{\text{single-hop}}$ | for tree-based |
| 8 | Energy per sync (approx) | $E_{\text{sync}} \approx n \cdot E_{\text{tx}} + n \cdot E_{\text{rx}}$ | Joules |
| 9 | Maximum tolerable skew | $a_{\max} = 1 + \frac{\epsilon_{\text{tol}}}{T_{\text{interval}}}$ | dimensionless |
| 10 | Precision of FTSP | $\sigma \approx 1 \text{ µs (single-hop)}$ | empirical TinyOS measurement |

## 2.5 Engineering Utility

- **Smart Grids:** Phasor Measurement Units (PMUs) need **1 µs** sync for state estimation.
- **Environmental Monitoring:** Distributed acoustic sensing across forests requires coherent timestamping for triangulation.
- **Industrial IoT:** Profinet IRT and EtherCAT use synchronization as a foundation for real-time control loops.
- **Healthcare WSNs:** Synchronized ECG / EEG packets across body-area networks enable accurate QRS complex detection.

## 2.6 Operating Principle of WSN Synchronization Protocols (High-Yield)

Every WSN sync protocol follows a **3-phase logical skeleton**:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Phase 1 — Discovery / Pairing:** nodes identify neighbors (hello/handshake).
> **Phase 2 — Exchange / Estimation:** timestamps transmitted/received, offset/skew calculated.
> **Phase 3 — Correction / Update:** local clock or virtual clock model updated, periodically re-synced.

Different protocols (RBS, TPSN, FTSP, LTS, Tiny/Mini-Sync, GTSP) implement these phases differently.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Pairwise Offset Estimation (Two-Way Timing Exchange)

Consider two nodes *A* (master) and *B* (slave) performing a bidirectional packet exchange. This is the cornerstone of TPSN-style protocols.

### Step 1 — Define Variables
Let:
- $T_1$ = local time at *A* when *A* sends the request packet.
- $T_2$ = local time at *B* when *B* receives the request packet.
- $T_3$ = local time at *B* when *B* sends the response packet.
- $T_4$ = local time at *A* when *A* receives the response packet.

### Step 2 — Express B's Clock in Terms of A's Clock
Assume clocks are related linearly (small time interval $\Delta T$):

$$
T_B = a \cdot T_A + b
$$

For very small intervals we may approximate $a \approx 1$ (i.e., only offset matters, not skew).

### Step 3 — Write the Forward-Path Equation
The packet travels from *A* to *B* in propagation + access + send time, denoted $d_{\text{fwd}}$.

$$
T_2 = T_1 + d_{\text{fwd}} + \theta_{AB}
$$

where $\theta_{AB}$ is the offset of *A* as seen from *B* (i.e., $T_B = T_A + \theta_{AB}$).

Rearranging:

$$
\theta_{AB} = T_2 - T_1 - d_{\text{fwd}} \quad \text{...(Eq. A)}
$$

### Step 4 — Write the Reverse-Path Equation

$$
T_4 = T_3 + d_{\text{rev}} - \theta_{AB}
$$

Rearranging:

$$
\theta_{AB} = T_3 - T_4 + d_{\text{rev}} \quad \text{...(Eq. B)}
$$

### Step 5 — Assume Symmetric Delay (Classical TPSN Assumption)
If $d_{\text{fwd}} = d_{\text{rev}} = d$, add Eq. A and Eq. B:

$$
2\theta_{AB} = (T_2 - T_1) + (T_3 - T_4)
$$

$$
\theta_{AB} = \frac{(T_2 - T_1) - (T_4 - T_3)}{2}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Result:**
> $$
> \boxed{\;\theta_{AB} = \frac{(T_2 - T_1) - (T_4 - T_3)}{2}\;}
> $$
> The one-way propagation delay follows as:
> $$
> d = \frac{(T_4 - T_3) + (T_2 - T_1)}{2}
> $$

### Step 6 — KTU Pitfall — Asymmetric Delay
If $d_{\text{fwd}} \ne d_{\text{rev}}$, the above equations yield a **biased** estimate. The bias equals:

$$
\delta = \frac{d_{\text{fwd}} - d_{\text{rev}}}{2}
$$

This is why **FTSP uses MAC-layer timestamping** to minimize this bias.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Clock Skew and Offset via Least Squares

Long-term sync requires modeling both skew $a$ and offset $b$. The standard method is **linear regression** on $N$ timestamp pairs $(T_{\text{global},k}, T_{\text{local},k})$.

### Step 1 — Objective Function
Minimize the sum of squared residuals:

$$
J(a,b) = \sum_{k=1}^{N} \left( T_{\text{local},k} - (a T_{\text{global},k} + b) \right)^2
$$

### Step 2 — Take Partial Derivatives

$$
\frac{\partial J}{\partial a} = -2 \sum_k T_{\text{global},k} \left( T_{\text{local},k} - a T_{\text{global},k} - b \right) = 0
$$

$$
\frac{\partial J}{\partial b} = -2 \sum_k \left( T_{\text{local},k} - a T_{\text{global},k} - b \right) = 0
$$

### Step 3 — Solve the Normal Equations

$$
a \sum_k T_{\text{global},k}^2 + b \sum_k T_{\text{global},k} = \sum_k T_{\text{global},k} T_{\text{local},k}
$$

$$
a \sum_k T_{\text{global},k} + N b = \sum_k T_{\text{local},k}
$$

### Step 4 — Closed-Form Solutions

$$
\bar{T_g} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_k T_{\text{global},k}, \qquad \bar{T_l} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_k T_{\text{local},k}
$$

$$
a = \frac{\sum_k (T_{\text{global},k} - \bar{T_g})(T_{\text{local},k} - \bar{T_l})}{\sum_k (T_{\text{global},k} - \bar{T_g})^2}
$$

$$
b = \bar{T_l} - a \bar{T_g}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> FTSP uses exactly this regression over 8 timestamps by default to obtain both skew and offset.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Reference Broadcast Synchronization (RBS) Offset

In **RBS** (Elson et al., 2002), a transmitter broadcasts a beacon; receivers exchange their reception timestamps.

### Step 1 — Receiver *i* records $T_{i,\text{recv}}$
### Step 2 — Receiver *j* records $T_{j,\text{recv}}$

### Step 3 — Phase Offset Between i and j

$$
\boxed{\;\phi_{ij} = T_{i,\text{recv}} - T_{j,\text{recv}}\;}
$$

### Step 4 — Skew Estimation
Over $N$ beacons, perform linear regression on $\{(t_k, \phi_{ij,k})\}$ to obtain the relative skew between *i* and *j*.

### Step 5 — Key Insight (KTU High-Yield)
RBS **eliminates** the send-side and access-side error because the beacon is broadcast — every receiver's timestamp shares the **same** transmit instant. The remaining non-determinism is purely receive-side and order of magnitude smaller.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Protocol Comparison (KTU Board-Ready)

| Protocol | Class | Precision (single hop) | Energy | Complexity | Key Idea |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **RBS** | Receiver-Receiver | ~29 µs | High (extra packets) | Moderate | Eliminate sender jitter |
| **TPSN** | Sender-Receiver | ~17 µs | Moderate | Moderate | Two-way handshake + tree |
| **FTSP** | Sender-Receiver | ~1 µs | Low | High | MAC stamping + regression |
| **LTS** | Pairwise | ~30 µs | Lowest | Low | On-demand, centralized |
| **Tiny/Mini-Sync** | Pairwise | ~100 µs | Low | Very Low | Probabilistic bounds |
| **GTSP** | Peer-to-peer | ~10 µs | Moderate | High | Gradient, no flooding |
| **DMTS** | Sender-Receiver | ~30 µs | Low | Very Low | One-way, coarse |

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — TPSN Offset

> **Problem:** In a TPSN exchange, $T_1 = 1000$ µs, $T_2 = 1245$ µs, $T_3 = 1300$ µs, $T_4 = 1545$ µs. Compute the offset $\theta_{AB}$ and the one-way delay *d*.

### Solution

Step 1 — Compute forward trip $T_2 - T_1$:

$$
T_2 - T_1 = 1245 - 1000 = 245 \text{ µs}
$$

Step 2 — Compute reverse trip $T_4 - T_3$:

$$
T_4 - T_3 = 1545 - 1300 = 245 \text{ µs}
$$

Step 3 — Substitute into the offset formula:

$$
\theta_{AB} = \frac{(T_2 - T_1) - (T_4 - T_3)}{2} = \frac{245 - 245}{2} = 0 \text{ µs}
$$

So both nodes are already perfectly offset-aligned.

Step 4 — Compute one-way delay:

$$
d = \frac{(T_2 - T_1) + (T_4 - T_3)}{2} = \frac{245 + 245}{2} = 245 \text{ µs}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> The 245 µs one-way delay is suspiciously large for a 10 m WSN — MAC contention dominates. Always comment on the **plausibility** of computed values in board exams.

---

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example — Skew via Linear Regression

> **Problem:** A node records 4 paired timestamps $(T_g, T_l)$: $(0, 0)$, $(1, 1.001)$, $(2, 2.002)$, $(3, 3.004)$. Compute the skew $a$ and offset $b$.

### Solution

Step 1 — Compute means:

$$
\bar{T_g} = \frac{0+1+2+3}{4} = 1.5
$$

$$
\bar{T_l} = \frac{0+1.001+2.002+3.004}{4} = 1.50175
$$

Step 2 — Compute numerator (covariance):

$$
\sum (T_{g,k} - 1.5)(T_{l,k} - 1.50175)
$$

$$
= (-1.5)(-1.50175) + (-0.5)(-0.50075) + (0.5)(0.50025) + (1.5)(1.50225)
$$

$$
= 2.25263 + 0.25038 + 0.25013 + 2.25338 = 5.0065
$$

Step 3 — Compute denominator (variance):

$$
\sum (T_{g,k} - 1.5)^2 = 2.25 + 0.25 + 0.25 + 2.25 = 5.0
$$

Step 4 — Compute skew:

$$
a = \frac{5.0065}{5.0} = 1.0013
$$

Step 5 — Compute offset:

$$
b = \bar{T_l} - a \bar{T_g} = 1.50175 - 1.0013 \times 1.5 = 1.50175 - 1.50195 = -0.0002
$$

> **Interpretation:** The node's clock ticks **1.0013×** faster than the global reference and has a small negative offset of **−0.2 µs**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Generic WSN Time-Synchronization Stack

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Application Layer: TDMA Scheduling Localization Security Fusion] --> B[Network Layer: Multi-hop Sync Tree Construction]
    B --> C[Datalink Layer: MAC Layer Timestamping CSMA TDMA Coordination]
    C --> D[Physical Layer: RF Transceiver Crystal Oscillator Counter]
    D --> E[Clock Drift Skew Offset Accumulation]
    E --> B
```

## 4.2 TPSN Protocol Flow (Sender-Receiver, Two-Way)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant M as Master Node A
    participant S as Slave Node B
    M->>M: T1 = readLocalClock
    M->>S: SyncRequest with T1
    S->>S: T2 = readLocalClock on arrival
    S->>S: T3 = readLocalClock just before reply
    S->>M: SyncAck with T2 and T3
    M->>M: T4 = readLocalClock on arrival
    M->>M: Compute offset theta and delay d
    M->>S: OffsetCorrectionPacket
    S->>S: Apply correction to local clock
```

## 4.3 RBS Protocol Flow (Receiver-Receiver)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant T as Transmitter B
    participant R1 as Receiver A
    participant R2 as Receiver C
    T->>R1: Beacon Broadcast
    T->>R2: Beacon Broadcast same instant
    R1->>R1: Record T_A_recv
    R2->>R2: Record T_C_recv
    R1->>R2: Exchange Timestamps
    R2->>R1: Exchange Timestamps
    R1->>R1: Compute offset phi AC
    R2->>R2: Compute offset phi CA
```

## 4.4 FTSP Operational Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Sender
        S1[Application Event] --> S2[MAC Layer Timestamp T1]
        S2 --> S3[Radio Transmit]
    end
    S3 -->|Over air| R1[Radio Receive]
    subgraph Receiver
        R1 --> R2[MAC Layer Timestamp T2]
        R2 --> R3[Store in Regression Buffer]
        R3 --> R4[Linear Regression over N pairs]
        R4 --> R5[Compute Skew and Offset]
        R5 --> R6[Update Local Clock Model]
    end
    R6 --> R7[Periodic Re-sync Trigger]
```

## 4.5 Multi-Hop Synchronization Tree (TPSN/GTSP Topology)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ROOT[Root Node Global Reference] --> H1A[Level 1 Node A]
    ROOT --> H1B[Level 1 Node B]
    H1A --> H2A[Level 2 Node A1]
    H1A --> H2B[Level 2 Node A2]
    H1B --> H2C[Level 2 Node B1]
    H2A --> H3A[Level 3 Leaf]
    H2A --> H3B[Level 3 Leaf]
    H2B --> H3C[Level 3 Leaf]
    H2C --> H3D[Level 3 Leaf]
    H2C --> H3E[Level 3 Leaf]
    H2C --> H3F[Level 3 Leaf]
```

## 4.6 Error Source Decomposition Block

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Sender Node Crystal Oscillator] -->|epsilon send| B[MAC Layer Encode Queue]
    B -->|epsilon access| C[CSMA CA Contention Back-off]
    C -->|epsilon propagation ~ 5 ns per metre| D[Wireless Channel Fading Noise]
    D -->|epsilon receive| E[Receiver MAC Decode Interrupt]
    E --> F[Local Timestamp Counter]
    F --> G[Pairwise Offset and Skew Estimate]
```

## 4.7 Clock Drift Compensation Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CLK[Local Oscillator f nominal] --> DIV[Frequency Divider]
    DIV --> CNT[Hardware Counter 32 bit or 64 bit]
    CNT --> CAL[Calibration Module reads N pairs]
    CAL --> REG[Linear Regression Skew and Offset]
    REG --> CORR[Apply Correction C corrected = a C raw + b]
    CORR --> OUT[Synchronized Time Output]
    OUT --> FEED[Periodic Resync Trigger every T resync seconds]
    FEED --> CAL
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**`[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`**
*What is meant by clock skew and clock offset in WSNs? Why are they important in time synchronization?*

**Model Answer:**
Clock skew $a_i$ represents the deviation of the oscillator frequency from its nominal value (i.e., how fast the clock ticks relative to true time). Clock offset $b_i$ represents the instantaneous phase difference between the node's local clock and the global reference time. They are fundamental because every node has its own imperfect oscillator, and without correction, the local clocks of distributed nodes drift apart, causing incorrect event ordering, data fusion errors, and TDMA misalignment.

---

### Question 2
**`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`**
*List any three time synchronization protocols used in WSNs and mention their key idea in one line each.*

**Model Answer:**
1. **RBS** — uses receiver-receiver comparison by eliminating sender-side jitter via broadcast beacons.
2. **TPSN** — performs sender-receiver two-way handshake along a spanning tree for pairwise synchronization.
3. **FTSP** — uses MAC-layer timestamping and linear regression on multiple packets to estimate both skew and offset with high precision.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1)
**`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / Model Paper 2024]`**

#### (a) [7 Marks] Explain the Reference Broadcast Synchronization (RBS) protocol in detail with a timing diagram. Discuss its advantages and limitations.

**Model Answer:**

**Concept:** RBS (Reference Broadcast Synchronization), proposed by Elson, Girod and Estrin (2002), eliminates the dominant non-determinism of the sender by using a *receiver-receiver* synchronization model.

**Operation Steps:**
1. A transmitter node sends a reference beacon containing no timestamp.
2. All receivers in broadcast range record the local reception time $T_{i,\text{recv}}$.
3. Every pair of receivers exchanges their reception timestamps through the network.
4. Each receiver computes the phase offset:
$$
\phi_{ij} = T_{i,\text{recv}} - T_{j,\text{recv}}
$$
5. Multiple beacons are exchanged; least-squares regression gives the relative skew.

**Timing Diagram:**

```
   Beacon ──────► Receiver A  rec@ T_A
   Beacon ──────► Receiver B  rec@ T_B
   Beacon ──────► Receiver C  rec@ T_C
        then pairwise exchange of (T_A, T_B, T_C)
```

**Advantages:**
- Removes send-side and access-side jitter (same transmit instant).
- Higher precision than NTP for short-range WSNs.
- Works for both single-hop and multi-hop (via gateway translation).

**Limitations:**
- Requires *N(N−1)/2* timestamp exchanges per beacon — high energy cost.
- Needs additional messaging infrastructure.
- Not suitable for very large or sparse networks.

---

#### (b) [7 Marks] With a neat diagram, explain the Timing-sync Protocol for Sensor Networks (TPSN). Derive the offset equation.

**Model Answer:**

**Concept:** TPSN, proposed by Ganeriwal, Kumar and Srivastava (2003), creates a spanning tree rooted at a master node and performs pairwise two-way timing exchange.

**Phases:**
1. **Level Discovery Phase:** Root broadcasts; nodes assign themselves increasing levels.
2. **Synchronization Phase:** A node at level *l* synchronizes with a node at level *l−1* via two-way exchange.

**Timing Diagram:**

```
   T1 @ A ─── packet ───► T2 @ B
   T4 @ A ◄── packet ──── T3 @ B
```

**Derivation:**
Assuming symmetric delay *d* and offset $\theta_{AB}$:

$$
T_2 = T_1 + d + \theta_{AB}
$$

$$
T_4 = T_3 + d - \theta_{AB}
$$

Adding both equations:

$$
\theta_{AB} = \frac{(T_2 - T_1) - (T_4 - T_3)}{2}
$$

**Limitation:** Assumes symmetric MAC delay, which is unrealistic in CSMA/CA — addressed by FTSP via MAC-layer timestamping.

---

### Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative Set)

#### (a) [7 Marks] Discuss the various sources of synchronization error in WSNs. Which is the dominant source and why?

**Model Answer:**

The total error is decomposed as:

$$
\epsilon = \epsilon_{\text{send}} + \epsilon_{\text{access}} + \epsilon_{\text{propagation}} + \epsilon_{\text{receive}}
$$

1. **Send-side error $\epsilon_{\text{send}}$:** Interrupt latency, OS scheduling, encoding time at the sender MAC/PHY. Order of microseconds.
2. **MAC access error $\epsilon_{\text{access}}$:** Back-off, contention, retransmissions under CSMA/CA. **Order of milliseconds — dominant.**
3. **Propagation error $\epsilon_{\text{propagation}}$:** RF speed $\approx 3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s; ~5 ns per metre — negligible.
4. **Receive-side error $\epsilon_{\text{receive}}$:** Decoding, interrupt handling — order of microseconds.

**Dominant source:** The MAC access delay is dominant because WSNs use contention-based MAC (CSMA/CA), and the random back-off window can vary from 0 to several slot times (e.g., 320 µs in IEEE 802.15.4). FTSP mitigates this by timestamping *inside* the MAC layer at the exact radio interrupt.

---

#### (b) [7 Marks] Explain the Flooding Time Synchronization Protocol (FTSP). Why does it achieve microsecond precision?

**Model Answer:**

**Architecture:**
- **Single sink** broadcasts SYNC packets.
- **Multiple receivers** perform MAC-layer timestamping on both send and receive.
- **Linear regression** on 8 timestamps per node yields skew $a$ and offset $b$.

**Key Features:**
1. **MAC-layer timestamping** removes send/receive jitter.
2. **Flooding-based dissemination** (no tree maintenance) — robust to topology changes.
3. **Compensation for byte alignment, encoding delay, propagation delay.**
4. **Multiple-sender election** — if a master fails, next node becomes leader.
5. **Cyclic redundancy check (CRC) + skew/offset regression** for robustness.

**Why microsecond precision?**
- Timestamping occurs *inside* the radio ISR, after the Start Frame Delimiter (SFD).
- The interrupt latency variance (jitter) is reduced to < 1 µs.
- Regression smooths out residual noise.

**Performance:** Empirically achieves ~1 µs average error and ~3 µs maximum error across a 6-hop network on Mica2 motes (TinyOS benchmark).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall 1 — Skipping the derivation.** Many students directly quote the formula $\theta_{AB} = \frac{(T_2 - T_1) - (T_4 - T_3)}{2}$ without deriving it. **Always show the two equations and the algebraic elimination** — KTU valuation key awards 4 of 7 marks specifically for derivation.
>
> **Pitfall 2 — Confusing "offset" with "delay."** Offset $\theta$ is the phase difference between two clocks; delay *d* is the one-way travel time of a packet. They are **not the same.**
>
> **Pitfall 3 — Forgetting to mention symmetric-delay assumption.** TPSN's offset formula is valid only if $d_{\text{fwd}} = d_{\text{rev}}$. Failing to state this assumption loses 1–2 marks.
>
> **Pitfall 4 — Ignoring propagation delay vs MAC delay magnitude.** Writing "propagation is the dominant error" is factually wrong — it is the smallest contributor. Examiners deduct 1 mark.
>
> **Pitfall 5 — Skew without offset, or vice versa.** A correct clock model includes *both* skew and offset. Mentioning only one is incomplete.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Time Synchronization (Module 4)**
>
> 1. **Clock model:** $C_i(t) = a_i t + b_i$ — skew $a_i$, offset $b_i$.
> 2. **Drift** = rate of change of skew with time / temperature.
> 3. **Error decomposition:** $\epsilon = \epsilon_s + \epsilon_a + \epsilon_p + \epsilon_r$ — MAC access delay is dominant.
> 4. **Propagation delay** is *negligible* (~5 ns/m).
> 5. **NTP/GPS unsuitable** for WSNs — energy, cost, LoS constraints.
> 6. **TPSN:** two-way handshake, tree-based, assumes symmetric delay.
> 7. **Offset formula (TPSN):** $\theta_{AB} = \frac{(T_2 - T_1) - (T_4 - T_3)}{2}$.
> 8. **Delay formula:** $d = \frac{(T_2 - T_1) + (T_4 - T_3)}{2}$.
> 9. **RBS:** receiver-receiver; eliminates send/access jitter.
> 10. **FTSP:** MAC-layer timestamping + linear regression → ~1 µs precision.
> 11. **LTS:** lightweight, on-demand, least overhead.
> 12. **Tiny/Mini-Sync:** probabilistic bounds using two-message exchange.
> 13. **GTSP:** gradient-based, no flooding, suited for dense networks.
> 14. **DMTS:** delay measurement time synchronization, low-cost coarse sync.
> 15. **Energy cost of sync:** $E_{\text{sync}} = n \cdot E_{\text{tx}} + n \cdot E_{\text{rx}}$.
> 16. **Hop-count error growth:** ~$\sqrt{h}$ for tree-based protocols.
> 17. **Linear regression** used to extract skew + offset from N timestamp pairs.
> 18. **MAC-layer timestamping** is the single most effective precision-enhancement technique.
> 19. **Use cases:** TDMA, localization (TDOA), data fusion, security, beamforming.
> 20. **Threshold of ~1 µs** is the practical engineering limit for WSN-class hardware.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Localization and Positioning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT634 - WIRELESS & SENSORS NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Localization and Positioning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Localization** in Wireless Sensor Networks (WSN) is the process of determining the spatial coordinates (typically in 2D or 3D Cartesian space) of sensor nodes whose positions are unknown, by exploiting measurements such as signal strength, time of arrival, angle of arrival, or connectivity information exchanged with a small set of reference nodes (called **anchor nodes** or **beacons**) whose locations are known a priori (often via GPS).

**Positioning** is the broader engineering discipline that subsumes localization, covering both the estimation of absolute geographical position and relative position with respect to neighbouring nodes.

Mathematically, for an unknown node $U$ and a set of $n$ anchor nodes $A_i$ with known positions $\mathbf{x}_i = (x_i, y_i)$, the localization problem seeks to estimate:

$$\hat{\mathbf{p}} = (\hat{x}, \hat{y}) \quad \text{or} \quad \hat{\mathbf{p}} = (\hat{x}, \hat{y}, \hat{z})$$

such that some error metric (typically Mean Squared Error) between the estimated position and the true position $\mathbf{p}^*$ is minimised:

$$\hat{\mathbf{p}} = \arg\min_{\mathbf{p}} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left[ \phi_i(\mathbf{p}) - d_i \right]^2$$

where $d_i$ is the measured distance/proximity between $U$ and $A_i$, and $\phi_i(\mathbf{p})$ is the geometric distance function.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**: Localization is the mechanism by which a sensor node estimates its own geographic coordinates using information from anchor nodes. It is fundamental because **location-tagged sensor data is meaningless without positional context** (e.g., "temperature = 38°C" is useless without knowing *where*).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy: Finding Yourself in a New City

Imagine you have just landed in an unfamiliar city (your sensor node has just been deployed). You need to find your current location on the city map. You have the following options:

1. **Use landmarks you can see (range-free / proximity)**: "I am inside the red-curve zone that contains the Central Library and City Hall." → This is **proximity-based localization** (like the Centroid or APIT algorithm).
2. **Measure how far each landmark is (range-based / ranging)**: "The Central Library is about 800 m away, and City Hall is 1.2 km away." By drawing circles of these radii on the map, their intersection gives your position. → This is **trilateration**.
3. **Measure the angle to each landmark (directional)**: "City Hall lies at 45° North-East, the railway station at 120° South-East." Two angle lines intersect at your position. → This is **triangulation**.
4. **Ask friends (hop counts)**: "My friend who knows the city is 3 streets away; each street averages 200 m, so I am roughly 600 m from her. Her position is known." → This is the **DV-Hop algorithm**.

The **known-position friends** are the *anchor nodes*. The **unknown you** is the *unknown (target) sensor node*. The **city map** is the *coordinate reference frame*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Localization is Hard in WSN:**
> - Sensors have **limited energy, memory, and computational power**.
> - **GPS receivers are expensive and power-hungry** — cannot be installed on every node.
> - Indoor / underwater / forest environments have severe **multipath fading and signal attenuation**.
> - Anchor nodes are typically a small fraction (often < 10%) of the total network.

---

## 1.3 Core Terminology

> [!NOTE]
> **Essential Vocabulary (Board-Exam High Frequency):**

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Anchor / Beacon node** | A sensor node with known, hard-coded or GPS-derived position; serves as a reference point. |
| **Unknown / Target node** | A sensor node whose position is to be estimated. |
| **Range-based localization** | Uses distance/angle measurements (RSSI, ToA, TDoA, AoA) between nodes. |
| **Range-free localization** | Uses only connectivity, hop counts, or proximity — no explicit distance. |
| **Trilateration** | Position estimation from distances to ≥ 3 anchors (2D) or ≥ 4 anchors (3D). |
| **Multilateration** | Generalized trilateration using more than the minimum anchors, with statistical estimation. |
| **Triangulation** | Position estimation from measured angles to ≥ 2 anchors with known baseline. |
| **Proximity-based** | Position estimated as the region containing nearby anchors (coarse-grained). |
| **GPS (Global Positioning System)** | Satellite-based absolute positioning system; requires line-of-sight to ≥ 4 satellites. |
| **Localization accuracy** | The mean Euclidean distance between estimated and true positions, expressed in metres. |

---

## 1.4 Classification of Localization Techniques

The KTU syllabus broadly classifies positioning techniques along three orthogonal axes:

1. **By information type**: Range-based vs. Range-free
2. **By granularity**: Fine-grained vs. Coarse-grained
3. **By deployment context**: Indoor vs. Outdoor

```
Localization & Positioning
 ├── Range-Based
 │    ├── RSSI (Received Signal Strength Indicator)
 │    ├── ToA / TDoA (Time of Arrival / Time Difference of Arrival)
 │    └── AoA (Angle of Arrival)
 ├── Range-Free
 │    ├── Centroid
 │    ├── APIT (Approximate Point-In-Triangulation)
 │    ├── DV-Hop (Distance Vector – Hop)
 │    ├── Amorphous
 │    └── Gradient / Convex Position Estimation
 └── Hybrid (Range + Connectivity)
```

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Range-Based Localization Techniques

### 2.1.1 RSSI (Received Signal Strength Indicator)

RSSI measures the power of the received radio signal at the receiver. Using a radio propagation model, this power is converted into an estimated distance.

**Log-Normal Shadowing Path Loss Model** (the standard model used in WSN localization):

$$PL(d) = PL(d_0) + 10 n \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{d}{d_0}\right) + X_\sigma$$

where:
- $PL(d)$ = path loss (dB) at distance $d$
- $PL(d_0)$ = path loss at reference distance $d_0$ (typically **1 m**)
- $n$ = **path loss exponent** (ranges from **2** in free space to **4–6** in obstructed environments)
- $X_\sigma$ = zero-mean Gaussian random variable with standard deviation $\sigma$ (in dB), modelling shadow fading.

The RSSI in dBm is related to path loss by:

$$RSSI = P_t - PL(d)$$

where $P_t$ is the transmit power. Solving for $d$:

$$d = d_0 \cdot 10^{\frac{PL(d_0) - RSSI}{10 n}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Advantages**: No additional hardware; uses existing radio.
> **Disadvantages**: Highly susceptible to multipath, NLOS (non-line-of-sight), and environmental dynamics; typical accuracy **3–5 m** in good conditions, much worse indoors.

---

### 2.1.2 ToA (Time of Arrival)

ToA measures the **one-way propagation time** of a signal between two nodes. The distance is:

$$d = c \cdot t_{prop}$$

where $c$ = speed of light ($\approx 3 \times 10^8$ m/s) for RF, or $c_{sound} \approx 343$ m/s for acoustic.

**Critical requirement**: Tight clock synchronisation between transmitter and receiver.

**Estimated distance variance** (Cramér-Rao Lower Bound):

$$\sigma_d^2 \geq \frac{c^2}{8 \pi^2 \cdot SNR \cdot \beta^2}$$

where $\beta$ is the effective signal bandwidth (Hz) and $SNR$ is the signal-to-noise ratio.

> [!WARNING]
> A timing error of just **1 microsecond (1 μs)** at the speed of light gives a distance error of **300 m** — ToA demands nanosecond-level synchronisation.

---

### 2.1.3 TDoA (Time Difference of Arrival)

TDoA measures the **difference in arrival times** of two signals — typically one RF and one acoustic (ultrasonic) — transmitted simultaneously by the anchor.

$$d_1 - d_2 = c \cdot (t_1 - t_2)$$

If signal 1 travels at speed $v_1$ and signal 2 at speed $v_2$:

$$d = (v_1 - v_2) \cdot \Delta t$$

The locus of constant TDoA is a **hyperbola** with the two anchors as foci. Two such hyperbolas intersect to give the position.

**Advantage over ToA**: Only the anchors need synchronised clocks; the unknown node does not.

---

### 2.1.4 AoA (Angle of Arrival)

AoA uses directional antennas or antenna arrays to estimate the angle $\theta_i$ at which the signal from anchor $A_i$ arrives.

$$\theta_i = \arctan\!\left(\frac{y_i - y}{x_i - x}\right)$$

Each anchor contributes one angular constraint. **Two anchors** (with a known baseline) suffice for 2D positioning via triangulation. **Three anchors** are needed for 3D.

**Hardware requirement**: Antenna array or rotating directional antenna — increases node cost and power consumption.

---

## 2.2 Range-Free Localization Techniques

### 2.2.1 Centroid Algorithm

The simplest range-free scheme. The unknown node estimates its position as the **arithmetic mean** of the positions of all anchors within radio range:

$$\hat{\mathbf{p}} = \frac{1}{k} \sum_{i=1}^{k} (x_i, y_i)$$

where $k$ is the number of heard anchors.

**Accuracy**: Depends on anchor density. Error roughly equals half the inter-anchor spacing.

---

### 2.2.2 DV-Hop (Distance Vector – Hop)

A three-stage distributed algorithm proposed by **Niculescu and Nath (2001)**:

1. **Stage 1 — Hop-count propagation**: Each anchor floods the network with a packet containing its position and a hop-count initialised to 0. Every node maintains a table of the minimum hop-count to each anchor.
2. **Stage 2 — Average one-hop distance**: Each anchor $A_i$ estimates the average physical distance per hop:

$$HopSize_i = \frac{\sum_{j \neq i} \sqrt{(x_i - x_j)^2 + (y_i - y_j)^2}}{\sum_{j \neq i} h_{ij}}$$

where $h_{ij}$ is the minimum hop count between anchors $A_i$ and $A_j$. Anchors broadcast their $HopSize$ with a controlled flooding (only the first received value is used by other nodes).
3. **Stage 3 — Position estimation**: Each unknown node multiplies its hop count to anchor $A_i$ by the HopSize, then applies trilateration/multilateration.

---

### 2.2.3 APIT (Approximate Point-In-Triangulation)

The unknown node collects all anchors it hears. For every combination of 3 anchors forming a triangle, the node tests whether it lies **inside** that triangle (using a neighbour-based "Point-In-Triangle" test). The final estimate is the **centroid of the intersection region** of all containing triangles.

---

## 2.3 Triangulation — Geometric Position from Angles

Consider two anchors $A_1$ and $A_2$ separated by a known baseline $d_{12}$. The unknown node measures angles $\theta_1$ and $\theta_2$ to them.

Using the **Law of Sines** in triangle $\triangle UA_1A_2$:

$$\frac{d_{12}}{\sin(\pi - \theta_1 - \theta_2)} = \frac{d_1}{\sin \theta_2} = \frac{d_2}{\sin \theta_1}$$

Solving:

$$d_1 = \frac{d_{12} \cdot \sin \theta_2}{\sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2)}, \quad d_2 = \frac{d_{12} \cdot \sin \theta_1}{\sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2)}$$

Once distances $d_1, d_2$ are known, position follows from 2-D trilateration.

---

## 2.4 Trilateration — Position from Distances

Given three anchors $A_1(x_1, y_1), A_2(x_2, y_2), A_3(x_3, y_3)$ at distances $d_1, d_2, d_3$ from the unknown node $U(x, y)$:

$$(x - x_1)^2 + (y - y_1)^2 = d_1^2$$
$$(x - x_2)^2 + (y - y_2)^2 = d_2^2$$
$$(x - x_3)^2 + (y - y_3)^2 = d_3^2$$

Subtracting the first equation from the second and third gives a **linear system** $\mathbf{A}\mathbf{p} = \mathbf{b}$:

$$\begin{bmatrix} 2(x_3 - x_1) & 2(y_3 - y_1) \\ 2(x_2 - x_1) & 2(y_2 - y_1) \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} x_3^2 - x_1^2 + y_3^2 - y_1^2 + d_1^2 - d_3^2 \\ x_2^2 - x_1^2 + y_2^2 - y_1^2 + d_1^2 - d_2^2 \end{bmatrix}$$

The unknown position is:

$$\begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \end{bmatrix} = \mathbf{A}^{-1} \mathbf{b}$$

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table summarises every formula you must know for KTU exam questions on this topic. Memorise both the equation and the underlying assumption.

| # | Technique | Core Equation | Key Parameters | Typical Accuracy |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | RSSI distance | $d = d_0 \cdot 10^{\frac{PL(d_0)-RSSI}{10n}}$ | Path loss exp. $n$, ref. dist. $d_0$ | 3–5 m |
| 2 | ToA distance | $d = c \cdot \Delta t$ | Speed of signal $c$, time $\Delta t$ | 1–3 m |
| 3 | TDoA distance | $d = (v_1 - v_2)\Delta t$ | Two signal speeds $v_1, v_2$ | < 1 m (acoustic) |
| 4 | AoA angle | $\theta = \arctan\!\left(\frac{\Delta y}{\Delta x}\right)$ | Antenna array geometry | 2–5° angular |
| 5 | Centroid | $\hat{\mathbf{p}} = \frac{1}{k}\sum_i \mathbf{x}_i$ | $k$ = heard anchors | ~½ inter-anchor spacing |
| 6 | DV-Hop avg hop | $HopSize = \frac{\sum_j d_{ij}}{\sum_j h_{ij}}$ | Pairwise distances, hop counts | Network-dependent |
| 7 | Trilateration | $\mathbf{A}\mathbf{p} = \mathbf{b}$ | 3 anchors (2D), 4 anchors (3D) | Sub-metre ideal |
| 8 | Path loss | $PL(d) = PL(d_0) + 10n\log_{10}\frac{d}{d_0} + X_\sigma$ | $n$, $\sigma$ | N/A (model) |
| 9 | GPS range | $d_i = \sqrt{(x-x_{s,i})^2+(y-y_{s,i})^2+(z-z_{s,i})^2}$ | 4 satellites minimum | 5–10 m |
| 10 | Localization error | $\epsilon = \Vert \hat{\mathbf{p}} - \mathbf{p}^* \Vert_2$ | L2 norm of position error | Reported in metres |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Environmental monitoring**: Forest fire sensors need to report the GPS coordinates of smoke detection.
- **Healthcare WSN**: Patient-worn sensors log location of falls in a hospital.
- **Smart agriculture**: Soil-moisture sensors in a field report their grid position.
- **Industrial IoT (IIoT)**: Asset tracking in warehouses.
- **Underwater WSN**: Localization using acoustic ToA — GPS does not work underwater.
- **Military / battlefield**: Soldier and vehicle tracking.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — RSSI Distance from Log-Normal Path Loss Model

We start from the received power relationship. The transmitted power $P_t$ (dBm) minus the path loss gives the RSSI:

$$RSSI = P_t - PL(d)$$

Substituting the log-normal path loss expression:

$$RSSI = P_t - \left[ PL(d_0) + 10 n \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{d}{d_0}\right) + X_\sigma \right]$$

$$RSSI = P_t - PL(d_0) - X_\sigma - 10 n \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{d}{d_0}\right)$$

Rearranging to isolate the log term:

$$RSSI - P_t + PL(d_0) + X_\sigma = - 10 n \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{d}{d_0}\right)$$

Multiplying both sides by $-1$:

$$10 n \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{d}{d_0}\right) = P_t - PL(d_0) - X_\sigma - RSSI$$

Dividing by $10n$:

$$\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{d}{d_0}\right) = \frac{P_t - PL(d_0) - X_\sigma - RSSI}{10 n}$$

Exponentiating (base 10):

$$\frac{d}{d_0} = 10^{\frac{P_t - PL(d_0) - X_\sigma - RSSI}{10 n}}$$

Hence the **final RSSI distance formula**:

$$\boxed{\,d = d_0 \cdot 10^{\frac{P_t - PL(d_0) - X_\sigma - RSSI}{10 n}}\,}$$

**Numerical example**: Let $P_t = 0$ dBm, $PL(d_0) = 40$ dB at $d_0 = 1$ m, $n = 2$, $X_\sigma = 0$ (ideal), and let $RSSI = -60$ dBm. Then:

$$\frac{d}{1} = 10^{\frac{0 - 40 - 0 - (-60)}{10 \times 2}} = 10^{\frac{20}{20}} = 10^1 = 10$$

So the estimated distance is **10 m**. ✓

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Trilateration Closed-Form Solution

We have three equations (writing the squared distances and subtracting pairwise):

$$(x - x_1)^2 + (y - y_1)^2 = d_1^2 \quad (1)$$
$$(x - x_2)^2 + (y - y_2)^2 = d_2^2 \quad (2)$$
$$(x - x_3)^2 + (y - y_3)^2 = d_3^2 \quad (3)$$

**Step A** — Expand each equation:
$$x^2 - 2xx_1 + x_1^2 + y^2 - 2yy_1 + y_1^2 = d_1^2$$
$$x^2 - 2xx_2 + x_2^2 + y^2 - 2yy_2 + y_2^2 = d_2^2$$
$$x^2 - 2xx_3 + x_3^2 + y^2 - 2yy_3 + y_3^2 = d_3^2$$

**Step B** — Subtract (1) from (2):
$$-2x(x_2 - x_1) - 2y(y_2 - y_1) + (x_2^2 - x_1^2) + (y_2^2 - y_1^2) = d_2^2 - d_1^2$$

Multiply by $-1$ and use the identity $(a^2 - b^2) = (a-b)(a+b)$:

$$2(x_2 - x_1)x + 2(y_2 - y_1)y = (x_2^2 - x_1^2) + (y_2^2 - y_1^2) + d_1^2 - d_2^2$$

This is **Equation (A)**.

**Step C** — Subtract (1) from (3) similarly:
$$2(x_3 - x_1)x + 2(y_3 - y_1)y = (x_3^2 - x_1^2) + (y_3^2 - y_1^2) + d_1^2 - d_3^2$$

This is **Equation (B)**.

**Step D** — Write in matrix form $\mathbf{A}\mathbf{p} = \mathbf{b}$:

$$\begin{bmatrix} 2(x_2 - x_1) & 2(y_2 - y_1) \\ 2(x_3 - x_1) & 2(y_3 - y_1) \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} x_2^2 - x_1^2 + y_2^2 - y_1^2 + d_1^2 - d_2^2 \\ x_3^2 - x_1^2 + y_3^2 - y_1^2 + d_1^2 - d_3^2 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step E** — Invert $\mathbf{A}$. Determinant:

$$\det(\mathbf{A}) = 4 \left[ (x_2 - x_1)(y_3 - y_1) - (x_3 - x_1)(y_2 - y_1) \right]$$

The position estimate:

$$\begin{bmatrix} \hat{x} \\ \hat{y} \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{\det(\mathbf{A})} \begin{bmatrix} 2(y_3 - y_1) & -2(y_2 - y_1) \\ -2(x_3 - x_1) & 2(x_2 - x_1) \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x_2^2 - x_1^2 + y_2^2 - y_1^2 + d_1^2 - d_2^2 \\ x_3^2 - x_1^2 + y_3^2 - y_1^2 + d_1^2 - d_3^2 \end{bmatrix}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The matrix $\mathbf{A}$ must be **non-singular**, i.e., its determinant must not be zero. Geometrically, this means the three anchors must be **non-collinear**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Centroid Algorithm Position Estimate

Suppose unknown node $U$ hears $k$ anchors at positions $(x_i, y_i)$ for $i = 1, \dots, k$. The Centroid algorithm defines:

$$\hat{x} = \frac{1}{k} \sum_{i=1}^{k} x_i$$

$$\hat{y} = \frac{1}{k} \sum_{i=1}^{k} y_i$$

**Mean squared error analysis** — let $\mathbf{p}^* = (x^*, y^*)$ be the true position. The error is:

$$\epsilon^2 = (\hat{x} - x^*)^2 + (\hat{y} - y^*)^2$$

For anchors uniformly distributed in a disk of radius $R$, the expected squared error is approximately:

$$\mathbb{E}[\epsilon^2] \approx \frac{R^2}{k}$$

Hence accuracy improves with $\mathbf{1/\sqrt{k}}$ — quadrupling the number of anchors halves the error.

**Example**: If anchors are at $(0,0), (10,0), (10,10), (0,10)$ — i.e., a square of side 10 m — and the node hears all 4:

$$\hat{x} = \frac{0+10+10+0}{4} = 5, \quad \hat{y} = \frac{0+0+10+10}{4} = 5$$

The estimate is the centre of the square, $(5, 5)$.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — DV-Hop Average Hop Distance

Let anchor $A_i$ have known position $(x_i, y_i)$. After Stage 1, $A_i$ knows the minimum hop count $h_{ij}$ to every other anchor $A_j$. Stage 2 computes:

$$HopSize_i = \frac{\sum_{j=1, j \neq i}^{n_A} \sqrt{(x_i - x_j)^2 + (y_i - y_j)^2}}{\sum_{j=1, j \neq i}^{n_A} h_{ij}}$$

**Interpretation**: $HopSize_i$ is the average Euclidean distance covered per hop in the local neighbourhood of $A_i$.

For an unknown node $U$, the estimated distance to anchor $A_i$ is:

$$\hat{d}_{Ui} = h_{Ui} \cdot HopSize_{nearest}$$

where $h_{Ui}$ is the minimum hop count from $U$ to $A_i$, and $HopSize_{nearest}$ is the HopSize of the anchor $A_i$ closest in hop count to $U$ (to minimise propagation error).

**Example**: $A_1$ knows distances to 3 other anchors $A_2, A_3, A_4$ as $30$ m, $40$ m, $50$ m, with hop counts $2, 3, 4$. Then:

$$HopSize_1 = \frac{30 + 40 + 50}{2 + 3 + 4} = \frac{120}{9} \approx 13.33 \text{ m/hop}$$

If $U$ is 5 hops from $A_1$, the estimated distance is $5 \times 13.33 = 66.67$ m.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — GPS Pseudorange to Position

GPS uses **pseudorange** measurements from $\geq 4$ satellites. Satellite $i$ has known position $(x_{s,i}, y_{s,i}, z_{s,i})$. The pseudorange is:

$$\rho_i = c \cdot (t_{received,i} - t_{transmitted,i}) = d_i + c \cdot \delta t_u + \epsilon_i$$

where:
- $d_i = \sqrt{(x - x_{s,i})^2 + (y - y_{s,i})^2 + (z - z_{s,i})^2}$ is the true geometric range,
- $\delta t_u$ is the receiver clock bias (unknown),
- $\epsilon_i$ is measurement noise.

Linearising by subtracting the $i=1$ equation (the same "subtraction trick" used in trilateration):

$$\rho_i - \rho_1 = d_i - d_1 + c \cdot (\epsilon_i - \epsilon_1)$$

Iterative least-squares or Bancroft's algorithm then yields $(x, y, z, \delta t_u)$. Hence **4 satellites are needed for 3D + time** (compared to 3 anchors for 2D trilateration without time).

---

## 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Range-Based vs Range-Free

| Criterion | Range-Based (RSSI/ToA/TDoA/AoA) | Range-Free (Centroid/DV-Hop/APIT) |
|---|---|---|
| **Hardware cost** | High (ToA: precise clock; AoA: antenna array) | Low (uses existing radio) |
| **Energy consumption** | High | Low |
| **Accuracy** | High (metres or sub-metres) | Moderate (tens of metres) |
| **Scalability** | Limited by measurement overhead | Excellent |
| **Environmental sensitivity** | High (multipath, NLOS) | Low |
| **Computation** | Trigonometric (light) | Combinatorial (heavier for APIT) |
| **Anchor density required** | Low (3–4 anchors) | High (dense deployment) |
| **KTU exam favourite** | Trilateration derivation | DV-Hop, Centroid formulas |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Taxonomy of Localization Techniques

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Localization and Positioning] --> B[Range Based]
    A --> C[Range Free]
    A --> D[Hybrid]
    B --> B1[RSSI]
    B --> B2[ToA]
    B --> B3[TDoA]
    B --> B4[AoA]
    C --> C1[Centroid]
    C --> C2[DV Hop]
    C --> C3[APIT]
    C --> C4[Amorphous]
    D --> D1[Weighted Centroid]
    D --> D2[Semi Range Free]
    B1 --> E1[Distance from Path Loss]
    B2 --> E2[Distance from Time]
    B3 --> E3[Distance from Time Diff]
    B4 --> E4[Angle from Antenna Array]
    C2 --> F1[Hop Count Propagation]
    C2 --> F2[Average Hop Size]
    C2 --> F3[Trilateration]
    C3 --> G1[Triangle Test]
    C3 --> G2[Region Intersection]
```

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Localization System

```mermaid
graph LR
    S1[Stimulus: Unknown Node Powers On] --> S2[Anchor Discovery Phase]
    S2 --> S3[Measurement Phase: RSSI or ToA or TDoA or AoA]
    S3 --> S4[Distance or Angle Estimation Module]
    S4 --> S5[Position Computation Engine: Trilateration or Multilateration]
    S5 --> S6[Refinement and Filtering: Kalman Filter or Least Squares]
    S6 --> S7[Final Position Estimate Output]
    S7 --> S8[Validation Against Error Threshold]
    S8 -->|Accept| S9[Store Position and Report to Sink]
    S8 -->|Reject| S2
    A1[Anchor A1 Position] --> S3
    A2[Anchor A2 Position] --> S3
    A3[Anchor A3 Position] --> S3
    A4[Anchor A4 Position] --> S3
    DB[Anchor Position Database] --> S5
    CLK[Synchronized Clock for ToA] --> S3
    ANT[Antenna Array for AoA] --> S3
```

---

## 4.3 Trilateration Geometry Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    P0[Unknown Node U at x y] --> P1[Step 1: Measure d1 from Anchor A1]
    P1 --> P2[Step 2: Measure d2 from Anchor A2]
    P2 --> P3[Step 3: Measure d3 from Anchor A3]
    P3 --> P4[Step 4: Form Circle of Radius d1 around A1]
    P4 --> P5[Step 5: Form Circle of Radius d2 around A2]
    P5 --> P6[Step 6: Form Circle of Radius d3 around A3]
    P6 --> P7[Step 7: Circles Intersect at Unique Point U]
    P7 --> P8[Step 8: Solve Linear System A p = b]
    P8 --> P9[Output Estimated Position xhat yhat]
```

---

## 4.4 DV-Hop Three-Stage Process

```mermaid
graph TD
    ST0[Network Initialization] --> ST1[Stage 1: Anchor Floods Position and Hop Count 0]
    ST1 --> ST2[Each Node Records Min Hop Count to Each Anchor]
    ST2 --> ST3[Stage 2: Each Anchor Computes Average Hop Size]
    ST3 --> ST4[Anchor Floods Hop Size Across Network]
    ST4 --> ST5[Each Unknown Node Receives Hop Sizes]
    ST5 --> ST6[Stage 3: Estimate Distance = Hop Count times Hop Size]
    ST6 --> ST7[Apply Trilateration to Estimate Position]
    ST7 --> ST8[Output Estimated Position]
```

---

## 4.5 Ranging Technique Decision Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    Q1{Need High Accuracy?} -->|Yes| Q2{Hardware Budget Available?}
    Q1 -->|No| RF[Use Range Free: Centroid or DV Hop]
    Q2 -->|Yes: Array Available| AOA[Use AoA]
    Q2 -->|No| Q3{Clock Synchronization Possible?}
    Q3 -->|Yes: Nanosecond| TOA[Use ToA]
    Q3 -->|No| Q4{Two Signals Different Speeds?}
    Q4 -->|Yes: RF + Acoustic| TDOA[Use TDoA]
    Q4 -->|No| RSSI[Use RSSI]
    RF --> OUT[Output: Selected Localization Method]
    AOA --> OUT
    TOA --> OUT
    TDOA --> OUT
    RSSI --> OUT
```

## 4.6 GPS Constellation Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    GPS[GPS Localization System] --> SAT[Satellite Segment: 24 to 32 MEO Satellites]
    GPS --> CTRL[Control Segment: Ground Stations]
    GPS --> USR[User Segment: Receiver Node]
    SAT --> S1[Each Satellite Transmits Almanac and Ephemeris]
    S1 --> S2[Signal Includes Timestamp and Orbital Position]
    USR --> R1[Receiver Measures Pseudorange to 4 or More Satellites]
    R1 --> R2[Receiver Solves 4 Equations for x y z and Clock Bias]
    CTRL --> C1[Monitors Satellite Health and Clock Drift]
    C1 --> S1
```
