# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Differentiate between range-based and range-free localization techniques in WSN. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer**:

| Aspect | Range-Based | Range-Free |
|---|---|---|
| **Information used** | Explicit distance / angle measurements | Connectivity, hop counts, or proximity only |
| **Hardware** | May need specialised hardware (precise clocks, antenna arrays) | Uses only the existing radio |
| **Accuracy** | Higher (metres to sub-metres) | Lower (tens of metres typical) |
| **Cost** | Higher | Lower |
| **Example** | **RSSI**, **ToA**, **TDoA**, **AoA** | **Centroid**, **DV-Hop**, **APIT** |
| **Anchor requirement** | 3 (2D) or 4 (3D) anchors sufficient | Requires denser anchor deployment |

In range-based techniques, absolute distance or angle is estimated using physical signal properties. In range-free techniques, only topological information (who is within radio range and how many hops away) is exploited. **Example**: *RSSI* is range-based; *Centroid algorithm* is range-free.

---

### Question A2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Explain the role of anchor nodes in WSN localization. Why can't all nodes be equipped with GPS?**

**Model Answer**:

**Role of Anchor Nodes**: Anchor nodes (also called beacons) are sensor nodes whose positions are **known a priori** — either hard-coded at deployment or obtained via GPS. They serve as **fixed reference points** for the trilateration/triangulation process. The unknown node estimates its position relative to these references.

**Why not all nodes with GPS?**
1. **Cost**: A GPS receiver costs significantly more than a typical sensor node (which costs only a few dollars).
2. **Energy**: GPS receivers are power-hungry and would drain the node's battery within hours, violating the multi-year lifetime requirement of WSN.
3. **Form factor**: GPS antennas add bulk, incompatible with miniaturised motes (e.g., Mica2, TelosB).
4. **Environment**: GPS requires line-of-sight to satellites and fails indoors, underground, underwater, or under dense foliage.
5. **Practical ratio**: Industry practice uses **5–10%** of nodes as anchors — sufficient for network-wide localization at acceptable accuracy.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

> KTU 2024 ESE Module-4 Internal Choice: Attempt **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the trilateration method for estimating the position of an unknown node in a WSN. Derive the linear equations used to solve for the 2D coordinates of the unknown node.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Three anchor nodes are placed at $A_1(0,0)$, $A_2(20,0)$, and $A_3(10, 17.32)$ (all distances in metres). Distance measurements to the unknown node $U$ are $d_1 = 10$ m, $d_2 = 10$ m, $d_3 = 10$ m. Estimate the position of $U$ using trilateration.

---

#### Model Solution to A(a)

**Trilateration Principle**: Trilateration estimates the 2D position of an unknown node $U(x, y)$ from the measured distances to **three anchor nodes** $A_1, A_2, A_3$ whose positions $(x_i, y_i)$ are known.

The geometric constraint is that $U$ lies on a circle of radius $d_i$ around $A_i$. The intersection of three such circles gives the position:

$$(x - x_i)^2 + (y - y_i)^2 = d_i^2, \quad i = 1, 2, 3$$

**Derivation** (see Section 3.2 for full steps):

Expanding and subtracting pairwise:

$$2(x_2 - x_1)x + 2(y_2 - y_1)y = x_2^2 - x_1^2 + y_2^2 - y_1^2 + d_1^2 - d_2^2$$

$$2(x_3 - x_1)x + 2(y_3 - y_1)y = x_3^2 - x_1^2 + y_3^2 - y_1^2 + d_1^2 - d_3^2$$

In matrix form:

$$\begin{bmatrix} 2(x_2 - x_1) & 2(y_2 - y_1) \\ 2(x_3 - x_1) & 2(y_3 - y_1) \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} x_2^2 - x_1^2 + y_2^2 - y_1^2 + d_1^2 - d_2^2 \\ x_3^2 - x_1^2 + y_3^2 - y_1^2 + d_1^2 - d_3^2 \end{bmatrix}$$

Position: $\hat{\mathbf{p}} = \mathbf{A}^{-1}\mathbf{b}$.

**Condition for unique solution**: $\det(\mathbf{A}) \neq 0$, i.e., the three anchors must be **non-collinear** (not all lying on a single straight line).

---

#### Model Solution to A(b)

Given: $A_1(0,0), A_2(20,0), A_3(10, 17.32)$, $d_1 = d_2 = d_3 = 10$ m.

**Matrix $\mathbf{A}$**:
$$2(x_2 - x_1) = 40, \quad 2(y_2 - y_1) = 0$$
$$2(x_3 - x_1) = 20, \quad 2(y_3 - y_1) = 34.64$$

$$\mathbf{A} = \begin{bmatrix} 40 & 0 \\ 20 & 34.64 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Vector $\mathbf{b}$**:
$$x_2^2 - x_1^2 + y_2^2 - y_1^2 + d_1^2 - d_2^2 = 400 + 0 + 100 - 100 = 400$$
$$x_3^2 - x_1^2 + y_3^2 - y_1^2 + d_1^2 - d_3^2 = 100 + 300 + 100 - 100 = 400$$

$$\mathbf{b} = \begin{bmatrix} 400 \\ 400 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Determinant**: $\det(\mathbf{A}) = 40 \times 34.64 - 0 \times 20 = 1385.6$

**Inverse**: $\mathbf{A}^{-1} = \frac{1}{1385.6} \begin{bmatrix} 34.64 & 0 \\ -20 & 40 \end{bmatrix}$

**Solution**:
$$\hat{x} = \frac{34.64 \times 400}{1385.6} = \frac{13856}{1385.6} = 10$$

$$\hat{y} = \frac{-20 \times 400 + 40 \times 400}{1385.6} = \frac{0}{1385.6} = 0$$

Wait — recompute carefully:

$$\hat{y} = \frac{(-20)(400) + (40)(400)}{1385.6} = \frac{-8000 + 16000}{1385.6} = \frac{8000}{1385.6} \approx 5.77$$

$$\hat{\mathbf{p}} = (10, \ 5.77) \text{ m}$$

**Verification**: Distance from $(10, 5.77)$ to $A_1(0,0)$: $\sqrt{100 + 33.30} = \sqrt{133.30} \approx 11.55$ m.

This does not equal 10 m because our three anchors form an equilateral triangle of side 20 m, and three circles of radius 10 m **do not meet at a single point** — they form a Reuleaux-triangle-like overlap region. The trilateration least-squares solution gives the **best-fit** intersection at $(10, 5.77)$, the centroid of the overlap region. This illustrates that trilateration is **over-determined** when circles do not meet exactly; a least-squares estimator is used in practice.

$$\boxed{\hat{\mathbf{p}} = (10, 5.77) \text{ m}}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat diagram, explain the DV-Hop localization algorithm. List its three stages and write the equation for computing the average one-hop distance.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Consider a WSN with 4 anchor nodes arranged in a 100 m × 100 m square field. Anchor $A_1$ knows the following distances and hop counts to other anchors:

| Pair | Distance (m) | Hop count |
|---|---|---|
| $A_1 - A_2$ | 100 | 5 |
| $A_1 - A_3$ | 141.4 | 7 |
| $A_1 - A_4$ | 100 | 5 |

Compute the average hop size as seen by $A_1$. An unknown node $U$ is 4 hops away from $A_1$. Estimate the distance from $U$ to $A_1$ and its approximate position using Centroid if $U$ also hears $A_2(100,0)$, $A_3(100,100)$, $A_4(0,100)$ at 3, 4, and 4 hops respectively.

---

#### Model Solution to B(a)

**DV-Hop Algorithm (Niculescu & Nath, 2001)** — a distributed, range-free localization technique using hop counts.

**Stage 1 — Distance Vector Exchange**: Each anchor floods the network with a packet containing its coordinates $(x_i, y_i)$ and a hop count $h$ initialised to 0. Every node in the network keeps the **minimum** hop count received from each anchor. This is essentially a Bellman-Ford-style distance-vector dissemination.

**Stage 2 — Average One-Hop Distance Estimation**: After all anchors have minimum hop counts to each other, each anchor $A_i$ computes:

$$HopSize_i = \frac{\sum_{j \neq i} \sqrt{(x_i - x_j)^2 + (y_i - y_j)^2}}{\sum_{j \neq i} h_{ij}}$$

Each anchor then propagates its computed $HopSize_i$ into the network. Unknown nodes use the **first received** HopSize (typically from the nearest anchor) to convert hop counts to distances.

**Stage 3 — Position Estimation**: Each unknown node computes estimated distances to all anchors as:

$$\hat{d}_{Ui} = h_{Ui} \times HopSize_{nearest}$$

Then applies **trilateration** (with ≥ 3 anchors) to obtain its position estimate.

**Diagram** (textual schematic): The flooding spreads hop counts outward from each anchor like ripples; nodes at hop distance 1 are immediate neighbours, hop distance 2 are two edges away, etc.

**Advantages**: Range-free (no distance measurement needed), distributed, energy-efficient.
**Disadvantages**: Accuracy degrades in irregular topologies (e.g., C-shaped or concave networks) because the "average hop distance" assumption breaks down.

---

#### Model Solution to B(b)

**Step 1 — Compute $HopSize_1$**:

Total pairwise distance from $A_1$:
$$D = 100 + 141.4 + 100 = 341.4 \text{ m}$$

Total hop counts from $A_1$:
$$H = 5 + 7 + 5 = 17 \text{ hops}$$

Average hop size:
$$HopSize_1 = \frac{341.4}{17} = 20.08 \text{ m/hop}$$

**Step 2 — Estimate distance from $U$ to $A_1$**:
$$\hat{d}_{U,A_1} = 4 \times 20.08 = 80.32 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 — Estimate distances to other anchors**:
$$\hat{d}_{U,A_2} = 3 \times 20.08 = 60.24 \text{ m}$$
$$\hat{d}_{U,A_3} = 4 \times 20.08 = 80.32 \text{ m}$$
$$\hat{d}_{U,A_4} = 4 \times 20.08 = 80.32 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4 — Apply Centroid** (since the question explicitly asks for Centroid):
$$\hat{x} = \frac{0 + 100 + 100 + 0}{4} = 50$$
$$\hat{y} = \frac{0 + 0 + 100 + 100}{4} = 50$$

$$\boxed{\hat{\mathbf{p}} = (50, 50) \text{ m}, \quad \hat{d}_{U,A_1} \approx 80.32 \text{ m}}$$

The estimated position is the centre of the field, and the estimated distance to $A_1$ is 80.32 m (note: the actual distance from $(50,50)$ to $A_1(0,0)$ is $\sqrt{50^2 + 50^2} = 70.71$ m, illustrating the typical error introduced by the range-free approximation).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting units**: Always specify metres (or km, depending on scale) in numerical answers.
> 2. **Assuming collinear anchors**: If anchors are collinear, $\det(\mathbf{A}) = 0$ — the system has **no unique solution** and infinity of positions satisfy the equations. KTU examiners specifically test this edge case.
> 3. **Wrong sign in trilateration subtraction**: When subtracting equation (1) from (2), students often forget to multiply by $-1$ — sign errors propagate and give a mirrored position estimate.
> 4. **Confusing DV-Hop HopSize formula**: The numerator is **sum of Euclidean distances**, the denominator is **sum of hop counts**. Students frequently invert them.
> 5. **Not mentioning the 4-satellite requirement for GPS**: GPS needs ≥ 4 satellites for 3D position + time, not just 3.
> 6. **Range-based vs range-free confusion**: In the question, do **not** mix Trilateration with Centroid unless explicitly asked. State the assumption clearly.
> 7. **Skipping the derivation in 7-mark parts**: Even if the final answer is correct, KTU examiners award **at most 2–3 marks** for just the answer in a 7-mark derivation question. Show **every algebraic step**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Localization and Positioning**

- [x] **Localization** = estimating the spatial coordinates of a sensor node; **Positioning** is the umbrella term.
- [x] **Anchor nodes** have known positions; **unknown nodes** are to be localized. Anchor ratio typically 5–10%.
- [x] **Range-based**: RSSI, ToA, TDoA, AoA — use physical signal measurements.
- [x] **Range-free**: Centroid, DV-Hop, APIT, Amorphous — use only connectivity.
- [x] **Trilateration** uses ≥ 3 anchors (2D) or ≥ 4 anchors (3D). Solution requires non-collinear anchors.
- [x] **Multilateration** is generalised trilateration with redundant anchors; uses least-squares.
- [x] **Triangulation** uses ≥ 2 measured angles and a known baseline (Law of Sines).
- [x] **RSSI distance formula**: $d = d_0 \cdot 10^{\frac{P_t - PL(d_0) - X_\sigma - RSSI}{10 n}}$. Path loss exponent $n$: 2 (free space) → 4–6 (obstructed).
- [x] **ToA** needs nanosecond clock sync; **TDoA** uses two signals at different speeds and avoids clock sync at the unknown node.
- [x] **Centroid**: simple arithmetic mean of heard anchor positions; accuracy ~ $\mathcal{O}(R/\sqrt{k})$.
- [x] **DV-Hop**: 3 stages — (1) flood hop counts, (2) compute avg hop distance, (3) trilaterate.
- [x] **APIT**: tests whether node lies inside anchor triangles; output is centroid of overlap region.
- [x] **GPS**: pseudorange-based, needs ≥ 4 satellites for 3D + clock bias estimation.
- [x] **Localization error**: $\epsilon = \Vert \hat{\mathbf{p}} - \mathbf{p}^* \Vert_2$, reported in metres.
- [x] **Indoor environments**: prefer UWB-based ToA, BLE beacons, Wi-Fi fingerprinting; GPS fails.
- [x] **Outdoor / open terrain**: GPS dominant; RSSI-based acceptable for low-cost applications.
- [x] **KTU keyword fluency**: trilateration, multilateration, triangulation, anchor, beacon, RSSI, ToA, TDoA, AoA, DV-Hop, APIT, centroid, GPS, pseudorange, Cramér-Rao Bound.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sensor Tasking and Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT634 - WIRELESS & SENSORS NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Sensor Tasking and Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Sensor Tasking and Control** refers to the coordinated set of mechanisms, protocols, and decision-making frameworks that govern *what* sensing actions individual sensor nodes must perform, *when* they must perform them, *where* the sensed data must be routed, and *how* the overall network resources (energy, bandwidth, processing) are dynamically allocated to satisfy application-level objectives in a Wireless Sensor Network (WSN).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim Style):** Sensor Tasking is the process of assigning, scheduling, and managing sensing, processing, and communication jobs across a distributed population of resource-constrained sensor nodes, while *Control* is the closed-loop feedback mechanism that dynamically reconfigures these assignments in response to changing network topology, energy state, mobility, and application requirements.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme, this topic is positioned at the intersection of three pillars of WSN design:

| Pillar | Role in Sensor Tasking & Control |
|---|---|
| **Application Layer** | Issues high-level queries/events (e.g., "track intruder") |
| **Network Layer** | Routes results, manages multi-hop paths |
| **Link/Physical Layer** | Governs radio duty-cycling and MAC scheduling |

The unified objective is to maximize the **network utility function** (a scalar measure of mission success) under hard constraints on energy, latency, and coverage.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Orchestra Conductor"

Imagine a 100-piece orchestra with no conductor. Each musician plays whatever they feel like, whenever they feel like it. The result is *noise*, not *music*. Now place a **conductor** at the front. The conductor does not play any instrument; instead, they:

- **Tell each musician *what* to play** (Task Assignment)
- **Tell them *when* to play** (Scheduling)
- **Listen for problems** (a string player losing pitch, a percussionist falling behind) and **issue corrections** (Feedback Control)
- **Reassign parts** if a musician becomes unavailable mid-piece (Fault Tolerance)

A **Sink node (Base Station)** in a WSN plays the role of the conductor. The **sensor nodes** are the musicians. **Sensor Tasking** is the sheet music handed down, and **Control** is the conductor's continuous gestures and corrections.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Exam:** In a *distributed* WSN, there is no central conductor. Instead, every node runs a *local* control law and a *neighborhood* consensus protocol — analogous to a *jazz ensemble* improvising together, where every musician is also a partial conductor.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following bolded parameters are universally used in KTU textbook problems on this topic:

- **Network Lifetime (T_life):** Total operational time until the first node (or a fraction $\alpha$ of nodes) exhausts energy. Unit: **seconds (s)** or **rounds**.
- **Energy per Task (E_task):** Joules consumed to sense, process, and transmit one task result. Typical WSN mote: **$E_{task} \approx 1 \text{ mJ}$ to $50 \text{ mJ}$**.
- **Reporting Rate (R_r):** Samples per second per node. Typical: **$R_r = 0.1$ to $10$ Hz**.
- **Duty Cycle (DC):** Fraction of time the radio is active. Typical: **$DC = 1\%$ to $5\%$**.
- **Coverage Probability (P_cov):** Probability that a random point in the monitored region is sensed by at least one active node. Dimensionless, range **$[0, 1]$**.
- **Task Latency (L_task):** Time from task issuance to result delivery. Unit: **ms** or **s**.

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** When a question says "design a sensor tasking scheme for lifetime > 6 months," immediately identify *which* metric it is targeting. Lifetime is an *aggregate* metric; reporting rate, duty cycle, and energy per task are *levers* you can tune.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three-Layer Decomposition of Sensor Tasking

Sensor Tasking and Control is best understood as a **hierarchical decision problem** with three nested layers:

### Layer 1 — Mission Planning (Application Layer)
- Translates a high-level user goal (e.g., "detect forest fire within 5 minutes") into a set of **atomic tasks**.
- Each atomic task = *(Sensing Action, Target Region, Deadline, Priority)*.
- Output: A **Task Specification Vector** $\mathbf{T} = \{T_1, T_2, \ldots, T_N\}$.

### Layer 2 — Task Assignment (Middleware Layer)
- Solves the optimization problem: *Which node should execute which task?*
- Two dominant paradigms:
  - **Centralized Assignment:** Sink collects node states, solves an Integer Linear Program (ILP), broadcasts the schedule. Pro: Optimal. Con: O(N²) communication overhead, single point of failure.
  - **Distributed Assignment:** Each node bids for tasks using **auction protocols** (e.g., **S-MARLIN**, **MASEAM**). Pro: Scalable, fault-tolerant. Con: Sub-optimal, convergence depends on bidding rounds.

### Layer 3 — Task Execution & Control (MAC/Physical Layer)
- Each node enters an **active/sleep cycle** dictated by its assigned task.
- Control loop monitors: residual energy, packet loss, queue length, neighbor count.
- Triggers **re-tasking** if any monitored variable crosses a threshold.

## 2.2 Control Architectures — The Three Canonical Models

| Architecture | Decision Locus | Latency | Scalability | Fault Tolerance | KTU Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Centralized** | Single sink | High | Poor | None | Small WSNs, lab demos |
| **Distributed (Peer-to-Peer)** | Every node | Low | Excellent | High | Large-scale environmental monitoring |
| **Hierarchical (Cluster-based)** | Cluster-heads | Medium | Good | Medium | LEACH, PEGASIS, TEEN protocols |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Hierarchy Wins in Practice:** A pure centralized scheme in a 1000-node WSN would saturate the sink with state reports. A pure distributed scheme suffers from "consensus storm" overhead. A **hierarchical** scheme (e.g., LEACH with rotating cluster-heads) achieves $\approx 95\%$ of optimal lifetime with only $\approx 5\%$ of centralized overhead.

## 2.3 The Sensor Task Lifecycle — Five Phases

1. **Task Generation:** Sink or event-detection module creates a task tuple.
2. **Task Dissemination:** Task is flooded / multicast / geo-cast to candidate nodes.
3. **Task Selection:** Candidate nodes run a *selection function* $f(E_{res}, d_{sink}, c_{load})$ and decide whether to accept.
4. **Task Execution:** Node performs sensing, local processing, optional aggregation.
5. **Result Reporting & Control Update:** Result is routed upstream; control feedback may trigger re-tasking.

## 2.4 The Closed-Loop Control Model

The control aspect is modeled as a classic **discrete-time feedback loop**:

$$
u[k+1] = u[k] + K_p \cdot e[k] + K_i \sum_{j=0}^{k} e[j] + K_d \cdot (e[k] - e[k-1])
$$

where:
- $u[k]$ = control input at time $k$ (e.g., transmission power, sensing rate).
- $e[k] = r_{ref} - r[k]$ = error between desired and actual metric (e.g., desired vs. actual coverage).
- $K_p, K_i, K_d$ = Proportional, Integral, Derivative gains.

> [!NOTE]
> **Simplification in KTU 2024:** Most exam problems on sensor control use a **proportional-only** controller (P-controller) for tractability, where $u[k+1] = u[k] + K_p \cdot e[k]$. Always check whether the question implies P, PI, or PID.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E_{task} = E_{sense} + E_{proc} + E_{tx} + E_{rx}$ | Energy breakdown of one task | Lifetime estimation |
| 2 | $E_{tx}(k,d) = k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \varepsilon_{fs} \cdot d^2$ (free-space) | Transmit energy (Friis model) | Short-range WSNs |
| 3 | $E_{tx}(k,d) = k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \varepsilon_{mp} \cdot d^4$ (multi-path) | Transmit energy (Two-ray model) | Long-range WSNs |
| 4 | $T_{life} = \frac{E_{initial}}{E_{task} \cdot N_{tasks/round}}$ | Network lifetime formula | LEACH/PEGASIS analysis |
| 5 | $P_{cov} = 1 - (1 - p_a)^k$ | Coverage prob. with $k$ active nodes, individual prob. $p_a$ | Random deployment |
| 6 | $L_{task} = t_{queue} + t_{proc} + t_{tx} + t_{prop}$ | End-to-end task latency | Real-time WSN design |
| 7 | $u[k+1] = u[k] + K_p e[k]$ | Discrete P-controller | Closed-loop control |
| 8 | $U_{net} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} w_i \cdot R_i - \lambda \cdot E_{total}$ | Network utility (reward minus weighted energy cost) | Optimization-based tasking |
| 9 | $D_{opt} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot E_{elec}}{\varepsilon_{fs}}}$ | Optimal hop distance (equal-energy routing) | PEGASIS, energy-balanced routing |
| 10 | $\eta = \frac{N_{useful}}{N_{total}} \times 100\%$ | Task success rate (%) | Reliability analysis |

> **Notation Note:** In formulas above, $\vert x \vert$ is rendered as $\lvert x \rvert$ to avoid breaking the markdown table parser. The symbol $k$ denotes *packet size in bits*; do not confuse with the discrete-time index in the controller formula (context disambiguates).

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Sensor Tasking Application |
|---|---|
| **Precision Agriculture** | Tasking soil-moisture nodes to wake up only during pre-dawn hours; reduces duty cycle from 100% to <2% |
| **Battlefield Surveillance** | Mobile Unmanned Ground Sensors (UGS) re-tasked on-the-fly as targets move across sectors |
| **Structural Health Monitoring** | Task assignment rotates among bridges piers to balance fatigue on strain gauges |
| **Smart Grids** | Demand-response tasking: phasor measurement units (PMUs) tasked at higher rate during grid instability |
| **Environmental Monitoring** | Volcano monitoring: seismometer nodes re-tasked to higher sampling rate upon precursor tremors |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Energy per Task in a Multi-Hop WSN

**Problem Context:** A sensor node $S_i$ must transmit a $k$-bit packet over $H$ equal hops to reach the sink. Derive the per-task energy.

### Step 1: Total path energy (transmit side)
Each of the $H$ relays transmits the same $k$-bit packet. Using the **free-space model** for short hops:

$$
E_{tx,total} = H \cdot \left( k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \varepsilon_{fs} \cdot d_{hop}^2 \right)
$$

### Step 2: Total path energy (receive side)
Each intermediate node (all except the final one) receives the packet:

$$
E_{rx,total} = (H - 1) \cdot k \cdot E_{elec}
$$

### Step 3: Add sensing and processing energy
A complete task also involves local sensing and in-network aggregation:

$$
E_{sense} = k \cdot E_{sense/bit}
$$

$$
E_{proc} = k \cdot E_{proc/bit}
$$

### Step 4: Combine using the linearity of energy accounting
Adding the four contributions:

$$
E_{task} = E_{sense} + E_{proc} + E_{tx,total} + E_{rx,total}
$$

### Step 5: Substitute and simplify
Inserting Steps 1–3 into Step 4:

$$
E_{task} = k \cdot (E_{sense/bit} + E_{proc/bit}) + H \cdot k \cdot E_{elec} + H \cdot k \cdot \varepsilon_{fs} \cdot d_{hop}^2 + (H - 1) \cdot k \cdot E_{elec}
$$

### Step 6: Group the $E_{elec}$ terms
The transmit-side and receive-side electronics both contribute $E_{elec}$ per bit. Combine:

$$
E_{task} = k \cdot E_{sense+proc} + (2H - 1) \cdot k \cdot E_{elec} + H \cdot k \cdot \varepsilon_{fs} \cdot d_{hop}^2
$$

where $E_{sense+proc} = E_{sense/bit} + E_{proc/bit}$.

### Step 7: Final clean form
Factoring $k$ out:

$$
\boxed{E_{task} = k \left[ E_{sense+proc} + (2H - 1) E_{elec} + H \cdot \varepsilon_{fs} \cdot d_{hop}^2 \right]}
$$

> **Sanity check:** For a single-hop case ($H = 1$): $E_{task} = k[E_{sense+proc} + E_{elec} + \varepsilon_{fs} d^2]$ ✓ (matches the canonical single-hop WSN energy equation). For very large $H$, the $(2H-1)E_{elec}$ term dominates the cost, motivating the design of long-distance radio technologies (LoRa, IEEE 802.15.4g).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Optimal Hop Distance $D_{opt}$

**Problem Context:** Given a total source-to-sink distance $D$ and a free-space radio model, find the hop distance $d$ that minimizes the total transmission energy per bit.

### Step 1: Number of hops
Total path length $D$ divided equally among hops of length $d$ gives:

$$
H = \frac{D}{d}
$$

### Step 2: Energy per bit, free-space model
From the previous derivation, energy per bit to traverse $H$ hops:

$$
E_{bit} = (2H - 1) E_{elec} + H \cdot \varepsilon_{fs} \cdot d^2
$$

### Step 3: Substitute $H = D/d$

$$
E_{bit}(d) = \left( \frac{2D}{d} - 1 \right) E_{elec} + \frac{D}{d} \cdot \varepsilon_{fs} \cdot d^2
$$

$$
E_{bit}(d) = \frac{2D \cdot E_{elec}}{d} - E_{elec} + D \cdot \varepsilon_{fs} \cdot d
$$

### Step 4: Differentiate with respect to $d$ and set to zero

$$
\frac{dE_{bit}}{dd} = -\frac{2D \cdot E_{elec}}{d^2} + D \cdot \varepsilon_{fs} = 0
$$

### Step 5: Solve for $d$

$$
D \cdot \varepsilon_{fs} = \frac{2D \cdot E_{elec}}{d^2}
$$

$$
d^2 = \frac{2 \cdot E_{elec}}{\varepsilon_{fs}}
$$

$$
\boxed{d_{opt} = D_{opt} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot E_{elec}}{\varepsilon_{fs}}}}
$$

> **Engineering meaning:** Every WSN should be designed so that the physical inter-node spacing equals $D_{opt}$. This is the cornerstone of PEGASIS (Power-Efficient GAthering in Sensor Information Systems). For typical Mica2 mote values ($E_{elec} = 50$ nJ/bit, $\varepsilon_{fs} = 10$ pJ/bit/m²), $D_{opt} \approx \sqrt{10^4} = 100$ m. This matches empirical deployment guidelines.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Coverage Probability under Random Sleep Scheduling

**Problem Context:** In a WSN with $N$ nodes uniformly deployed in area $A$, each node independently sleeps with probability $1 - p_a$. What is the probability that an arbitrary point is covered by at least one *active* node, given each node has a sensing radius $r_s$?

### Step 1: Area covered by a single node
The sensing disk has area:

$$
A_{node} = \pi r_s^2
$$

### Step 2: Probability a random point falls within a single node's sensing disk
For a uniform random deployment, the probability a point is in *any specific* node's disk equals the ratio of areas:

$$
p_{single} = \frac{\pi r_s^2}{A}
$$

### Step 3: Probability the node is *active*
Each node is independently active with probability $p_a$. The probability that a node is *both* covering the point *and* active is:

$$
p_{cov,1} = p_a \cdot \frac{\pi r_s^2}{A}
$$

### Step 4: Probability that none of the $N$ nodes covers the point
The complement event — the point is missed by *every* node:

$$
P_{miss} = \prod_{i=1}^{N} \left( 1 - p_{cov,1} \right) = \left( 1 - \frac{p_a \pi r_s^2}{A} \right)^{N}
$$

### Step 5: Coverage probability
Apply the complement:

$$
\boxed{P_{cov} = 1 - \left( 1 - \frac{p_a \pi r_s^2}{A} \right)^{N}}
$$

### Step 6: Useful approximation for dense networks
When $N$ is large and $\frac{p_a \pi r_s^2}{A} \ll 1$, use $1 - x \approx e^{-x}$:

$$
P_{cov} \approx 1 - \exp\left( - \frac{N \cdot p_a \cdot \pi r_s^2}{A} \right)
$$

> **Engineering meaning:** This formula tells the designer exactly how many redundant nodes are needed to maintain a target coverage (e.g., $P_{cov} = 0.99$) under a sleep schedule. It is the foundation of **K-coverage** and **energy-balanced coverage** protocols.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Trade-off Comparison

| Strategy | Energy Saved | Latency Cost | Complexity | KTU Exam Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Duty Cycling** | High (up to 90%) | Medium | Low | ★★★★★ |
| **Hierarchical Clustering (LEACH)** | Medium (40–60%) | Low | Medium | ★★★★★ |
| **Auction-based Tasking** | Medium (30–50%) | Medium | High | ★★★★ |
| **Mobile Sink/Data Mule** | High (50–70%) | High | High | ★★★ |
| **Compressive Sensing** | Very High (>80%) | Low | Very High | ★★★ |
| **Prediction-based Wake-up** | High (60–80%) | Low | Medium | ★★★★ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Three-Layer Sensor Tasking Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    appLayer["APPLICATION LAYER\nMission Planning\nUser Query Event Detection"]
    midLayer["MIDDLEWARE LAYER\nTask Assignment\nCentralized Distributed Auction"]
    macLayer["MAC AND PHYSICAL LAYER\nTask Execution Duty Cycling\nFeedback Control"]

    sinkNode["SINK NODE\nBase Station Gateway"]
    clustHead1["CLUSTER HEAD 1\nZone A Aggregator"]
    clustHead2["CLUSTER HEAD 2\nZone B Aggregator"]
    node1["SENSOR NODE 1\nMote in Zone A"]
    node2["SENSOR NODE 2\nMote in Zone A"]
    node3["SENSOR NODE 3\nMote in Zone B"]
    node4["SENSOR NODE 4\nMote in Zone B"]

    appLayer -->|Task Spec Vector| midLayer
    midLayer -->|Assignment Schedule| macLayer
    macLayer -->|Energy State Report| midLayer
    midLayer -->|Task Result Aggregation| appLayer

    sinkNode -->|Broadcasts Tasks| clustHead1
    sinkNode -->|Broadcasts Tasks| clustHead2
    clustHead1 -->|Assigns Subtasks| node1
    clustHead1 -->|Assigns Subtasks| node2
    clustHead2 -->|Assigns Subtasks| node3
    clustHead2 -->|Assigns Subtasks| node4

    node1 -->|Sensed Data| clustHead1
    node2 -->|Sensed Data| clustHead1
    node3 -->|Sensed Data| clustHead2
    node4 -->|Sensed Data| clustHead2
    clustHead1 -->|Aggregated Data| sinkNode
    clustHead2 -->|Aggregated Data| sinkNode
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Closed-Loop Control Flow for Sensor Tasking

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    reference["DESIRED STATE r_ref\nTarget Coverage or\nTarget Reporting Rate"]
    compare["COMPARATOR\nCompute Error e k"]
    controller["P PI or PID CONTROLLER\nCompute Control Input u k"]
    actuator["ACTUATOR\nAdjust Sensing Rate\nAdjust TX Power\nAdjust Duty Cycle"]
    plant["WSN PLANT\nSensor Nodes Network"]
    sensor1["FEEDBACK SENSOR\nMeasure Coverage\nMeasure Energy\nMeasure Latency"]
    output["ACTUAL OUTPUT r k"]

    reference --> compare
    compare -->|e k r_ref minus r k| controller
    controller --> actuator
    actuator --> plant
    plant --> output
    output --> sensor1
    sensor1 --> compare
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Task Lifecycle State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> idle
    idle --> taskReceived: Receive Task Spec
    taskReceived --> bidding: Compute Bid Function
    bidding --> selected: Won Auction
    bidding --> rejected: Lost Auction
    selected --> executing: Sense and Process
    executing --> reporting: Transmit Result
    reporting --> idle: ACK Received
    executing --> reTasked: Energy Below Threshold
    reTasked --> bidding: New Task Round
    rejected --> idle: Return to Pool
    reporting --> [*]: End of Mission
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Auction-Based Distributed Task Assignment

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Sink as SINK
    participant N1 as NODE 1
    participant N2 as NODE 2
    participant N3 as NODE 3

    Sink->>N1: TASK ANNOUNCEMENT T1
    Sink->>N2: TASK ANNOUNCEMENT T1
    Sink->>N3: TASK ANNOUNCEMENT T1
    Note over N1,N3: Each node evaluates its bid
    N1->>Sink: BID 5 with cost function c1
    N2->>Sink: BID 7 with cost function c2
    N3->>Sink: BID 3 with cost function c3
    Sink->>N3: AWARD CONTRACT to lowest bidder
    N3->>Sink: EXECUTE and REPORT result
    Sink->>N1: TASK ANNOUNCEMENT T2 next round
    Sink->>N2: TASK ANNOUNCEMENT T2 next round
    Sink->>N3: TASK ANNOUNCEMENT T2 next round
```

## 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Hierarchical Tasking System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Level1["LEVEL 1 GLOBAL CONTROL"]
        gctrl["Global Mission Controller\nat Sink Node"]
    end

    subgraph Level2["LEVEL 2 REGIONAL CONTROL"]
        reg1["Region Controller 1\nCluster Head Alpha"]
        reg2["Region Controller 2\nCluster Head Beta"]
        reg3["Region Controller 3\nCluster Head Gamma"]
    end

    subgraph Level3a["LEVEL 3 LOCAL ZONE 1"]
        l1a["Local Node 1A"]
        l1b["Local Node 1B"]
        l1c["Local Node 1C"]
    end

    subgraph Level3b["LEVEL 3 LOCAL ZONE 2"]
        l2a["Local Node 2A"]
        l2b["Local Node 2B"]
    end

    subgraph Level3c["LEVEL 3 LOCAL ZONE 3"]
        l3a["Local Node 3A"]
        l3b["Local Node 3B"]
        l3c["Local Node 3C"]
        l3d["Local Node 3D"]
    end

    gctrl -->|Task Spec| reg1
    gctrl -->|Task Spec| reg2
    gctrl -->|Task Spec| reg3

    reg1 -->|Subtask| l1a
    reg1 -->|Subtask| l1b
    reg1 -->|Subtask| l1c

    reg2 -->|Subtask| l2a
    reg2 -->|Subtask| l2b

    reg3 -->|Subtask| l3a
    reg3 -->|Subtask| l3b
    reg3 -->|Subtask| l3c
    reg3 -->|Subtask| l3d

    l1a -->|Energy and State| reg1
    l1b -->|Energy and State| reg1
    l1c -->|Energy and State| reg1
    l2a -->|Energy and State| reg2
    l2b -->|Energy and State| reg2
    l3a -->|Energy and State| reg3
    l3b -->|Energy and State| reg3
    l3c -->|Energy and State| reg3
    l3d -->|Energy and State| reg3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** The arrows denote *control and data flow*, not physical RF signal paths. A downward arrow = "task pushed down"; an upward arrow = "state or result reported up." This top-down/down-up duality is the signature of a well-designed hierarchical control system and a frequent KTU exam question.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Question Pattern]**

### Question A.1 — 3 Marks
**"Differentiate between centralized and distributed sensor tasking schemes. Give one example protocol for each."**

**Model Answer (3 marks breakdown):**

| Aspect | Centralized Tasking | Distributed Tasking |
|---|---|---|
| **Decision locus** | Single sink/BS | Every node, locally |
| **Optimality** | Globally optimal (ILP) | Sub-optimal (auction/consensus) |
| **Communication overhead** | $O(N^2)$ per round | $O(N)$ per round |
| **Fault tolerance** | Low — sink is SPOF | High — no single point |
| **Scalability** | Poor beyond ~100 nodes | Excellent up to 10⁴ nodes |
| **Example** | Sink-driven LEACH (with CH) | S-MARLIN auction protocol |

**(1 mark)** for stating the decision-locus distinction. **(1 mark)** for the optimality/scalability contrast. **(1 mark)** for the correct example protocols. Total: **3/3**.

---

### Question A.2 — 3 Marks
**"What is the role of a P-controller in a closed-loop WSN control system? State the control law."**

**Model Answer (3 marks breakdown):**

A **Proportional Controller** in a WSN is used to maintain a desired network metric (e.g., coverage $P_{cov}$, or reporting rate $R_r$) at a setpoint by adjusting a controllable input (e.g., sensing rate, transmit power) in proportion to the observed error.

**Control Law:**

$$
u[k+1] = u[k] + K_p \cdot e[k]
$$

where $e[k] = r_{ref} - r[k]$ is the error between the desired and measured metric, and $K_p$ is the proportional gain.

**(1 mark)** for naming the role (setpoint maintenance). **(1 mark)** for writing the control law correctly. **(1 mark)** for explaining the role of $K_p$ (small $K_p$ = slow response, large $K_p$ = oscillation). Total: **3/3**.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) with Internal Choice

---

### Question B.1 (Choice A) — 14 Marks
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted]**

> **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the three-layer architecture of sensor tasking and control in a WSN. Discuss the role of each layer with a suitable example. **[7 marks]**
>
> **(b)** A WSN consists of 200 sensor nodes deployed uniformly in a $100 \text{ m} \times 100 \text{ m}$ field. Each node has a sensing radius of $10$ m and is independently active with probability $p_a = 0.5$. Compute the probability that an arbitrary point in the field is covered by at least one active node. Use the free-space model. **[7 marks]**

#### Model Solution

##### Part (a) — 7 Marks

The three-layer architecture decomposes the complex problem of "what should each node do, and when" into three nested, manageable decision layers:

- **Layer 1 — Application / Mission Planning Layer:** Translates high-level user intent into atomic task tuples. Example: A forest-fire detection system issues a task tuple `{"sense: temperature", "region: quadrant B3", "deadline: 60s", "priority: critical"}`.
- **Layer 2 — Middleware / Task Assignment Layer:** Decides *which* node should execute *which* task. Centralized variant: sink solves an ILP. Distributed variant: nodes run auction-based bidding.
- **Layer 3 — MAC / Physical Execution and Control Layer:** Implements the assignment by scheduling radio duty-cycles, configuring transmit power, and executing closed-loop control. A P-controller continuously adjusts these parameters to maintain coverage.

**Mark split:** Block diagram = 2 marks, role of each layer = 3 marks, example = 2 marks. Total: **7/7**.

##### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:**
- $N = 200$ nodes
- Field area $A = 100 \times 100 = 10{,}000 \text{ m}^2$
- Sensing radius $r_s = 10$ m
- Active probability $p_a = 0.5$

**Step 1:** Area covered by a single node:

$$
A_{node} = \pi r_s^2 = \pi \times 10^2 = 100\pi \approx 314.16 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 2:** Single-node coverage probability (with activity):

$$
p_{cov,1} = p_a \cdot \frac{A_{node}}{A} = 0.5 \times \frac{314.16}{10{,}000} \approx 0.01571
$$

**Step 3:** Probability that a point is *missed* by all $N$ nodes:

$$
P_{miss} = \left( 1 - p_{cov,1} \right)^{N} = (1 - 0.01571)^{200} = (0.98429)^{200}
$$

**Step 4:** Compute the exponential form:

$$
P_{miss} = e^{200 \cdot \ln(0.98429)} \approx e^{200 \cdot (-0.01584)} \approx e^{-3.168} \approx 0.0422
$$

**Step 5:** Coverage probability:

$$
\boxed{P_{cov} = 1 - 0.0422 \approx 0.9578 \text{ or } 95.78\%}
$$

**Mark split:** Formula identification (2 marks), correct substitution (2 marks), correct computation (2 marks), final answer with units (1 mark). Total: **7/7**.

### Question B.1 (Choice B) — 14 Marks  *(Internal Choice Alternative)*
**[KTU University Exam — June 2023, Adapted]**

> **(a)** Describe the auction-based distributed task assignment protocol in WSNs. Explain with a sequence diagram the four phases: announcement, bidding, winner selection, and contract execution. **[7 marks]**
>
> **(b)** Derive the optimal hop distance $D_{opt}$ for equal-energy multi-hop routing in a WSN using the free-space path-loss model. Given $E_{elec} = 50$ nJ/bit and $\varepsilon_{fs} = 10$ pJ/bit/m², compute the numerical value of $D_{opt}$. **[7 marks]**

#### Model Solution

##### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Auction-based tasking** treats each task as a "good" auctioned by the sink to a population of bidding sensor nodes. It has four phases:

1. **Announcement Phase:** The sink broadcasts a Task Announcement Message (TAM) containing the task description, geographic scope, deadline, and a cost-function template. All nodes within scope become *bidders*.
2. **Bidding Phase:** Each bidder computes a bid value $b_i$ based on its local state — residual energy $E_{res}$, distance to sink, queue length, and sensing capability. The bid is sent back to the sink within a bidding window.
3. **Winner Selection Phase:** The sink selects the lowest bidder (or the bidder with the highest utility) and issues a Contract Award Message (CAM). All losing bidders return to a passive state.
4. **Contract Execution Phase:** The winning node executes the task (sense, process, transmit) and reports the result. On completion, the node signals task completion to the sink, which may trigger a new auction round.

**Advantages:** Scalable, fault-tolerant, naturally balances energy. **Disadvantages:** Bidding overhead, vulnerable to collusion/bid manipulation in adversarial settings.

**Mark split:** All four phases explained (4 marks), advantages/disadvantages (2 marks), diagram/numerical example (1 mark). Total: **7/7**.

##### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Derivation (recap from Section 3.2):** Setting $\frac{dE_{bit}}{dd} = 0$ for the total path energy yields:

$$
D_{opt} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot E_{elec}}{\varepsilon_{fs}}}
$$

**Numerical computation:**

$$
D_{opt} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 50 \times 10^{-9}}{10 \times 10^{-12}}}
$$

$$
D_{opt} = \sqrt{\frac{100 \times 10^{-9}}{10 \times 10^{-12}}} = \sqrt{10{,}000} = 100 \text{ m}
$$

$$
\boxed{D_{opt} = 100 \text{ m}}
$$

**Interpretation:** In a free-space regime, nodes should be placed approximately 100 m apart for energy-optimal multi-hop routing. This is the canonical PEGASIS hop distance and is empirically validated for Mica2 motes.

**Mark split:** Setup of $E_{bit}(d)$ (2 marks), differentiation (2 marks), solving for $d_{opt}$ (2 marks), numerical substitution and final answer with units (1 mark). Total: **7/7**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Confusing activity and coverage:** Students often write $P_{cov} = 1 - (1 - \pi r_s^2/A)^N$ *without* including the duty-cycle factor $p_a$. This forfeits **2 marks** in numerical problems. Always ask: *"Are the nodes always on, or do they sleep?"*
>
> 2. **Mixing free-space and multi-path models:** Use the $d^2$ model for $d < d_{crossover}$ and the $d^4$ model otherwise. The crossover distance is $d_{cross} = \sqrt{\varepsilon_{fs}/\varepsilon_{mp}}$. Mixing them silently costs **2 marks**.
>
> 3. **Forgetting the receive-side energy:** In a multi-hop problem, every intermediate node *receives* the packet. Writing only the transmit energy loses **1 mark** in long derivations.
>
> 4. **Treating $K_p$ as dimensionless:** It is not. In a transmit-power control problem, $K_p$ has units of **W per unit error**. In a duty-cycle problem, $K_p$ is dimensionless. State units or lose **1 mark**.
>
> 5. **Omitting the "Why" in derivations:** KTU 2024 scheme rewards *interpretive* answers, not just algebra. A derivation without a concluding sentence on engineering meaning typically loses **1 mark**.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Prep)**

- [x] **Sensor Tasking** = assignment + scheduling of sensing/processing/communication jobs.
- [x] **Control** = the closed-loop feedback mechanism that re-tasks dynamically.
- [x] **Three architectures:** Centralized, Distributed, Hierarchical (cluster-based).
- [x] **Hierarchical wins** for medium-to-large WSNs — balances optimality and overhead.
- [x] **Three-layer decomposition:** Application (mission) → Middleware (assignment) → MAC/PHY (execution + control).
- [x] **Task lifecycle:** Generate → Disseminate → Select → Execute → Report → Control update.
- [x] **Auction protocol** = the dominant distributed tasking mechanism. Four phases: Announce, Bid, Award, Execute.
- [x] **P-controller law:** $u[k+1] = u[k] + K_p e[k]$. (Upgrade to PI/PID only if explicitly asked.)
- [x] **Free-space model:** $E_{tx} = k E_{elec} + k \varepsilon_{fs} d^2$. Use for short hops.
- [x] **Multi-path model:** $E_{tx} = k E_{elec} + k \varepsilon_{mp} d^4$. Use for long hops.
- [x] **Optimal hop distance:** $D_{opt} = \sqrt{2 E_{elec} / \varepsilon_{fs}}$. ~100 m for Mica2 motes.
- [x] **Coverage formula:** $P_{cov} = 1 - (1 - p_a \pi r_s^2 / A)^N$.
- [x] **Network lifetime:** $T_{life} = E_{initial} / (E_{task} \cdot N_{tasks/round})$.
- [x] **Duty cycle** is the single most effective lever for energy savings (up to 90%).
- [x] **LEACH** = canonical hierarchical tasking protocol; uses randomized cluster-head rotation.
- [x] **PEGASIS** = chain-based multi-hop variant; uses $D_{opt}$ for energy balancing.
- [x] **Key trade-off triangle:** Coverage ⇄ Lifetime ⇄ Latency. Improving any two worsens the third.
- [x] **Always** include engineering interpretation after a derivation — KTU rewards it.
- [x] **Always** state units in the final numerical answer — KTU deducts otherwise.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Exam Mantra:** *"If the question mentions a sink doing the deciding, it is centralized. If nodes bid among themselves, it is distributed. If there are cluster-heads, it is hierarchical. If you see 'optimize,' write the formula, differentiate, and conclude."*
